topics.title: Fundamentals: Frequency and Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals: Frequency and Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **High Speed Digital Design**, *Frequency* and *Time* form the dual foundational axes upon which all signal integrity, timing analysis, and clock distribution theories are built. **Frequency** is defined as the number of complete cycles of a periodic signal that occur per unit time, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. **Time** in digital design refers to the temporal characterization of signal transitions, including propagation delay, rise time, fall time, setup time, and hold time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The relationship between frequency-domain and time-domain representations of digital signals forms the cornerstone of high-speed design. A signal's rise time determines its highest significant frequency component (bandwidth), which in turn dictates transmission line behavior, crosstalk, and EMI considerations.

The mathematical relationship between the fundamental frequency $f_0$ and the period $T$ of a periodic digital signal is given by:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{T}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool watching waves roll in:
- **Frequency** is like *how many waves crash at your feet per second*. A calm sea has low frequency (0.1 Hz = 1 wave every 10 seconds); choppy waters have high frequency (2 Hz = 2 waves per second).
- **Time** (period) is the *time between two consecutive waves* — the gap between one wave's crest and the next.

In digital circuits, instead of water waves, we have **voltage waves** (square-ish pulses) traveling along copper traces. The faster your digital system operates (higher clock frequency), the *shorter* the time available for signals to settle. This is why "high speed" doesn't just mean "fast clock" — it means the **edges (transitions)** of those clock pulses are so sharp that the signal behaves more like a high-frequency analog wave than a simple on/off digital switch.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in High-Speed Digital Design

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clock Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **MHz**, **GHz** | Oscillations per second of the reference clock |
| Clock Period | $T_{clk}$ | **ns**, **ps** | Duration of one full clock cycle |
| Rise Time | $t_r$ | **ps**, **ns** | Time for signal to transition from 10% to 90% of final value |
| Fall Time | $t_f$ | **ps**, **ns** | Time for signal to transition from 90% to 10% of initial value |
| Propagation Delay | $t_{pd}$ | **ps**, **ns** | Time delay between input transition and output transition |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | **Hz**, **GHz** | Range of frequencies a channel can pass without significant attenuation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant of Note:** The speed of signal propagation in a typical FR4 PCB trace is approximately $v \approx 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s (about half the speed of light, due to the dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r \approx 4$ of FR4).

### 1.4 Frequency Domain vs. Time Domain — The Two Views

A digital signal can be analyzed in two complementary ways:
- **Time Domain:** Shows amplitude vs. time (what you see on an oscilloscope). Reveals rise time, delay, overshoot.
- **Frequency Domain:** Shows amplitude vs. frequency (what you see on a spectrum analyzer). Reveals harmonic content, bandwidth, spectral energy distribution.

The two domains are linked mathematically by the **Fourier Transform** (for aperiodic signals) and the **Fourier Series** (for periodic signals).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** A faster edge (smaller $t_r$) means a **higher** bandwidth. The two are inversely related. This is the single most important concept in high-speed digital design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

**Step 1: The Fundamental Period-Frequency Relationship**

For any periodic signal $x(t)$ with period $T$, the fundamental frequency is the reciprocal of the period:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{T}$$

**Why it matters:** A 1 GHz clock has a period of 1 ns. Every flip-flop must complete its setup, hold, and propagation within fractions of this 1 ns window.

**How it's used:** Timing budgets in synchronous digital systems are directly derived from $T_{clk}$.

---

**Step 2: Harmonic Content of a Periodic Digital Signal**

A perfect square wave (50% duty cycle) is the sum of sinusoids at odd harmonics of the fundamental frequency. The Fourier Series representation is:

$$x(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(2\pi n f_0 t)$$

**Why it matters:** Even an "ideal" digital signal is *not* a single frequency. It contains the fundamental frequency plus all odd harmonics. Higher-frequency harmonics are responsible for the *sharp edges*.

**How it's used:** To approximate a square wave, you sum its odd harmonics. The more harmonics you include, the sharper the edges become.

---

**Step 3: Rise Time — Bandwidth Relationship**

The single most cited relationship in high-speed digital design is the empirical formula linking the 10–90% rise time of a digital signal to its maximum significant frequency (the knee frequency, or bandwidth):

$$BW \approx \frac{0.35}{t_r}$$

**Why it matters:** This is the bridge between time-domain measurements and frequency-domain analysis. An engineer measuring a 100 ps rise time on an oscilloscope can immediately determine that the signal contains significant frequency components up to approximately **3.5 GHz**.

**How it's used:** Channel bandwidth, connector bandwidth, and PCB trace losses are all specified in the frequency domain, but signals are described in the time domain.

---

**Step 4: Propagation Delay and Length**

The time for a signal to traverse a physical length $L$ of transmission medium is:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light in vacuum.

**Why it matters:** At 1 GHz, the clock period is 1 ns. In 1 ns, light travels only 15 cm in vacuum (or about 7.5 cm in FR4). Trace lengths become *electrical lengths* that matter.

---

**Step 5: Wavelength and Critical Length**

The wavelength of a signal in a medium with effective relative permittivity $\varepsilon_{r,eff}$ is:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}$$

A trace is considered *electrically long* (requiring transmission line analysis) when its physical length exceeds $\lambda/10$ or $\lambda/6$ (rule of thumb).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_0 = 1/T$ | Fundamental frequency | Hz | Period-frequency conversion |
| 2 | $T = 1/f_0$ | Period | s | Frequency-period conversion |
| 3 | $x(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{n \text{ odd}} \frac{1}{n} \sin(2\pi n f_0 t)$ | Fourier Series of square wave | V | Harmonic analysis |
| 4 | $BW \approx 0.35 / t_r$ | Rise-time bandwidth | Hz | Edge to bandwidth |
| 5 | $f_{knee} \approx 0.5 / t_r$ | Knee frequency (stricter) | Hz | Conservative BW estimate |
| 6 | $t_{pd} = L/v = L\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/c$ | Propagation delay | s | Trace timing |
| 7 | $v = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | Signal velocity in dielectric | m/s | PCB trace analysis |
| 8 | $\lambda = v/f$ | Wavelength | m | Critical length analysis |
| 9 | $E = h f$ | Photon energy (quantum limit) | J | Optical/EHF limit |
| 10 | $dB = 20 \log_{10}(V_2/V_1)$ | Voltage gain in dB | dB | Loss/attenuation specs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not just academic. A PCB designer uses Formula 4 daily: given a 50 ps edge from a fast SerDes, they know the channel must have at least 7 GHz of bandwidth to pass that edge faithfully. Formula 8 tells them whether a 5 cm trace needs transmission-line treatment at 5 GHz (it does).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **CPU Design:** A 4 GHz processor has a 250 ps clock period. Signal edges of 50 ps require channel bandwidth of 7 GHz.
- **DDR Memory:** DDR5-6400 transfers data at 6.4 GT/s, with edges around 30 ps, requiring 11+ GHz channel bandwidth on the DIMM slots.
- **SerDes Links:** 112 Gbps PAM4 SerDes has symbol rates of 56 GBaud, with fundamental frequencies of ~28 GHz.
- **Cable & Connector Specs:** USB4, HDMI 2.1, and Thunderbolt cables are spec'd by their bandwidth in GHz directly traceable to edge rates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fourier Series of a 50% Duty Cycle Square Wave

**Goal:** Show that a perfect square wave is composed of an infinite sum of odd harmonic sinusoids.

**Setup:** Consider a periodic square wave $x(t)$ with amplitude $\pm A$, period $T$, fundamental angular frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T$, and 50% duty cycle centered at $t=0$.

**Step 1:** Because the signal is odd-symmetric (about $t=0$), the Fourier Series contains only sine terms. The general Fourier Series is:

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t) \right]$$

**Step 2:** Since the signal is odd, $a_0 = 0$ and all $a_n = 0$. We compute only the sine coefficients:

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(t) \sin(n\omega_0 t) \, dt$$

**Step 3:** For a 50% duty cycle square wave alternating between $+A$ and $-A$:

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T/2} A \sin(n\omega_0 t) \, dt - \int_{T/2}^{T} A \sin(n\omega_0 t) \, dt \right]$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the first integral (note: $\int \sin(n\omega_0 t) \, dt = -\frac{\cos(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0}$):

$$\int_{0}^{T/2} A \sin(n\omega_0 t) \, dt = A \left[ -\frac{\cos(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0} \right]_{0}^{T/2}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the limits. Since $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T$, we have $n\omega_0 \cdot T/2 = n\pi$ and $n\omega_0 \cdot 0 = 0$:

$$= A \left[ -\frac{\cos(n\pi)}{n\omega_0} + \frac{1}{n\omega_0} \right] = \frac{A}{n\omega_0} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right]$$

**Step 6:** Similarly evaluate the second integral from $T/2$ to $T$:

$$\int_{T/2}^{T} A \sin(n\omega_0 t) \, dt = \frac{A}{n\omega_0} \left[ \cos(n\pi) - \cos(2n\pi) \right] = \frac{A}{n\omega_0} \left[ \cos(n\pi) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 7:** Subtract and combine:

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{A}{n\omega_0} \left\{ \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right] - \left[ \cos(n\pi) - 1 \right] \right\}$$

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{A}{n\omega_0} \cdot \left[ 2 - 2\cos(n\pi) \right] = \frac{4A}{T \cdot n\omega_0} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right]$$

**Step 8:** Note that $T \cdot \omega_0 = T \cdot 2\pi/T = 2\pi$, so:

$$b_n = \frac{4A}{2\pi n} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right] = \frac{2A}{\pi n} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right]$$

**Step 9:** Analyze the factor $[1 - \cos(n\pi)]$:
- For $n$ even: $\cos(n\pi) = +1$, so $[1 - 1] = 0$. **Even harmonics vanish.**
- For $n$ odd: $\cos(n\pi) = -1$, so $[1 - (-1)] = 2$. **Odd harmonics survive with magnitude $4A/(\pi n)$.**

**Step 10:** Final result for the square wave Fourier Series:

$$x(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n\omega_0 t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega_0 t) + \cdots \right]$$

**Conclusion:** A perfect square wave has zero even harmonics, and odd harmonics roll off as $1/n$ in amplitude. The infinite bandwidth is required for *perfectly sharp* edges — which is why real digital signals have finite bandwidth and consequently finite edge rates.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Rise-Time to Bandwidth Relationship

**Goal:** Show that $BW \approx 0.35/t_r$ for a first-order RC low-pass system.

**Setup:** Consider a step input passed through an RC low-pass filter with transfer function:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 1:** The magnitude response is:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 2:** The 10–90% rise time of an RC low-pass system responding to a step is:

$$t_r = 2.2 \, RC$$

This comes from solving $V(t)/V_{final} = 1 - e^{-t/RC}$ at the 10% and 90% points and taking the difference.

**Step 3:** The $-3$ dB bandwidth (where $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$) occurs when $\omega_{3dB} RC = 1$, i.e.:

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 4:** Eliminate $RC$ from the two equations:

$$RC = \frac{t_r}{2.2} = \frac{1}{2\pi f_{3dB}}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $f_{3dB}$:

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{2.2}{2\pi t_r} = \frac{0.35}{t_r}$$

**Result:** $BW \approx 0.35/t_r$ (or 0.35/Hz·s). For a 100 ps rise time, $BW \approx 3.5$ GHz. For a 1 ns rise time, $BW \approx 350$ MHz.

> [!NOTE]
> This $0.35/t_r$ factor is for a first-order Gaussian or RC system. The more conservative "knee frequency" used in practice is $f_{knee} = 0.5/t_r$ to ensure all meaningful spectral content is preserved.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Time and Frequency Equivalences

| Rise Time $t_r$ | Bandwidth $BW = 0.35/t_r$ | Equivalent Frequency | Example System |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 ns | 35 MHz | FM radio band | Legacy 74-series TTL |
| 1 ns | 350 MHz | UHF TV | 74F, 74AS logic |
| 100 ps | 3.5 GHz | WiFi 5 GHz | Fast CMOS, early SerDes |
| 30 ps | 11.7 GHz | X-band radar | DDR5, PCIe Gen 4 |
| 10 ps | 35 GHz | mmWave | 100G+ SerDes, optical |
| 1 ps | 350 GHz | Sub-mmWave / far IR | Cutting-edge research / THz |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Time–Frequency Domain Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time Domain Signal xt] --> B{Fourier Transform}
    B --> C[Frequency Domain Xf]
    C --> D{Inverse Fourier Transform}
    D --> A
    A --> E[Oscilloscope Display]
    C --> F[Spectrum Analyzer Display]
    A2[Rise Time tr] --> G[BW = 0.35 / tr]
    G --> A3[Bandwidth BW]
    A3 --> H{Channel BW Greater Than BW}
    H -->|Yes| I[Signal Passes Faithfully]
    H -->|No| J[Signal Distorted Edge Slowing]
    K[Clock Period Tclk] --> L[Clock Frequency fclk]
    L --> M[1 / Tclk]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Square Wave Construction from Harmonics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[1st Harmonic: sin wt, Amp 4A/pi] --> SUM((SUM))
    S2[3rd Harmonic: sin 3wt, Amp 4A/3pi] --> SUM
    S3[5th Harmonic: sin 5wt, Amp 4A/5pi] --> SUM
    S4[7th Harmonic: sin 7wt, Amp 4A/7pi] --> SUM
    SN[Higher Odd Harmonics n greater than 7] --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Approximate Square Wave]
    SUM2((SUM)) --> OUT2[Better Approximation]
    S1 --> SUM2
    S2 --> SUM2
    S3 --> SUM2
    S4 --> SUM2
    SN --> SUM2
    SN2[All Higher Harmonics] --> SUM2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Signal Path from Source to Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Digital Driver: Edge Rate tr] --> CH[Channel: Bandwidth BWch]
    CH --> RCV[Receiver: Sampling at fclk]
    SRC -.->|Contains freq up to 0.35/tr| CH
    CH -.->|Attenuates above BWch| RCV
    RCV --> Q[Quality: Eye Diagram]
    CH --> L[Loss: Skin Effect, Dielectric]
    CH --> X[Crosstalk: Near End, Far End]
    CH --> R[Reflections: Impedance Mismatch]
    L --> Q
    X --> Q
    R --> Q
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Frequency-Domain Spectrum of a Square Wave

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0[f0: Fundamental, Amp 4A/pi] --> SP((Spectrum))
    F2[2f0: ZERO Amplitude] --> SP
    F3[3f0: Amp 4A/3pi] --> SP
    F4[4f0: ZERO] --> SP
    F5[5f0: Amp 4A/5pi] --> SP
    F7[7f0: Amp 4A/7pi] --> SP
    FH[HARMONIC ENVELOPE: 1/n Roll Off] --> SP
    SP --> RESULT[Discrete Spectrum: Odd Harmonics Only]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** When asked to "explain the harmonic content of a digital signal," a well-labeled diagram showing only odd harmonics with the $1/n$ roll-off envelope will fetch full marks. Examiners reward the connection between the time-domain waveform and its frequency-domain representation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define rise time and fall time. Why are these parameters critical in high-speed digital design?*

**Model Answer:**
Rise time ($t_r$) is defined as the time taken for a signal to transition from **10% to 90%** of its final steady-state value. Fall time ($t_f$) is the time taken for the signal to transition from **90% to 10%** of its initial value. These parameters are critical because:
1. They determine the **bandwidth** of the signal via $BW = 0.35/t_r$.
2. They control **crosstalk, ringing, and reflections** on interconnects.
3. They set the **timing budget** for setup and hold at the receiver.
4. They govern **EMI/EMC emissions** (sharper edges radiate more).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the relationship between clock frequency and clock period. A system operates at 2.5 GHz. Calculate the clock period.*

**Model Answer:**
The clock frequency and period are reciprocals: $T = 1/f$. For $f = 2.5$ GHz:

$$T = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 400 \text{ ps}$$

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the Fourier series representation of a 50% duty cycle square wave and explain the significance of odd harmonics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a digital signal with a measured rise time of 250 ps on an FR4 PCB ($\varepsilon_r = 4.2$), determine: (i) the signal bandwidth, (ii) the propagation velocity, (iii) the wavelength, and (iv) whether a 5 cm trace must be treated as a transmission line. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *See Section 3.1 above for the full derivation. The final form is:*

$$x(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(2\pi n f_0 t)$$

*Significance of odd harmonics:* (1) Even harmonics vanish due to half-wave symmetry of a 50% duty cycle square wave. (2) The amplitude of odd harmonics rolls off as $1/n$, meaning the fundamental carries the most energy. (3) Higher odd harmonics create the sharp edges; truncating the series produces slower edges. (4) The infinite series is required for perfectly vertical edges; any real signal has finite bandwidth and hence finite edge rates.

**(b)** Given: $t_r = 250$ ps, $\varepsilon_r = 4.2$, $L = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m.

**(i) Bandwidth:**

$$BW = \frac{0.35}{t_r} = \frac{0.35}{250 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.4 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz} = 1.4 \text{ GHz}$$

**(ii) Propagation velocity:**

$$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{4.2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.0494} = 1.464 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Wavelength at the fundamental frequency $BW = 1.4$ GHz:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{1.464 \times 10^8}{1.4 \times 10^9} = 0.1046 \text{ m} = 10.46 \text{ cm}$$

**(iv) Is the 5 cm trace a transmission line?**

A trace is electrically long if $L \geq \lambda/10$:

$$\lambda/10 = 10.46/10 = 1.046 \text{ cm}$$

Since $L = 5$ cm $\gg$ 1.046 cm = $\lambda/10$, the trace **must be treated as a transmission line**, and impedance matching, termination, and controlled-impedance routing are required.

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the time-domain and frequency-domain views of a digital signal. Why does a faster edge rate imply a larger bandwidth? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A clock distribution network operates at 800 MHz on an FR4 board. The signal has a 20–80% rise time of 200 ps. Calculate: (i) the clock period, (ii) the signal bandwidth using the 10–90% approximation, (iii) the propagation delay per cm, and (iv) the maximum unterminated trace length (rule: $L_{max} = t_r \cdot v / 6$). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The **time domain** view plots signal amplitude vs. time on an oscilloscope and reveals edge rates, overshoot, ringing, and timing relationships. The **frequency domain** view plots amplitude (or power) vs. frequency on a spectrum analyzer and reveals the spectral content and energy distribution of the signal. The two are linked by the Fourier transform.

*Why faster edges imply larger bandwidth:* Mathematically, a faster edge has a shorter time-domain feature (smaller $\Delta t$). By the time-frequency uncertainty principle, $\Delta f \cdot \Delta t \geq k$ (a constant). Therefore, a smaller $t_r$ forces a larger $\Delta f$, i.e., larger bandwidth. Equivalently, sharper edges require higher-frequency harmonics to synthesize, and these are precisely what $BW = 0.35/t_r$ quantifies.

**(b)** Given: $f_{clk} = 800$ MHz, $t_r$(20–80%) $= 200$ ps, $\varepsilon_r = 4.2$ (assume FR4), $v = 1.464 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Clock period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{800 \times 10^6} = 1.25 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Bandwidth (10–90% rise time):** Convert 20–80% to 10–90% by multiplying by 1.25 (standard convention):

$$t_{r(10\text{–}90\%)} \approx 1.25 \times 200 = 250 \text{ ps}$$

$$BW = \frac{0.35}{250 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.4 \text{ GHz}$$

**(iii) Propagation delay per cm:**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{1 \text{ cm}}{v} = \frac{0.01}{1.464 \times 10^8} = 68.3 \text{ ps/cm}$$

**(iv) Maximum unterminated trace length:**

$$L_{max} = \frac{t_r \cdot v}{6} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.464 \times 10^8}{6} = \frac{0.0366}{6} = 6.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 6.1 \text{ mm}$$

Any trace longer than ~6 mm at this edge rate should be treated as a transmission line.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students often forget to convert ps to seconds in $BW = 0.35/t_r$ calculations. Always write units explicitly.
> 2. **20–80% vs. 10–90%:** If rise time is given in 20–80%, multiply by 1.25 to get 10–90% before applying the $0.35/t_r$ formula. Failing to do so yields 25% error.
> 3. **Square Wave Spectrum:** Examiners expect *both* "only odd harmonics" *and* the $1/n$ roll-off. A diagram earns more marks than a paragraph.
> 4. **Transmission Line Threshold:** A common error is to claim a trace is short simply because it's "only 5 cm." Always check against $\lambda/10$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Period-Frequency Reciprocity:** $f = 1/T$ — memorize this; it underpins every timing calculation.
- **Rise Time to Bandwidth:** $BW = 0.35/t_r$ is the most-used formula in high-speed design. Pair it with the knee-frequency variant $f_{knee} = 0.5/t_r$ for safety margins.
- **Square Wave Spectrum:** Contains *only odd harmonics* with amplitude roll-off of $1/n$. The $n$-th harmonic is at frequency $n \cdot f_0$ with amplitude $\frac{4A}{\pi n}$.
- **Edge Rate, Not Frequency, Sets Bandwidth:** A 1 MHz clock with 100 ps edges has more high-frequency content than a 100 MHz clock with 10 ns edges.
- **Propagation Velocity in FR4:** $v \approx 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s (rule of thumb). Exact value: $v = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$.
- **Electrical Length Criterion:** Trace length $> \lambda/10$ implies transmission-line behavior. Calculate $\lambda = v/f$ at the *highest significant frequency*, not the clock fundamental.
- **Time-Frequency Duality:** A narrow pulse in time spreads its energy over a wide frequency range. Conversely, a long, slow edge concentrates energy at low frequencies.
- **Units Checklist:** Hz, kHz, MHz, GHz for frequency; ps, ns, μs, ms for time. 1 GHz = 1 ns period. Memorize: 1 ns ↔ 1 GHz ↔ 15 cm in vacuum.
- **Engineering Reality:** The "high speed" threshold is *not* a fixed frequency. It depends on edge rate and trace length. A system at 50 MHz with 1 ns edges on a 20 cm board is "high speed"; a 1 GHz system on a 2 mm die is not.
- **Practical Numbers to Memorize:** $0.35 \approx 2.2/(2\pi)$ (RC rise time), $0.5$ (knee-frequency coefficient), $1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s (FR4 velocity), $\sqrt{4.2} \approx 2.05$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time and Distance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Time and Distance # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Context) In **High Speed Digital Design**, the term **"Time and Distance"** refers to the fundamental relationship between the **physical length of an interconnect** (PCB trace, wire, cable, or package lead) and the **time taken by an electrical signal to traverse that distance**. It is a first-principles concept that governs every timing margin, setup/hold window, and clock-skew calculation in modern digital systems. Mathematically, the time $T$ required for a signal to travel a physical distance $D$ is governed by the **propagation velocity** $v$, which itself depends on the surrounding dielectric medium: $$T = \frac{D}{v}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The first module of PEECT526 builds the physical foundation. "Time and Distance" is the *cornerstone concept* — every later topic (transmission lines, reflections, crosstalk, clock distribution) is built on top of understanding how long signals take to physically move from point A to point B on a board. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡 Imagine you are standing at one end of a long football field (≈ 100 m) and you shout a word. The person at the other end does **not** hear it instantly — there is a small delay of about **0.29 seconds** because **sound travels at a finite speed** (≈ 343 m/s in air). Electrical signals behave **identically**, except their "shouting speed" is much, much higher: | Medium | Signal Speed | Time to cross 1 meter | |---|---|---| | Sound in air | ≈ 343 m/s | ≈ 2.91 ms | | Light in vacuum | ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s | ≈ 3.33 ns | | Signal in FR4 PCB (εᵣ ≈ 4.5) | ≈ 1.41 × 10⁸ m/s | ≈ 7.08 ns | So when a digital gate drives a long PCB trace, the receiving gate does **not** see the transition *instantly* — the transition is **delayed** by a few nanoseconds per inch of trace. If your clock period is 1 ns, then **a 6-inch trace uses up an entire clock cycle** just in propagation! > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** "Time and Distance" is the *engineering reality* that wires are not infinitely fast. The longer the wire and the slower the medium, the longer the signal takes to arrive — and the more important this delay becomes relative to your clock period. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Memorize These for KTU Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following constants appear in nearly every KTU problem under this topic. Memorize them in **bold** as given below: - **Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s = 186,000 miles/s = 12 inches/ns** - **Relative permittivity of vacuum: $\varepsilon_r = 1$** - **Relative permittivity of FR4 (standard PCB): $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.0$ to $4.6$** (use 4.5 for KTU problems unless stated) - **Relative permittivity of air: $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.0006$ ≈ 1** - **Relative permittivity of alumina ceramic: $\varepsilon_r \approx 9.8$** - **Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m** --- ## 1.4 Why "Time and Distance" is the First Topic in HSD Every KTU question paper on this module assumes you already know: 1. How long it takes a signal to go from Driver → Receiver. 2. Whether that delay is small or large **compared to the rise time** of the signal. 3. Whether the interconnect should be modeled as a *lumped* element or a *distributed* (transmission line) element. All three judgments come from a single, basic **Time vs. Distance** calculation. Hence, KTU places this topic at the very start of the course. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework ### Step 1: Identify the Two Fundamental Quantities There are exactly **two** physical quantities that govern every "Time and Distance" problem in HSD: - **Distance ($D$):** the physical length of the conducting path (trace, wire, via, package lead). Measured in **meters, inches, or centimeters**. - **Time ($T$):** the *propagation delay* or *time of flight* of the signal across that distance. Measured in **seconds, nanoseconds (ns), or picoseconds (ps)**. ### Step 2: The Governing Equation The relationship is identical to the basic physics equation $v = d/t$: $$T = \frac{D}{v} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad v = \frac{D}{T}$$ The *clever part* in HSD is that $v$ is **not always** the speed of light — it depends on what surrounds the conductor. ### Step 3: Why $v$ Depends on the Dielectric When a signal travels down a wire, it is actually an **electromagnetic (EM) wave** propagating between the conductor and its return path. The EM wave's velocity in any medium is: $$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r \mu_r}}$$ For almost all PCB and packaging materials, $\mu_r \approx 1$ (non-magnetic). Therefore: $$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does the dielectric slow it down?** The EM wave's energy is stored partly in the **electric field** inside the dielectric. A higher $\varepsilon_r$ means the dielectric polarizes more strongly, which *stores more energy per volt*, which in turn means the wave's energy front must "fill up" more storage per unit distance — slowing it down. ### Step 4: Propagation Delay *Per Unit Length* (The Practical Quantity) Most KTU problems ask: *"How much delay does 1 cm / 1 inch of trace introduce?"* Define the **propagation delay per unit length** $t_{pd}$ as the reciprocal of velocity: $$t_{pd} = \frac{1}{v} = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c} \quad \text{(seconds per meter)}$$ In the field, this is usually quoted in **ps/inch** or **ns/m**: | Substrate Material | $\varepsilon_r$ (typical) | $t_{pd}$ (ps/inch) | $t_{pd}$ (ns/m) | |---|---|---|---| | Air (free space) | 1.0 | **33.0** | **3.33** | | Coax (polyethylene) | 2.3 | 50.0 | ~5.05 | | FR4 PCB (standard) | 4.0 – 4.6 | **66 – 71** | **6.6 – 7.1** | | Alumina ceramic | 9.8 | 103 | ~10.4 | | Silicon (on-chip) | 11.7 – 12 | 113 | ~11.3 | > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb widely accepted in industry & KTU textbooks:** For FR4, **$t_{pd} \approx 170$ ps/inch** is the *board-level measured* value (which includes the effect of fields partly in air). For pure strip-line/micro-strip modeling, the **theoretical** value is closer to 150 ps/inch. KTU questions typically specify the formula — **read carefully!** ### Step 5: The Critical Length Concept (When Does Distance Matter?) The single most important *engineering* question is: *"At what physical length does the propagation delay become significant compared to the signal rise time?"* Define the **critical length** $L_{crit}$ as the length for which the propagation delay equals the rise time $t_{rise}$: $$L_{crit} = v \cdot t_{rise} = \frac{t_{rise}}{t_{pd}}$$ - If **trace length $L \ll L_{crit}$** → **Lumped circuit model** is sufficient (no transmission line behavior). - If **trace length $L \approx L_{crit}$** or greater → **Transmission line model is mandatory**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is the *rise time* — not the clock period — the correct comparison?** Because a fast edge with a slow clock (e.g., 1 ns rise, 100 MHz clock) still needs transmission-line treatment, while a slow edge with a fast clock (e.g., 10 ns rise, 1 GHz clock) does not! The transition speed, not the data rate, determines everything. ### Step 6: Electrical Length and Wavelength A trace of length $L$ has an **electrical length** $\theta$ in radians: $$\theta = \omega \cdot T_{flight} = 2\pi f \cdot \frac{L}{v} = \frac{2\pi L}{\lambda}$$ where $\lambda = v/f$ is the signal wavelength. A trace is considered "electrically long" if $L > \lambda/10$ (one-tenth wavelength rule of thumb). --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **complete toolkit** for any KTU 2024 problem on this topic. Memorize the **formula + the units + the assumption** together. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Assumption | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Time of Flight | $T_{flight} = D / v$ | seconds | Signal travels at constant $v$ | | 2 | Propagation Velocity (general) | $v = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r \mu_r}$ | m/s | Lossless, uniform medium | | 3 | Propagation Velocity (non-magnetic) | $v = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | m/s | $\mu_r = 1$ | | 4 | Propagation Delay per unit length | $t_{pd} = 1/v = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/c$ | s/m | Lossless dielectric | | 5 | Time of Flight in terms of $t_{pd}$ | $T_{flight} = t_{pd} \cdot D$ | seconds | $D$ in same length units as $t_{pd}$ | | 6 | Critical Length | $L_{crit} = v \cdot t_{rise}$ | meters | $t_{rise}$ = 10–90% rise time | | 7 | Electrical Length (radians) | $\theta = 2\pi L / \lambda$ | rad | $L \le \lambda$ typically | | 8 | Wavelength in dielectric | $\lambda = v / f = c / (f \sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$ | meters | Sine-wave signal at frequency $f$ | | 9 | Refractive Index | $n = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r \mu_r}$ | — | Equals $c/v$ | | 10 | FR4 Rule of Thumb | $t_{pd} \approx 170$ ps/in | ps/inch | Field-tested, includes air region | | 11 | FR4 Rule of Thumb | $t_{pd} \approx 6$ to $7$ ns/m | ns/m | Theoretical strip-line value | | 12 | Speed of light in vacuum | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s = 12 in/ns | — | Fundamental constant | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Conversion Tip:** Always keep $c$ in **m/s** and $D$ in **meters** when using SI units. When working in **inches**, use $c = 12$ in/ns and $t_{pd}$ in **ns/inch** or **ps/inch**. Mixing units is the #1 source of mistakes in KTU answer sheets. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | How "Time and Distance" is Used | |---|---| | **PCB Design** | Determining maximum trace length before a clock signal skews too much. | | **IC Packaging** | Calculating bond-wire delay (often dominates total path delay in low-cost packages). | | **High-Speed Serial Links (PCIe, USB 3, SerDes)** | Setting pre-emphasis and de-emphasis equalization taps based on channel loss-vs-length. | | **Multi-board Systems (Backplanes)** | Budgeting flight time across the backplane in the timing closure of the system. | | **Radar / Microwave Antennas** | Phase-matching cables so signals from multiple antennas arrive simultaneously. | | **Supercomputers / Data Centers** | Coordinating clock distribution trees with matched cable lengths. | In short: **every** high-speed design starts with a *Time-and-Distance* calculation before any other analysis is meaningful. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Propagation Velocity from Maxwell's Equations (Lossless Case) For a lossless transmission line, the per-unit-length parameters are $L$ (inductance, H/m) and $C$ (capacitance, F/m). The Telegrapher's equations in the lossless limit reduce to the wave equation: $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = LC \cdot \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$ Comparing with the standard 1-D wave equation $\partial^2 V / \partial x^2 = (1/v^2) \cdot \partial^2 V / \partial t^2$, we identify the **propagation velocity**: $$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$ For a uniform medium with permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ and permeability $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$, we have $LC = \varepsilon \mu$, giving: $$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \mu_0 \mu_r}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \mu_0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r \mu_r}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r \mu_r}}$$ Since $\mu_r \approx 1$ for almost all PCB substrates, the standard HSD result is: $$\boxed{v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Propagation Delay per Unit Length Starting from the velocity equation and taking its reciprocal: $$t_{pd} = \frac{1}{v} = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$$ Substituting $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s: $$t_{pd} = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{3 \times 10^8} \text{ seconds per meter}$$ Converting to **picoseconds per inch** (1 m = 39.37 in, 1 s = 10¹² ps): $$t_{pd} = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{3 \times 10^8} \times 10^{12} \times \frac{1}{39.37} \approx 84.7 \sqrt{\varepsilon_r} \text{ ps/inch}$$ > For FR4 with $\varepsilon_r = 4.0$: $t_{pd} = 84.7 \times 2 = 169.4$ ps/inch ≈ **170 ps/in** ✓ (matches the rule of thumb) > For FR4 with $\varepsilon_r = 4.5$: $t_{pd} = 84.7 \times 2.121 = 179.7$ ps/inch ≈ **180 ps/in** ✓ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time of Flight for a Trace of Given Length Given: - Trace length $D$ (m) - Dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ The **time of flight** is: $$T_{flight} = \frac{D}{v} = D \cdot t_{pd} = \frac{D \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$$ **Exhaustive Derivation Step:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{flight} &= \frac{D}{v} \\[6pt] &= D \times \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c} \\[6pt] &= \frac{D \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{3 \times 10^8} \quad \text{seconds (when } D \text{ is in metres)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{D \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{0.3} \quad \text{ns (when } D \text{ is in metres)} \\[6pt] &= 3.33 \, D \sqrt{\varepsilon_r} \quad \text{ns (} D \text{ in metres)} \end{aligned} $$ If $D$ is given in **inches** instead, the formula becomes: $$T_{flight} = 84.7 \times D \times \sqrt{\varepsilon_r} \quad \text{ps (} D \text{ in inches)}$$ > For FR4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.0$), $D$ in inches: $T_{flight} \approx 170 \, D$ ps. --- ## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format) ### **Example 1: Time of Flight on a PCB Trace** > **[KTU University Exam – April 2024 Style Problem]** > > A clock signal propagates through a PCB trace of length **15 cm** on an FR4 substrate ($\varepsilon_r = 4.0$). The trace runs on the **outer layer (microstrip)** where the empirical propagation delay is $t_{pd} = 150$ ps/in. Calculate: > > (a) The time of flight of the signal across the trace. > (b) The total delay if the clock has a 2 ns rise time — should we treat this as a transmission line? (Use the critical length criterion.) > (c) The propagation velocity of the signal. #### **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert length to inches (because $t_{pd}$ is in ps/inch).** $$ \begin{aligned} D &= 15 \text{ cm} = 15 \times 0.3937 \text{ in} \\[4pt] &= 5.9055 \text{ inches} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute time of flight.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{flight} &= t_{pd} \times D \\[4pt] &= 150 \text{ ps/in} \times 5.9055 \text{ in} \\[4pt] &= 885.8 \text{ ps} \\[4pt] &\approx 0.886 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the critical length criterion.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{crit} &= v \cdot t_{rise} = \frac{t_{rise}}{t_{pd}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2 \text{ ns}}{150 \text{ ps/in}} = \frac{2000 \text{ ps}}{150 \text{ ps/in}} \\[4pt] &= 13.33 \text{ inches} \end{aligned} $$ Since the actual trace length (5.91 in) **< critical length (13.33 in)**, the trace is **electrically short**. The transmission-line model is **not strictly required**; a lumped-element model will suffice. However, since $T_{flight}$ (0.886 ns) is not negligible compared to $t_{rise}$ (2 ns) — it is about **44% of the rise time** — engineers would *still* recommend a transmission-line analysis for safety. **Step 4 — Compute propagation velocity.** $$ \begin{aligned} v &= \frac{1}{t_{pd}} = \frac{1 \text{ in}}{150 \text{ ps}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0.0254 \text{ m}}{150 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s}} \\[4pt] &= 1.693 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} \\[4pt] &\approx 0.565 \, c \end{aligned} $$ **Verification using the formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} v_{theoretical} &= \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{4.0}} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} = 0.5 \, c \end{aligned} $$ The empirical value (0.565 c) is slightly higher than the theoretical (0.5 c) because the microstrip fields are partially in **air** (εᵣ = 1), not fully in the FR4. This is the standard KTU-level observation expected for full marks. > ✅ **Final Answer (a) $T_{flight} = 0.886$ ns, (b) Lumped model is acceptable (5.91 in < 13.33 in critical length), (c) $v \approx 1.69 \times 10^8$ m/s ≈ 0.565 c.** --- ### **Example 2: Estimating Maximum Trace Length Before Transmission-Line Effects Appear** > **[KTU Practice Problem]** > > A digital output buffer has a rise time of **500 ps**. The board uses FR4 with $\varepsilon_r = 4.5$. What is the *minimum* physical length beyond which you **must** treat the trace as a transmission line? #### **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute $t_{pd}$ in ps/in.** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{pd} &= 84.7 \sqrt{\varepsilon_r} \text{ ps/in} \\[4pt] &= 84.7 \times \sqrt{4.5} = 84.7 \times 2.1213 \\[4pt] &= 179.7 \text{ ps/in} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute critical length.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{crit} &= \frac{t_{rise}}{t_{pd}} = \frac{500 \text{ ps}}{179.7 \text{ ps/in}} \\[4pt] &= 2.78 \text{ inches} \approx 7.07 \text{ cm} \end{aligned} $$ > ✅ **Final Answer: $L_{crit} \approx 2.78$ in (7 cm). Any trace longer than this on this board must be treated as a transmission line.** --- ### **Example 3: Time-Distance Budget for a Backplane** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Problem]** > > A 32-bit data bus runs across a backplane of total length **0.4 m**. The dielectric constant of the backplane material is $\varepsilon_r = 3.5$. If the clock period is **5 ns**, what fraction of the clock period is consumed just by signal propagation? #### **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute velocity.** $$ v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{3.5}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.8708} = 1.603 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute time of flight.** $$ T_{flight} = \frac{D}{v} = \frac{0.4 \text{ m}}{1.603 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 2.495 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 3 — Fraction of clock period.** $$ \text{Fraction} = \frac{T_{flight}}{T_{clock}} = \frac{2.495}{5} = 0.499 = 49.9\% $$ > ✅ **Final Answer: ~50% of the clock period is spent on signal flight time alone.** This is a *huge* number and demonstrates why high-speed backplane design is so challenging. The remaining 50% is all that's left for gate switching, setup/hold, and skew! # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Time & Distance Fits into HSD Design This diagram shows the *logical flow* of a Time-and-Distance analysis as a senior HSD engineer would perform it. Use **numbered blocks** (1, 2, 3...) instead of this graphic, but follow the same logical sequence: **Input → Material → Velocity → Flight time → Critical length → Model decision**. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Time-Distance Interaction in a Digital System ```mermaid block-beta blockLabel["Physical Structure of a Time-Distance Path"] columns 4 Driver["Driver Gate

The trace block is the focus of the entire "Time and Distance" module. --- ## 4.3 State Diagram: When a Trace Transitions from Lumped to Distributed ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> LumpedModel: Short trace D less than L_crit [*] --> TLineModel: Long trace D greater than L_crit LumpedModel --> TLineModel: Rise time decreases

KTU problems often test this *subtle* point. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for PEECT526 — High Speed Digital Design, Module 1. The pattern is: **Part A = 3-mark short answers (no choice)**; **Part B = 14-mark long answers with internal choice**. Each Part-B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define "time of flight" in the context of a high-speed digital interconnect. State the formula and explain what physical parameters govern it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **time of flight** of a signal is the time taken for the electromagnetic wave front to travel from the driver's output pin to the receiver's input pin across a physical interconnect of length $D$. $$T_{flight} = \frac{D}{v} = \frac{D \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$$ The two governing parameters are: - **Distance $D$** (physical length of trace in metres or inches). - **Propagation velocity $v$**, which depends on the **relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$** of the surrounding dielectric (since $v = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$). For a non-magnetic medium with $\mu_r \approx 1$, higher $\varepsilon_r$ gives lower velocity and longer flight time. > **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Parameters (1). --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - April 2024]` **What is the critical length of an interconnect? Why is the *rise time* — and not the clock period — used to compute it?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **critical length $L_{crit}$** of an interconnect is the physical length at which the propagation delay of the signal equals the **rise time** of the driving edge: $$L_{crit} = v \cdot t_{rise} = \frac{t_{rise}}{t_{pd}}$$ The *rise time* is used (and not the clock period) because the transmission-line effects — such as reflections, ringing, and ringing-induced logic errors — are triggered by the *edge rate*, not by the clock frequency. A slow clock with a fast edge still needs transmission-line treatment; a fast clock with a slow edge may not. The transition is what disturbs the line, and the rise time measures the steepness of that transition. > **Mark Distribution:** Definition + formula (1) + rise-time rationale (1) + example contrast (1). --- ## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **(a)** Derive an expression for the **propagation velocity of a signal** in a lossless dielectric medium, starting from the basic relationship between inductance and capacitance per unit length. Explain why the velocity is *less* than the speed of light in vacuum for any real PCB substrate. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A clock trace of length **8 inches** is routed on a standard FR4 PCB ($\varepsilon_r = 4.0$). The board fabricator specifies an empirical propagation delay of **170 ps/inch** for this stack-up. Calculate: > - (i) The total time of flight of the clock edge. > - (ii) The propagation velocity in m/s. > - (iii) The maximum allowed clock rise time such that the transmission-line model is **not** required (i.e., trace length equals critical length). Comment on whether a rise time of **3 ns** is acceptable for this board. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** For a lossless two-conductor transmission line with per-unit-length inductance $L$ (H/m) and per-unit-length capacitance $C$ (F/m), the Telegrapher's equations reduce to the 1-D wave equation: $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = LC \cdot \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$ Comparing with the standard wave equation $\partial^2 V / \partial x^2 = (1/v^2) \cdot \partial^2 V / \partial t^2$, the **propagation velocity** is: $$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$ For a uniform medium with $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ and $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$, we have $LC = \varepsilon \mu$, therefore: $$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \mu_0 \varepsilon_r \mu_r}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \mu_0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r \mu_r}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r \mu_r}}$$ For nearly all PCB materials (non-magnetic), $\mu_r = 1$, so: $$\boxed{v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}}$$ **Why $v < c$:** Because $\varepsilon_r > 1$ for all real PCB materials (FR4 has $\varepsilon_r \approx 4$, alumina $\approx 9.8$). The electric field of the propagating wave is partly stored in the dielectric; a higher $\varepsilon_r$ means more energy is "tied up" polarizing the material per unit distance, which slows the wave front. The energy of the wave therefore propagates at $v < c$. #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **(i) Time of flight:** $$ T_{flight} = t_{pd} \times D = 170 \text{ ps/in} \times 8 \text{ in} = 1360 \text{ ps} = 1.36 \text{ ns} $$ **(ii) Propagation velocity:** $$ v = \frac{1}{t_{pd}} = \frac{1 \text{ in}}{170 \text{ ps}} = \frac{0.0254 \text{ m}}{170 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s}} = 1.494 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.498 \, c $$ **(iii) Maximum allowed rise time (critical-length condition):** $$ t_{rise, max} = T_{flight} = D \times t_{pd} = 1.36 \text{ ns} $$ If $t_{rise} \le 1.36$ ns, the trace is **electrically long** (transmission-line behavior dominates). If $t_{rise} > 1.36$ ns, the lumped model is acceptable. **Comment on $t_{rise} = 3$ ns:** Since 3 ns > 1.36 ns, the trace is *electrically short* for this driver, and a lumped RC analysis is sufficient. Transmission-line treatment is **not required** for this combination of length, substrate, and rise time. > **Mark Distribution — (a):** Derivation setup (3) + final formula (2) + physical reason (2). **(b):** Sub-parts (i)(ii)(iii) at 2, 2, 3 marks respectively. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - April 2024]` > **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **logical sequence of steps** that an HSD engineer follows when performing a Time-and-Distance analysis. Clearly indicate the decision point where the engineer chooses between a *lumped* model and a *transmission-line* model. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A high-speed serial link runs at a data rate of **5 Gbps** (rise time ≈ 10% of UI ≈ 20 ps). The PCB uses FR4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.5$, empirical $t_{pd} = 180$ ps/in). Calculate: > - (i) The **wavelength** $\lambda$ of the 2.5 GHz fundamental component in the dielectric. > - (ii) The **electrical length** (in radians) of a **6-inch** trace at this frequency. > - (iii) Determine whether the 6-inch trace qualifies as *electrically long* (use the $\lambda/10$ rule). Comment on the implications for signal integrity. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Logical sequence of a Time-and-Distance analysis (Block diagram in text form):** ``` Block 1: IDENTIFY physical length D of the interconnect. ↓ Block 2: DETERMINE substrate ε_r (FR4, ceramic, silicon, ...). ↓ Block 3: COMPUTE propagation velocity v = c / √ε_r (or use empirical t_pd). ↓ Block 4: COMPUTE time of flight T_flight = D / v. ↓ Block 5: DETERMINE signal rise time t_rise of the driver. ↓ Block 6: COMPUTE critical length L_crit = v · t_rise. ↓ Block 7: DECISION POINT ┌──────────────────────────────┐ │ If D < L_crit (≈ 1/6 rule) │ │ → LUMPED MODEL │ │ If D ≥ L_crit │ │ → TRANSMISSION-LINE MODEL│ └──────────────────────────────┘ ↓ Block 8: PROCEED with appropriate analysis (RC for lumped, Z0/Γ for T-line). ``` **Key Decision Point (Block 7):** This is the *engineering judgment* that determines whether reflections, impedance matching, and termination must be considered. KTU expects this block to be clearly highlighted in your answer. #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **(i) Wavelength in the dielectric:** The fundamental frequency is $f = 5 \text{ Gbps} / 2 = 2.5 \text{ GHz}$ (for NRZ, the fundamental is half the bit rate). $$ v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{4.5}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.1213} = 1.414 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ \lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{1.414 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}} = 0.0566 \text{ m} = 5.66 \text{ cm} = 2.23 \text{ inches} $$ **(ii) Electrical length of 6-inch trace:** The propagation delay for 6 inches: $$ T_{flight} = 180 \text{ ps/in} \times 6 \text{ in} = 1080 \text{ ps} = 1.08 \text{ ns} $$ The electrical length in radians is: $$ \theta = 2\pi f \cdot T_{flight} = 2\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^9 \times 1.08 \times 10^{-9} = 16.96 \text{ radians} $$ Equivalently, $\theta = (2\pi L / \lambda) = (2\pi \times 6 / 2.23) = 16.91$ rad. (Slight rounding — both answers accepted.) **(iii) Is the 6-inch trace electrically long?** The $\lambda/10$ rule says a trace is "electrically long" if: $$ L > \frac{\lambda}{10} = \frac{2.23 \text{ in}}{10} = 0.223 \text{ inches} $$ Since $L = 6$ in is **27 times** larger than $\lambda/10$, the trace is **definitely electrically long** at 2.5 GHz. Transmission-line analysis is **mandatory**. Implications: - Reflections at impedance discontinuities (vias, connectors, package leads) will return to the driver *before* the bit period ends, causing intersymbol interference (ISI). - The trace must be impedance-controlled to 50 Ω (or 85 Ω differential). - Termination at the receiver is essential to absorb the incident wave. - Skin-effect loss, dielectric loss, and dispersion in FR4 will degrade the 5 Gbps eye diagram — likely requiring pre-emphasis/equalization. > **Mark Distribution — (a):** 5 blocks at 1 mark each, decision block worth 2 marks. **(b):** Sub-parts (i)(ii)(iii) at 2, 2, 3 marks. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks on "Time and Distance" questions:** > > 1. **Unit Mix-up Disaster (loses 2-3 marks):** Using $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s while $D$ is in inches — produces a numerically wrong answer by a factor of ~40. **Always convert $D$ to metres first, or use the inch-based rule $c = 12$ in/ns.** > > 2. **Forgetting the empirical vs. theoretical $t_{pd}$ distinction (loses 1-2 marks):** KTU often *gives* an empirical $t_{pd}$ in the problem (e.g., 170 ps/in). Students who ignore this and blindly use $c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ lose the "applies the given data" credit. **Read the problem stem carefully.** > > 3. **Computing $L_{crit}$ with the wrong reference (loses 2 marks):** Some students use the clock period instead of the rise time. The textbook (and KTU) standard is the *rise time*. > > 4. **Skipping the "why $v < c$" physical explanation (loses 1-2 marks in derivations):** A formula-only answer gets partial credit. KTU's outcome-based education rewards the *physical reasoning*. > > 5. **Not commenting on the engineering implication (loses 1-2 marks in numericals):** Always end a numerical answer with **one sentence** of engineering judgment (e.g., "Since $D \gg L_{crit}$, the transmission-line model is mandatory, and termination at the receiver is required."). This single sentence is worth 1-2 marks and is the *easiest* mark to gain. > > 6. **Confusing *time of flight* with *propagation delay per unit length* (loses 2 marks):** These are different quantities — one has units of seconds, the other seconds per metre. KTU examiners penalize this conflation. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print or bookmark this section — it is your last-minute KTU revision sheet for "Time and Distance".** - ✅ **Time of Flight** = $T_{flight} = D / v$. It is the *delay* introduced by an interconnect of length $D$. - ✅ **Propagation velocity** in a non-magnetic dielectric = $v = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$. Higher $\varepsilon_r$ → slower signal. - ✅ **Propagation delay per unit length** $t_{pd} = 1/v = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/c$. For FR4, $t_{pd} \approx 170$ ps/in (empirical) or 150 ps/in (theoretical strip-line). - ✅ **Critical length** = $L_{crit} = v \cdot t_{rise} = t_{rise} / t_{pd}$. Above this length, transmission-line model is required. - ✅ **The "rise time", NOT the clock period, determines whether transmission-line effects matter.** - ✅ **Speed of light in vacuum** = $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s = **12 in/ns** — memorize both forms. - ✅ **FR4 has $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.0$ to $4.5$** — use the value given in the problem. - ✅ **Wavelength** in a dielectric: $\lambda = v/f = c/(f\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$. - ✅ **Electrically long** trace rule: $L > \lambda / 10$ (or sometimes $L > \lambda/20$ for stricter designs). - ✅ **Rule of thumb for FR4:** Signal travels **6 inches in ~1 ns** (i.e., 170 ps/in × 6 in ≈ 1.02 ns). - ✅ **Unit discipline** — always keep $D$, $v$, and $T$ in *consistent* units (m/m/s/s OR in/ns/ps). - ✅ **Engineering implication** — always comment on whether the answer implies lumped or distributed behavior at the end of a numerical problem. - ✅ **Why $v < c$** — energy is partly stored in the dielectric; a denser dielectric = more energy storage per unit length = slower wave front. - ✅ **Real-world anchors:** 5 Gbps link on 6-inch FR4 → transmission line. 100 MHz clock on 2-inch trace → lumped model. Always check $L_{crit}$. - ✅ **For Module 2 preparation:** Once $L \ge L_{crit}$, you *must* invoke characteristic impedance $Z_0$, reflections ($\Gamma$), and termination — these are the topics of the next module. --- > 🎯 **End of KTU 2024 Module 1 — Time and Distance Study Notes**

topics.title: Lumped Versus Distributed Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lumped Versus Distributed Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of high-speed digital design, an electrical interconnect is classified as either a **lumped system** or a **distributed system** based on the relationship between its **physical dimensions** and the **electrical wavelength (or rise time)** of the propagating signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lumped System:** A circuit whose physical dimensions are electrically small compared to the wavelength of the highest frequency component of the signal, such that the voltage and current along the conductor can be assumed to be spatially uniform (constant) at any instant of time. All elements (R, L, C) are treated as discrete, point-like components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed System:** A circuit whose physical dimensions are comparable to (or greater than) a significant fraction of the signal wavelength, such that the voltage and current vary in both **space and time**. The system must be modeled using the **Telegrapher's Equations** and exhibits **wave propagation** behavior.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose vs. The Long Water Pipe

Imagine water flowing through two pipes:

- **Short Garden Hose (Lumped):** When you turn the tap on at one end, the water exits the other end almost instantly. You can model the hose with a single resistance value, because the pressure inside is the same everywhere at the same time.
- **Long Industrial Pipeline (Distributed):** If the pipe is 5 km long, when you turn the pump on, it takes seconds before water emerges at the far end. The pressure at the source is *not* the same as the pressure at the far end at a given instant. You must account for the **time delay** along the pipe and the wave-like behavior of the fluid column.

Electrical interconnects behave identically. A short PCB trace on a low-speed board is a "hose"; a long backplane trace carrying a gigabit signal is a "pipeline."

## 1.3 The Deciding Metric — The Electrical Wavelength

The governing standard metric is the **wavelength ($\lambda$)** of the signal in the medium, given by:

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

where:
- $v$ is the **propagation velocity** of the signal in the medium (m/s).
- $f$ is the **frequency** of the signal (Hz).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Board Examiner's Favorite):** An interconnect can be treated as a **lumped element** if its physical length $L$ satisfies:
> 
> $$L < \frac{\lambda}{10} \quad \text{(sometimes } \frac{\lambda}{20}\text{)}$$
> 
> If $L \geq \lambda/10$, it **must** be modeled as a **distributed (transmission line)** system.

For digital signals, the more practical rule uses **rise time** instead of frequency:

$$L < \frac{t_r}{6 \cdot t_{pd}}$$

where $t_{pd}$ is the **propagation delay per unit length** (ns/cm) and $t_r$ is the **10–90% rise time** of the signal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Distinction Matters

The choice between a lumped and a distributed model determines:

1. **Whether wave phenomena occur** — Reflections, ringing, crosstalk, and standing waves only appear in distributed systems.
2. **The governing equations** — Lumped systems use ordinary differential equations (ODEs); distributed systems use **partial differential equations (PDEs)**, the Telegrapher's Equations.
3. **Simulation methodology** — SPICE lumped analysis vs. full-wave electromagnetic (EM) simulation or transmission-line solvers.

## 2.2 The Lumped Element Model

In a purely lumped system, the interconnect is represented as a network of discrete **R, L, C** elements all at a single node. Kirchhoff's laws hold at every node, and the relationship between input and output is described by ordinary differential equations such as:

$$V_{out}(t) + RC \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = V_{in}(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The implicit assumption is that the **propagation delay across the element is negligible** compared to the rise/fall time of the signal.

## 2.3 The Distributed Element Model

A distributed system is modeled as an **infinite cascade of infinitesimal R, L, G, C sections** (per-unit-length parameters). The governing equations are the **Telegrapher's Equations**:

$$\frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial x} = -L \frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t} - R \cdot I(x,t)$$

$$\frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial x} = -C \frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial t} - G \cdot V(x,t)$$

For a **lossless** transmission line ($R = 0$, $G = 0$):

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = LC \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$

This is the classic **1-D wave equation** with solution $V(x,t) = V^+(x - vt) + V^-(x + vt)$, confirming **forward- and backward-traveling waves**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = v / f$ | meters (m) |
| 2 | Propagation velocity | $v = 1 / \sqrt{LC}$ | m/s (lossless) |
| 3 | Propagation delay (per unit length) | $t_{pd} = \sqrt{LC}$ | s/m |
| 4 | Time-of-flight across length L | $t_{flight} = L \cdot \sqrt{LC}$ | seconds |
| 5 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L / C}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 6 | Lumped-system boundary | $L < \lambda / 10$ | meters |
| 7 | Digital lumped boundary | $L < t_r / (6 \cdot t_{pd})$ | meters |
| 8 | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0) / (Z_L + Z_0)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | VSWR | $VSWR = (1 + \vert\Gamma\vert) / (1 - \vert\Gamma\vert)$ | ratio |
| 10 | Knee frequency (digital) | $f_{knee} \approx 0.5 / t_r$ | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA for Board Exams:** The single most tested equation on this topic is the lumped-vs-distributed boundary:
> 
> $$L < \frac{\lambda}{10} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad f < \frac{v}{10 L}$$
> 
> Memorize the relationship between $L$, $\lambda$, $f$, and $v$ in both directions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCB Design:** Determines whether a trace needs $50\,\Omega$ impedance control or can be routed as a simple wire.
- **Cable & Connector Design:** High-speed serial links (PCIe, USB 3.x, HDMI) are always distributed; LVDS lines may be lumped.
- **Signal Integrity:** The classification dictates whether you need a **field solver** (distributed) or a **circuit solver** (lumped).
- **EMC/EMI:** Only distributed systems exhibit radiated wave behavior at frequencies of interest.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Lumped-System Criterion from First Principles

We want to find the condition under which a line of length $L$ carrying a sinusoidal signal of frequency $f$ can be treated as lumped.

**Step 1 — Define the spatial phase variation.**

For a wave traveling along the line, voltage varies as:

$$V(x,t) = V_0 \cos(\omega t - \beta x)$$

where $\beta$ is the phase constant (rad/m), given by $\beta = 2\pi / \lambda$.

**Step 2 — Quantify the phase difference between the two ends of the line.**

The phase difference between $x = 0$ and $x = L$ is:

$$\Delta \phi = \beta L = \frac{2\pi L}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the lumped-system assumption.**

For the line to be lumped, the phase difference must be small enough that $\cos(\Delta \phi) \approx 1$ (to first order), which requires:

$$\Delta \phi \ll \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ radians} \quad \text{(i.e., } \Delta \phi \leq 36^\circ\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the inequality.**

$$\frac{2\pi L}{\lambda} \leq \frac{\pi}{5}$$

$$L \leq \frac{\lambda}{10}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to frequency form using** $\lambda = v/f$.

$$L \leq \frac{v}{10 f} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad f \leq \frac{v}{10 L}$$

This is the classical **one-tenth wavelength rule** that board examiners love to test.

## 3.2 Derivation: Characteristic Impedance from the Telegrapher's Equations

**Step 1 — Assume a sinusoidal steady-state forward-traveling wave.**

Let $V(x,t) = V^+ e^{j(\omega t - \beta x)}$ and $I(x,t) = I^+ e^{j(\omega t - \beta x)}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the lossless telegrapher equation.**

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -L \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}$$

$$-j\beta V^+ = -j\omega L \cdot I^+$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the voltage-to-current ratio.**

$$\frac{V^+}{I^+} = \frac{\omega L}{\beta}$$

**Step 4 — Use the dispersion relation for the lossless line.**

From the wave equation $V_{xx} = LC V_{tt}$ we have $\beta^2 = \omega^2 LC$, so $\omega / \beta = 1 / \sqrt{LC}$.

**Step 5 — Substitute to obtain $Z_0$.**

$$Z_0 = \frac{V^+}{I^+} = \frac{\omega L}{\beta} = L \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example (Typical 7-Mark KTU Question Type)

**Problem:** A copper PCB trace on FR-4 has a per-unit-length inductance $L = 8\,\text{nH/cm}$ and capacitance $C = 2.5\,\text{pF/cm}$. The signal has a rise time $t_r = 1\,\text{ns}$. Determine (a) the characteristic impedance, (b) the propagation delay per cm, and (c) the maximum trace length that can still be treated as lumped.

**Solution (a) — Characteristic Impedance:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 10^{-9}}{2.5 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{3200} \approx 56.6\,\Omega$$

**Solution (b) — Propagation Delay per cm:**

$$t_{pd} = \sqrt{L \cdot C} = \sqrt{(8 \times 10^{-9})(2.5 \times 10^{-12})}$$

$$t_{pd} = \sqrt{2.0 \times 10^{-20}} = 1.414 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s/cm} = 141.4\,\text{ps/cm}$$

This matches the well-known **FR-4 propagation delay of ~140 ps/cm** (≈ 7 ns/m, or about half the speed of light).

**Solution (c) — Maximum lumped length:**

$$L_{max} = \frac{t_r}{6 \cdot t_{pd}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{6 \cdot (1.414 \times 10^{-10})}$$

$$L_{max} = \frac{10^{-9}}{8.485 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.18\,\text{cm}$$

So a trace longer than about **1.18 cm** with a 1 ns rise time on this FR-4 stack-up **must be treated as a transmission line** (distributed model).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Lumped vs Distributed System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Interconnect] --> B{Check Physical Length L}
    B -->|"L < lambda / 10"| C[LUMPED SYSTEM]
    B -->|"L >= lambda / 10"| D[DISTRIBUTED SYSTEM]
    C --> C1[Discrete R, L, C elements]
    C --> C2[Kirchhoffs laws only]
    C --> C3[ODE based analysis]
    C --> C4[No wave propagation]
    C --> C5[SPICE circuit solver]
    D --> D1[Continuous R, L, G, C per unit length]
    D --> D2[Telegrapher equations]
    D --> D3[PDE based analysis]
    D --> D4[Forward and backward waves]
    D --> D5[EM or TLINE solver]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Lumped Element Model (Discrete Network)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VS[Voltage Source Vs] --> R[Resistor R]
    R --> L[Inductor L]
    L --> C[Capacitor C]
    C --> GND1[Ground]
    C --> OUT[Output Node Vout]
    OUT --> RL[Load Resistor]
    RL --> GND2[Ground]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Distributed Element Model (Transmission Line)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Driver Source Zs] --> TL[Transmission Line Z0, length L, delay Td]
    TL --> LD[Load ZL]
    TL -.->|"Forward Wave V+"| FW[Incident wave]
    TL -.->|"Reflected Wave V-"| RW[Reflection from load mismatch]
    FW --> RL2[Reflection coefficient gamma]
    RW --> SRC
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix Diagram: When to Use Each Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: New Interconnect] --> Q1{Is L less than tr divided by 6 times tpd?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is frequency content below knee?}
    Q1 -->|No| DIST[Use DISTRIBUTED model with transmission line]
    Q2 -->|Yes| LMP[Use LUMPED model with R, L, C]
    Q2 -->|No| DIST
    LMP --> SIM1[Simulate with SPICE]
    DIST --> SIM2[Simulate with EM or 2D field solver]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a lumped and a distributed system. State the rule of thumb used to classify them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Lumped system:** Physical dimensions are electrically small compared to the signal wavelength; voltage and current are assumed spatially uniform; modeled with discrete R, L, C. **(1 mark)**
- **Distributed system:** Physical dimensions are comparable to the signal wavelength; voltage and current vary in space and time; modeled using the Telegrapher's equations. **(1 mark)**
- **Rule of thumb:** A system is treated as lumped if $L < \lambda/10$ (or equivalently $L < t_r / (6 \cdot t_{pd})$ for digital signals). **(1 mark)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define characteristic impedance. Write its expression for a lossless transmission line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Characteristic impedance $Z_0$ is the ratio of the voltage to the current of a single forward-traveling wave on a transmission line, equal to the impedance seen looking into an infinitely long line. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Expression:** For a lossless line, $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$, where $L$ and $C$ are per-unit-length inductance and capacitance. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the characteristic impedance of a lossless transmission line starting from the Telegrapher's equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal of frequency 500 MHz propagates on a PCB trace with propagation velocity $1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s. Determine whether a 4 cm trace must be modeled as a lumped or distributed system. Justify with calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of $Z_0$ (7 marks)

**Step 1:** State the lossless Telegrapher's equations (1 mark):

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -L \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = -C \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2:** Assume a sinusoidal forward-traveling wave (1 mark):

$$V(x,t) = V_0 e^{j(\omega t - \beta x)} \quad ; \quad I(x,t) = I_0 e^{j(\omega t - \beta x)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the first equation (1 mark):

$$-j\beta V_0 = -j\omega L \cdot I_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_0}{I_0} = \frac{\omega L}{\beta}$$

**Step 4:** Derive the dispersion relation from the wave equation (2 marks):

$$V_{xx} = LC \cdot V_{tt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (j\beta)^2 V_0 = LC \cdot (j\omega)^2 V_0$$

$$\beta^2 = \omega^2 LC \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back to obtain $Z_0$ (2 marks):

$$Z_0 = \frac{V_0}{I_0} = L \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}\;\Omega}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Lumped vs Distributed Classification (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Calculate the wavelength of the signal (2 marks):

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^8}{500 \times 10^6} = 0.3\,\text{m} = 30\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the one-tenth wavelength rule (2 marks):

$$L_{lumped\,max} = \frac{\lambda}{10} = \frac{30\,\text{cm}}{10} = 3\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the actual trace length (1 mark):

$$L_{actual} = 4\,\text{cm} > L_{lumped\,max} = 3\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion (2 marks):

Since the physical length (4 cm) exceeds one-tenth of the wavelength (3 cm), the trace **cannot be treated as a lumped element**. It **must be modeled as a distributed system** (transmission line), and the effects of characteristic impedance, propagation delay ($t_{flight} = 4 \times 10^{-2} / 1.5 \times 10^8 \approx 267\,\text{ps}$), and reflections must be considered.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`  *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain with neat derivations the significance of the propagation delay per unit length and velocity of propagation in a transmission line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CMOS driver with rise time $t_r = 0.5$ ns driving a trace on FR-4 ($t_{pd} = 140$ ps/cm), find the maximum length for lumped treatment. If the trace is 10 cm long, classify it and state the consequences. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Propagation Delay and Velocity (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Define per-unit-length parameters $L$ (H/m) and $C$ (F/m) (1 mark).

**Step 2:** From the Telegrapher's equations, derive the wave equation (2 marks):

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = LC \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 3:** General solution is a forward wave $V^+(x - vt)$ plus a reflected wave $V^-(x + vt)$ (1 mark).

**Step 4:** Compare with the standard wave equation $V_{xx} = (1/v^2) V_{tt}$ to identify (2 marks):

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}\,\text{m/s} \quad ; \quad t_{pd} = \sqrt{LC}\,\text{s/m}$$

**Step 5:** Engineering significance (1 mark):
- $v$ is typically ~50–67% of the speed of light for FR-4.
- $t_{pd}$ is ~140 ps/cm for FR-4 and ~90 ps/cm for high-speed substrates.
- Determines flight time, skew, and setup/hold margins.

#### Part (b) — Lumped Length Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Apply the digital lumped criterion (2 marks):

$$L_{max} = \frac{t_r}{6 \cdot t_{pd}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-9}}{6 \cdot 140 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{500}{840} \approx 0.595\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with the actual length (1 mark):

$$L_{actual} = 10\,\text{cm} \gg L_{max} = 0.595\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 3:** Classify the system (1 mark): Clearly **distributed**.

**Step 4:** Consequences (3 marks):
- Signal integrity issues — reflections, ringing, overshoot/undershoot.
- Must use $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ matching at source and load.
- Impedance discontinuities cause non-monotonic edges (multiple crossings), which can cause **double-clocking** and **metastability** in flip-flops.
- Flight time $t_{flight} = 10 \times 140\,\text{ps} = 1.4\,\text{ns}$, comparable to $t_r$, so wave behavior dominates.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $t_r / 6$ with $t_r / 2$.** The factor of 6 comes from approximating a digital edge as a sinusoid at the **knee frequency** $f_{knee} = 0.5 / t_r$. The $\lambda/10$ rule corresponds to $t_r / 6 \cdot t_{pd}$. Using $t_r/2$ gives a wrong, overly optimistic limit.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** $t_{pd}$ in ps/cm vs ns/cm vs s/m is a frequent source of unit-conversion errors. Convert to a common base (e.g., s and m) before substituting.
> 3. **Mixing up characteristic impedance and load impedance.** $Z_0$ is a property of the line, not the load. Reflections arise from the *mismatch* between $Z_0$ and $Z_L$.
> 4. **Stating "Lumped = no delay."** Lumped systems still have a delay (the $RC$ time constant), it is just *small* compared to the rise time. The lumped model **ignores** the spatial delay, not all delay.
> 5. **Forgetting the lossless assumption when deriving $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$.** For lossy lines, $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L) / (G + j\omega C)}$. Always state the assumption explicitly in the exam.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lumped** = electrically short; one spatial node; ODEs; no wave effects.
- **Distributed** = electrically long; spatial variation; PDEs (Telegrapher); wave effects.
- **Boundary rule:** $L < \lambda/10$ (frequency domain) **or** $L < t_r / (6 t_{pd})$ (digital domain).
- **Wavelength formula:** $\lambda = v/f$, where $v = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ for a lossless line.
- **Characteristic impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (lossless, dimensionless in $\Omega$).
- **Propagation delay per unit length:** $t_{pd} = \sqrt{LC}$; FR-4 ≈ 140 ps/cm; high-speed substrates ≈ 90 ps/cm.
- **Knee frequency:** $f_{knee} = 0.5 / t_r$ — the highest frequency a digital edge contains with significant energy.
- **Reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0) / (Z_L + Z_0)$ — only meaningful in the distributed regime.
- **Time-of-flight:** $t_{flight} = L / v = L \cdot t_{pd}$ — the basic delay of a distributed interconnect.
- **Rule of thumb values to remember:** $v_{FR4} \approx 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s, $Z_{0,FR4} \approx 50$–$75\,\Omega$ (microstrip/stripline).
- **Design takeaway:** If $L$ is more than a centimeter or so at sub-nanosecond rise times, the interconnect is **distributed** — design for controlled impedance, not for discrete components.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Four Kinds of Reactance - Ordinary Capacitance and Inductance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Four Kinds of Reactance - Ordinary Capacitance and Inductance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview In the discipline of **High-Speed Digital Design**, the classification of reactance into four distinct categories is a foundational concept introduced by Howard W. Johnson and Martin Graham. Every interconnection in a digital system exhibits some form of capacitive or inductive behavior, but the *kind* of reactance depends entirely on the **geometry of the current return path** and the **spatial distribution of the fields**. The four kinds of reactance are: 1. **Ordinary Capacitance** (lumped electric-field storage between separated conductors) 2. **Ordinary Inductance** (lumped magnetic-field storage in a localized loop) 3. **Distributed Capacitance** (per-unit-length, along a transmission line) 4. **Distributed Inductance** (per-unit-length, along a transmission line) This section focuses on the **first two** — the *ordinary* (lumped) forms. ### 1.1 Ordinary Capacitance — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Ordinary Capacitance** is the *lumped* property of two separated conductors that accumulate equal and opposite electric charge, storing energy in the electric field between them, with the return current following a **path geometrically distinct** from the path of the outgoing current. Mathematically, it is defined by the linear relation: $$Q = C \cdot V$$ where $Q$ is the stored charge (in Coulombs), $C$ is the capacitance (in Farads), and $V$ is the voltage across the conductors. The fundamental current-voltage relation follows by time-differentiation: $$I = C \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In a KTU 2024 examination context, the keyword **"ordinary"** carries a precise meaning: the structure's physical dimensions are small compared to the **wavelength** of the highest signal frequency of interest (typically, dimensions $<\lambda/6$, or equivalently, propagation delay $< t_r/6$ where $t_r$ is the signal rise time). ### 1.2 Ordinary Inductance — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Ordinary Inductance** is the *lumped* property of a current loop (or partial loop) that stores energy in the magnetic field encircled by the loop, with the **return current flowing back through the same physical path** as the outgoing current. The defining relations are: $$\lambda = L \cdot I \quad \text{and} \quad V = L \cdot \frac{dI}{dt}$$ where $\lambda$ is the magnetic flux linkage (in Weber-turns), $L$ is the inductance (in Henrys), and $V$ is the voltage induced across the structure due to a time-varying current $I$. > [!IMPORTANT] > The single most distinguishing feature between **ordinary** and **distributed** inductance is the *return-path geometry*. Ordinary inductance assumes a **co-located** return path (e.g., a wire above a ground plane, a bond wire with its corresponding return pin).
