# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Coupled Telegrapher's Equations (Foundation)

**Step 1:** Start with the per-unit-length circuit model. Two parallel conductors form a 4-port distributed network with self terms ($L$, $C$) and mutual terms ($L_m$, $C_m$).

**Step 2:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) across a differential segment $dx$ of line 1:

$$
V_1(x,t) - V_1(x+dx,t) = L\,dx \frac{\partial I_1}{\partial t} + L_m\,dx \frac{\partial I_2}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 3:** Take the limit $dx \to 0$ and divide by $dx$:

$$
-\frac{\partial V_1}{\partial x} = L \frac{\partial I_1}{\partial t} + L_m \frac{\partial I_2}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 4:** Repeat for line 2 by symmetry of the mutual network:

$$
-\frac{\partial V_2}{\partial x} = L_m \frac{\partial I_1}{\partial t} + L \frac{\partial I_2}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the node where the mutual capacitor connects to line 1:

$$
-\frac{\partial I_1}{\partial x} = C \frac{\partial V_1}{\partial t} - C_m \frac{\partial V_2}{\partial t}
$$

(The negative sign on $C_m$ reflects that the displacement current from line 1 to line 2 appears as an out-flow from line 1.)

**Step 6:** Similarly for line 2:

$$
-\frac{\partial I_2}{\partial x} = -C_m \frac{\partial V_1}{\partial t} + C \frac{\partial V_2}{\partial t}
$$

These four equations constitute the **coupled-mode Telegrapher's equations** in matrix form (shown in Section 2.1).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Backward and Forward Cross Talk Coefficients

**Step 1:** Assume both lines are matched at the far ends, and an aggressor step of amplitude $V_a$ with rise time $t_r$ is launched on line 1. The signal is approximated by a ramp:

$$
V_1(x,t) = V_a \cdot \frac{t - x/v}{t_r} \quad \text{for } 0 \le t - x/v \le t_r
$$

**Step 2:** Express the voltages and currents as a superposition of the **driven mode** (aggressor) and the **coupled modes** (forward and backward cross talk on victim).

**Step 3:** Substitute into the coupled-line equations and decouple them by assuming symmetry of the structure.

**Step 4:** The backward wave is generated at every point $x$ along the line and propagates **backward** (toward the source end of the victim) with velocity $v$. It sums coherently because the source of the backward wave moves at velocity $v$ — so the backward wave at the source end is the integral of all differential contributions:

$$
V_b(t) = \int_0^{\min(v t,\, \ell)} \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right) \cdot \frac{V_a}{t_r} \, dx
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral. Once $t \cdot v \ge \ell$, the integration limit saturates:

$$
V_b^{\text{sat}} = \frac{V_a}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right) \cdot \frac{\ell}{t_r v} \quad \text{when } \ell \le l_{\text{sat}}
$$

**Step 6:** When the coupled length exceeds the saturation length ($l_{\text{sat}} = 0.5 \cdot t_r \cdot v$), the backward pulse cannot grow further because the early-time aggressor ramp has finished rising. The steady-state backward amplitude is:

$$
V_b^{\text{peak}} = \frac{V_a}{4} \cdot \left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right)
$$

Dividing by $V_a$ defines the **backward cross talk coefficient**:

$$
K_b = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right) \quad \text{(initial)} ; \quad \lim_{\ell \to \infty} K_b = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cleanest textbook form (Howard Johnson's *High-Speed Digital Design*) is the **saturated** form: $K_b = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right)$ where the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor from the ramp averaging and the $\frac{1}{2}$ from mode splitting combine. The KTU exam accepts either the $\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right)$ form (per-mode definition) or the $\frac{1}{4}$ form (full saturated amplitude). Verify the question's wording.

**Step 7:** The forward wave, by contrast, propagates **in the same direction** as the aggressor with the same velocity $v$ (in a homogeneous dielectric). The forward pulse is generated at every point along the line, but each differential piece travels with the aggressor and sums up. The result depends on whether the dielectric is homogeneous:

$$
V_f = \frac{V_a}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{L_m}{L} - \frac{C_m}{C}\right) \cdot \frac{\ell - l_{\text{sat}}}{t_r v}
$$

**Step 8:** For a **homogeneous dielectric**, the fundamental field-continuity condition gives:

$$
\frac{L_m}{L} = \frac{C_m}{C}
$$

Substituting this into the forward equation:

$$
V_f = \frac{V_a}{2} \cdot 0 \cdot \frac{\ell - l_{\text{sat}}}{t_r v} = 0
$$

This proves the **key theorem**: **In a homogeneous dielectric, there is NO forward cross talk.**

## 3.3 Derivation of the Saturation Length

**Step 1:** The backward cross talk builds up over a time equal to the time it takes the aggressor edge to sweep past the entire coupled length. That is, the integration window has width equal to the rise time $t_r$ measured at the source end.

**Step 2:** The time for the aggressor to traverse the coupled region is $T = \dfrac{\ell}{v}$.

**Step 3:** As long as $T \le t_r$, the entire coupled length contributes simultaneously to the backward wave at the source end. So the integral keeps growing linearly with $\ell$.

**Step 4:** Once $T > t_r$, the back of the aggressor ramp has already passed the front of the coupled region before the back end of the coupled region finishes contributing. The integration window is now fixed at $t_r \cdot v$ in spatial extent, and the backward amplitude saturates.

**Step 5:** Equate the two regimes:

$$
l_{\text{sat}} = v \cdot t_r
$$

**Step 6:** Since the ramp's effective spatial extent that contributes is half the rise time (because the ramp amplitude is averaged), the more accurate formula is:

$$
l_{\text{sat}} = 0.5 \cdot v \cdot t_r
$$

This is the **critical length** used throughout cross talk analysis.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Two parallel microstrip lines on FR4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.2$) are 100 mm long. The aggressor has a step of $V_a = 3.3$ V with $t_r = 1$ ns. The per-unit-length parameters are $L = 300$ nH/m, $C = 100$ pF/m, $L_m = 30$ nH/m, $C_m = 10$ pF/m.

**Step 1:** Calculate velocity:

$$
v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{300 \times 10^{-9} \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3 \times 10^{-17}}}
$$

$$
v = \frac{1}{5.477 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.826 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate saturation length:

$$
l_{\text{sat}} = 0.5 \cdot v \cdot t_r = 0.5 \cdot 1.826 \times 10^{8} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9} = 0.0913 \text{ m} = 91.3 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $\ell = 100$ mm $> l_{\text{sat}} = 91.3$ mm, the line is **saturated**.

**Step 4:** Compute the backward cross talk coefficient:

$$
K_b = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{10}{100} - \frac{30}{300}\right) = \frac{1}{4}(0.10 - 0.10) = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surprising Result:** Because this structure satisfies $C_m/C = L_m/L = 0.10$, the dielectric appears electrically homogeneous from the cross talk perspective, and the **backward** cross talk is **zero** too! This is the ideal scenario for high-speed design.

**Step 5:** Forward cross talk check:

$$
K_f = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} + \frac{L_m}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(0.10 + 0.10) = 0.10
$$

The forward pulse amplitude (in the inhomogeneous case) would be:

$$
V_f = V_a \cdot K_f \cdot \frac{\ell - l_{\text{sat}}}{t_r v} = 3.3 \cdot 0.10 \cdot \frac{0.100 - 0.0913}{1 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 1.826 \times 10^{8}} = 3.3 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 0.0475 \approx 15.7 \text{ mV}
$$

This is below the noise margin of $\frac{V_{DD}}{4} = 0.825$ V, so the design is safe.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q -->|YES Stripline Coax| R[Compute Cm over C equals Lm over L]
    R --> S[Set Forward Coeff Kf equals 0]
    S --> T[Compute Backward Coeff Kb equals 1 over 4 times Cm over C minus Lm over L]
    Q -->|NO Microstrip| U[Set Kf nonzero equals 1 over 2 times Lm over L minus Cm over C]
    U --> V[Compute Backward Coeff Kb = 1 over 4 times Cm over C minus Lm over L]
    T --> W{Is coupled length ℓ less than l_sat equals 0.5 vt_r}
    V --> W
    W -->|YES| X[Backward grows linearly Vb equals Va by 2 times Kb times ℓ over t_r v]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    AGG --|via Lm| IND[Inductive Coupling Generates Vf Forward at victim load]
    AGG --|via Cm| CAP[Capacitive Coupling Generates Vb Backward at victim source]
    IND --> VICTIMF[Victim Load End sees Forward Pulse Vf]
    CAP --> VICTIMB[Victim Source End sees Backward Pulse Vb]
    VICTIMF --> RX1[Receiver Far End]
    VICTIMB --> RX2[Receiver Near End]
    RX1 --> OUT1[Cross Talk Noise at Load]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Q:** Define mutual capacitance and mutual inductance in the context of two coupled transmission lines. State the units.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Mutual Capacitance ($C_m$):** The distributed capacitance that exists between two adjacent transmission lines, representing the electric-field coupling. The displacement current flowing between the two lines per unit length per unit voltage difference is $C_m$. Unit: **Farads per unit length (F/m)**. (1.5 marks)
- **Mutual Inductance ($L_m$):** The distributed inductance that exists between two adjacent transmission lines, representing the magnetic-field coupling. A time-varying current on the aggressor line induces a voltage on the victim line through the shared magnetic field, per unit length. Unit: **Henries per unit length (H/m)**. (1.5 marks)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Q:** What is the significance of the saturation length in cross talk analysis? Give the formula.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **saturation length** $l_{\text{sat}}$ is the maximum length of coupled region over which the backward cross talk pulse can keep growing as the aggressor edge sweeps across it. Beyond $l_{\text{sat}}$, the backward pulse amplitude saturates and cannot increase further. (2 marks)

$$
l_{\text{sat}} = 0.5 \cdot v \cdot t_r
$$

where $v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ is the propagation velocity and $t_r$ is the rise time. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model Paper Style)]**

**(a) Derive the expressions for forward and backward cross talk coefficients $K_f$ and $K_b$ in terms of the per-unit-length mutual capacitance and mutual inductance. Clearly state the assumptions used.** (7 marks)

**(b) For two coupled striplines on FR4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.2$), the parameters are: $L = 250$ nH/m, $C = 110$ pF/m, $L_m = 25$ nH/m, $C_m = 11$ pF/m. If the coupled length is $\ell = 80$ mm and the aggressor has a step of 3.3 V with $t_r = 1$ ns, compute (i) the saturation length, (ii) the peak backward cross talk voltage, and (iii) the peak forward cross talk voltage.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. Lossless coupled transmission lines
2. Symmetric geometry (line 1 and line 2 identical)
3. Lines are matched at the far ends (no reflections)
4. Quasi-TEM mode of propagation
5. Weak coupling: $L_m \ll L$ and $C_m \ll C$

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Start with the coupled Telegrapher's equations:

$$
-\frac{\partial V_1}{\partial x} = L \frac{\partial I_1}{\partial t} + L_m \frac{\partial I_2}{\partial t}
$$

$$
-\frac{\partial V_2}{\partial x} = L_m \frac{\partial I_1}{\partial t} + L \frac{\partial I_2}{\partial t}
$$

$$
-\frac{\partial I_1}{\partial x} = C \frac{\partial V_1}{\partial t} - C_m \frac{\partial V_2}{\partial t}
$$

$$
-\frac{\partial I_2}{\partial x} = -C_m \frac{\partial V_1}{\partial t} + C \frac{\partial V_2}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Define the cross talk voltages and currents. Let $V_1 = V_a + \Delta V$ where $\Delta V$ is small. Substitute and linearize. After algebraic elimination of the cross terms and using matched-line boundary conditions, the coupled equations decouple into **forward and backward wave** components with cross talk coefficients:

$$
K_b = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right)
$$

$$
K_f = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} + \frac{L_m}{L}\right)
$$

(The factor of $\frac{1}{4}$ in $K_b$ comes from the modal splitting of energy between the two lines plus the temporal averaging over the ramp rise time.) (2 marks)

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The **backward** pulse $V_b = K_b \cdot V_a$ travels toward the source end of the victim line, growing linearly with $\ell$ up to $l_{\text{sat}}$ and saturating thereafter. The **forward** pulse $V_f = K_f \cdot V_a \cdot (\ell - l_{\text{sat}})/(t_r v)$ travels toward the load end of the victim line, in step with the aggressor edge.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the propagation velocity:

$$
v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{250 \times 10^{-9} \times 110 \times 10^{-12}}}
$$

$$
v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.75 \times 10^{-17}}} = \frac{1}{5.244 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.907 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the saturation length:

$$
l_{\text{sat}} = 0.5 \cdot v \cdot t_r = 0.5 \cdot 1.907 \times 10^{8} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9} = 0.0954 \text{ m} = 95.4 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $\ell = 80$ mm $< l_{\text{sat}} = 95.4$ mm, the line is in the **linear regime**, not yet saturated.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute the peak backward cross talk voltage:

$$
K_b = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{11}{110} - \frac{25}{250}\right) = \frac{1}{4}(0.10 - 0.10) = 0
$$

The backward cross talk is **zero** because the line satisfies the homogeneous-dielectric condition $C_m/C = L_m/L = 0.10$.

$$
V_b^{\text{peak}} = 0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute the peak forward cross talk voltage:

$$
K_f = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} + \frac{L_m}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(0.10 + 0.10) = 0.10
$$

Since $\ell < l_{\text{sat}}$, the forward cross talk is **zero** in a homogeneous dielectric (stripline) regardless of $K_f$:

$$
V_f^{\text{peak}} = 0 \text{ V}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is a well-designed stripline pair with zero cross talk. The design is valid for any noise margin.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Paper Style)]**

**(a) Explain the concept of forward cross talk (FEXT) and backward cross talk (NEXT) in coupled transmission lines. Why does forward cross talk vanish in a homogeneous dielectric?** (7 marks)

**(b) Two PCB microstrip lines (inhomogeneous dielectric) have $L = 330$ nH/m, $C = 120$ pF/m, $L_m = 66$ nH/m, $C_m = 12$ pF/m. The aggressor is a 2.5 V step with $t_r = 0.5$ ns, and the coupled length is $\ell = 50$ mm. Calculate the peak backward and forward cross talk voltages, justifying whether the design meets the Keech noise margin of $V_{DD}/4$.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) — Concept Explanation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Backward Cross Talk (NEXT — Near-End Cross Talk):** When an aggressor edge propagates along line 1, the mutual coupling continuously induces a small voltage on line 2 that propagates **backward** (toward the source end of line 2). This is called "near-end" because it appears at the **near end** (source side) of the victim line. The backward pulse grows as the aggressor edge sweeps past the coupled region.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Forward Cross Talk (FEXT — Far-End Cross Talk):** The mutual coupling also induces a voltage on line 2 that propagates **forward** (toward the load end of line 2). This is called "far-end" because it appears at the **far end** (load side) of the victim line, travelling in step with the aggressor edge. The forward pulse depends on the **imbalance** between capacitive and inductive coupling.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** **Why FEXT vanishes in a homogeneous dielectric:** In a homogeneous dielectric (e.g., stripline between two ground planes, or coax), the electric and magnetic field lines are perfectly confined within the same medium. By the field-continuity and reciprocity theorems, this forces the per-unit-length ratios $C_m/C$ and $L_m/L$ to be **exactly equal**. Substituting into the forward coefficient:

$$
K_f = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} + \frac{L_m}{L}\right) \quad \text{...(per-mode form)}
$$

The forward cross talk **voltage** depends on the **difference** $L_m/L - C_m/C$, not the sum. So:

$$
V_f \propto \left(\frac{L_m}{L} - \frac{C_m}{C}\right) = 0
$$

In a homogeneous dielectric, the **capacitive** forward coupling and the **inductive** forward coupling are equal in magnitude and **opposite in sign** at the load end, cancelling exactly. This is sometimes called the "balanced coupling" condition. Hence, **FEXT = 0** for any length and any rise time. In contrast, NEXT is non-zero and saturates as derived above.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute velocity and saturation length:

$$
v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{330 \times 10^{-9} \times 120 \times 10^{-12}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3.96 \times 10^{-17}}} \approx 1.589 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
l_{\text{sat}} = 0.5 \cdot v \cdot t_r = 0.5 \cdot 1.589 \times 10^{8} \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-9} = 0.0397 \text{ m} = 39.7 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $\ell = 50$ mm $> l_{\text{sat}} = 39.7$ mm, the line is in the **saturated regime**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute peak backward cross talk voltage:

$$
K_b = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{12}{120} - \frac{66}{330}\right) = \frac{1}{4}(0.10 - 0.20) = -0.025
$$

$$
V_b^{\text{peak}} = \vert K_b \vert \cdot V_a = 0.025 \cdot 2.5 = 0.0625 \text{ V} = 62.5 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute peak forward cross talk voltage:

$$
\frac{L_m}{L} - \frac{C_m}{C} = 0.20 - 0.10 = 0.10
$$

$$
V_f^{\text{peak}} = \frac{V_a}{2} \cdot 0.10 \cdot \frac{\ell - l_{\text{sat}}}{t_r v}
$$

$$
V_f^{\text{peak}} = \frac{2.5}{2} \cdot 0.10 \cdot \frac{(50 - 39.7) \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 1.589 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
V_f^{\text{peak}} = 0.125 \cdot \frac{10.3 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0795} = 0.125 \cdot 0.1296 = 0.0162 \text{ V} \approx 16.2 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Check the Keech noise margin:

$$
V_{\text{noise}} = V_b^{\text{peak}} + V_f^{\text{peak}} = 62.5 + 16.2 = 78.7 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
\text{Noise margin threshold} = \frac{V_{DD}}{4} = \frac{2.5}{4} = 0.625 \text{ V} = 625 \text{ mV}
$$

Since $78.7$ mV $\ll 625$ mV, the design **comfortably meets** the Keech noise margin. The PCB microstrip pair is acceptable for this application.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Points (KTU Board Examiner Feedback):**
>
> 1. **Sign of $C_m$ in matrix:** Students often write $+C_m$ instead of $-C_m$ in the KCL equation. The negative sign is mandatory because the displacement current from line 1 to line 2 represents an out-flow from line 1. (Loss: 1 mark)
>
> 2. **Confusing $K_f$ and $K_b$ formula:** The forward coefficient has the **SUM** $C_m/C + L_m/L$, while the backward coefficient has the **DIFFERENCE** $C_m/C - L_m/L$. This swap is the #1 reason for full-mark failure on 7-mark derivations. (Loss: 4 marks)
>
> 3. **Forgetting the saturation length condition:** A student may compute $V_b$ using the linear formula $\ell/(t_r v)$ even when $\ell > l_{\text{sat}}$, getting inflated answers. Always state the regime explicitly. (Loss: 2 marks)
>
> 4. **Forgetting units of $L_m$ and $C_m$:** In PCB notation, the units are nH/inch or pF/inch, NOT just H or F. Always specify "per unit length." (Loss: 0.5 mark)
>
> 5. **Concluding "no cross talk" without justification:** Even when $C_m/C = L_m/L$, the student must explicitly state that this corresponds to a **homogeneous dielectric** to earn full marks. (Loss: 1 mark)
>
> 6. **Mistaking the homogeneous theorem:** Some students think that stripline eliminates backward cross talk. In fact, only forward cross talk is zero in a homogeneous dielectric. Backward cross talk is non-zero in stripline unless $C_m = 0$ (no coupling at all). (Loss: 3 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Cross Talk Definition:** Unwanted coupling between adjacent traces; sources are $C_m$ (electric) and $L_m$ (magnetic).
- **Mutual Capacitance $C_m$:** F/m; produces displacement current; couples **both** forward and backward waves.
- **Mutual Inductance $L_m$:** H/m; produces induced EMF; couples **both** forward and backward waves but with opposite sign to $C_m$ in the backward direction.
- **Backward Cross Talk (NEXT):** Travels to source end of victim. Coefficient: $K_b = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} - \frac{L_m}{L}\right)$. Saturates after $l_{\text{sat}}$.
- **Forward Cross Talk (FEXT):** Travels to load end of victim. Coefficient: $K_f = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_m}{C} + \frac{L_m}{L}\right)$. **Zero in homogeneous dielectric.**
- **Saturation Length:** $l_{\text{sat}} = 0.5 \cdot v \cdot t_r$ where $v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.
- **Key Inequality:** $\vert K_b \vert \le 0.5$ and $\vert K_f \vert \le 1.0$.
- **Homogeneous Dielectric Theorem:** When $\frac{C_m}{C} = \frac{L_m}{L}$, both FEXT and (in symmetric geometries) NEXT are minimized.
- **Stripline vs Microstrip:** Stripline has homogeneous dielectric → FEXT = 0. Microstrip is inhomogeneous → FEXT is non-zero.
- **Engineering Design Rule:** Maintain trace spacing $\ge 3W$ (3W rule) to achieve $C_m/C < 10\%$.
- **Keech Noise Margin:** Total cross talk noise must be less than $\frac{V_{DD}}{4}$ for reliable operation.
- **Practical Mitigation:** Use guard traces, ground pours, differential signaling, and orthogonal routing between adjacent layers.
- **Common Units on PCBs:** $L$ in nH/inch, $C$ in pF/inch, $Z_0$ in $\Omega$, $t_r$ in ps or ns.
- **Velocity on FR4:** $v \approx 1.5 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, corresponding to a propagation delay of about 7 ps/mm.
- **Priority for KTU 14-mark questions:** Always show the matrix form, derive the coefficients, identify the regime (linear vs saturated), then plug in numbers with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **High-Speed Digital Design (PEECT526)**, **Power** refers to the rate at which electrical energy is consumed (and dissipated as heat) by a digital CMOS circuit during operation. Power dissipation is a first-class design constraint alongside speed, area, and cost — often summarized by the classical design trilemma of **Power–Performance–Area (PPA)**.

The **total power** consumed by a CMOS gate is the sum of two fundamental components:

$$P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic}$$

where:
- **P_static** = leakage power consumed even when the circuit is *idle* (no switching)
- **P_dynamic** = active power consumed during logic transitions (charging/discharging of capacitive loads and momentary short-circuit paths)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim from syllabus):** *Power dissipation in digital CMOS circuits — static and dynamic components, switching power, short-circuit power, leakage mechanisms, power-delay product (PDP), energy-delay product (EDP), and power optimization techniques.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Model

Imagine a CMOS inverter as a **leaky water bucket** connected to a tap (the supply V_DD):

| CMOS Element | Real-World Analogy | Behavior |
|--------------|--------------------|----------|
| **V_DD Supply** | Overhead water tank with constant pressure | Provides the "head" that drives current |
| **Load Capacitance (C_L)** | A bucket placed under the tap | Fills up on a 0→1 transition, empties on 1→0 |
| **Switching Power** | The actual water needed to fill/empty the bucket | Proportional to C, V_DD², and switching frequency |
| **Static Leakage** | A pinhole in the bucket — water drips even when "off" | Wastes energy continuously, even when idle |
| **Short-Circuit Current** | A moment when both the tap is on AND the drain is open | Brief wasteful flow during the transition window |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a high-speed IC with **billions of transistors**, even nano-amps of leakage per device aggregate to **watts of standby power**. This is why "static power" is no longer negligible in nanometer CMOS (≤ 65 nm) — it often *dominates* dynamic power in idle mobile/IoT chips.

---

### 1.3 Why Power Matters in High-Speed Design

Power is not merely a battery-life concern — it directly impacts:

- **Thermal Reliability** — every additional watt raises junction temperature, reducing chip lifetime (Arrhenius equation: lifetime halves for every ~10 °C rise).
- **Signal Integrity** — high current surges cause **IR drop** and **ground bounce**, which are *signal integrity problems* that the PEECT526 syllabus directly addresses.
- **Packaging Cost** — higher power demands more expensive packages (heat sinks, fans, ceramic vs. plastic).
- **Electromigration** — sustained high current density physically damages metal interconnects.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Favorite Constants to Memorize:**
> - **V_DD** (supply voltage) — typically **1.0 V – 1.2 V** for 28 nm/16 nm nodes
> - **Threshold Voltage (V_th)** — typically **0.3 V – 0.5 V**
> - **Subthreshold Slope (S)** — ideally ~**60 mV/decade** at room temperature (300 K)
> - **Thermal Voltage (V_T = kT/q)** ≈ **25.85 mV** at 300 K
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component 1 — Dynamic Switching Power (P_switch)

When a CMOS gate switches from logic 0 → 1, current flows from V_DD through the PMOS network to charge the load capacitance C_L from 0 to V_DD. On the 1 → 0 transition, C_L is discharged to ground through the NMOS network. **No current flows from V_DD to ground** in a properly designed CMOS gate (this is the famous CMOS advantage!).

**Step-by-step logical reasoning:**

1. Each switching event transfers a charge of **Q = C_L · V_DD** from V_DD to ground.
2. The energy drawn from the supply per transition is **E_supply = C_L · V_DD²**.
3. Half of this energy is stored on C_L; the other half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS.
4. On discharge, the stored energy is dissipated as heat in the NMOS.
5. Thus, **energy dissipated per transition = (1/2) C_L V_DD²** in C_L charging + (1/2) C_L V_DD² in C_L discharging = **C_L V_DD²** total per full cycle.
6. If the gate switches at frequency f with switching activity factor α (0 ≤ α ≤ 1), the average power is:

$$P_{switching} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

---

### 2.2 Component 2 — Short-Circuit Power (P_sc)

During every logic transition, both the PMOS and NMOS transistors are **simultaneously ON** for a brief window (the rise/fall time of the input). A direct current path exists from V_DD to GND during this window.

For a symmetric CMOS inverter with equal rise/fall time τ, the short-circuit power is approximately:

$$P_{short\text{-}circuit} = \frac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^{3} \cdot \tau \cdot f_{clk}$$

where **β = μC_ox (W/L)** is the transistor transconductance parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer Tip:** P_sc is minimized when **input rise/fall time << output rise/fall time**, i.e., when output load is large relative to input slope. This is why deep pipelining with sharp clock edges helps.

---

### 2.3 Component 3 — Static (Leakage) Power (P_static)

In modern nanometer CMOS, static power is no longer negligible. There are **four principal leakage mechanisms**:

| # | Leakage Mechanism | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude (45 nm) |
|---|-------------------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Subthreshold Leakage (I_sub)** | Carrier diffusion between source/drain when V_GS < V_th | Dominant — μA per device |
| 2 | **Gate Oxide Tunneling (I_gate)** | Quantum-mechanical tunneling through thin SiO₂ (≤ 2 nm) | nA per device |
| 3 | **Reverse-Biased Junction Leakage (I_rev)** | Band-to-band tunneling at drain-body p-n junction | pA per device |
| 4 | **GIDL (Gate-Induced Drain Leakage)** | Band tunneling at high V_DG overlap | nA per device |

The dominant subthreshold current is given by the **BSIM-style equation**:

$$I_{sub} = \mu_0 C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^{2} \cdot e^{\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th} - V_{off}}{n V_T}\right)} \cdot \left(1 - e^{\frac{-V_{DS}}{V_T}}\right)$$

In strong inversion with V_DS >> V_T, this simplifies to the more familiar form used in KTU derivations:

$$I_{leak} \approx I_0 \cdot 10^{\left(\frac{-V_{th} + V_{GS}}{S}\right)}$$

where **S** is the subthreshold swing (mV/decade). Hence:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$

---

### 2.4 Figure of Merits — PDP and EDP

Two important metrics that trade off **power** and **performance** are:

$$PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p = \text{Energy per switching event (Joules)}$$

$$EDP = PDP \cdot t_p = \text{Energy-Delay Product}$$

> [!TIP]
> **EDP** is the *better* metric when comparing technologies for high-performance applications, because it penalizes both *high energy* and *long delay* simultaneously.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | P_switch = α C_L V_DD² f | Average switching power | Watts (W) |
| 2 | P_sc = (β/12)(V_DD − 2V_th)³ τ f | Short-circuit (crowbar) power | Watts (W) |
| 3 | P_static = V_DD · I_leak | Leakage (standby) power | Watts (W) |
| 4 | P_total = P_static + P_switch + P_sc | Total chip power | Watts (W) |
| 5 | E_per_switch = C_L · V_DD² | Energy per 0→1→0 cycle | Joules (J) |
| 6 | PDP = P · t_p | Energy per operation | Joules (J) |
| 7 | EDP = PDP · t_p | Quality-of-service metric | J·s |
| 8 | I_sub = I_0 · 10^{−V_th/S} | Subthreshold leakage (V_GS=0) | Amps (A) |
| 9 | P_dynamic ∝ C_L V_DD² f | Quadratic voltage dependence! | — |
| 10 | f_max ∝ (V_DD − V_th)^α / V_DD | Gate delay scaling law | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL OBSERVATION (Examiner's Pet Topic):** Because P_switch is **quadratic in V_DD**, reducing V_DD from 1.2 V to 0.8 V cuts switching power by a factor of **(0.8/1.2)² ≈ 0.44** — a **56% reduction**. This is the physical basis of **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)**, the single most effective low-power technique taught in this module.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations drive every modern low-power design decision:

- **Mobile SoCs (Apple A-series, Snapdragon):** use **DVFS** with V_DD steps of 25 mV to minimize battery drain.
- **IoT / Wearables (ARM Cortex-M0+):** use **sleep modes** with I_leak in nA range.
- **Server CPUs (Intel Xeon):** use **clock gating** to cut α to near zero in idle cores.
- **GPUs (NVIDIA):** use **power gating** to turn off entire SM blocks.
- **FPGAs (Xilinx, Altera):** expose **multi-Vt libraries** so synthesis tools trade speed vs leakage per cell.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Switching Power (P_switch)

We derive P_switch = α C_L V_DD² f from first principles using **charge-flow analysis**.

**Step 1 — Charge transferred per 0→1 transition:**

When the output node transitions from 0 V to V_DD, the PMOS network sources charge to the load capacitance C_L.

$$Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Step 2 — Energy drawn from the supply per 0→1 transition:**

The instantaneous current from the supply is i(t) = C_L · (dV_out/dt). The energy supplied by V_DD is the integral of V_DD · i(t) dt from 0 to ∞:

$$E_{supply,0\to1} = \int_{0}^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot i(t)\, dt = V_{DD} \cdot Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy stored on the capacitor after the transition:**

The energy stored on a capacitor charged to V_DD is:

$$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated in the PMOS during the 0→1 transition:**

By energy conservation, the difference goes to heat in the PMOS channel:

$$E_{PMOS} = E_{supply,0\to1} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Energy dissipated in the NMOS during the 1→0 transition:**

During 1→0, the charge stored on C_L is dumped to ground through the NMOS. **No energy is drawn from V_DD during this transition** (the supply is disconnected). All stored energy is dissipated as heat in the NMOS channel.

$$E_{NMOS} = E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Total energy dissipated per full 0→1→0 cycle:**

$$E_{cycle} = E_{PMOS} + E_{NMOS} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to average power:**

If the gate performs N such cycles per second, the switching frequency is f = N. If only a fraction α of cycles actually transition (activity factor), then the average switching power is:

$$P_{switching} = \frac{\alpha \cdot E_{cycle}}{T} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

This completes the derivation. **Key result:** P_switch scales **quadratically with V_DD** and **linearly with f, C_L, and α**.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Short-Circuit Power (P_sc)

We derive the approximate short-circuit power equation for a symmetric CMOS inverter.

**Step 1 — Identify the short-circuit window:**

A short-circuit current I_sc flows from V_DD to GND through both PMOS and NMOS whenever the input voltage V_in is in the range:

$$V_{th,n} \le V_{in} \le V_{DD} - V_{th,p}$$

For a symmetric inverter with V_th,n = V_th,p = V_th, the window width is:

$$\Delta V = V_{DD} - 2V_{th}$$

**Step 2 — Saturation current at the peak of the short-circuit:**

The short-circuit current peaks when V_in = V_DD/2, where both devices are in saturation. Using the square-law model:

$$I_{sc,peak} = \frac{\beta}{2} \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{th}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Average short-circuit current over a ramp input:**

Assuming a linear input ramp with rise time τ, the short-circuit current is a triangular pulse of base ΔV and peak I_sc,peak. The average current during the ramp is:

$$I_{sc,avg} = \frac{1}{2} I_{sc,peak} = \frac{\beta}{4} \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{th}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Charge transferred per ramp event:**

$$Q_{sc} = I_{sc,avg} \cdot \tau = \frac{\beta \tau}{4} \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{th}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Energy per ramp event (drawn from V_DD):**

$$E_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot Q_{sc} = \frac{\beta \tau V_{DD}}{4} \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{th}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Account for both rise and fall edges per clock cycle (factor of 2):**

$$E_{sc,cycle} = 2 \cdot E_{sc} = \frac{\beta \tau V_{DD}}{2} \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{th}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to average power with activity factor α and clock frequency f:**

$$P_{sc} = \alpha f \cdot E_{sc,cycle} = \frac{\alpha \beta \tau f V_{DD}}{2} \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{th}\right)^{2}$$

A commonly quoted simplified form (after expanding the cubed term in symmetric conditions) is:

$$P_{sc} \approx \frac{\alpha \beta \tau f}{12} \left(V_{DD} - 2V_{th}\right)^{3}$$

Both forms are accepted at KTU. The (V_DD − 2V_th)³ dependence shows that P_sc becomes **negligible** as V_DD approaches 2V_th — another reason to use low V_DD in modern design.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Power Optimization Techniques

| # | Technique | Power Component Reduced | Typical Reduction | Trade-off |
|---|-----------|------------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Clock Gating** | P_switch (α↓) | 20–40% chip power | Added latency on wake |
| 2 | **Power Gating (Sleep Transistors)** | P_static → ≈ 0 | 90–99% standby | Wake-up latency, area |
| 3 | **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)** | P_switch (V²↓) | Up to 50–60% | Slower circuits |
| 4 | **Dynamic Frequency Scaling (DFS)** | P_switch (f↓) | Linear in f | Slower execution |
| 5 | **Multi-Vt Libraries** | P_static (I_sub↓) | 5–20× leakage | Slower LVT cells |
| 6 | **Transistor Sizing (down)** | P_switch (C_L↓) | 10–20% | Slower drive strength |
| 7 | **Operand Isolation** | P_switch (α↓) | 5–15% in datapaths | Extra gating logic |
| 8 | **Adiabatic / Reversible Logic** | P_switch (recovery) | Theoretical 100% | Complex clocking |
| 9 | **Multi-V_DD Domains** | P_switch (V²↓ on non-critical) | 30–50% | Level shifters needed |
| 10 | **Body Biasing (ABB / RBB)** | P_static via V_th modulation | 2–5× | Forward bias ↑ I_gate |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (Analytical)

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter in a 28 nm technology drives C_L = 50 fF, V_DD = 1.0 V, f = 1 GHz, α = 0.2, V_th = 0.4 V. Compute (a) P_switch, (b) P_sc with β = 200 μA/V² and τ = 50 ps, (c) P_total if I_leak = 100 nA.

**Solution:**

(a) Switching power:
$$P_{switch} = 0.2 \times 50\times10^{-15} \times (1.0)^{2} \times 10^{9} = 10\ \mu W$$

(b) Short-circuit power (using full form):
$$P_{sc} = \frac{0.2 \times 200\times10^{-6} \times 50\times10^{-12} \times 10^{9}}{12} \times (1.0 - 0.8)^{3}$$
$$P_{sc} = \frac{2\times10^{-6}}{12} \times 0.008 \approx 1.33\ pW \quad \text{(negligible!)}$$

(c) Static power:
$$P_{static} = 1.0 \times 100\times10^{-9} = 100\ nW$$

(d) Total power:
$$P_{total} = 10\ \mu W + 1.33\ pW + 100\ nW \approx 10.1\ \mu W$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For an inverter at 28 nm, switching power **dominates** leakage by ~100×. However, for **billions of idle transistors** in a server, leakage dominates. The relative balance shifts with technology node and utilization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Distribution Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External V_DD Supply] --> B[PCB Power Plane]
    B --> C[Package Pin BGA]
    C --> D[On-Chip Power Ring]
    D --> E[Power Distribution Grid MESH]
    E --> F[Decoupling Capacitors]
    E --> G[Standard Cell Rows V_DD Rail]
    E --> H[Clock Tree Buffers]
    E --> I[SRAM / Macros]
    F -->|Charge Reservoir| G
    F -->|Charge Reservoir| H
    F -->|Charge Reservoir| I
    G --> J[Local Switching Activity]
    H --> K[Clock Gating Cells]
    I --> L[Memory Access Surges]
    J --> M[IR Drop + Ground Bounce]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Power Supply Noise SSN]
    style A fill:#ffe599
    style E fill:#c5e0b4
    style F fill:#bdd7ee
    style M fill:#f4b183
    style N fill:#e06666
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Yellow** = off-chip source.
- **Green** = the power grid (must be a low-impedance mesh to minimize IR drop).
- **Blue** = decoupling capacitors (on-chip and package) that act as **local charge reservoirs** to supply instantaneous current surges.
- **Orange/Red** = the **noise injection points** — wherever current surges occur (switching logic, clock edges, memory access), the parasitic resistance and inductance of the grid convert these surges into voltage fluctuations on V_DD/GND.

---

### 4.2 CMOS Inverter Power Dissipation Flow (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input V_in Transition] --> B{P_in is High or Low}
    B -->|Rising Edge| C[Both PMOS and NMOS ON]
    C --> D[Short-Circuit Current I_sc Flows]
    D --> E[P_sc Dissipated]
    B -->|After Transition| F[Only PMOS or NMOS ON]
    F --> G{C_L Charging or Discharging}
    G -->|Charging| H[Energy from V_DD]
    H --> I[Half Stored on C_L]
    H --> J[Half Dissipated in PMOS]
    G -->|Discharging| K[Energy from C_L]
    K --> L[All Dissipated in NMOS]
    M[Always On] --> N[Subthreshold Leakage I_leak]
    M --> O[Gate Tunneling I_gate]
    M --> P[Junction Leakage I_rev]
    N --> Q[P_static Dissipated]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    style E fill:#f4b183
    style J fill:#f4b183
    style L fill:#f4b183
    style Q fill:#e06666
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Orange paths** = dynamic (switching) dissipation — happens only during transitions.
- **Red paths** = static (leakage) dissipation — happens *all the time*, regardless of switching.

---

### 4.3 Power Optimization Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Power Budget Exceeded] --> B{Performance Critical?}
    B -->|YES| C[Use Clock Gating]
    B -->|NO| D[Use Power Gating]
    C --> E{Still Over Budget?}
    D --> E
    E -->|YES| F{V_DD Already Minimum?}
    F -->|NO| G[Apply DVFS Lower V_DD]
    F -->|YES| H{Frequency Already Minimum?}
    H -->|NO| I[Apply DFS Lower f]
    H -->|YES| J[Multi-Vt Library Swap]
    J --> K{Leakage or Speed Bound?}
    K -->|Leakage| L[Use HVT Cells]
    K -->|Speed| M[Use LVT Cells on Critical Path]
    L --> N{Still Over Budget?}
    M --> N
    N -->|YES| O[Operand Isolation]
    N -->|NO| P[Budget Met]
    O --> P
    style A fill:#ffe599
    style P fill:#93c47d
    style N fill:#f4b183
```

**Decision Logic Summary:**
1. First try **clock gating** (cheapest, lowest risk).
2. Then **power gating** for idle blocks.
3. If performance slack exists, **lower V_DD first** (quadratic gain).
4. Then lower **frequency**.
5. Finally, swap **Vt libraries** — last resort because it requires resynthesis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
*Define dynamic power dissipation in CMOS circuits. Write the equation for switching power and explain why the dependence on V_DD is quadratic.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Dynamic power is the power consumed by a CMOS circuit during logic transitions, primarily due to the charging and discharging of load capacitances.
- **Equation (1 mark):** P_switch = α · C_L · V_DD² · f, where α = switching activity, C_L = load capacitance, V_DD = supply voltage, f = clock frequency.
- **Quadratic V_DD explanation (1 mark):** During each 0→1 transition, the energy drawn from the supply is C_L V_DD². This energy is the integral of V_DD · i(t) dt, where V_DD is the voltage across which the charge C_L V_DD is transferred. Hence the V_DD² dependence. Consequently, reducing V_DD from 1.2 V to 0.8 V reduces switching power to (0.8/1.2)² ≈ 44%, i.e., a ~56% reduction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any THREE sources of static power dissipation in nanometer CMOS. Why is static power a serious concern in sub-65 nm technologies?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Three sources (2 marks):**
  1. Subthreshold leakage (I_sub) — carriers diffusing from source to drain when V_GS < V_th.
  2. Gate-oxide tunneling (I_gate) — quantum-mechanical tunneling through thin gate dielectric.
  3. Reverse-biased junction leakage (I_rev) — band-to-band tunneling at the drain-body p-n junction.
- **Concern explanation (1 mark):** In sub-65 nm nodes, V_th is aggressively scaled to maintain drive current, which causes exponential growth in I_sub. Additionally, gate oxide is < 2 nm, making I_gate comparable to I_sub. Hence static power is no longer negligible — in many idle mobile/IoT chips, P_static now equals or exceeds P_dynamic.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **average dynamic switching power** in a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance C_L at clock frequency f. Clearly state the meaning of the activity factor α. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 32-bit ripple-carry adder in 45 nm CMOS has the following specifications:
- Total load capacitance: C_total = 8 pF
- V_DD = 1.0 V, V_th = 0.35 V
- Clock frequency f = 500 MHz
- Activity factor α = 0.15
- Leakage current I_leak = 5 μA

Calculate: (i) Switching power, (ii) Static power, (iii) Total power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of dynamic switching power (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark): On a 0→1 transition, the PMOS network sources charge Q = C_L V_DD to the load capacitance.

Step 2 (1 mark): Energy drawn from supply per 0→1 transition:
$$E_{supply,0\to1} = V_{DD} \cdot Q = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

Step 3 (1 mark): Energy stored on C_L after charging = ½ C_L V_DD².

Step 4 (1 mark): Energy dissipated in PMOS = C_L V_DD² − ½ C_L V_DD² = ½ C_L V_DD².

Step 5 (1 mark): During 1→0 transition, the stored ½ C_L V_DD² is fully dissipated in the NMOS. No energy is drawn from V_DD.

Step 6 (1 mark): Total energy per full cycle = ½ C_L V_DD² + ½ C_L V_DD² = C_L V_DD².

Step 7 (1 mark): With switching activity α and clock frequency f, average switching power:
$$P_{switch} = \alpha f C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

The **activity factor α** represents the probability (or fraction of clock cycles) that the gate output actually transitions. For example, α = 1 means every clock edge toggles the output; α = 0.1 means only 10% of cycles toggle. For a clock buffer, α = 1; for a static data line holding the same value, α ≈ 0.

---

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

(i) Switching power (2 marks):
$$P_{switch} = 0.15 \times 8\times10^{-12} \times (1.0)^{2} \times 500\times10^{6}$$
$$P_{switch} = 0.15 \times 8 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 600\ \mu W$$

(ii) Static power (2 marks):
$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} = 1.0 \times 5\times10^{-6} = 5\ \mu W$$

(iii) Total power (1 mark, neglect P_sc):
$$P_{total} = 600\ \mu W + 5\ \mu W = 605\ \mu W$$

Conclusion (2 marks): Switching power dominates by 120×, indicating this adder is performance-active. For an idle adder, the same chip would dissipate only 5 μW — illustrating the importance of **clock gating** to reduce α when computation is not needed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam — Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **four major leakage mechanisms** in nanometer CMOS with neat sketches of energy band diagrams. Derive the subthreshold current expression in terms of V_th and the subthreshold swing S. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system-on-chip (SoC) consumes P = 2 W at V_DD = 1.2 V, f = 1.2 GHz, and C_total = 10 nF. The system is to be migrated to a low-power mode by reducing V_DD to 0.8 V and f to 600 MHz (assuming dynamic power dominates). Calculate: (i) The new power consumption, (ii) The percentage power reduction, (iii) Comment on the effect on system throughput. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four leakage mechanisms and subthreshold derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Subthreshold leakage I_sub (2 marks):** When V_GS < V_th, the channel is nominally off, but carriers still diffuse from source to drain. It is modeled as:
$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot 10^{(V_{GS} - V_{th})/S}$$
where S = subthreshold swing (mV/decade), typically ~70 mV/decade at room temp.

2. **Gate-oxide tunneling I_gate (1.5 marks):** In ultra-thin oxide (< 2 nm), electrons tunnel quantum-mechanically from the gate through the SiO₂ into the channel. This is **Fowler-Nordheim** or **direct tunneling**.

3. **Reverse-biased junction leakage I_rev (1 mark):** At the drain-body p-n junction (reverse-biased), band-to-band tunneling (BTBT) generates a small leakage current.

4. **GIDL — Gate-Induced Drain Leakage (1 mark):** When V_DG is large, the band bending at the drain surface causes BTBT, especially in devices with thin oxide and high V_DD.

Subthreshold derivation (1.5 marks): Starting from the diffusion current equation in weak inversion, taking the log10 and defining the subthreshold swing S = (n V_T) ln(10) gives the linear-in-log10 form:
$$\log_{10}(I_{sub}) = \log_{10}(I_0) + \frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{S}$$
which shows that every 100 mV reduction in V_th → 10× increase in I_sub (for S = 100 mV/dec).

---

**(b) DVFS calculation (7 marks):**

(i) Initial power and new power (3 marks):
$$P_{old} = \alpha C V_{DD,old}^{2} f_{old} = 1 \times 10\times10^{-9} \times (1.2)^{2} \times 1.2\times10^{9} = 17.28\ W$$

(Note: the chip has additional non-capacitive power; we'll assume P = α C V² f as the dynamic component only. Using the given P_old = 2 W as the baseline, the new power scales as:)
$$\frac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} = \left(\frac{V_{DD,new}}{V_{DD,old}}\right)^{2} \times \frac{f_{new}}{f_{old}}$$
$$\frac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} = \left(\frac{0.8}{1.2}\right)^{2} \times \frac{600}{1200} = 0.444 \times 0.5 = 0.222$$

$$P_{new} = 2 \times 0.222 = 0.444\ W$$

(ii) Percentage reduction (2 marks):
$$\% \text{reduction} = \frac{2 - 0.444}{2} \times 100\% = 77.8\%$$

(iii) Throughput comment (2 marks): Throughput is directly proportional to clock frequency (instructions/second). Reducing f from 1.2 GHz to 600 MHz halves the maximum throughput. To compensate, the system would need ~2× more parallelism (e.g., dual-core) to maintain the original throughput at this low power. This trade-off is the central tenet of **DVFS-based power management** in modern mobile SoCs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing energy and power** — writing "P = C V²" instead of "E = C V²". Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the activity factor α** — many students write P = C V² f, which is wrong; α is *not* implicitly 1.
> 3. **Confusing subthreshold swing S (mV/decade) with thermal voltage V_T (~25.85 mV)** — they are related but not equal: S = n · V_T · ln(10) ≈ 2.3 · n · V_T.
> 4. **Mixing up V_th, V_GS, V_DS** in the subthreshold equation — write them clearly.
> 5. **Not specifying units** in numerical problems — KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 6. **Saying "power = I²R" for CMOS** — this is a circuits answer, not a digital CMOS answer. The CMOS power equation is **P = α C V² f**, not I²R.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention both charging AND discharging losses** in switching power derivation — both contribute ½ C V² each, totaling C V² per cycle.
> 8. **Missing the (V_DD − 2V_th)³ term** in short-circuit power — this is the signature feature of that derivation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power in High-Speed Digital Design (PEECT526 Module 2)**
> - [x] **P_total = P_static + P_dynamic** (P_sc usually minor and often lumped with P_dynamic).
> - [x] **P_switch = α · C_L · V_DD² · f** — quadratic in V_DD, linear in f and C_L.
> - [x] **P_sc ≈ (β/12)(V_DD − 2V_th)³ τ f** — negligible when V_DD ≈ 2 V_th.
> - [x] **P_static = V_DD · I_leak**, where I_leak is dominated by I_sub in nanometer CMOS.
> - [x] **I_sub = I_0 · 10^{(V_GS − V_th)/S}**, S ≈ 70 mV/dec.
> - [x] **4 leakage mechanisms:** I_sub, I_gate, I_rev, GIDL.
> - [x] **PDP = P · t_p** (energy per operation); **EDP = PDP · t_p** (best quality metric).
> - [x] **Clock gating** reduces α; **Power gating** reduces P_static to ~0.
> - [x] **DVFS** is the most effective dynamic technique (quadratic V² savings).
> - [x] **Multi-Vt** trades speed for leakage — LVT for critical path, HVT for non-critical.
> - [x] **Power integrity** = low-impedance PDN + adequate decoupling to manage SSN and IR drop.
> - [x] **Thermal rule of thumb:** every 10 °C junction rise → ~50% reduction in chip lifetime.
> - [x] **Memory aid:** "**V²**" — think **"Voltage-squared gives Big Savings"** in low-power design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quiescent vs Active Dissipation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quiescent vs Active Dissipation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **High Speed Digital Design** and CMOS VLSI systems, *power dissipation* in a digital gate or interconnect is broadly classified into two macroscopic regimes based on the *switching activity* of the node:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quiescent (Static) Dissipation** is the power consumed by a CMOS logic gate when it is in a **steady-state (non-switching) condition** — i.e., its inputs and outputs are held constant at valid logic levels (logic '0' or logic '1'). It is dominated by *leakage currents* flowing from $V_{DD}$ to ground even when the transistor network is nominally OFF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active (Dynamic) Dissipation** is the power consumed by a CMOS logic gate when it is **actively switching** — i.e., its output node is being charged or discharged through the load capacitance. It is dominated by the *charging/discharging of parasitic capacitances* and *short-circuit (crow-bar) currents* during the finite input rise/fall time.

The **total average power dissipation** of a CMOS gate is therefore expressed as the linear superposition of these two orthogonal components:

$$P_{total} = P_{active} + P_{quiescent}$$

Where:
- $P_{active}$ scales with the **switching frequency** and **node activity factor** $\alpha$
- $P_{quiescent}$ is largely **frequency-independent** and is set by the technology's *leakage characteristics*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plumbing System"

Imagine a water distribution system in a building:

- **Quiescent dissipation** is like the **small, constant trickle of water** that leaks through a slightly open valve or seeps through a worn pipe joint — even when no taps are open. You pay for this water (power) constantly, 24×7, regardless of whether anyone is using the bathroom.
- **Active dissipation** is like the **large surge of water** that gushes through the pipes when someone actually opens a tap and fills a bucket. You only pay for this during the actual usage event.

In a modern smartphone SoC running an idle home screen, the *quiescent* leakage power can be **30%–50% of the total power budget** — a stark contrast to older technologies (>180 nm) where quiescent power was negligible. This is why high-speed digital designers obsess over *power gating*, *body biasing*, and *MTCMOS* techniques.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Supply Voltage $V_{DD}$**: typically **0.7 V – 1.2 V** in modern sub-22 nm nodes
> - **Threshold Voltage $V_T$**: typically **0.2 V – 0.4 V**
> - **Subthreshold Swing $S$**: ideally **≥ 60 mV/decade** (thermal limit, $kT/q \cdot \ln(10)$)
> - **Load Capacitance $C_L$**: typically **1 fF – 100 fF** per gate output
> - **Activity Factor $\alpha$**: probability that a node switches in a clock cycle (0 ≤ $\alpha$ ≤ 1)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

The two dissipation mechanisms arise from **fundamentally different physical phenomena**:

#### A. Active (Dynamic) Dissipation — Three Sub-Components

1. **Switching Power ($P_{sw}$)** — The dominant contributor.
   - **Why?** Every CMOS output node has a *parasitic capacitance* $C_L$ (gate capacitance of fan-out + diffusion capacitance of drain regions + interconnect capacitance).
   - **How?** When the output transitions from $0 \to 1$, $C_L$ is charged through the PMOS network, drawing energy from $V_{DD}$. When it transitions $1 \to 0$, $C_L$ is discharged to ground through the NMOS network — but the energy previously stored in the capacitor is *dissipated as heat* in the NMOS (not recovered).

2. **Short-Circuit Power ($P_{sc}$)** — The "crow-bar" current.
   - **Why?** Real input signals have *finite rise/fall times*, so during the brief interval when $V_{in}$ is between $V_{TN}$ and $V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$, both the NMOS and PMOS transistors are simultaneously ON, creating a *direct low-resistance path* from $V_{DD}$ to GND.
   - **How?** This direct current pulse (typically lasting 10–100 ps) dumps charge to ground every switching event.

3. **Glitching Power** — Caused by unequal gate propagation delays within a combinational path, leading to spurious intermediate transitions.

#### B. Quiescent (Static) Dissipation — Leakage Sub-Components

1. **Subthreshold Leakage ($I_{sub}$)** — The dominant leakage in modern nodes.
   - **Why?** Even when $V_{GS} < V_T$, a small *exponential* current flows from drain to source in the "OFF" transistor, governed by the weak-inversion MOSFET equation.
   - **How?** Modelled by the BSIM framework; depends exponentially on $V_T$ and linearly (approximately) on $V_{DS}$.

2. **Gate Oxide Tunneling Leakage ($I_{gate}$)** — Dominant in nodes ≤ 45 nm.
   - **Why?** When the gate oxide ($\text{SiO}_2$ or High-$\kappa$) becomes ultra-thin (≤ 1.5 nm), electrons *quantum-mechanically tunnel* through the potential barrier, even when the transistor is OFF.

3. **Reverse-Biased Junction Leakage ($I_{rev}$)** — Band-to-band tunneling at the drain-body junction.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Symbol | Governing Equation | Unit | Boundary / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Switching Power** | $P_{sw}$ | $\alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | W | Dominant in $>90$ nm |
| 2 | **Short-Circuit Power** | $P_{sc}$ | $\alpha \cdot V_{DD} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot t_{sc} \cdot f_{clk}$ | W | Negligible if $t_r, t_f$ are sharp |
| 3 | **Total Active Power** | $P_{active}$ | $P_{sw} + P_{sc} + P_{glitch}$ | W | Frequency-dependent |
| 4 | **Subthreshold Leakage** | $I_{sub}$ | $I_0 \cdot e^{(V_{GS}-V_T)/(nV_T)} \cdot (1 - e^{-V_{DS}/V_T})$ | A | Dominant leakage |
| 5 | **Static Quiescent Power** | $P_{quiescent}$ | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak,total}$ | W | Frequency-independent |
| 6 | **Total Power** | $P_{total}$ | $P_{active} + P_{quiescent}$ | W | Engineering design target |
| 7 | **Energy per Transition** | $E_{sw}$ | $\frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | J | Energy *delivered* by $V_{DD}$ |
| 8 | **Energy Dissipated** | $E_{diss}$ | $\frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | J | Energy *lost* as heat per $0\!\to\!1$ |
| 9 | **Power-Delay Product** | PDP | $P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | J | Figure of merit |
| 10 | **Subthreshold Swing** | $S$ | $\ln(10) \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | V/decade | $\geq 60$ mV/dec (thermal limit) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $V_T$ (lowercase $t$ in $V_T$ subscript) refers to the **thermal voltage** $kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K — *not* the threshold voltage of the MOSFET (also conventionally written $V_T$). Context disambiguates.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoC design**: At 7 nm, $P_{quiescent}$ can equal $P_{active}$ in lightly-loaded modules (e.g., always-on domain), forcing designers to use *power-gating* to physically disconnect $V_{DD}$ from idle blocks.
- **Datacenter ASICs**: At 5 nm, leakage dominates the *dark silicon* problem — chips cannot be fully powered on simultaneously due to thermal limits.
- **IoT / Edge devices**: Quiescent power directly determines *battery standby lifetime* (months to years). Designers minimize $I_{leak}$ via **High-$V_T$** (HVT) and **Reverse Body Biasing (RBB)**.
- **High-Performance CPUs**: Active power still dominates at full turbo boost; designers use *DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)* to control the cubic $P \propto V^2 f$ relationship.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Active Switching Power ($P_{sw}$)

**Step 1 — Model the Output Node:**
We model the CMOS inverter output node as a single lumped *load capacitance* $C_L$ connected between the output and ground. The pull-up network (PMOS) acts as a switch with on-resistance $R_p$ when charging; the pull-down network (NMOS) has $R_n$ when discharging.

**Step 2 — Compute the Energy Drawn from $V_{DD}$ During a $0 \to 1$ Transition:**
The capacitor $C_L$ is uncharged initially (energy = 0). The final voltage across it is $V_{DD}$, with final stored energy:

$$E_{final} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

The total charge delivered by the $V_{DD}$ source is $Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$, so the *total energy drawn from the supply* is:

$$E_{V_{DD}} = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Energy Drawn from $V_{DD}$ During a $1 \to 0$ Transition:**
The capacitor is discharged through the NMOS to ground. **No energy is drawn from $V_{DD}$** during this phase. The energy stored in the capacitor ($E_{final}$) is fully *dissipated as heat* in the NMOS channel resistance.

**Step 4 — Compute the Average Energy Dissipated per Full Switching Cycle:**
A full cycle consists of one $0 \to 1$ and one $1 \to 0$ transition:

$$E_{cycle} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \quad \text{(drawn from supply on } 0\!\to\!1\text{)} + 0 \quad \text{(on } 1\!\to\!0\text{)} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

Subtracting the *recoverable* energy stored in the capacitor at the end of the $0 \to 1$ phase (which is NOT recoverable in standard CMOS), the *net energy dissipated as heat* per cycle is:

$$E_{diss/cycle} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the Activity Factor $\alpha$ and Clock Frequency $f$:**
In a real circuit, a node switches with probability $\alpha$ per clock cycle (statistical switching activity). The average number of *full cycles per second* is $\alpha \cdot f$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}$$

> **Conversion logic note:** This is the single most important equation in digital VLSI power design. It is *quadratic* in $V_{DD}$, which is why *voltage scaling* is the most powerful knob to reduce active power. A 20% voltage reduction yields a ~36% power reduction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Subthreshold Leakage Current ($I_{sub}$)

**Step 1 — Start from the EKV/BSIM Weak-Inversion Model:**
When $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$, the MOSFET operates in the *subthreshold (weak-inversion) regime*. The drain current is dominated by *diffusion* of carriers and is exponentially dependent on gate overdrive:

$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot e^{(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) / (n \cdot U_T)} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-V_{DS}/U_T}\right)$$

Where:
- $I_0 = \mu C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} U_T^2 \, e^{1.8}$ (technology-dependent pre-factor)
- $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$ (subthreshold ideality factor, typically 1.3–1.5)
- $U_T = kT/q$ (thermal voltage, 25.85 mV at 300 K)
- $V_{TH}$ is the *threshold voltage* (different from $U_T$!)

**Step 2 — Simplify for $V_{DS} \gg U_T$:**
For $V_{DS} > 200$ mV, the term $(1 - e^{-V_{DS}/U_T}) \to 1$, and we obtain the *canonical* subthreshold leakage expression:

$$I_{sub} \approx I_0 \cdot e^{(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) / (n \cdot U_T)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to an OFF NMOS in a Steady-State CMOS Inverter:**
When the inverter input is HIGH ($V_{in} = V_{DD}$), the NMOS is OFF, so $V_{GS} = 0$ (since source is at ground). The subthreshold current becomes:

$$I_{leak,off} = I_0 \cdot e^{(-V_{TH}) / (n \cdot U_T)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Quiescent Power:**
Summing all OFF-state leakage currents in the gate and multiplying by $V_{DD}$:

$$\boxed{P_{quiescent} = V_{DD} \cdot \sum_{i} I_{leak,i}}$$

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Active vs. Quiescent Dissipation

| Engineering Attribute | Active (Dynamic) Dissipation | Quiescent (Static) Dissipation |
|---|---|---|
| **When does it occur?** | Only during logic transitions | Always — even at steady state |
| **Primary physical cause** | Capacitor charging/discharging | Leakage currents through OFF devices |
| **Dependency on $f$** | Linear in $f$ | **Frequency-independent** |
| **Dependency on $V_{DD}$** | **Quadratic** ($V_{DD}^2$) | Near-linear (exponential in $V_T$) |
| **Dependency on $V_{TH}$** | Weak (slight via $t_p$ change) | **Exponential** (a 100 mV $\uparrow V_T$ ⇒ 10× $\downarrow$ leakage) |
| **Dependency on Temperature** | Modest increase (mobility $\downarrow$) | **Strong exponential increase** ($I_{sub}$ doubles every ~10 °C) |
| **Dominant technology regime** | $>90$ nm nodes | $\leq 65$ nm nodes |
| **Mitigation techniques** | Clock gating, DVFS, pipelining | Power gating, MTCMOS, body biasing, HVT cells |
| **Energy per operation** | $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Continuous draw — *no energy per event* |
| **Typical magnitude (5 nm)** | ~60–70% of $P_{total}$ (active mode) | ~30–40% of $P_{total}$ |
| **Typical magnitude (180 nm)** | ~95% of $P_{total}$ | ~5% of $P_{total}$ |
| **Is it zero if clock is stopped?** | **Yes** (if all activity stops) | **No** — leakage persists |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Power Dissipation Taxonomy in CMOS

The following block-level flow diagram maps the *functional decomposition* of total power dissipation into its sub-mechanisms, the physical origins, and the standard engineering mitigation techniques:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total CMOS Power Dissipation P_total] --> B[Active Dynamic Power P_active]
    A --> C[Quiescent Static Power P_quiescent]

    B --> B1[Switching Power P_sw]
    B --> B2[Short Circuit Crowbar Power P_sc]
    B --> B3[Glitching Power P_glitch]

    B1 --> B1A[Origin Charging Discharging of C_L]
    B1A --> B1A1[Formula alpha times C_L times VDD squared times f]

    B2 --> B2A[Origin Finite input rise fall time]
    B2A --> B2A1[Both NMOS and PMOS ON briefly]

    B3 --> B3A[Origin Unequal gate delays in combinational paths]

    C --> C1[Subthreshold Leakage I_sub]
    C --> C2[Gate Oxide Tunneling I_gate]
    C --> C3[Reverse Biased Junction Leakage I_rev]

    C1 --> C1A[Origin Diffusion current when V_GS less than V_TH]
    C2 --> C2A[Origin Quantum tunneling through thin oxide]
    C3 --> C3A[Origin Band to band tunneling at drain body junction]

    B1A1 --> M1[Mitigation Clock Gating DVFS Pipelining]
    C1A --> M2[Mitigation Power Gating MTCMOS HVT Cells Body Biasing]
    C2A --> M3[Mitigation High K Dielectric Multi Gate Devices]
    C3A --> M4[Mitigation Halo Implants Retrograde Wells]

    M1 --> OUT[Optimized Low Power High Speed Design]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Cross-Section — Leakage Pathways in a CMOS Inverter at Steady State

The following flow diagram illustrates the three primary leakage current paths in a *single OFF NMOS transistor* of a CMOS inverter with input held HIGH:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[VDD Rail] -->|No path during steady state| PMOSnode[PMOS is OFF]
    PMOSnode --> GateNode[GATE NODE V_in HIGH]

    GateNode --> N1[NMOS Source at GND]
    GateNode --> N2[NMOS Drain at Output Node V_out HIGH]

    N1 --> I1[Subthreshold Leakage I_sub from Drain to Source]
    N2 --> I2[Gate Tunneling Leakage I_gate from Drain to Gate]
    N2 --> I3[Junction Leakage I_rev from Drain to Body Substrate]

    I1 --> GND[GND Rail]
    I2 --> GateNode
    I3 --> BodyTied[Body tied to GND]
    BodyTied --> GND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Even with the input "stuck" at logic HIGH (NMOS supposedly OFF), three independent physical mechanisms continue to bleed current from $V_{DD}$ to GND. This is the *physical origin* of quiescent dissipation. The output node is also HIGH, so the PMOS is ON and the output is held at $V_{DD}$ — but the OFF NMOS still leaks.

### 4.3 Time-Domain Power Profile — Active vs. Quiescent (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Time Axis] --> P1[t0 Clock Edge Rise]
    P1 --> P2[Active Spike: P_sw plus P_sc dominates]
    P2 --> P3[t1 to t2 Output held at logic 1]
    P3 --> P4[Quiescent Plateau: P_leak only]
    P4 --> P5[t2 Clock Edge Fall]
    P5 --> P6[Active Spike: P_sw plus P_sc again]
    P6 --> P7[t3 to t4 Output held at logic 0]
    P7 --> P8[Quiescent Plateau: P_leak only]
    P8 --> P9[t4 Next Clock Cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the *instantaneous* power profile: sharp spikes during clock edges (active dissipation) and a constant low-level plateau between transitions (quiescent dissipation). In high-speed designs with $f_{clk} > 1$ GHz, the active spikes can overlap with the quiescent plateau, making the total power effectively a continuous, elevated value.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[Q1. KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024 similar pattern]**

**Differentiate between Quiescent power dissipation and Active power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Mention the dominant physical mechanism in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Quiescent (Static) Power | Active (Dynamic) Power |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Power consumed when the gate output is in steady state (no switching) | Power consumed during output switching (charging/discharging of load capacitance) |
| **Dominant physical mechanism** | Leakage currents: subthreshold leakage, gate oxide tunneling, reverse-biased junction leakage | Capacitive charging/discharging of $C_L$ and short-circuit (crow-bar) current |
| **Frequency dependence** | Frequency-independent — occurs always | Linearly proportional to switching frequency $f$ |
| **Voltage dependence** | Weak / exponential via $V_T$ | Quadratic in $V_{DD}$: $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ |
| **Typical dominant regime** | Deep submicron (≤ 65 nm) | Older nodes (> 90 nm) |

**Conclusion:** As technology scales below 65 nm, quiescent power becomes a major design concern, requiring power gating and MTCMOS techniques, whereas active power is controlled by clock gating and DVFS. *(1 mark for tabulated distinction, 1 mark for dominant mechanism, 1 mark for the conclusion / design implication)*

---

**[Q2. KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**Derive the expression for average active power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. State clearly the meaning of the activity factor $\alpha$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Energy dissipated per $0 \to 1$ transition = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$. Energy dissipated per $1 \to 0$ transition = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ (energy stored in $C_L$ from previous cycle is lost in NMOS).

Total energy dissipated per full switching cycle: $C_L V_{DD}^2$.

**Activity factor $\alpha$:** The probability that the output node makes a power-consuming transition (i.e., $0 \to 1$ followed by $1 \to 0$, or vice versa) in one clock period. For a random data stream, $\alpha = p_0 \cdot p_1$ where $p_0, p_1$ are the static probabilities of the node being at logic 0 and logic 1 respectively.

Average active power: $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ *(1 mark for energy expression, 1 mark for $\alpha$ definition, 1 mark for the final formula)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**[Q3. KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice, 14 Marks]**

### **Question A**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable block-level diagram, explain the *complete taxonomy* of CMOS power dissipation. Distinguish clearly between active and quiescent components, citing the physical origin of each sub-component. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance of $C_L = 50$ fF, with $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, clock frequency $f = 500$ MHz, and activity factor $\alpha = 0.25$, compute:
- (i) The average switching power dissipation.
- (ii) The energy dissipated per switching event.
- (iii) If the activity factor doubles to $\alpha = 0.5$, what is the percentage change in switching power?
- (iv) If the supply voltage is reduced to $V_{DD} = 0.7$ V (with all other parameters held constant at $\alpha = 0.25$), what is the new switching power? *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Taxonomy of CMOS Power Dissipation — 7 marks:**

The total power dissipation in a CMOS circuit is:

$$P_{total} = P_{active} + P_{quiescent}$$

**Active (Dynamic) Components — 4 marks:**

1. **Switching Power ($P_{sw}$):** Arises from charging and discharging the load capacitance $C_L$ at the output node. Energy is drawn from $V_{DD}$ on $0 \to 1$ transitions and dissipated as heat in the NMOS on $1 \to 0$ transitions. Formula: $P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$.

2. **Short-Circuit Power ($P_{sc}$):** Flows when both NMOS and PMOS are simultaneously ON during finite input rise/fall time, creating a direct $V_{DD} \to \text{GND}$ path. This is a transient "crow-bar" effect.

3. **Glitching Power:** Caused by hazards in combinational logic due to unequal propagation delays; results in unnecessary intermediate transitions.

**Quiescent (Static) Components — 3 marks:**

1. **Subthreshold Leakage ($I_{sub}$):** Diffusion current when $V_{GS} < V_T$. Exponentially dependent on $V_T$ and temperature.

2. **Gate Oxide Tunneling ($I_{gate}$):** Quantum-mechanical tunneling through ultra-thin (≤ 1.5 nm) gate dielectric.

3. **Junction Leakage ($I_{rev}$):** Reverse-biased pn-junction band-to-band tunneling at drain-body.

A block-level diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes) should accompany the answer. The student is expected to draw a clear hierarchical tree showing the $P_{active}$ and $P_{quiescent}$ branches and their sub-components.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $C_L = 50$ fF $= 50 \times 10^{-15}$ F, $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $f = 500$ MHz $= 5 \times 10^{8}$ Hz, $\alpha = 0.25$.

**(i) Average Switching Power:** Substituting into $P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$:

$$P_{sw} = 0.25 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.0)^2 \times (5 \times 10^{8})$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 5 \times 10^{8}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.25 \times 250 \times 10^{-7} = 0.25 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 6.25 \; \mu\text{W}}$$

**(ii) Energy per Switching Event:**

$$E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.0)^2$$

$$\boxed{E_{sw} = 25 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 25 \text{ fJ}}$$

*(Cross-check: $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot E_{sw} \cdot f = 0.25 \times 25 \text{ fJ} \times 500 \text{ MHz} = 3.125 \text{ fJ} \times 5 \times 10^{8} = 6.25 \; \mu\text{W}$ ✓)*

**(iii) Percentage Change when $\alpha$ doubles to 0.5:**

Since $P_{sw} \propto \alpha$, doubling $\alpha$ doubles $P_{sw}$:

$$P_{sw,new} = 2 \times 6.25 \; \mu\text{W} = 12.5 \; \mu\text{W}$$

$$\text{Percentage change} = \frac{12.5 - 6.25}{6.25} \times 100\% = \boxed{+100\%}$$

**(iv) New Switching Power at $V_{DD} = 0.7$ V, $\alpha = 0.25$:**

$$P_{sw,new} = 0.25 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (0.7)^2 \times (5 \times 10^{8})$$

$$P_{sw,new} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.49 \times 5 \times 10^{8}$$

$$P_{sw,new} = 0.25 \times 0.49 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P_{sw,new} = 0.1225 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 3.0625 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{P_{sw,new} = 3.0625 \; \mu\text{W}}$$

**Observation:** Reducing $V_{DD}$ from 1.0 V to 0.7 V reduces switching power by a factor of $(0.7/1.0)^2 = 0.49$, i.e., a **51% reduction**. This demonstrates the dominant impact of voltage scaling on active power.

---

### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the *physical mechanism of subthreshold leakage* in a short-channel MOSFET. Derive the expression for $I_{sub}$ and identify the factors that exponentially influence it. Discuss the impact of temperature on quiescent power. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CMOS gate in a 5 nm node ASIC has the following measured parameters: $I_{leak,total} = 5 \; \mu\text{A}$ at 25 °C, $V_{DD} = 0.8$ V, and the active power (when switching at $\alpha = 0.2$, $C_L = 20$ fF, $f = 2$ GHz) is $6.4 \; \mu\text{W}$. Compute:
- (i) The quiescent power dissipation.
- (ii) The total power dissipation.
- (iii) The percentage contribution of quiescent and active power to the total.
- (iv) If the temperature rises from 25 °C to 85 °C, and the leakage current *doubles every 10 °C*, compute the new quiescent power and the new total power. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Subthreshold Leakage — 7 marks:**

**Physical Mechanism (3 marks):**
When the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ falls below the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$, the MOSFET enters the *weak-inversion (subthreshold) regime*. Unlike the strong-inversion regime where current is drift-dominated, here the current is *diffusion-dominated* — minority carriers diffuse from source to drain across the channel, similar to a bipolar transistor in the forward-active region. The channel is not strongly inverted, but a small exponential current still flows.

**Derivation of $I_{sub}$ (2 marks):**
From the BSIM/EKV model:

$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot e^{(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) / (nU_T)} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-V_{DS}/U_T}\right)$$

For $V_{DS} \gg U_T$ (typical digital operation):

$$I_{sub} \approx I_0 \cdot e^{(V_{GS} - V_{TH})/(nU_T)}$$

**Exponential Dependencies (1 mark):**
- **Threshold voltage $V_{TH}$:** A 100 mV increase in $V_{TH}$ causes $I_{sub}$ to drop by a factor of $e^{0.1/(1.4 \times 0.02585)} \approx e^{2.76} \approx 15.8\times$. This is why **High-$V_T$ (HVT)** cells are used in non-critical paths.
- **Subthreshold ideality factor $n$:** Lower $n$ gives sharper turn-off.
- **Temperature (via $U_T = kT/q$):** As $T$ increases, $U_T$ increases, which *reduces* the exponent's denominator effect — but $I_0$ also increases exponentially. The net effect: $I_{sub}$ **doubles every ~10 °C** in typical silicon. *(see part b-iv for a worked example)*

**Temperature Impact on $P_{quiescent}$:**
At 25 °C (room temp), $I_{sub}$ is moderate. At 85 °C (typical hot SoC junction temp), $I_{sub}$ can increase by 2^6 = **64×**. At 125 °C (automotive Grade-1), it can be >1000×. Designers must budget for the *worst-case* leakage.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $I_{leak,total} = 5 \; \mu\text{A}$, $V_{DD} = 0.8$ V, $\alpha = 0.2$, $C_L = 20$ fF, $f = 2$ GHz, $P_{active} = 6.4 \; \mu\text{W}$.

**(i) Quiescent Power:**

$$P_{quiescent} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak,total} = 0.8 \text{ V} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{P_{quiescent} = 4.0 \; \mu\text{W}}$$

**(ii) Total Power:**

$$P_{total} = P_{active} + P_{quiescent} = 6.4 + 4.0$$

$$\boxed{P_{total} = 10.4 \; \mu\text{W}}$$

**(iii) Percentage Contributions:**

$$\% P_{quiescent} = \frac{4.0}{10.4} \times 100\% \approx 38.46\%$$

$$\% P_{active} = \frac{6.4}{10.4} \times 100\% \approx 61.54\%$$

$$\boxed{\% P_{quiescent} \approx 38.5\%, \quad \% P_{active} \approx 61.5\%}$$

**Observation:** In this 5 nm example, **quiescent power contributes ~38% of the total** — a stark contrast to older nodes where this was < 5%. This is the modern "leakage wall" problem.

**(iv) Temperature Rise from 25 °C to 85 °C:**
$\Delta T = 85 - 25 = 60$ °C. Number of doublings = $60/10 = 6$.

$$I_{leak,new} = I_{leak,25} \times 2^6 = 5 \; \mu\text{A} \times 64 = 320 \; \mu\text{A}$$

$$P_{quiescent,new} = 0.8 \text{ V} \times 320 \; \mu\text{A} = \boxed{256 \; \mu\text{W}}$$

$$P_{total,new} = P_{active} + P_{quiescent,new} = 6.4 + 256 = \boxed{262.4 \; \mu\text{W}}$$

**Critical Engineering Insight:** A mere 60 °C temperature rise caused the total power to *increase by 25×* — almost entirely due to leakage. This is why high-performance ASICs require **aggressive thermal management** (heat sinks, liquid cooling) and **dynamic thermal throttling** to keep junction temperature bounded.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the activity factor $\alpha$** in the switching power formula — this is a *favourite* 1–2 mark deduction in KTU papers. Always state the full equation with $\alpha$.
