<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Input Power

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Canonical):**
> In the context of **High Speed Digital Design (PEECT526)**, **Input Power** refers to the aggregate electrical power consumed at the *input node* and the *input stage* of a high-speed digital gate or interconnect. It is the sum of three physically distinct components: **(a) Static (leakage) power** drawn continuously regardless of switching, **(b) Dynamic switching power** required to charge and discharge the input capacitance $C_{in}$ of the gate, and **(c) Short-circuit (crowbar) power** dissipated during the finite rise/fall time of the input transition when both NMOS and PMOS transistors conduct simultaneously.

Mathematically, the canonical KTU expression is:

$$
P_{input} \;=\; P_{static} \;+\; P_{switching} \;+\; P_{short\text{-}circuit}
$$

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Office Water Cooler

Imagine a digital input as a **water tap attached to a large overhead tank (VDD)**. The tap controls a pipe (input capacitance) that fills a bucket (gate's internal load). Every time you open or close the tap, you either pour water into the bucket (charging) or let it drain (discharging) — that is **switching power**. While the tap is *half-open* during a transition, a thin stream leaks from the tank straight to the floor (VSS) — that is **short-circuit power**. Finally, even when the tap is fully shut, a few drops always drip from worn-out gaskets — that is **static leakage power**. **Input Power is the total water bill** — switching, leakage, and wastage combined.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Input Power the focal point in high-speed design?**
> At GHz-class edge rates, the input capacitance is charged/discharged *billions* of times per second, so the input pin becomes the single largest contributor to package, board, and on-die power. KTU examiners almost always frame the question around why minimizing $C_{in}$, $V_{DD}$, and the activity factor $\alpha$ is the *first* lever in low-power design.

### 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (90 nm CMOS) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **0.9 – 1.2** | V |
| Threshold Voltage | $V_{th}$ | **0.25 – 0.45** | V |
| Input Capacitance | $C_{in}$ | **1 – 50** | fF |
| Clock Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **0.1 – 5** | GHz |
| Activity Factor | $\alpha$ | **0.01 – 0.5** | dimensionless |
| Leakage Current | $I_{leak}$ | **1 nA – 1 µA** | A per gate |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Input Power

1. **Static (Leakage) Power — $P_{static}$**
   Drawn whenever the circuit is *powered*, even if the input never toggles.
   - Subthreshold leakage (weak-inversion current in OFF transistors)
   - Gate-oxide tunneling leakage (especially in sub-65 nm nodes)
   - Reverse-biased junction leakage at source/drain diffusions

2. **Dynamic Switching Power — $P_{switching}$**
   The dominant component in modern CMOS. Energy is drawn from $V_{DD}$ to charge the input capacitance to $V_{DD}$ on a 0→1 transition, and is dissipated as heat in the pull-down network on the 1→0 transition (only half the energy is recycled if an ideal ground-referenced capacitance is used).

3. **Short-Circuit (Crowbar) Power — $P_{short}$**
   During the finite transition window, both PMOS and NMOS are ON simultaneously, forming a direct resistive path $V_{DD} \rightarrow V_{SS}$. The current is non-zero only for the rise/fall interval $\tau$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Switching Power Derivation

**Step 1 — Charge delivered to the capacitor per clock cycle:**
Every 0→1 transition delivers $Q = C_{in} V_{DD}$ of charge from the supply.

**Step 2 — Energy delivered per transition:**
The voltage across the capacitor ramps from 0 to $V_{DD}$ as the supply pushes charge at constant potential $V_{DD}$. Therefore, energy per 0→1 event is $E = C_{in} V_{DD}^2$.

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated per transition:**
On the 1→0 transition, this stored energy $C_{in} V_{DD}^2 / 2$ is dumped to ground through the NMOS; the other half was already dissipated in the PMOS during charging. So **all** $C_{in} V_{DD}^2$ is eventually converted to heat.

**Step 4 — Average power over $T$ seconds with activity factor $\alpha$:**
If the input toggles $\alpha \cdot f$ times per second, the average switching power is $P_{switching} = \alpha C_{in} V_{DD}^2 f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind the $V_{DD}^2$ dependence:**
> The supply has to push charge $Q$ *across the full rail voltage* $V_{DD}$, doing work $QV_{DD} = C_{in} V_{DD}^2$. This is why *voltage scaling* (lowering $V_{DD}$) is the most powerful knob in low-power design — the power scales as the **square** of the voltage.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Switching Power (per gate) | $P_{sw} = \alpha C_{in} V_{DD}^2 f$ | W | Dominant for $\alpha \ge 0.05$ |
| 2 | Static (Leakage) Power | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | W | Dominant in sleep / standby |
| 3 | Short-Circuit Power | $P_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD}-2V_{th})^3 \tau f$ | W | Significant when $\tau \approx t_{rise} \approx t_{fall}$ |
| 4 | Total Input Power | $P_{in} = P_{sw} + P_{static} + P_{sc}$ | W | KTU 14-mark standard |
| 5 | Energy per Transition | $E_{sw} = C_{in} V_{DD}^2$ | J | Independent of $t_{rise}$ |
| 6 | Subthreshold Leakage | $I_{leak} = I_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{V_{gs}-V_{th}}{S}}$ | A | $S$ = subthreshold slope |
| 7 | Power-Delay Product | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_{pd}$ | J | Used to compare logic families |
| 8 | Energy-Delay Product | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_{pd}$ | J·s | Used in optimization |
| 9 | Activity Factor Bound | $0 \le \alpha \le 0.5$ for random data | — | Maximum at clock-like toggling |
| 10 | Charge per Cycle | $Q = C_{in} V_{DD}$ | C | Multiplied by $f$ gives average supply current |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Input-power equations are used in RTL power estimation (Synopsys PrimePower), package thermal budgeting, IR-drop analysis, and battery-life projection for IoT SoCs. Every KTU problem on this topic ultimately tests whether the student can plug numbers into these ten formulas correctly.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Dynamic Switching Power

We derive $P_{sw} = \alpha C_{in} V_{DD}^2 f$ from first principles, using the definition of energy stored in a capacitor and average power.

**Step 1: Define the energy stored in a capacitor**
A capacitor $C$ charged to voltage $V$ stores energy

$$
E = \int_0^V v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt
$$

Using the constitutive relation $i = C \dfrac{dv}{dt}$, we substitute:

$$
E = \int_0^V v \cdot C \, dv = C \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_0^V = \frac{1}{2} C V^2
$$

**Step 2: Energy drawn from the supply during charging**
The supply sits at constant potential $V_{DD}$ while delivering charge $Q = C_{in} V_{DD}$ to the input node. The work done by the supply is therefore:

$$
E_{supply} = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_{in} V_{DD} = C_{in} V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 3: Energy dissipated during charging**
Of the $C_{in} V_{DD}^2$ delivered, only $\dfrac{1}{2} C_{in} V_{DD}^2$ is stored in $C_{in}$. The remainder, $\dfrac{1}{2} C_{in} V_{DD}^2$, is dissipated *as heat in the PMOS pull-up network* during the charging transient.

**Step 4: Energy dissipated during discharging**
On the subsequent 1 → 0 transition, the stored energy $\dfrac{1}{2} C_{in} V_{DD}^2$ is discharged through the NMOS pull-down network to ground. **All** of it is converted to heat (none of it returns to the supply, since ground is a heat sink).

**Step 5: Total heat dissipated per 0→1→0 cycle**

$$
E_{cycle} = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} C_{in} V_{DD}^2}_{\text{PMOS heat}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} C_{in} V_{DD}^2}_{\text{NMOS heat}} = C_{in} V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 6: Average switching power**
If the input toggles with activity factor $\alpha$ (i.e., the probability of a 0→1 transition per clock cycle is $\alpha$), the number of full charge/discharge cycles per second is $\alpha f$. Therefore:

$$
P_{sw} = \alpha f \cdot E_{cycle} = \alpha f \cdot C_{in} V_{DD}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{sw} = \alpha \, C_{in} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f}
$$

This is the KTU *golden* result.

### 3.2 Derivation of Short-Circuit Power

When the input ramps linearly from 0 to $V_{DD}$ in time $t_{r}$, the output PMOS is OFF and the NMOS is ON only for $V_{in} < V_{th}$ or $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{thp} \vert$. In the overlap window of width $(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})$, both devices conduct.

**Step 1: Saturation current through both transistors**
For a symmetric CMOS inverter with $\beta_n = \beta_p = \beta$, the short-circuit current during the overlap is well approximated as:

$$
I_{sc}(t) \;\approx\; \beta \, [\, V_{DD} - 2 V_{th} \,]^2 \cdot \frac{t_r - \vert t \vert}{t_r^2} \quad \text{for } \vert t \vert \le t_r
$$

**Step 2: Integrate over one transition**

$$
E_{sc} = \int_{-t_r/2}^{t_r/2} V_{DD} \cdot I_{sc}(t) \, dt = \frac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2 V_{th})^3 \cdot t_r
$$

**Step 3: Average power**
With $f$ transitions per second (factor of 2 for rise + fall combined), the short-circuit power is:

$$
P_{sc} = \frac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot t_r \cdot f
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU:** $P_{sc}$ is negligible when $t_r \ll t_{fall}$ (i.e., the input is a near-ideal step). It becomes significant when the input is *slow*, as in long interconnects or poorly terminated nets — a key reason to use sharp-edge clock drivers.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark flavor)

> **Problem:** A CMOS gate has $C_{in} = 25$ fF, $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $f = 2$ GHz, and average activity factor $\alpha = 0.15$. Compute (a) the dynamic switching power, (b) the energy per transition, and (c) the supply current averaged over 1 ms.

**Solution (a):**

$$
P_{sw} = \alpha \, C_{in} \, V_{DD}^2 \, f = 0.15 \times 25 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.2)^2 \times 2 \times 10^9
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.15 \times 25 \times 1.44 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 10.8 \; \mu W
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
E_{sw} = C_{in} V_{DD}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 = 36 \; fJ
$$

**Solution (c):**

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{P_{sw}}{V_{DD}} = \frac{10.8 \; \mu W}{1.2 \; V} = 9 \; \mu A
$$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Input Power Components

```mermaid

    C["OFF-state Leakage"]:::leak --> B
    D["Clock or Data Driver"]:::driver --> B
    B --> E["Cin Charging PMOS Network"]:::pmos
    B --> F["Cin Discharging NMOS Network"]:::nmos
    E --> G["Heat in PMOS = 0.5 Cin VDD squared"]:::heat
    F --> H["Heat in NMOS = 0.5 Cin VDD squared"]:::heat
    B --> I["Overlap Window Vth to VDD minus Vth"]:::overlap
    I --> J["Direct VDD to VSS Path"]:::short
    J --> K["Short Circuit Power Psc"]:::short
    G --> L["Total Input Power Pin"]:::total
    H --> L
    K --> L
    C --> L

    classDef power fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000

```

### 4.2 Input Power vs. Frequency Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Switching Power vs Frequency at VDD 1.2 V, Cin 25 fF, alpha 0.15"
    x-axis "Frequency GHz" [0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0]

    line [2.7, 5.4, 8.1, 10.8, 13.5, 16.2, 18.9, 21.6]
```

### 4.3 CMOS Inverter Input Stage (Schematic Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Input Vin"]:::node
    IN --> G1["Gate of PMOS MP"]:::pmos
    IN --> G2["Gate of NMOS MN"]:::nmos
    VDD["VDD"]:::power --> SRC1["Source of MP"]:::pmos
    DR1["Drain of MP"]:::pmos --> OUT["Output Node Vout"]:::node
    SRC2["Source of MN"]:::nmos --> VSS["VSS"]:::gnd
    DR2["Drain of MN"]:::nmos --> OUT
    CIN["Cin lumped"]:::cap --> OUT
    CIN -.-> VSS

    classDef power fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef pmos fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef nmos fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    classDef gnd fill:#d6d8db,stroke:#1b1e21,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#800020,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The PMOS conducts when $V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{thp} \vert$ (charging $C_{in}$); the NMOS conducts when $V_{in} > V_{thn}$ (discharging $C_{in}$). The **overlap region** $V_{thn} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{thp} \vert$ is precisely where the short-circuit path $V_{DD} \rightarrow V_{SS}$ exists.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define activity factor $\alpha$ in the context of CMOS input power. State the standard switching-power expression and identify which parameter is most effective for low-power design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **activity factor** $\alpha$ is the probability that the input node of a logic gate makes a 0→1 transition in any given clock cycle. For random data, $\alpha \le 0.5$. The switching power is $P_{sw} = \alpha C_{in} V_{DD}^2 f$. The most effective parameter for low-power design is $V_{DD}$, because power scales with the *square* of the supply voltage. (½ + 1 + 1 + ½ = 3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between static power dissipation and dynamic switching power in a CMOS input stage. When does each dominate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Static power** is the power consumed when the gate is idle, primarily due to leakage currents (subthreshold, gate tunneling). It dominates in **standby / sleep modes** and in nanometer nodes. **Dynamic switching power** is consumed only during state transitions, when the input capacitance is charged and discharged via the supply rail. It dominates during **active operation** at high clock frequencies. (1½ + 1½ = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module-Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable derivation, obtain the expression for the average dynamic switching power dissipated at the input of a CMOS gate. Clearly state the role of the activity factor and the supply voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS inverter drives a load of $C_L = 50$ fF at $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, with $f = 1$ GHz and $\alpha = 0.25$. The leakage current is $I_{leak} = 100$ nA. Compute: (i) switching power, (ii) static power, (iii) total input power, and (iv) the average supply current. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
- Define the input node capacitance $C_{in}$ and the energy stored as $\frac{1}{2} C_{in} V_{DD}^2$. **(1 mark)**
- During a 0→1 transition, the supply delivers charge $Q = C_{in} V_{DD}$ at potential $V_{DD}$, doing work $E_{supply} = C_{in} V_{DD}^2$. Half is stored, half is dissipated in the PMOS. **(2 marks)**
- During the 1→0 transition, the stored energy is dissipated in the NMOS. So the energy *dissipated per full cycle* is $E_{cycle} = C_{in} V_{DD}^2$. **(1 mark)**
- Multiplying by the average number of transitions per second ($\alpha f$) gives $P_{sw} = \alpha C_{in} V_{DD}^2 f$. **(2 marks)**
- Note that $V_{DD}^2$ dependence is the reason *voltage scaling* is the most effective lever. **(1 mark)**

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) **Switching power:**

$$
P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f = 0.25 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.0)^2 \times 10^9 = 12.5 \; \mu W
$$

**(2 marks)**

(ii) **Static power:**

$$
P_{static} = V_{DD} \times I_{leak} = 1.0 \times 100 \times 10^{-9} = 100 \; nW
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

(iii) **Total input power:**

$$
P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{static} = 12.5 \; \mu W + 100 \; nW \approx 12.6 \; \mu W
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

(iv) **Average supply current:**

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{P_{total}}{V_{DD}} = \frac{12.6 \; \mu W}{1.0 \; V} = 12.6 \; \mu A
$$

**(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is short-circuit (crowbar) power in a CMOS input stage? Derive its expression. Why does it vanish for a perfectly ideal step input? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the impact of halving $V_{DD}$ versus halving $f$ on the input switching power. A design currently consumes $P_0 = 80$ µW at $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V and $f_0 = 1.5$ GHz. Find the new power if (i) $V_{DD}$ is reduced to 0.8 V at the same frequency, and (ii) $f$ is reduced to 0.75 GHz at the original $V_{DD}$. State which approach yields greater savings. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) — Short-Circuit Power (7 marks):**
- Definition: $P_{sc}$ is the power dissipated due to a direct $V_{DD} \to V_{SS}$ current path during the finite input transition time. **(1 mark)**
- It exists only while both PMOS and NMOS are simultaneously ON, i.e., when $V_{th} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{th} \vert$. **(2 marks)**
- Derive the expression: $E_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 t_r$, and $P_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 t_r f$. **(3 marks)**
- For a perfect step ($t_r \to 0$), the overlap window collapses to zero, so $P_{sc} \to 0$. **(1 mark)**

**(b) — Voltage vs. Frequency Scaling (7 marks):**

(i) New power with $V_{DD}' = 0.8$ V:

$$
P_{new} = P_0 \times \left( \frac{0.8}{1.2} \right)^2 = 80 \times \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^2 = 80 \times \frac{4}{9} \approx 35.6 \; \mu W
$$

**(3 marks)**

(ii) New power with $f' = 0.75$ GHz:

$$
P_{new}' = P_0 \times \left( \frac{0.75}{1.5} \right) = 80 \times 0.5 = 40 \; \mu W
$$

**(2 marks)**

(iii) **Comparison:** Halving $V_{DD}$ saves more energy (≈ 56 %) than halving $f$ (50 %) for the same percentage change, because $P \propto V_{DD}^2$ but $P \propto f^1$. **Voltage scaling is more effective.** **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in Input Power questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the activity factor $\alpha$.** Many students write $P = CV^2f$ instead of $P = \alpha C V^2 f$. KTU valuation keys deduct **1 mark** for omitting $\alpha$ unless the problem statement specifies $\alpha = 1$.
> 2. **Unit conversion slip-ups.** $C_{in}$ is usually given in **fF or pF**; failing to convert to farads (e.g., writing $50 \times 10^{-15}$ without showing the conversion from 50 fF) is a frequent error. **Always show units step-by-step.**
> 3. **Confusing energy and power.** Energy per transition is $C_{in} V_{DD}^2$ (Joules); power is that times $\alpha f$ (Watts). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring the static-leakage term** when $I_{leak}$ is provided. Total power is *always* the sum, even if leakage is small.
> 5. **Incorrect $\alpha$ for clocks.** Clock signals toggle every cycle, so $\alpha_{clock} = 1$ (not 0.5). This is a one-mark trap.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Golden Formula:** $P_{input} = \alpha C_{in} V_{DD}^2 f + V_{DD} I_{leak} + \dfrac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 t_r f$
- **Switching power dominates** active operation; **leakage dominates** standby.
- $P_{sw} \propto V_{DD}^{\,2}$ → **voltage scaling** is the most powerful lever.
- $P_{sw} \propto f$ → **clock gating** directly reduces input power.
- $P_{sw} \propto C_{in}$ → use **smaller gates, shorter wires, tapered buffers** at the input.
- **Activity factor $\alpha$** is the *probability of a 0→1 transition* per cycle; $\alpha_{clock} = 1$, $\alpha_{static} = 0$.
- **Energy per transition** is **independent of rise/fall time** — the speed of the input edge does not change the *total* heat dissipated per cycle, only its *distribution* between $P_{sw}$ and $P_{sc}$.
- **Short-circuit power** scales with $(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 t_r f$ — it grows rapidly as $V_{DD}$ rises above $2V_{th}$.
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** and **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** are the standard KTU figures of merit for logic-family comparison.
- In **deep-submicron CMOS (≤ 65 nm)**, gate-oxide tunneling leakage becomes a significant fraction of $P_{static}$ — high-K dielectrics are used to suppress it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Speed # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Speed ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **High Speed Digital Design**, *Speed* is formally defined as the **rate at which a digital circuit or system can perform a logical operation and propagate a valid signal from its input to its output**, subject to the technology, topology, loading, and interconnect constraints imposed by the physical implementation. Quantitatively, the *speed* of a digital gate or system is expressed through three principal timing metrics: 1. **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$)** – The time interval between the 50 % transition point of the input waveform and the corresponding 50 % transition point of the output waveform. 2. **Rise Time ($t_r$)** – The time taken for a signal to transition from **10 % to 90 %** of its final logic-high value. 3. **Fall Time ($t_f$)** – The time taken for a signal to transition from **90 % to 10 %** of its initial logic-high value. The *inverse* of the minimum propagation delay determines the **maximum clocking frequency ( $f_{max}$ )** at which the system can reliably operate. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – PEECT526 / Module 2** > The topic *Speed* sits at the intersection of **device physics**, **circuit topology**, and **interconnect engineering**. Mastering it is mandatory to address propagation delay, rise/fall times, fan-out, and the *speed–power trade-off* that defines modern VLSI design. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket-Brigade Relay" Imagine a row of fire-fighters passing a water bucket down a line to extinguish a fire: - Each fire-fighter is a **logic gate**. - The bucket is the **electrical signal** (a 1 or a 0). - The time it takes to hand the bucket from one person to the next is the **gate delay**. - The total time for the water to reach the fire is the **propagation delay through the entire circuit**. - If the bucket is very heavy (large load capacitance), the hand-off is slower — analogous to a **higher load increasing delay**. - If a fire-fighter is exceptionally well trained (high drive-strength transistor), the handoff is faster — analogous to **higher $W/L$ ratios reducing delay**. The **speed** of the system is therefore the *total time taken for the meaningful information to traverse the logic chain*. A faster system is one where this hand-off occurs in the *shortest possible time*, allowing the digital circuit to perform more operations per second. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** Speed is *not* a single number — it is a *family of timing parameters* ($t_{pd}, t_r, t_f, t_{su}, t_h, t_{cq}$) that together characterise the *temporal behaviour* of a digital block. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following metrics are universally used to characterise the **speed** of a digital system in the KTU 2024 scheme: | Symbol | Metric | Typical Magnitude (CMOS 90 nm) | |--------|--------|------------------------------| | $t_{pd}$ | Propagation Delay | **20 – 200 ps** | | $t_r$ | Rise Time (10–90 %) | **30 – 100 ps** | | $t_f$ | Fall Time (90–10 %) | **30 – 100 ps** | | $f_{max}$ | Max. Toggle Frequency | **1 – 10 GHz** | | $V_{DD}$ | Supply Voltage | **0.7 – 1.2 V** | | $\tau$ | RC Time Constant | **$\varepsilon$/$\sigma$ × L² (interconnect)** | > [!TIP] > **Examination Tip:** When asked to *define speed*, always quantify it using **at least one timing parameter** (commonly $t_{pd}$ or $f_{max}$). A qualitative-only answer will not receive full marks under KTU valuation. --- ## 1.4 Why "Speed" is a Central Concern in Modern Design As transistor geometries shrink to the deep-submicron regime (≤ 22 nm), three physical phenomena conspire to *limit* speed: 1. **Interconnect RC delay** — grows quadratically with length for global wires. 2. **Gate leakage & velocity saturation** — mobility models break down, and $I_{DS}$ saturates. 3. **Capacitive loading** — fan-out and wire capacitance dominate over intrinsic gate capacitance. Consequently, **speed engineering** has evolved from a simple "use faster transistors" mindset into a *multi-variable optimisation problem* involving device sizing, pipelining, clock-tree synthesis, and interconnect engineering. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Speed Speed in a digital system is governed by a *stack of contributing factors*. Understanding the hierarchy is essential for the KTU 2024 examination: ### A. Device-Level (Intrinsic) Speed - Determined by the **transistor's transit time** ($\tau_t = L / v_{sat}$). - For a 65 nm NMOS, $L \approx 65~\text{nm}$ and $v_{sat} \approx 10^7~\text{cm/s}$ → $\tau_t \approx 6.5~\text{ps}$. - This is the **theoretical lower bound** on gate delay. ### B. Gate-Level (Logical) Speed - Governed by the **RC delay of the switching transistor** driving the load. - Dominated by charging/discharging the *load capacitance* $C_L$ through the *on-resistance* $R_{on}$ of the MOSFET. - First-order delay: $t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \, R_{on} \, C_L$. ### C. Interconnect-Level Speed - Global wires exhibit distributed RC behaviour. - **Elmore delay** of an $n$-segment RC ladder: $t_d = \sum_{k=1}^{n} C_k \, R_{ik}$, where $R_{ik}$ is the *common* resistance from the input to node $k$. - For a uniform line of length $L$: $t_d \approx 0.5 \, r \, c \, L^2$ (where $r, c$ are per-unit-length parameters). ### D. System-Level Speed - Determined by the **critical path delay**: $T_{clk} \ge t_{pd,\,cq} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter}$. - The maximum operating frequency: $f_{max} = 1 / T_{clk,\,min}$. --- ## 2.2 Speed–Power Trade-off Speed and power dissipation are *fundamentally antagonistic* in CMOS design: - **Dynamic power** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f$. - **Delay** $t_{pd} \propto C_L \, V_{DD} / (V_{DD} - V_T)^\alpha$. Increasing $V_{DD}$ → faster switching **but** higher power. Decreasing $V_{DD}$ → lower power **but** exponentially higher delay (especially near the sub-threshold region). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Speed-Power Product (PDP) / Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** > The *Figure of Merit* for a logic family is the **Power-Delay Product**: > $$\text{PDP} = P_{avg} \times t_{pd} \quad \text{[Joules]}$$ > $$\text{EDP} = \text{PDP} \times t_{pd} \quad \text{[Joule·seconds]}$$ > A *lower* PDP/EDP indicates a more efficient technology. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table below, all vertical bars (e.g., for absolute value or magnitude) are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to **prevent breaking the markdown table syntax**. This is a strict requirement under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Validity / Boundary | |---|---------|---------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay | s / ps | Symmetric CMOS | | 2 | $t_{pLH} = 0.69 \, R_p \, C_L$ | High-to-low delay through PMOS | s | Step input | | 3 | $t_{pHL} = 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L$ | Low-to-high delay through NMOS | s | Step input | | 4 | $R_{on} = \dfrac{1}{\mu \, C_{ox} \, (W/L) \, (V_{GS} - V_T)}$ | MOSFET on-resistance | $\Omega$ | Long-channel, triode | | 5 | $t_{pd} = 0.69 \, R_{on} \, C_L$ | First-order gate delay | s | $R_{on} C_L$ model | | 6 | $t_{r} = 2.2 \, R_{on} \, C_L$ | 10 %–90 % rise time | s | Single-pole RC | | 7 | $\tau = R \, C$ | RC time constant | s | Lumped model | | 8 | $t_{d,\,Elmore} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} C_k \, R_{ik}$ | Elmore delay of RC tree | s | RC tree | | 9 | $t_{d,\,line} \approx 0.5 \, r \, c \, L^2$ | Distributed RC line delay | s | $\vert r c L^2 \vert \gg 2.3$ | | 10 | $f_{max} = 1 / T_{clk,\,min}$ | Max. operating frequency | Hz | Critical path limited | | 11 | $\text{PDP} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2$ | Power-Delay Product | J | CMOS only | | 12 | $f_{3dB} = 1 / (2 \pi \, R_{on} C_L)$ | Bandwidth of gate | Hz | Single-pole | | 13 | $v_{sig} = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | Signal velocity on line | m/s | Lossless line | | 14 | $t_f = L / v_{sig}$ | Flight time on line | s | Transmission line | | 15 | $t_{cq} = t_{cd} + t_{r,\,out}/2$ | Clock-to-Q delay | s | Flip-flop | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The metrics above are not academic abstractions — they govern every layer of modern engineering: - **Microprocessor design**: $f_{max}$ directly determines CPU clock speed; $t_{cq}$ and $t_{su}$ limit pipeline depth. - **Memory design (SRAM/DRAM)**: Access time $\approx t_{pd,\,decoder} + t_{pd,\,sense-amp}$. - **PCB & Package design**: Flight time $t_f = L / v_{sig}$ determines whether a trace behaves as a *lumped*, *distributed*, or *transmission-line* element. - **High-speed serial I/O (PCIe, USB 3.x, SerDes)**: Bit period $\ge t_r$ to avoid inter-symbol interference (ISI). > [!TIP] > In the KTU 2024 ESE, when a question asks for the *maximum clock frequency* of a given logic path, the examiner expects: $f_{max} = 1 / (\Sigma t_{pd,\,gates} + t_{su} + t_{skew})$. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order RC Gate Delay **Problem Setup:** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance $C_L$ through the on-resistance $R_{on}$ of the NMOS transistor (during a high-to-low transition at the output). **Step 1 — Write the KVL equation at the output node:** $$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t / (R_{on} C_L)}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the 50 % transition point** (the formal definition of $t_{pHL}$): At $t = t_{pHL}$, $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}/2$. $$V_{DD}/2 = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t_{pHL} / (R_{on} C_L)}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $t_{pHL}$:** $$1/2 = e^{-t_{pHL} / (R_{on} C_L)}$$ $$\ln(1/2) = -t_{pHL} / (R_{on} C_L)$$ $$-\ln(2) = -t_{pHL} / (R_{on} C_L)$$ **Step 4 — Final expression:** $$t_{pHL} = R_{on} C_L \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.69 \, R_{on} \, C_L$$ > **Reasoning:** $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$ arises naturally from the 50 % measurement convention. The *same* derivation applies to the PMOS for $t_{pLH}$ using $R_p$ in place of $R_n$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Propagation Delay By definition: $$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute the expressions from Section 3.1:** $$t_{pd} = \frac{0.69 \, R_p \, C_L + 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Factor the common terms:** $$t_{pd} = 0.69 \, C_L \cdot \frac{R_p + R_n}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Express in terms of the *average* on-resistance $R_{avg}$:** $$\boxed{t_{pd} = 0.69 \, R_{avg} \, C_L}$$ > **Interpretation:** This is the *single canonical* delay equation used throughout KTU examination problems. Memorise it. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rise Time from the RC Step Response The output of a single-pole RC network to a step input is: $$V_{out}(t) = V_{final} (1 - e^{-t / \tau}), \quad \tau = R_{on} C_L$$ **Step 1 — Compute $V_{out}$ at $t = t_{10\%}$ (10 % point):** $$0.1 \, V_{final} = V_{final}(1 - e^{-t_{10\%} / \tau})$$ $$e^{-t_{10\%} / \tau} = 0.9 \implies t_{10\%} = -\tau \ln(0.9)$$ **Step 2 — Compute $V_{out}$ at $t = t_{90\%}$ (90 % point):** $$0.9 \, V_{final} = V_{final}(1 - e^{-t_{90\%} / \tau})$$ $$e^{-t_{90\%} / \tau} = 0.1 \implies t_{90\%} = -\tau \ln(0.1)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the rise time $t_r = t_{90\%} - t_{10\%}$:** $$t_r = -\tau \ln(0.1) + \tau \ln(0.9) = \tau \cdot \ln(0.9/0.1) = \tau \cdot \ln(9)$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate numerically** ($\ln(9) \approx 2.197$): $$\boxed{t_r \approx 2.2 \, R_{on} \, C_L}$$ > **Engineering Note:** The factor **2.2** is the canonical "rise-time to time-constant" ratio. KTU 2024 ESE questions frequently ask students to derive this from first principles. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Elmore Delay of a Uniform 4-Segment RC Ladder **Network:** A wire is modelled as 4 equal RC segments, each of resistance $r$ and capacitance $c$, with $C_0$ at the input node. **Step 1 — Write the per-segment resistance and capacitance:** Each segment contributes $R_i = r$ and $C_i = c$ (for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$). The total line resistance is $R_{tot} = 4r$, total capacitance is $C_{tot} = 4c$. **Step 2 — Apply the Elmore formula:** $$t_d = \sum_{k=1}^{4} C_k \, R_{ik}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the common path resistance $R_{ik}$ for each node:** | Node $k$ | $R_{ik}$ (resistance from input to node $k$) | $C_k$ | |----------|----------------------------------------------|-------| | 1 | $r$ | $c$ | | 2 | $2r$ | $c$ | | 3 | $3r$ | $c$ | | 4 | $4r$ | $c$ | **Step 4 — Substitute into the Elmore sum:** $$t_d = c \cdot r + c \cdot 2r + c \cdot 3r + c \cdot 4r = c \, r (1 + 2 + 3 + 4) = 10 \, r \, c$$ **Step 5 — Generalisation to $n$ segments:** $$t_d = r \, c \sum_{k=1}^{n} k = r \, c \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to per-unit-length parameters** (where $L$ is line length, $n$ segments of length $L/n$ each): $$r_{pul} = R_{tot} / L, \quad c_{pul} = C_{tot} / L, \quad r \, c = \frac{R_{tot} C_{tot}}{n^2}$$ For $n \to \infty$: $$\boxed{t_d \approx 0.5 \, r_{pul} \, c_{pul} \, L^2}$$ > **Critical Insight:** The delay grows as the **square of the line length** — this is the fundamental reason why global wires do not scale with technology and why *repeater insertion* is mandatory in modern VLSI design. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Maximum Operating Frequency from a Critical Path **Given:** A combinational logic path consists of 4 NAND2 gates, each with $t_{pd} = 50~\text{ps}$. A flip-flop at the end has $t_{cq} = 30~\text{ps}$ and requires $t_{su} = 20~\text{ps}$. Clock skew $t_{skew} = 10~\text{ps}$. **Step 1 — Write the timing budget equation:** $$T_{clk} \ge t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the combinational logic delay:** $$t_{logic} = 4 \times 50~\text{ps} = 200~\text{ps}$$ **Step 3 — Sum all components:** $$T_{clk} \ge 30 + 200 + 20 + 10 = 260~\text{ps}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $f_{max}$:** $$\boxed{f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk}} = \frac{1}{260 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 3.85~\text{GHz}}$$ > **Examination Note:** This exact problem-type appears frequently in KTU 2024 ESE under "compute the maximum clock frequency" sub-parts. Always show *all four* terms in the timing budget. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Speed Impact of Design Choices | Design Lever | Effect on $t_{pd}$ | Effect on Power | Trade-off Verdict | |--------------|--------------------|------------------|--------------------| | Increase $V_{DD}$ | $\downarrow$ (faster) | $\uparrow$ (quadratically) | Used in performance-critical cores | | Increase $W/L$ of driver | $\downarrow$ (lower $R_{on}$) | $\uparrow$ (larger gate cap) | Standard for output buffers | | Reduce $C_L$ (smaller fan-out) | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Always beneficial | | Reduce temperature | $\downarrow$ (mobility $\uparrow$) | $\downarrow$ (leakage $\downarrow$) | Server rooms prefer cooler | | Shorter interconnect | $\downarrow$ (quadratically) | $\downarrow$ | Use repeaters instead | | Use pipelining | $f_{max} \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (more flip-flops) | Increases throughput, not latency | | Lower $V_T$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ (leakage exponential) | High-performance only | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Speed Hierarchy — Block-Level Functional Flow The diagram below maps how *device physics* propagates upward to determine *system-level speed*. Each block is a *causal contributor* to the final timing metric. ```mermaid graph TD A[Device Physics
Mobility, Vth, Tox, Leff] --> B[Intrinsic Gate Delay
tt = L vsat] B --> C[Gate Delay with Load
tpd 0.69 Ron CL] C --> D[Interconnect Delay
Elmore RC Tree] D --> E[Critical Path Delay
Sum of all tpd on path] E --> F[Clock Period Tclk
Tclk t cq t logic t su t skew] F --> G[Maximum Frequency fmax
fmax 1 over Tclk] G --> H[System Throughput
IPS instructions per second] A --> I[Leakage Currents
Ioff sub Vt] I --> J[Static Power Pstat] C --> K[Dynamic Power Pdyn] J --> L[Power Delay Product PDP
FoM for technology] K --> L ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Speed is a *cascade* — from atoms to applications. Optimisation at *any* lower block flows upward, but optimisation must respect the *power* branch (right side) to remain physically feasible. --- ## 4.2 The RC Delay Model of a Single Gate ```mermaid graph LR Vin[Vin Step Input] --> R[Ron
MOSFET On Resistance] R --> NodeA[Output Node] NodeA --> CL[CL
Load Capacitance] NodeA --> Vout[Vout t] NodeA --> Def[Define tpd at Vout 0.5 Vdd] Def --> Result[tpd 0.69 Ron CL] ``` > **Key Annotation:** The output node is the *meeting point* of resistance and capacitance. The "0.5 $V_{DD}$" annotation is the formal KTU definition of the propagation delay measurement point. --- ## 4.3 The Elmore Delay of an RC Tree ```mermaid graph TD In[Input Source] --> R1[R1] R1 --> N1[Node 1 with C1] R1 --> R2[R2] R2 --> N2[Node 2 with C2] R2 --> R3[R3] R3 --> N3[Node 3 with C3] R3 --> R4[R4] R4 --> N4[Node 4 Output with C4] N1 --> Emore[Elmore Delay Sum] N2 --> Emore N3 --> Emore N4 --> Emore Emore --> Eqn[td Sigma Ck x Rik] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Each node $k$ contributes a *delay product* $C_k \cdot R_{ik}$ where $R_{ik}$ is the *common* (shared) path resistance from input to that node. The total Elmore delay is the sum of all such products. --- ## 4.4 Speed-Power Trade-off Surface ```mermaid graph TD VDD[Increase VDD] --> Faster[Faster Switching
tpd down] VDD --> PowUp[Dynamic Power Up
Pdyn prop to VDD squared] Vt[Decrease Vth] --> Faster2[Faster Switching
higher Idsat] Vt --> LeakUp[Leakage Power Up
Ioff exponential in Vth] WL[Increase W over L] --> Faster3[Faster Switching
Ron down] WL --> CapUp[Load Cap Up
Cgate up] Capacitance[Reduce CL] --> Faster4[Faster Switching
tpd prop to CL] Capacitance --> PowDown[Both Powers Down] Faster --> NetSpeed[Net Speed Gain] Faster2 --> NetSpeed Faster3 --> NetSpeed Faster4 --> NetSpeed PowUp --> Cost[Power Cost] LeakUp --> Cost CapUp --> Cost NetSpeed --> FoM[Figure of Merit
PDP EDP] Cost --> FoM PowDown --> Win[Net Win
Lower PDP] Win --> FoM ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Reducing load capacitance is the *only* lever that improves speed **and** reduces power simultaneously. All other levers trade one for the other. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q2a]` **Define the term "propagation delay" of a digital gate. How is the average propagation delay computed from $t_{pLH}$ and $t_{pHL}$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Propagation delay $t_{pd}$ of a digital gate is the time interval between the **50 % transition point of the input signal** and the corresponding **50 % transition point of the output signal**, measured for a given direction of logic transition. > > **Average delay (1 mark):** The average propagation delay is the arithmetic mean of the high-to-low and low-to-high delays: > > $$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$ > > **Significance (0.5 marks):** A lower $t_{pd}$ implies a faster gate and hence a higher achievable clock frequency $f_{max} = 1/T_{clk}$. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1c]` **What is the Power-Delay Product (PDP)? Why is it called the Figure-of-Merit of a logic family?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Power-Delay Product (PDP) is defined as the product of the average power dissipation $P_{avg}$ of a gate and its average propagation delay $t_{pd}$: > > $$\text{PDP} = P_{avg} \times t_{pd} \quad \text{[Joules]}$$ > > For CMOS, this simplifies to: > > $$\text{PDP} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2$$ > > **Why Figure-of-Merit (1.5 marks):** PDP has the units of *energy* (Joules) and represents the *energy consumed per switching event*. A *smaller* PDP indicates a logic family that is simultaneously *fast* and *low-power*. Hence, it captures the fundamental speed-power trade-off in a single scalar metric, making it the canonical Figure-of-Merit for any digital technology. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q7]` **(a) Derive the first-order RC delay equation for a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. State the assumptions clearly. (7 marks)** **(b) A 4-stage ring oscillator uses identical inverters, each with an average propagation delay of $t_{pd} = 75~\text{ps}$. Compute the oscillation frequency. If the supply voltage is reduced by 20 %, explain qualitatively how the frequency will change. (7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to B-A(a)** **Step 1 — Assumption (1 mark):** Assume a step input at the gate of the NMOS, the load $C_L$ is initially charged to $V_{DD}$, and the PMOS is in cutoff (i.e., only the NMOS discharge path is active). **Step 2 — Model the NMOS as a linear resistor** of value $R_n$ during saturation (1 mark): $$R_n = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{GS} - V_T)}$$ **Step 3 — Write the output discharge equation** (1 mark): $$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \, e^{-t / (R_n C_L)}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the 50 %-point definition of $t_{pHL}$** (1 mark): $$V_{out}(t_{pHL}) = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \implies \frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_{pHL}/(R_n C_L)}$$ **Step 5 — Solve** (1 mark): $$t_{pHL} = R_n C_L \ln 2 \approx 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L$$ **Step 6 — Symmetric result for the PMOS** (1 mark): $$t_{pLH} \approx 0.69 \, R_p \, C_L$$ **Step 7 — Average delay** (1 mark): $$\boxed{t_{pd} = 0.69 \, C_L \cdot \frac{R_p + R_n}{2} = 0.69 \, R_{avg} \, C_L}$$ --- #### **Model Solution to B-A(b)** **Step 1 — Ring oscillator period formula** (2 marks): A ring oscillator with $N$ identical inverters oscillates with a period: $$T_{osc} = 2 \, N \, t_{pd}$$ The factor of 2 accounts for the fact that the signal must traverse the loop *twice* (once for each transition) to return to its initial state. **Step 2 — Substitute the values** (1 mark): $$T_{osc} = 2 \times 4 \times 75~\text{ps} = 600~\text{ps}$$ **Step 3 — Compute frequency** (1 mark): $$f_{osc} = \frac{1}{T_{osc}} = \frac{1}{600 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.67~\text{GHz}$$ **Step 4 — Qualitative effect of 20 % supply reduction** (3 marks): Reducing $V_{DD}$ by 20 % (e.g., from 1.0 V to 0.8 V) has the following effects: - The over-drive voltage $(V_{GS} - V_T)$ decreases → $R_{on}$ of both NMOS and PMOS **increases**. - This *increases* the propagation delay $t_{pd}$ (which is inversely proportional to $(V_{DD} - V_T)$). - Consequently, $T_{osc}$ *increases* and $f_{osc}$ *decreases*. - The degradation is *non-linear*: as $V_{DD}$ approaches $V_T$, the delay rises sharply because the device enters the sub-threshold region. Hence, frequency may drop by 30–50 %, not just 20 %. **Final boxed answer:** $f_{osc} \approx 1.67~\text{GHz}$; reduction in $V_{DD}$ will *decrease* the frequency non-linearly. --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Q8]` **(a) With a neat sketch, explain the Elmore delay model for a 3-segment uniform RC interconnect. Derive the expression for the delay. (7 marks)** **(b) An 8 mm long interconnect on a silicon IC has a per-unit-length resistance $r = 0.1~\Omega/\mu m$ and per-unit-length capacitance $c = 0.2~\text{fF}/\mu m$. Estimate its RC delay. If repeaters are inserted to break the line into 4 equal segments, recompute the delay. Comment on the improvement. (7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to B-B(a)** **Step 1 — Describe the model** (1 mark): The Elmore delay of an RC tree is defined as the first moment of the impulse response, computed as the sum over all nodes of (node capacitance) × (common-path resistance from input to that node). **Step 2 — Network** (1 mark): A 3-segment uniform line has 3 resistors $r$ in series and 3 grounded capacitors $c$ (one at each node), plus an output capacitor $c$ at the far end (4 capacitors total in the lumped model). **Step 3 — Identify common-path resistances** (2 marks): | Node $k$ | $C_k$ | $R_{ik}$ | |----------|-------|----------| | 1 | $c$ | $r$ | | 2 | $c$ | $2r$ | | 3 | $c$ | $3r$ | | 4 (output) | $c$ | $3r$ | **Step 4 — Apply Elmore formula** (2 marks): $$t_d = \sum_{k} C_k R_{ik} = c \cdot r + c \cdot 2r + c \cdot 3r + c \cdot 3r = 9 \, r \, c$$ **Step 5 — Generalise to $n$ segments** (1 mark): $$\boxed{t_d = r c \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2}}$$ --- #### **Model Solution to B-B(b)** **Step 1 — Convert units to SI** (1 mark): $$r = 0.1~\Omega/\mu m = 0.1 \times 10^6~\Omega/m = 10^5~\Omega/m$$ $$c = 0.2~\text{fF}/\mu m = 0.2 \times 10^{-15} \times 10^6~\text{F/m} = 2 \times 10^{-10}~\text{F/m}$$ $$L = 8~\text{mm} = 8 \times 10^{-3}~\text{m}$$ **Step 2 — Total line resistance and capacitance** (0.5 marks): $$R_{tot} = r L = 10^5 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 800~\Omega$$ $$C_{tot} = c L = 2 \times 10^{-10} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 1.6 \times 10^{-12}~\text{F} = 1.6~\text{pF}$$ **Step 3 — Unsegmented Elmore delay** (1.5 marks): For a *continuous* line, the Elmore delay reduces to the well-known formula: $$t_{d,\,unseg} = 0.5 \, r \, c \, L^2 = 0.5 \times 10^5 \times 2 \times 10^{-10} \times (8 \times 10^{-3})^2$$ $$= 0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 6.4 \times 10^{-10}~\text{s} = 640~\text{ps}$$ **Step 4 — Segmented (4 repeaters) delay** (2 marks): Each segment is now $L/4 = 2~\text{mm}$ long. Using the lumped-segment Elmore formula with 4 segments of $r' = r \cdot L/4$ and $c' = c \cdot L/4$: $$r' c' = r \cdot (L/4) \cdot c \cdot (L/4) = rcL^2/16$$ $$t_{d,\,seg} = r'c' \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \frac{rcL^2}{16} \cdot \frac{4 \times 5}{2} = \frac{rcL^2}{16} \cdot 10 = 0.625 \, rcL^2$$ Wait — re-deriving carefully. With 4 equal segments each of resistance $R_s$ and capacitance $C_s$: $$t_d = R_s C_s + 2 R_s C_s + 3 R_s C_s + 4 R_s C_s = 10 R_s C_s$$ where $R_s = R_{tot}/4 = 200~\Omega$ and $C_s = C_{tot}/4 = 0.4~\text{pF}$. $$t_{d,\,seg} = 10 \times 200 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-12} = 800 \times 10^{-12} = 800~\text{ps}$$ (Substituting repeaters with finite drive strength gives a *much* smaller delay in practice — the Elmore of the passive segments is a worst-case; with optimally-sized CMOS repeaters the actual delay drops to ~150–200 ps. The textbook treatment includes the inverter delay, but for this problem we isolate the *interconnect* contribution.) **Step 5 — Comment on improvement** (2 marks): - The *unsegmented* line has $t_d \propto L^2$ (640 ps). - Naive lumping of the line into 4 equal RC segments (without active repeaters) yields 800 ps, *worse* than the continuous line — because the lumped model overestimates. - However, with *active* CMOS repeaters inserted at the breakpoints, the effective driving resistance is reduced, the $L^2$ dependence is broken, and the delay becomes *linear* in $L$ with a much smaller constant — typically a **3× to 5× speed-up** in practice. **Final boxed answer:** $$t_{d,\,unsegmented} = 640~\text{ps}, \quad t_{d,\,segmented (passive)} = 800~\text{ps}, \quad t_{d,\,segmented (with CMOS repeaters)} \ll 640~\text{ps}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. *Forgetting the 50 %-point convention*: Always define delay at $V_{out} = 0.5 V_{DD}$, not at the 10 %/90 % points (those are rise/fall times). > 2. *Confusing $t_r$ with $t_{pd}$*: $t_r \approx 2.2 R C$, while $t_{pd} \approx 0.69 R C$. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks. > 3. *Ignoring the factor of 2 in the ring-oscillator period*: $T = 2 N t_{pd}$, **not** $N t_{pd}$. > 4. *Treating lumped RC segments as equivalent to a continuous line*: They are NOT — a continuous line has *distributed* capacitance; lumped segments *overestimate* delay unless active repeaters are inserted. > 5. *Forgetting to include $t_{cq}$, $t_{su}$, and $t_{skew}$* in the clock-period budget: KTU explicitly tests this in 14-mark problems. > 6. *Mixing up PDP and EDP*: PDP has units of energy (J); EDP has units of energy × time (J·s). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Speed (PEECT526 / Module 2)** ### Core Definitions - ☐ **Speed** is quantified by $t_{pd}, t_r, t_f, f_{max}$. - ☐ **Propagation delay** $t_{pd} = (t_{pLH} + t_{pHL})/2 = 0.69 R_{avg} C_L$. - ☐ **Rise time** $t_r \approx 2.2 R_{on} C_L$ (10–90 %). - ☐ **Fall time** $t_f \approx 2.2 R_{on} C_L$ (90–10 %). - ☐ **Ring oscillator** $T = 2 N t_{pd}$, $f = 1/T$. - ☐ **Clock budget** $T_{clk} \ge t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew}$. - ☐ **Maximum frequency** $f_{max} = 1/T_{clk,\,min}$. ### Interconnect Delay - ☐ **Elmore delay** $t_d = \sum C_k R_{ik}$. - ☐ **Uniform line** $t_d \approx 0.5 \, r c L^2$ (quadratic in length). - ☐ **Repeater insertion** breaks the $L^2$ dependence and reduces delay. - ☐ **Flight time** $t_f = L / v_{sig}$ on transmission lines. ### Power–Speed Trade-off - ☐ **PDP** = $P_{avg} \times t_{pd}$ = $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2$ (units: Joules). - ☐ **EDP** = PDP $\times t_{pd}$ (units: J·s). - ☐ **Reducing $C_L$** is the only lever that improves BOTH speed and power. - ☐ **Lowering $V_{DD}$** reduces power but increases delay non-linearly. - ☐ **Lowering $V_T$** speeds up switching but exponentially raises leakage. ### Numerical Shortcuts to Memorise - ☐ $0.69 = \ln 2$ (propagation delay factor). - ☐ $2.2 = \ln 9$ (rise-time factor). - ☐ $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$; $\ln 9 \approx 2.197$. ### Common KTU Question Patterns - ☐ "Derive the first-order RC delay" → 7 marks → use $0.69 R C$. - ☐ "Compute $f_{max}$ of a logic path" → 7 marks → sum all four timing terms. - ☐ "Ring oscillator frequency" → 7 marks → $T = 2 N t_{pd}$. - ☐ "Elmore delay of an RC tree" → 7 marks → sum of $C_k R_{ik}$ products. - ☐ "Justify PDP as Figure-of-Merit" → 3 marks → energy per switching event. > **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the *timing diagram* (input and output waveforms with the 50 % points marked) when solving delay problems — it earns you **easy methodology marks** under KTU valuation, even if the final numerical answer has a minor error.

topics.title: Packaging (Power Dissipation Analysis of Only CMOS Logic Gates are Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Packaging (Power Dissipation Analysis of Only CMOS Logic Gates are Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Packaging — The Physical & Electrical Envelope of an IC

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
*Packaging* in high-speed digital design refers to the technology and methodology used to enclose a semiconductor die (chip), provide mechanical protection, establish electrical interconnections between the die and the printed circuit board (PCB), and manage thermal, electrical, and electromagnetic performance. It is the critical bridge between the on-die nanoscale transistors and the macroscopic world of the PCB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** For this module, packaging is examined from a **high-speed signal integrity** and **thermal/electrical performance** standpoint — not from a manufacturing/process perspective. The parasitic resistance, capacitance, and inductance introduced by the package are the central concerns.

### 1.2 Power Dissipation in CMOS Logic Gates

**Definition:**
*Power dissipation* in a CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) logic gate is the rate at which electrical energy is converted into heat (and, to a negligible extent, electromagnetic radiation) as the gate performs its switching function. It determines the power budget, battery life, cooling requirements, and reliability of any digital system.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plumbing & Heating" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Think of a CMOS gate as a smart water faucet system:**
>
> - **V_DD (Supply Voltage)** = Water pressure from the main tank.
> - **Ground (V_SS)** = The drainage system.
> - **PMOS (Pull-up network)** = The upper valve that opens when you want NO water.
> - **NMOS (Pull-down network)** = The lower valve that opens when you want water flow.
> - **Output Capacitance (C_L)** = A bucket that fills with water (charge) and later drains it.
>
> Every time you toggle the faucet, the bucket fills and empties — this *pouring water back and forth* represents **dynamic switching power**, the dominant consumer of energy. Any small leak in the pipes (subthreshold leakage, reverse-biased junction leakage) is **static leakage power** — small but always present, like a dripping tap at night. A brief moment when both upper and lower valves are slightly open during transition causes a wasteful cross-flow — this is **short-circuit power**.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Dielectric Constant of SiO₂:** **k ≈ 3.9** (relative permittivity)
- **Free Space Permeability:** **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- **Speed of Light in Vacuum:** **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Thermal Conductivity of Silicon:** **k_Si ≈ 150 W/(m·K)**
- **Thermal Conductivity of FR-4 PCB:** **k_PCB ≈ 0.3 W/(m·K)**
- **Junction-to-Ambient Thermal Resistance (θ_JA):** measured in **°C/W**
- **Unit of Power Dissipation:** **Watts (W)** — typically **μW to mW per gate**, **W per chip**.
- **Energy per Transition:** **Joules (J)** — typically **fJ (femtojoules)** per switching event for modern CMOS.

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in High-Speed Digital Design

At multi-gigahertz clock rates, a single chip may switch billions of times per second. Even a picojoule of energy per transition, accumulated over billions of operations, translates into watts of heat. Packaging must:
1. Deliver power with minimal IR drop (low parasitic resistance).
2. Maintain signal integrity by minimizing inductance (avoiding ground bounce / V_DD droop).
3. Conduct heat away from the die (low θ_JA).
4. Provide controlled-impedance interconnects (transmission-line behavior above ~1 GHz).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of CMOS Power Dissipation — The Three Pillars

CMOS power dissipation is decomposed into **three orthogonal mechanisms**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of CMOS Power:**
> 1. **Static (Leakage) Power** — P_static
> 2. **Dynamic Switching Power** — P_switch (dominant in active CMOS)
> 3. **Short-Circuit Power** — P_sc (transient, during input rise/fall)

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### Pillar 1: Static (Leakage) Power Dissipation — P_static

- **When it occurs:** Continuously, regardless of switching activity.
- **Physical Origin:**
  - **Subthreshold leakage (I_sub):** Weak inversion current flowing from drain to source even when V_GS < V_th.
  - **Reverse-biased junction leakage (I_rev):** From source/drain n⁺-p and p⁺-n diodes to the body.
  - **Gate-oxide tunneling (I_gate):** Quantum-mechanical tunneling through the thin gate oxide (dominant below 90 nm nodes).
  - **Punch-through & GIDL** (advanced nodes).
- **Governing Equation:**

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$$

- **Why it matters:** As feature sizes shrink, V_th decreases, exponentially increasing I_sub. In nanometer CMOS (65 nm and below), static power can rival dynamic power — a major paradigm shift.

#### Pillar 2: Dynamic Switching Power Dissipation — P_switch

- **When it occurs:** During logic transitions (0 → 1 and 1 → 0).
- **Physical Origin:** Charging and discharging the **load capacitance** C_L of the gate output (interconnect, fan-out gates, parasitic capacitances).
- **Governing Equation (most important in CMOS):**

$$P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

- **Where:**
  - **α (switching activity factor):** Fraction of clock cycles in which the output actually transitions. For a clock signal α = 1; for random data, α ≈ 0.5 for two transitions per cycle, or 0.25 for typical data streams.
  - **C_L:** Total load capacitance at the output node (Farads).
  - **V_DD:** Supply voltage (Volts).
  - **f_clk:** Clock frequency (Hz).

- **The Energy/Operation viewpoint:** The energy drawn from the supply to charge C_L from 0 to V_DD is C_L·V_DD² (half of this is stored, half is dissipated in the PMOS). The energy dissipated when discharging through the NMOS is C_L·V_DD²/2. Hence **per switching cycle**, total energy = **C_L·V_DD²**.

#### Pillar 3: Short-Circuit Power Dissipation — P_sc

- **When it occurs:** During the finite rise/fall time of the input signal, when both PMOS and NMOS networks are simultaneously ON.
- **Physical Origin:** A direct current path from V_DD to V_SS exists momentarily, producing a triangular current pulse.
- **Governing Equation:**

$$P_{sc} = \frac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^{3} \cdot \tau \cdot f_{clk}$$

- **Where β** is the gain factor (μ·C_ox·W/L), **V_th** is the threshold voltage, and **τ** is the input rise/fall time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Short-circuit power is **MINIMIZED** when input rise/fall times are very small (abrupt transitions) — but if τ becomes too large, the current pulse duration increases and short-circuit power rises. There is an optimum τ for minimum total dynamic power.

### 2.3 Total CMOS Power Dissipation

$$P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{switch} + P_{sc}$$

For older, larger-geometry CMOS (above ~180 nm), the dominance ordering is:

$$P_{switch} \gg P_{sc} \gg P_{static}$$

For modern deep-submicron CMOS (65 nm and below):

$$P_{switch} \approx P_{static} \gg P_{sc}$$

### 2.4 Packaging — Parasitic & Thermal Perspective

- A package introduces **parasitic R, L, C**:
  - **Bond wire inductance:** Typically **1 nH/mm**; for a 2 mm bond wire, L ≈ **2 nH**.
  - **Lead capacitance:** Typically **0.5 – 5 pF** per pin.
  - **Lead resistance:** Typically **10 – 100 mΩ**.
- **Thermal Resistance (θ_JA):** Determines temperature rise: **T_j = T_a + P_total · θ_JA**.
- **Package types (in order of performance for high-speed):**
  1. **DIP (Dual In-line Package)** — legacy, high parasitic.
  2. **SOIC (Small Outline IC)** — surface mount, moderate.
  3. **QFP (Quad Flat Pack)** — fine-pitch, many I/O.
  4. **BGA (Ball Grid Array)** — area-array, superior electrical/thermal.
  5. **CSP (Chip-Scale Package)** — near-die-size, best for mobile/handheld.
  6. **Flip-Chip / WLP (Wafer-Level Package)** — state-of-the-art for GHz designs.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Leakage power | W | Always present, dominant < 90 nm |
| 2 | $E_{switch} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Energy per transition | J | Half stored, half dissipated in PMOS |
| 3 | $P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Capacitive switching power | W | Dominant in active CMOS |
| 4 | $P_{sc} = \frac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^{3} \cdot \tau \cdot f_{clk}$ | Short-circuit power | W | $\tau < $ output rise time |
| 5 | $P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{switch} + P_{sc}$ | Total chip power | W | Universal |
| 6 | $T_j = T_a + P_{total} \cdot \theta_{JA}$ | Junction temperature | °C | $\theta_{JA}$ in °C/W |
| 7 | $L_{bond} \approx 1 \text{ nH/mm}$ | Bond-wire inductance | nH | Rule of thumb |
| 8 | $f_{knee} = \frac{0.35}{t_{r}}$ | Maximum frequency for lumped model | Hz | For $t_r$ = 10–90% rise time |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Battery-powered devices (smartphones, IoT sensors):** Drives aggressive use of **power gating**, **clock gating**, and **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling)** to reduce P_switch (since it scales as V_DD²).
- **Data centers:** P_total per chip × billions of transistors × millions of servers = multi-megawatt facility loads. Cooling becomes a primary cost driver.
- **Automotive ECU & aerospace:** Junction temperature must remain below 125°C / 150°C; packaging's θ_JA is a mission-critical parameter.
- **AI accelerators (GPUs, TPUs):** Push V_DD and f_clk to extremes; power dissipation is the **fundamental physical wall** limiting further compute scaling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in Charging a Capacitor Through a PMOS

**Problem Setup:** Consider a CMOS inverter with output load capacitance C_L. Assume PMOS is ON and NMOS is OFF, so C_L charges from 0 to V_DD through the PMOS pull-up network (modeled as a resistor R_p for simplicity, though real analysis uses the non-linear I-V curve).

**Step 1 — Charge delivered by the supply:**
The total charge moved from V_DD to the capacitor is:

$$Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Step 2 — Energy supplied by the V_DD source:**
The energy delivered by the supply is:

$$E_{supply} = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy stored in the capacitor:**
The instantaneous energy stored is $\frac{1}{2} C_L v^{2}(t)$. Integrating from 0 to V_DD:

$$E_{stored} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} v \cdot C_L \, dv = C_L \cdot \left[ \frac{v^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{V_{DD}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated in the PMOS (charging half):**

$$E_{dissipated,charge} = E_{supply} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Discharge through NMOS:**
When the output transitions from 1 → 0, the capacitor discharges through the NMOS. The energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ stored in C_L is fully dissipated in the NMOS pull-down network.

$$E_{dissipated,discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Total energy dissipated per switching cycle:**

$$E_{total\,per\,cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** For every full switching cycle (0→1 followed by 1→0), the energy dissipated in the CMOS gate is exactly **C_L · V_DD²**, independent of the transistor resistance or any process parameters. This is one of the most elegant and counter-intuitive results in VLSI design.

**Step 7 — Average power per gate:**

If the gate switches with activity factor α at clock frequency f_clk, the average power is:

$$P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot E_{total\,per\,cycle} \cdot f_{clk} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

**Substitution of typical values for sanity check:**

Let α = 0.5, C_L = 50 fF = 50 × 10⁻¹⁵ F, V_DD = 1.0 V, f_clk = 1 GHz = 10⁹ Hz:

$$P_{switch} = 0.5 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1^{2} \times 10^{9} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 25 \, \mu W$$

For a chip with 10 million such gates, the chip-level power is:

$$P_{chip} = 10^{7} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 250 \text{ W}$$

This demonstrates why modern CPUs limit V_DD and clock frequency — power grows **quadratically** with V_DD and **linearly** with frequency and capacitance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Short-Circuit Power (Triangular Current Approximation)

**Step 1 — Model assumption:** During input transition, the short-circuit current I_sc(t) approximates a triangle of peak value I_peak and base equal to the time both transistors are on, denoted t_sc.

**Step 2 — Peak current approximation:**
For a symmetric inverter with both transistors in saturation when the input is near V_DD/2:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{\beta}{2}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Duration of short-circuit pulse:**
Assuming linear input ramp, the duration is approximately proportional to the input rise time τ and inversely proportional to V_DD:

$$t_{sc} \approx \frac{\tau \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})}{V_{DD}}$$

**Step 4 — Energy per short-circuit event (area of triangle × V_DD):**

$$E_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot t_{sc}\right)$$

$$E_{sc} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \cdot \frac{\beta}{2}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^{2} \cdot \frac{\tau(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})}{V_{DD}}$$

$$E_{sc} = \frac{\beta}{4} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^{3} \cdot \tau$$

**Step 5 — Average short-circuit power (assuming both edges contribute):**

$$P_{sc} = 2 \cdot E_{sc} \cdot \alpha \cdot f_{clk} = \frac{\beta}{2} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^{3} \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot f_{clk}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The most commonly cited simplified form (used in KTU textbook problems) is:**
>
> $$P_{sc} = \frac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^{3} \cdot \tau \cdot f_{clk}$$
>
> The factor of 6 difference (1/2 vs 1/12) arises from more accurate integration of the saturation-region current over the time both transistors conduct. The form with 1/12 is the standard textbook result.

**Step 6 — Limiting behavior analysis:**

- If **τ → 0** (instantaneous input transition): $P_{sc} \to 0$ ✓
- If **V_DD → 2·V_th**: $P_{sc} \to 0$ (no overlap region) ✓
- If **V_DD ≫ 2·V_th**: $P_{sc}$ grows as $V_{DD}^{3}$ — very expensive in high-voltage designs.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Problem — Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Given:** A CMOS inverter in a 65 nm process has:
- C_L = 30 fF
- V_DD = 1.2 V
- f_clk = 2.5 GHz
- α = 0.2 (data-dependent activity factor)
- V_th = 0.35 V
- β = 200 μA/V²
- τ = 50 ps
- I_leak = 50 nA

**Find:** P_total.

**Step 1 — Switching power:**

$$P_{switch} = 0.2 \times 30 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.2^{2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{9}$$

$$P_{switch} = 0.2 \times 30 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 2.5 \times 10^{9}$$

$$P_{switch} = 0.2 \times 30 \times 1.44 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{switch} = 21.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 21.6 \, \mu W$$

**Step 2 — Short-circuit power:**

$$P_{sc} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{12} \times (1.2 - 0.7)^{3} \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{9}$$

$$P_{sc} = 16.67 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.125 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{9}$$

$$P_{sc} = 16.67 \times 0.125 \times 125 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$P_{sc} = 260.4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} = 0.26 \, \mu W$$

**Step 3 — Static power:**

$$P_{static} = 1.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 60 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} = 0.06 \, \mu W$$

**Step 4 — Total power:**

$$P_{total} = 21.6 + 0.26 + 0.06 = 21.92 \, \mu W \approx 22 \, \mu W$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
Switching power dominates (98.6%), short-circuit and static are negligible at this node — a hallmark of older "high-V_th, high-V_DD" designs. At 14 nm with V_DD = 0.85 V and V_th = 0.3 V, the picture would shift dramatically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — CMOS Power Dissipation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PowerSupply["VDD Supply Source"]:::supply
    StaticBlock["P_static = VDD x I_leak"]:::leak
    DynamicBlock["P_switch = alpha x CL x VDD^2 x f_clk"]:::switch
    ShortBlock["P_sc triangular current spike during transition"]:::short
    ParasiticBlock["Bond wire L, pin C, lead R from package"]:::pkg
    ThermalBlock["Thermal model theta_JA junction to ambient"]:::thermal
    JunctionT["Junction Temperature Tj = Ta + P_total x theta_JA"]:::junction
    OutputBlock["P_total = P_static + P_switch + P_sc"]:::total

    PowerSupply --> StaticBlock
    PowerSupply --> DynamicBlock
    PowerSupply --> ShortBlock
    ParasiticBlock --> StaticBlock
    ParasiticBlock --> DynamicBlock
    ParasiticBlock --> ShortBlock
    StaticBlock --> OutputBlock
    DynamicBlock --> OutputBlock
    ShortBlock --> OutputBlock
    OutputBlock --> ThermalBlock
    ThermalBlock --> JunctionT

    classDef supply fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leak fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef switch fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef short fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef pkg fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef thermal fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef junction fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef total fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```
