# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the self-inductance of a probe ground loop. Why is it a critical parameter in high-speed digital measurements?**

**Model Answer:**

The self-inductance of a probe ground loop is the parasitic inductance associated with the closed conductive path formed between the probe's signal tip, the device under test (DUT) ground plane, and the probe's physical ground lead (clip, spring, or wire). Mathematically, for a rectangular loop of length $a$ and width $b$:

$$L \approx \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \cdot b \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2a}{b}\right)$$

**Why it is critical:** At high frequencies, this inductance introduces a series impedance $X_L = 2\pi f L$ in the ground return path. For example, a 5 nH loop presents about 31 Ω at 1 GHz, which is comparable to the source impedance (50 Ω). This causes ringing, overshoot, and ground bounce artifacts that do not exist in the actual circuit, leading to **false signal-integrity diagnoses**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the engineering rule of thumb for probe ground lead inductance and explain the relationship between lead length and measurement bandwidth.**

**Model Answer:**

**Rule of thumb:** A typical probe ground lead contributes approximately **6 to 10 nH per inch (~25 mm)** of length.

**Relationship to bandwidth:** The usable measurement bandwidth is inversely proportional to the loop inductance:

$$f_{3dB} \approx \frac{R_{source}}{2\pi L_{loop}}$$

For a 50 Ω system:
- 25 mm lead → $L \approx 5$ nH → $f_{3dB} \approx 1.6$ GHz
- 5 mm spring tip → $L \approx 1$ nH → $f_{3dB} \approx 8$ GHz

**Conclusion:** Halving the ground lead length nearly doubles the usable bandwidth. This is why **short ground springs** are mandatory above 1 GHz.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the self-inductance expression for a long, thin rectangular loop of length $a$ and width $b$ (where $a \gg b$) made of wire with circular cross-section of radius $r$.** [7 Marks]

**(b) A probe has a ground lead of length $a = 30$ mm, loop width $b = 4$ mm, and wire radius $r = 0.25$ mm. Calculate the self-inductance, the inductive reactance at 2 GHz, and the maximum usable measurement bandwidth for a 50 Ω source.** [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

Starting from **Neumann's formula** for the self-inductance of a thin wire loop:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \oint \oint \frac{\vec{dl_1} \cdot \vec{dl_2}}{R}$$

**Step 1:** Identify the four sides of the rectangular loop. Two sides of length $a$ are parallel and separated by $b$. Two sides of length $b$ are parallel and separated by $a$.

**Step 2:** Compute the partial inductance of two parallel wires of length $a$ at separation $b$:

$$L_{1} = \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \left[ a \ln\left(\frac{2a}{r}\right) - a + \frac{r}{2} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Similarly for the two shorter sides:

$$L_{2} = \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \left[ b \ln\left(\frac{2b}{r}\right) - b + \frac{r}{2} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Add diagonal contributions. The complete Grover formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \Bigg[ -2(a+b) + 2a \ln\left(\frac{2a}{r}\right) + 2b \ln\left(\frac{2b}{r}\right) \\
&\quad + 2\sqrt{a^2+b^2} - (a+b) \ln\left(\frac{2ab}{r^2}\right) \Bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the thin-loop approximation $a \gg b$. The dominant contribution is from the two long sides, and the logarithmic term simplifies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &\approx \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \cdot b \cdot \left[ \ln\left(\frac{2a}{b}\right) + \frac{1}{2} \right] \\
&\approx \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \cdot b \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2a}{b}\right) \quad \text{(for engineering accuracy)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Inductance grows linearly with loop width $b$ (wider loop = more flux) and logarithmically with length $a$ (longer loop = larger current path). It decreases as wire radius increases (thicker wire distributes current over a larger cross-section, reducing internal flux linkage).

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Given:** $a = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m, $b = 4$ mm $= 0.004$ m, $r = 0.25$ mm $= 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $f = 2$ GHz, $R = 50 \, \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Calculate Self-Inductance:**

Since $a/b = 7.5 \gg 3$, thin-loop approximation is valid.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \cdot b \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2a}{b}\right) \\
&= \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{\pi} \times 0.004 \times \ln\left(\frac{2 \times 0.030}{0.004}\right) \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.004 \times \ln(15) \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.004 \times 2.708 \\
&= 4.33 \times 10^{-9} \text{ H} \\
L &\approx 4.33 \text{ nH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Inductive Reactance at 2 GHz:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_L &= 2\pi f L \\
&= 2\pi \times 2 \times 10^9 \times 4.33 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 54.42 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Usable Bandwidth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{3dB} &= \frac{R_{source}}{2\pi L} \\
&= \frac{50}{2\pi \times 4.33 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= 1.838 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz} \\
f_{3dB} &\approx 1.84 \text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This probe ground lead will produce significant measurement distortion above approximately 1.84 GHz.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain with a mathematical model why a long probe ground lead causes ringing in high-speed digital signals. Derive the expression for the resonant frequency of the resulting LC circuit.** [7 Marks]

**(b) Three probing options are available for a 5 GHz signal: (i) 25 mm clip lead, (ii) 5 mm spring tip, (iii) differential Z-active probe. Compare their inductances and predicted bandwidths. Recommend the best option with justification.** [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

When a probe ground lead with inductance $L$ is connected to a node with total capacitance $C$ (DUT capacitance + probe input capacitance), a **series RLC resonant circuit** is formed.

**The governing differential equation** for the loop with the probe ground lead and node capacitance is:

$$L \frac{d^2 v(t)}{dt^2} + R \frac{dv(t)}{dt} + \frac{v(t)}{C} = 0$$

**Natural resonant frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_0 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \\
f_0 &= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** When a fast edge excites this circuit, energy oscillates between the magnetic field of the ground loop inductor and the electric field of the node capacitance. This oscillation appears as **ringing** on the oscilloscope screen, even if the actual DUT signal is clean.

**Numerical example:** For $L = 5$ nH and $C = 2$ pF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-9} \times 2 \times 10^{-12}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \times \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-20}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-10}} \\
&\approx 1.59 \text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 25 mm lead on a node with 2 pF capacitance will ring at ~1.6 GHz — directly inside the bandwidth of most high-speed digital signals, causing severe false measurement artifacts.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

| Probe Type | Length $a$ | Loop Width $b$ | Inductance $L$ | Bandwidth $f_{3dB}$ (50 Ω) |
|------------|-----------|----------------|----------------|----------------------------|
| (i) 25 mm clip lead | 25 mm | 4 mm | $\approx 4.6$ nH | $\approx 1.7$ GHz |
| (ii) 5 mm spring tip | 5 mm | 2 mm | $\approx 0.8$ nH | $\approx 10$ GHz |
| (iii) Z-active probe | $< 1$ mm | active | $\approx 0.1$ nH | $\approx 80$ GHz |

**Calculations for spring tip:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \times 0.002 \times \ln\left(\frac{2 \times 0.005}{0.002}\right) \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.002 \times \ln(5) \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.002 \times 1.609 \\
&\approx 1.29 \text{ nH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** For a 5 GHz signal, **option (ii) spring tip** is the most practical cost-effective solution, providing bandwidth well above the signal frequency. The Z-active probe is ideal if budget permits and the signal exceeds 10 GHz or is differential. The 25 mm clip lead is **unsuitable** and must not be used at 5 GHz.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to meters** in the inductance formula. The unit trap costs 1 full mark almost every time.
> 2. **Using $b$ instead of $a$** in the logarithm. The long side $a$ goes inside the log, the short side $b$ multiplies outside.
> 3. **Ignoring the thin-loop approximation validity check.** Always verify $a \gg b$ (typically $a/b > 3$) before using the simplified formula.
> 4. **Confusing internal and external inductance.** At GHz frequencies, the internal inductance is zero due to skin effect; only external inductance remains.
> 5. **Forgetting the natural log** — the formula uses $\ln$ (natural log, base $e$), not $\log_{10}$. Many students compute wrong values.
> 6. **Not relating to engineering impact** — KTU examiners award marks for explaining *why* a 5 nH loop ruins a measurement, not just calculating the number.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam 2-Minute Revision:**
>
> - The probe ground loop is a **parasitic inductor** formed by the ground lead and signal path return.
> - The governing formula is $L \approx \dfrac{\mu_0}{\pi} \cdot b \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{2a}{b}\right)$ for $a \gg b$.
> - **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** is non-negotiable — memorize it.
> - Inductive reactance grows linearly with frequency: $X_L = 2\pi f L$.
> - Bandwidth shrinks as inductance grows: $f_{3dB} = R / (2\pi L)$.
> - **Rule of thumb:** ~10 nH per inch of probe ground lead.
> - Resonance with node capacitance: $f_0 = 1 / (2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ — this is what causes ringing.
> - Always **minimize loop area** ($a \times b$) to reduce inductance.
> - Use **spring-tip grounds** for signals above 500 MHz.
> - Use **Z-active or differential probes** above 2 GHz.
> - **Convert all dimensions to meters** before plugging into any formula.
> - A probe ground lead is **never** a true short at high frequencies — this is the single most important conceptual point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spurious Signal Pick Up from Probe Ground Loops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spurious Signal Pick Up from Probe Ground Loops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spurious Signal Pickup from Probe Ground Loops** refers to the unwanted electromagnetic interference (EMI) and inductive voltage artifacts that contaminate oscilloscope measurements when the probe's ground lead, together with the probe tip, forms a closed conductive loop that acts as a single-turn loop antenna.

In high-speed digital systems, an oscilloscope probe's **ground clip lead** is intended to provide a low-impedance return path between the circuit under test (CUT) ground and the oscilloscope's ground reference. However, when this lead is physically long (typically **25 mm to 150 mm**, i.e., 1" to 6"), the combination of:
1. The **probe tip-to-CUT trace** path
2. The **long ground lead** returning to scope ground
3. The **internal scope ground-to-probe ground spring**

forms a closed loop. This loop has both:
- **Self-inductance** (causing $V = L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ switching noise)
- **Loop area** (acting as a magnetic antenna per Faraday's Law)

It couples spurious high-frequency energy from adjacent fast-changing digital signals, switching power supply currents, and ambient RF fields into the measurement channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Tag (PEECT526 – Module 2):** *Measurement techniques for high-speed signals — grounding artefacts, probe loop inductance, and mitigation strategies.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Listening Antenna" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **listen to a friend whispering** in a quiet room. You naturally cup your hand behind your ear — a small loop — to direct the sound into your ear canal. The cupped hand acts as an *acoustic loop antenna*, capturing extra sound.

Now suppose you walk into a **noisy factory** with a big metal hoop around your ear (a *giant* loop). The hoop will pick up all the surrounding noise, drowning out your friend's whisper. **This is exactly what a long oscilloscope probe ground lead does** in a high-speed digital PCB environment:

- **Your friend's whisper** = the small, clean signal you want to measure.
- **The big metal hoop** = the long probe ground lead forming a large loop area.
- **The factory noise** = nearby clock traces, switching currents, RF emissions.

A *short* ground lead (cupped hand) preserves the whisper; a *long* ground lead (metal hoop) corrupts the measurement with surrounding electrical "noise."

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks (Standard Reference Values):**
> - Probe ground lead inductance: **$\approx$ 1 nH per mm** (or **20–25 nH per inch**)
> - Typical 4-inch ground clip inductance: **$L \approx 100 \text{ nH}$**
> - Characteristic impedance of probe ground loop (at 100 MHz): **$X_L \approx 62.8\ \Omega$**
> - Acceptable measurement bandwidth figure-of-merit: ground lead length **$< 5$ mm** for signals above **$100$ MHz**

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU High-Speed Digital Design

In modern KTU-relevant systems (PCIe Gen 4/5, DDR4/5, USB 3.x, SERDES, GHz microcontrollers), signal edge rates have dropped below **100 ps**, equivalent to spectral content reaching **3–10 GHz**. At such frequencies, even **1 cm of ground lead becomes a significant inductive impedance** comparable to or greater than the **50 Ω** scope input impedance, making ground-loop-induced artifacts dominant over the actual signal of interest.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of the Spurious Signal — Two Physical Mechanisms

The probe ground loop produces spurious signals through **two independent physical mechanisms**, which must be analysed separately.

### Mechanism A — Self-Inductance Voltage (L·di/dt Effect)

The ground lead, being a finite conductor, possesses **self-inductance** $L$. Any current $i(t)$ flowing through it produces a voltage drop:

$$V_{\text{spurious}} = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$

The current $i(t)$ is the displacement current and high-frequency return current flowing between the CUT ground and the scope chassis. The derivative $\frac{di}{dt}$ is enormous in high-speed digital systems:

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{\text{fast CMOS}} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t} \approx \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ ns}} = 3.3 \times 10^9 \text{ V/s equivalent current slew}$$

When scaled by typical probe inductance, this voltage can reach **tens to hundreds of millivolts** — easily comparable to small logic-signal amplitudes.

### Mechanism B — Far-Field Magnetic Pickup (Faraday's Law)

A loop of area $A$ immersed in a time-varying magnetic flux density $B(t)$ induces an EMF per Faraday's Law:

$$V_{\text{induced}} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -A \cdot \frac{dB}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering insight:** *Both* mechanisms scale **linearly with the loop area $A$** and the **loop inductance $L$**. Reducing ground lead length is the single most effective mitigation because:
> - Loop area $A \propto (\text{length}) \times (\text{width})$
> - Loop inductance $L \propto \text{length}$

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown of the Problem

1. **Engineer connects oscilloscope probe** to a high-speed node using a standard 4-inch (100 mm) ground clip.
2. **The ground lead** (a straight wire of length $\ell \approx 100 \text{ mm}$) plus the **probe tip-to-node trace** form a physical loop.
3. **A nearby aggressor** (clock trace, switching power MOSFET, RF source) generates a time-varying magnetic field $B(t)$.
4. **The probe loop** intercepts a portion of the flux: $\Phi = B \cdot A_{\text{loop}}$.
5. **By Faraday's Law**, an EMF appears in series with the desired signal at the scope input.
6. **Additionally**, the high-frequency return current $i(t)$ flowing through the ground lead's inductance $L$ produces a self-induced voltage $L \cdot di/dt$.
7. **Both effects superimpose** on the actual signal, distorting edges, adding ringing, overshoot, and false "glitches."

---

## 2.3 Mitigation Strategies Hierarchy

| # | Strategy | Effectiveness | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|----------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Use **spring-tip (pigtail) ground** ($\ell < 5$ mm) | Excellent | ⭐⭐⭐ Most commonly asked |
| 2 | Use **active FET probe** with very short tip | Excellent | ⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Use **differential probe** (rejects common-mode) | Excellent | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Use **coaxial-tip ground socket** (semi-rigid) | Very good | ⭐ |
| 5 | **Avoid probing** — use test vias and onboard 50 Ω termination | Best | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Maintain **massive ground plane** under probe area | Good | ⭐ |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme Examination — Master These:**

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|
| $V_L$ | $V_L = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Self-inductance voltage drop | V |
| $L_{\text{wire}}$ | $L \approx 1 \text{ nH/mm}$ | Wire self-inductance per unit length | H/m |
| $V_{\text{emf}}$ | $V_{\text{emf}} = -A \cdot \dfrac{dB}{dt}$ | Faraday induced EMF | V |
| $X_L$ | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Reactive impedance of ground lead | Ω |
| $\tau$ | $\tau = L / R$ | Time constant of ground lead (R = 50 Ω) | s |
| $Z_{\text{loop}}$ | $Z = R + j\omega L$ | Total loop impedance | Ω |
| $f_{\text{res}}$ | $f_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC_{\text{par}}}}$ | Resonance with parasitic capacitance | Hz |
| $V_{\text{pickup ratio}}$ | $\dfrac{V_{\text{noise}}}{V_{\text{signal}}} \propto \dfrac{A_{\text{loop}}}{A_{\text{signal-trace}}}$ | Relative noise coupling | dimensionless |
| $BW_{\text{usable}}$ | $BW \leq \dfrac{0.35}{t_{\text{rise}}}$ | Probe bandwidth ≥ signal bandwidth | Hz |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Syntax Reminder:** All absolute values, norms, and "such that" bars in the equations above are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or via clear $\text{abs}(\cdot)$ notation. Do **not** confuse the table-cell delimiter `|` with mathematical absolute value bars.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding probe ground loops is essential in:
- **Signal Integrity (SI) verification** of high-speed serial links
- **EMI/EMC compliance testing** (avoiding false emissions violations)
- **Power Integrity** measurements of PDN (Power Distribution Network) ripple
- **Failure analysis** of digital systems showing "phantom" glitches
- **Mixed-signal SoC debug** (ADC input noise contamination)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inductance of a Straight-Wire Ground Lead

For a straight round conductor of length $\ell$ and diameter $d$ carrying uniform current, the **external partial inductance** (relevant at high frequencies where skin effect confines current to the surface) is approximated by:

$$L \approx \frac{\mu_0 \, \ell}{2\pi} \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{2\ell}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step-by-step substitution for the typical 100 mm ground clip:**

Assume:
- $\ell = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $d = 0.65 \text{ mm} = 6.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$ (typical 22 AWG clip wire)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$

**Step (i):** Compute the logarithmic term:

$$\frac{2\ell}{d} = \frac{2 \times 0.1}{6.5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.2}{0.00065} \approx 307.69$$

$$\ln(307.69) \approx 5.729$$

**Step (ii):** Compute the bracket:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{2\ell}{d}\right) - 1 = 5.729 - 1 = 4.729$$

**Step (iii):** Compute the prefactor:

$$\frac{\mu_0 \, \ell}{2\pi} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 0.1}{2\pi} = 2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ H} = 20 \text{ nH}$$

**Step (iv):** Multiply:

$$L = 20 \text{ nH} \times 4.729 \approx 94.6 \text{ nH}$$

This matches the engineering rule-of-thumb: **$L \approx 1 \text{ nH/mm}$** (since $94.6 \text{ nH} / 100 \text{ mm} \approx 0.95 \text{ nH/mm}$). ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spurious Voltage from Switching Current

Consider a CMOS output driving a 50 Ω transmission line with 3.3 V swing in 1 ns. The dynamic current is:

$$I = \frac{\Delta V}{R} = \frac{3.3}{50} = 66 \text{ mA}$$

The current slew rate:

$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{66 \text{ mA}}{1 \text{ ns}} = 6.6 \times 10^7 \text{ A/s}$$

Voltage induced across the **100 mm** ground lead:

$$V_{\text{spurious}} = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = 94.6 \times 10^{-9} \times 6.6 \times 10^7$$

$$V_{\text{spurious}} = 6.24 \text{ V} \quad \text{(!!!)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Counter-intuitive but critical insight:** When the *ground lead* itself carries the return current of the CUT (e.g., via a poor ground), the inductive drop can be **larger than the signal itself**, completely destroying measurement fidelity. This is *not* the typical small artifact — it is a *signal-destroying* effect.

> **In practice**, well-designed scopes use the **probe-tip-to-CUT-trace** path for signal and a *separate dedicated ground spring* to avoid this current, but the *loop area* effect (Mechanism B) still applies.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Frequency-Domain Impedance of the Ground Lead

The reactive impedance at angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$X_L(f) = 2\pi f \cdot L$$

| Frequency $f$ | $X_L$ (for $L = 100$ nH) | Compared to 50 Ω scope input |
|---------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 MHz | $0.628\ \Omega$ | Negligible |
| 10 MHz | $6.28\ \Omega$ | Small |
| 100 MHz | $62.8\ \Omega$ | **Larger than 50 Ω** — dominates |
| 500 MHz | $314\ \Omega$ | Completely dominant |
| 1 GHz | $628\ \Omega$ | Scope is "blind" |

**Engineering takeaway:** For any measurement above **~80 MHz**, a 100 mm ground lead makes the scope behave as if it had a **series inductor** that blocks high-frequency content, smearing edges and producing the false "ringing" appearance.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Faraday-Loop Pickup Quantification

Let a nearby aggressor trace carry a current $i(t) = I_0 \sin(2\pi f t)$ at distance $r$ from the probe loop of area $A$.

The magnetic field from a long straight wire (Biot–Savart):

$$B(r) = \frac{\mu_0 \, i(t)}{2\pi r}$$

The flux through the probe loop:

$$\Phi(t) = B \cdot A = \frac{\mu_0 \, A \, I_0 \sin(2\pi f t)}{2\pi r}$$

The induced EMF (Faraday):

$$V_{\text{emf}} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\frac{\mu_0 \, A \, I_0 \, 2\pi f \cos(2\pi f t)}{2\pi r}$$

$$V_{\text{emf}} = -\frac{\mu_0 \, A \, I_0 \, f \cos(2\pi f t)}{r}$$

**Magnitude:**

$$V_{\text{emf,peak}} = \frac{\mu_0 \, A \, I_0 \, f}{r}$$

**Numerical example:**

- $A = (100 \text{ mm}) \times (5 \text{ mm}) = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ (loop area)
- $I_0 = 10 \text{ mA}$, $f = 100 \text{ MHz}$, $r = 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$$V_{\text{emf}} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(5 \times 10^{-4})(10^{-2})(10^8)}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$V_{\text{emf}} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$V_{\text{emf}} = \frac{4\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4\pi \times 10^{-2}$$

$$V_{\text{emf}} \approx 0.126 \text{ V} = 126 \text{ mV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **126 mV artifact** from a *10 mA* aggressor at *5 mm* distance — this is *comparable to a 3.3 V CMOS "low" noise margin*, easily mistaken for a real logic-level disturbance.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Mitigation — Short Ground Lead Improvement

Compare 100 mm (long) vs 5 mm (pigtail) lead.

Inductance scaling (linear): $L \propto \ell$

$$\frac{L_{\text{pigtail}}}{L_{\text{long}}} = \frac{5}{100} = \frac{1}{20}$$

Induced voltage scales linearly:

$$\frac{V_{\text{pigtail}}}{V_{\text{long}}} = \frac{1}{20} = 5\%$$

Induced voltage from Faraday pickup scales with **area**, and area $\propto \ell \times w$ (assuming $w$ constant):

$$\frac{V_{\text{pigtail}}}{V_{\text{long}}} = \frac{5 \times w}{100 \times w} = 5\%$$

> **Conclusion:** A 5 mm pigtail ground reduces spurious pickup by **~95% (a 26 dB improvement)**. This is the single most effective, low-cost mitigation step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Probe Ground Loop Anatomy

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the probe-ground-loop measurement scenario, identifying the parasitic pickup paths and the mitigation insertion points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CUT Device Under Test"] --> B["Signal Trace on PCB"]
    B --> C["Probe Tip Contact Point"]
    C --> D["Probe Tip Internal Conductor"]
    D --> E["Oscilloscope 50 Ohm Input Amplifier"]
    A --> F["CUT Ground Plane"]
    F --> G["Long Ground Clip 100 mm"]
    G --> H["Probe Ground Spring Reference"]
    H --> E
    subgraph Aggressors
        I["Adjacent Clock Trace di/dt"]
        J["Switching Power Inductor"]
        K["Ambient RF 100 MHz to 1 GHz"]
    end
    I -. "Mutual Inductance M" .-> G
    J -. "Magnetic Field Coupling" .-> G
    K -. "Far Field Loop Antenna" .-> G
    G -. "L di/dt Voltage Drop" .-> E
    subgraph Mitigations
        M1["Use Pigtail Ground less than 5 mm"]
        M2["Use Active FET Probe"]
        M3["Use Differential Probe"]
        M4["Add 50 Ohm Termination at Tip"]
    end
    G -->|Replace by| M1
    H -->|Replace by| M2
    E -->|Replace by| M3
    C -->|Add| M4
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Spurious Coupling Path (Mechanism A vs B)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src1["Aggressor Current di/dt"] --> mag1["Magnetic Flux Phi through Loop A"]
    src2["Return Current through Ground Lead"] --> mag2["Self Inductance L of Lead"]
    mag1 --> faraday["V = minus A dB/dt Mechanism B"]
    mag2 --> selfL["V = L di/dt Mechanism A"]
    faraday --> sum["Sum at Scope Input"]
    selfL --> sum
    signal["Desired CUT Signal"] --> sum
    sum --> output["Corrupted Scope Display"]
    style src1 fill:#ffe5e5
    style src2 fill:#ffe5e5
    style output fill:#fff4cc
    style sum fill:#ffd6d6
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Long-Lead vs Short-Lead Comparison

```
    ==== LONG GROUND LEAD (BAD) ====              ==== SHORT GROUND LEAD (GOOD) ====

    Signal ---[TIP]===~~~~~~L~~~~~~~              Signal ---[TIP]===~L'~---
                       |                                          |
                       | 100 mm                                   | 5 mm
                       |                                          |
    GND --------------[CLIP]-------Scope GND        GND ---------[SPRING]---Scope GND
                       <-- A_loop -->                            <-- A_loop' -->
                       (Large area)                              (Tiny area)

    V_spurious ∝ L · di/dt  +  A · dB/dt        V_spurious reduced by ~95% (26 dB)
```

## 4.4 Equivalent Circuit Model (Textual)

```
    Signal_in --[R_tip]--+--[L_tip]--+--[C_in]---> Scope
                          |          |
                          +--[L_gnd]-+
                                 |
                                GND_ref

    Where:
    R_tip ≈ 1–10 Ω (probe tip resistance)
    L_tip ≈ few nH (probe tip inductance)
    L_gnd ≈ 1 nH/mm × length  ← DOMINANT PARASITIC
    C_in  ≈ 10–20 pF (scope input + probe capacitance)
    L_gnd resonates with C_in → RINGING ARTIFACTS on edges
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "spurious signal pickup from probe ground loops" and state its primary cause.**

**Model Answer:**
Spurious signal pickup from probe ground loops is the contamination of an oscilloscope measurement by unwanted electromagnetic interference and inductive voltage artifacts introduced when the probe's ground lead forms a closed conductive loop with the signal tip. The **primary cause is the loop area enclosed by the long ground lead**, which acts both as a single-turn inductor (causing $L \cdot di/dt$ voltage drops) and as a loop antenna (coupling external magnetic flux via Faraday's Law).

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is a short "pigtail" ground lead preferred over a long ground clip for measurements above 100 MHz?**

**Model Answer:**
A short pigtail ground lead minimizes both the loop inductance $L$ and the loop area $A$. Since spurious pickup voltages scale **linearly** with $L$ (via $L \cdot di/dt$) and with $A$ (via Faraday's Law $V = -A \cdot dB/dt$), reducing the lead length from **100 mm to 5 mm** cuts the spurious voltage by approximately **95% (a 26 dB improvement)**. Additionally, a shorter lead has lower reactive impedance $X_L = 2\pi f L$, preserving high-frequency signal content that would otherwise be filtered out.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **mechanism of spurious signal pickup from probe ground loops** in high-speed digital measurements. Discuss the **two physical mechanisms** responsible and derive expressions for the induced voltages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A high-speed oscilloscope probe is connected using a standard **100 mm ground clip** to measure a 3.3 V CMOS signal with a 1 ns edge rate across a 50 Ω transmission line. The probe ground lead has an inductance of **$L = 95$ nH**. Compute:
   - (i) The reactive impedance $X_L$ of the ground lead at **100 MHz**.
   - (ii) The peak spurious voltage $V_L$ generated by the return current.
   - (iii) The reduction factor achieved by replacing the lead with a **5 mm pigtail** (assume $L_{\text{pigtail}} = 4.75$ nH). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Mechanism Explanation (7 marks)

A high-speed digital probe has two electrical connections to the CUT: a **signal tip** and a **ground clip**. Physically, these form a closed loop. The ground lead, being a finite conductor of length $\ell$, exhibits:
- **Self-inductance** $L$ (typically $1$ nH/mm)
- **Enclosed loop area** $A$ (length $\times$ width)

**Mechanism 1 — Inductive Voltage Drop (Self-Inductance):**
The high-frequency return current $i(t)$ flowing through the ground lead induces a voltage:

$$V_L = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$

**Mechanism 2 — Magnetic Flux Pickup (Faraday's Law):**
A time-varying external magnetic field $B(t)$ intersecting the loop area induces an EMF:

$$V_{\text{emf}} = -A \cdot \frac{dB}{dt}$$

The two voltages add in series with the desired signal at the scope input, producing **edge distortion, ringing, overshoot, and false glitches** on the displayed waveform. The artifact magnitude is:

- $V_L \propto L \cdot di/dt \propto \ell$
- $V_{\text{emf}} \propto A \cdot f \propto \ell$

Thus, **shortening the lead reduces both effects linearly**.

> *[Examiner's note: A clear sketch showing the probe tip, ground clip, loop area, and an adjacent aggressor trace is essential for full marks.]*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $L = 95$ nH, $\Delta V = 3.3$ V, $t_r = 1$ ns, $R = 50\ \Omega$, target frequency $f = 100$ MHz.

**(i) Reactive impedance at 100 MHz:**

$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2\pi \times (100 \times 10^6) \times (95 \times 10^{-9})$$

$$X_L = 6.2832 \times 10^{-2}\ \Omega \times 95 = 5.969\ \Omega \approx 59.7\ \Omega$$

**Result:** $X_L \approx 59.7\ \Omega$ — this is **larger than the 50 Ω scope input impedance**, so the lead dominates the measurement path. ✅

**(ii) Peak spurious voltage:**

The dynamic current through 50 Ω:

$$I = \frac{\Delta V}{R} = \frac{3.3}{50} = 66 \text{ mA}$$

The current slew rate:

$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{I}{t_r} = \frac{66 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.6 \times 10^7 \text{ A/s}$$

Induced voltage:

$$V_L = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = (95 \times 10^{-9}) \times (6.6 \times 10^7)$$

$$V_L = 6.27 \text{ V}$$

**Result:** $V_L \approx 6.27$ V — a **massive artifact** that is **nearly twice the actual signal amplitude (3.3 V)**, completely swamping the measurement. ⚠️

**(iii) Reduction factor with 5 mm pigtail ($L_{\text{pigtail}} = 4.75$ nH):**

$$\frac{V_{L,\text{pigtail}}}{V_{L,\text{long}}} = \frac{L_{\text{pigtail}}}{L_{\text{long}}} = \frac{4.75}{95} = 0.05 = 5\%$$

In decibels:

$$20 \log_{10}(0.05) = -26.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Result:** The pigtail ground provides a **95% reduction (26 dB improvement)** in spurious voltage. ✅

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Alternative]`

**(a)** Discuss the various **mitigation techniques** to suppress spurious signal pickup from probe ground loops. Compare at least four methods in a tabular form with their effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A probe ground loop encloses an area of $5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$. An adjacent aggressor trace at 5 mm distance carries a sinusoidal current of amplitude 10 mA at 100 MHz. Compute the **peak Faraday-induced EMF** in the probe loop and comment on whether it is significant for a 3.3 V CMOS logic measurement. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Mitigation Techniques (7 marks)

Four primary mitigation techniques:

| # | Technique | Principle | Effectiveness | Cost |
|---|-----------|-----------|---------------|------|
| 1 | **Pigtail / Spring-tip ground** ($\ell < 5$ mm) | Minimizes $L$ and $A$ | ~26 dB reduction | Very low |
| 2 | **Active FET probe** | On-tip amplifier, very low tip impedance | Excellent >1 GHz | High |
| 3 | **Differential probe** | Rejects common-mode ground noise | Excellent CMRR > 60 dB | High |
| 4 | **On-board test termination** (50 Ω SMA) | Eliminates probe loop entirely | Best possible | Medium |
| 5 | **Coaxial-tip socket** | Controlled-impedance ground | Very good | Low |
| 6 | **Maintaining solid ground plane** | Reduces return-path impedance | Good | Free (design) |

**Conclusion:** The optimal hierarchy is: **on-board termination > differential probe > pigtail ground**. A combination of techniques is used in practice.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Faraday Pickup (7 marks)

**Given:** $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $I_0 = 10$ mA $= 10^{-2}$ A, $f = 100$ MHz, $r = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

**Formula (derived earlier):**

$$V_{\text{emf,peak}} = \frac{\mu_0 \, A \, I_0 \, f}{r}$$

**Substitution:**

$$V_{\text{emf,peak}} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(5 \times 10^{-4})(10^{-2})(10^8)}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step-wise simplification:**

Numerator:
$$(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) = 20\pi \times 10^{-11} = 6.283 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\times (10^{-2}) = 6.283 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\times (10^8) = 6.283 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator: $5 \times 10^{-3}$

$$V_{\text{emf,peak}} = \frac{6.283 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.257 \times 10^{-1} = 0.1257 \text{ V}$$

**Result:** $V_{\text{emf,peak}} \approx 126$ mV.

**Significance comment:** A 126 mV induced artifact is **significant** for a 3.3 V CMOS logic level. While it does not exceed the standard $\pm 825$ mV noise margin, it is comparable to typical signal-integrity budgets of $\pm 100$–$200$ mV, and may be **mistaken for a real logic disturbance or ringing** during debug. The artifact is approximately **3.8%** of the signal swing, exceeding the typical 2–3% measurement error budget.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in Faraday's law ($V = -d\Phi/dt$): not always penalized, but be consistent.
> 2. **Mixing $f$ and $\omega$**: Use $f$ when formula is in Hz; use $\omega$ when formula has $2\pi$.
> 3. **Unit errors in $r$ and $A$**: Convert mm² to m² and mm to m before substitution. A common error is $r = 5$ mm used directly instead of $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m.
> 4. **Forgetting $2\pi$** in $X_L = 2\pi f L$: A full-mark deduction.
> 5. **Stating "126 mV is negligible"** without comparison to the actual noise margin — examiner expects quantified judgment.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** in part (a): Up to **2 marks** are reserved for a labeled sketch of the probe loop, ground lead, and aggressor coupling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Spurious Signal Pickup from Probe Ground Loops**

- **Definition:** Unwanted artifacts in scope measurements caused by the probe ground lead forming an inductive loop antenna with the signal tip.
- **Two mechanisms:**
  - **Self-inductance voltage:** $V_L = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ — caused by return current through lead inductance.
  - **Faraday-induced EMF:** $V_{\text{emf}} = -A \cdot \dfrac{dB}{dt}$ — caused by external magnetic flux through loop area.
- **Rule of thumb:** Ground lead inductance $L \approx 1$ nH/mm (or $20$–$25$ nH/inch).
- **Critical frequency threshold:** Above $\sim 80$ MHz, a 100 mm ground lead's $X_L$ exceeds the 50 Ω scope input.
- **Dominant scaling:** $V_{\text{spurious}} \propto \ell$ (length) for both mechanisms.
- **Best mitigation:** Short pigtail ground ($\ell < 5$ mm) — provides $\sim 26$ dB (95%) improvement.
- **Premium mitigations:** Active FET probe, differential probe, on-board 50 Ω test termination.
- **Practical test:** Compare waveform with long vs short ground — a 2–3× difference confirms ground-loop contamination.
- **PCB design rule:** Always provide a nearby ground via adjacent to the test point to enable short pigtail probing.
- **Never** trust ringing/glitches seen with long ground lead without re-measuring with proper short ground.

> **Master these 10 points and you are fully prepared for any KTU 2024 question on this topic.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Probing Fixtures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Special Probing Fixtures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **High Speed Digital Design (PEECT526)**, **Special Probing Fixtures** refer to the engineered mechanical and electromagnetic interface structures used to couple a measurement instrument (oscilloscope, spectrum analyzer, network analyzer, TDR) to a Device Under Test (DUT) without corrupting the high-bandwidth signal. They are specifically designed to:

1. Maintain a **controlled characteristic impedance of 50 Ω** (the industry-standard system impedance).
2. Minimize parasitic **capacitive loading, inductive ground-loop reactance, and dielectric loss**.
3. Provide a repeatable, calibration-friendly transition between the **coaxial cable environment** and the **planar transmission-line environment** (microstrip, stripline, coplanar waveguide).
4. Enable accurate extraction of S-parameters and time-domain waveforms at edge speeds in the sub-nanosecond regime (rise time $\tau_r < 1$ ns).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 2):** This topic falls under *Measurement Techniques for High-Speed Digital Systems*. The expected outcome is the ability to **select, design, and de-embed** a probing fixture suitable for gigabit-rate signals, and to recognize the artifacts introduced by improper fixturing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Stethoscope for a Heart Surgeon**
> 
> Imagine a cardiologist listening to a patient's heart. A regular, long, rubber stethoscope distorts the faint "lub-dub" sound and picks up ambient noise — the doctor hears echoes of the room, not the heart.
> 
> A high-fidelity electronic stethoscope has a **short, impedance-matched acoustic channel** that captures the true heart waveform with minimal reflection.
> 
> In high-speed digital design, the **circuit trace is the "heartbeat"** (a 5 Gbps NRZ signal with a 50 ps edge), and the **oscilloscope probe is the "stethoscope."** A normal passive probe (10:1, 1 m long) acts like the rubber stethoscope — its long ground lead forms an inductive loop that rings and distorts the edge. A **Special Probing Fixture** (e.g., a coaxial pigtail, a 2.92 mm launcher, or an active differential probe tip) is the *high-fidelity stethoscope* — short, matched, and calibrated.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values are assumed throughout the KTU 2024 scheme and are required to be committed to memory:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **System Reference Impedance ($Z_0$)** | **50** | $\Omega$ |
| **Propagation velocity in vacuum ($c$)** | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| **Relative permittivity of FR-4** | $4.2 - 4.6$ | dimensionless |
| **Relative permittivity of Rogers RO4350B** | $3.48$ | dimensionless |
| **Bandwidth-edge relationship (Knee frequency)** | $f_{knee} = 0.5 / \tau_r$ | Hz |
| **SOLR (Source-Open-Load-Reciprocal) calibration reference plane** | connector mating plane | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge-Speed Rule of Thumb:** When a digital edge has a rise time $\tau_r$, the **critical length** beyond which a trace must be treated as a *transmission line* is:
> 
> $$L_{crit} = \frac{\tau_r \cdot v_p}{6}$$
> 
> If your probe fixture lead exceeds $L_{crit}$, the fixture is no longer electrically short and **will reflect the edge**, producing ringing on the oscilloscope screen.

## 1.4 Classification of Special Probing Fixtures (KTU Taxonomy)

Special Probing Fixtures are classified along three orthogonal axes used in the KTU examination:

1. **By Physical Coupling Mechanism**
   - *Conductive (galvanic) fixtures* — direct metallic contact (SMA launchers, pogo pins, microstrip-to-coaxial adapters).
   - *Non-contact (electromagnetic) fixtures* — near-field loop probes, electro-optic (EO) probes, magnetic field pickup loops.

2. **By Bandwidth Class**
   - *Sub-GHz* — standard 10:1 passive probes with short ground springs.
   - *Multi-GHz* — active FET probes (e.g., 1–6 GHz), coaxial pigtail launchers.
   - *Millimeter-wave (mmWave)* — 2.92 mm, 2.40 mm, 1.85 mm coaxial connectors (valid up to 40, 50, 67 GHz respectively).

3. **By Signal Polarity**
   - *Single-ended fixtures* — measure one node vs ground.
   - *Differential fixtures* — capture the difference of two complementary nodes (essential for LVDS, USB 3.0, PCIe).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory: Why a "Special" Fixture is Needed

A standard 10:1 passive oscilloscope probe, although seemingly benign, violates three conditions of high-speed measurement:

1. **Long ground lead** creates a series inductance $L_g$ that resonates with the probe tip capacitance $C_{tip}$, generating **ground-lead ringing** at frequency $f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_g C_{tip}}}$.
2. **The 10:1 attenuation network** is a compensated RC divider — at frequencies where the compensating capacitor $C_{comp}$ drifts from its nominal trim value, the probe behaves as a **low-pass filter**, attenuating harmonics and slowing the observed edge.
3. **The 1–2 m cable** acts as a lossy transmission line; above ~500 MHz, the **skin-effect loss** in the inner conductor exceeds 6 dB, corrupting the spectral content of a 5 Gbps signal.

A **Special Probing Fixture** is engineered to mitigate each of these. The design logic follows this hierarchy:

- **Step 1 — Impedance matching:** All metallic paths are dimensioned to a $Z_0$ of **50 Ω** using the microstrip/stripline width-formulas (see Section 2.2).
- **Step 2 — Shortest possible ground path:** The fixture uses a **ground-blade**, **pogo-pin ground cluster**, or a **coplanar ground plane** that places the return current within a few hundred micrometers of the signal tip.
- **Step 3 — Bandwidth verification:** The fixture is characterized by its own S-parameters (typically $S_{11}, S_{21}$); it is considered transparent if $\vert S_{21} \vert \geq -3$ dB up to the **3rd harmonic** of the fundamental clock.
- **Step 4 — De-embedding readiness:** The fixture is designed with a definable **reference plane** (e.g., the connector-saver mating flange) so that the fixture's effect can be mathematically removed from the measured waveform.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Boundary Conditions | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Z_0^{MS} = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8h}{w} + \dfrac{w}{4h}\right)$ | Microstrip impedance, valid for $w/h \leq 1$ | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $Z_0^{MS} = \dfrac{120\pi}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}} \left[ \dfrac{w}{h} + 1.393 + 0.667 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{w}{h} + 1.444\right) \right]}$ | Microstrip impedance, valid for $w/h \geq 1$ | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \left( 1 + 12 \dfrac{h}{w} \right)^{-1/2}$ | Effective permittivity, microstrip | dimensionless |
| 4 | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}$ | Phase velocity on planar line | m/s |
| 5 | $C_{load} = C_{tip} (1 + \dfrac{R_s}{R_{in}})$ | Total capacitive load of a 10:1 probe with source $R_s$, scope $R_{in} = 1$ M$\Omega$ | F |
| 6 | $\tau_{10\%-90\%} \approx 2.2 \cdot R_{th} \cdot C_{load}$ | Output rise time due to capacitive loading (single-pole RC) | s |
| 7 | $f_{ring} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_g C_{tip}}}$ | Ground-lead resonance frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $f_{knee} = \dfrac{0.5}{\tau_r}$ | Frequency above which a digital edge has significant spectral content | Hz |
| 9 | $L_{crit} = \dfrac{\tau_r \cdot v_p}{6}$ | Maximum electrical length for lumped analysis | m |
| 10 | $V_{meas}(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{ H_{fixture}(f) \cdot V_{DUT}(f) \right\}$ | Measured signal is DUT convolved with fixture transfer function | V |
| 11 | $H_{de-embed}(f) = \dfrac{1}{H_{fixture}(f)} = \dfrac{1}{S_{21,fixture}(f)}$ | De-embedding transfer function | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Reflection coefficient at load $Z_L$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition reminder:** All of the above formulas assume a **lossless, homogeneous, quasi-TEM** propagation mode, which is valid for line widths and heights that are small compared to the signal wavelength $\lambda$ at the **3rd harmonic**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Special Probing Fixtures are *not* an academic exercise — they are the silent enablers of every modern gigabit product:

- **PCIe Gen 5 / 6 compliance testing** at 32 GT/s / 64 GT/s is impossible without 25–50 GHz bandwidth fixtures and precision **TRL (Thru-Reflect-Line)** calibration standards fabricated directly on the test coupon.
- **DDR5 eye-diagram validation** at 6400 MT/s requires **interposers** (a special class of fixture) that route the LPDDR signals to the oscilloscope with $< 35$ ps of skew between channels.
- **mmWave 5G front-end modules** (28 GHz, 39 GHz) use **GSSG (Ground-Signal-Signal-Ground) waveguide-coaxial launches** with built-in calibration substrates.
- **Automotive Ethernet (1000BASE-T1)** uses **directional balun fixtures** to convert the differential link into single-ended for spectral analysis.

In every case, the engineer's workflow is: **(1) build/select the fixture, (2) calibrate at its reference plane, (3) measure the DUT, (4) de-embed the fixture's effect.**

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Permittivity and Characteristic Impedance of a Microstrip Probe Pad

This derivation establishes the foundational $Z_0$ for a **microstrip launch pad** — the most common element in any special probing fixture on a PCB.

**Given:**
- Substrate height $h$ between conductor and ground plane
- Conductor width $w$
- Substrate relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$
- Conductor thickness $t \approx 0$ (negligible at KTU board)

**Goal:** Derive $Z_0$ for $w/h \leq 1$.

### Step 1: Conceptually decompose the field

A microstrip line supports a quasi-TEM wave. The electric field exists **partly in the dielectric** (fraction $\varepsilon_r$) and **partly in the air** (fraction 1). The single effective permittivity is therefore a weighted average:

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = q \cdot \varepsilon_r + (1 - q) \cdot 1
$$

where $q$ is the *filling factor* — the fraction of stored electric energy that resides in the dielectric.

### Step 2: Empirical formula for $q$

Schneider (1969) showed empirically that the filling factor is well approximated by:

$$
q = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12 h / w}} \right]
$$

### Step 3: Expand $\varepsilon_{eff}$

Substitute $q$ into the field-fraction equation:

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12h/w}}\right] \varepsilon_r + \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12h/w}}\right] (1)
$$

Collect the $\varepsilon_r$ and constant terms:

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \left[1 + 12 \frac{h}{w}\right]^{-1/2}
$$

This is **Formula #3** in the KTU cheat sheet.

### Step 4: Derive $Z_0$ for $w/h \leq 1$

For narrow lines, the magnetic field is well approximated by a parallel-plate configuration with a logarithmic fringing correction. The Wheeler formula for narrow microstrip is:

$$
Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} \ln\!\left[ \frac{8h}{w} + \frac{w}{4h} \right]
$$

This is **Formula #1** — it is valid strictly for $w/h \leq 1$, where the dominant field is between the conductor and the ground plane and fringing is small.

### Step 5: Cross-check at $w/h = 1$

At $w/h = 1$ on an FR-4 substrate ($\varepsilon_r = 4.4$):

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{4.4 + 1}{2} + \frac{4.4 - 1}{2}(1 + 12)^{-1/2} = 2.7 + 1.7 \cdot (13)^{-1/2} = 2.7 + 1.7 \cdot 0.277 = 2.7 + 0.471 = 3.171
$$

$$
Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{3.171}} \ln(8 + 0.25) = \frac{60}{1.781} \cdot \ln(8.25) = 33.69 \cdot 2.110 = 71.1\ \Omega
$$

The result $\approx 71\ \Omega$ is consistent with standard microstrip design tables — the cross-check passes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Probe Capacitive Loading — Rise-Time Degradation

**Scenario:** A CMOS driver with source resistance $R_s = 25\ \Omega$ is probed by a 10:1 passive probe with tip capacitance $C_{tip} = 10$ pF. The oscilloscope input is $R_{in} = 1$ M$\Omega$ with $C_{in} = 15$ pF. Determine the rise-time degradation.

### Step 1: Construct the small-signal model

The probe's 10:1 attenuation is formed by a compensated divider. From the **DUT's perspective**, the probe presents an input impedance of:

$$
R_{probe, in} = R_{att} \, \Vert \, R_{in} \quad ; \quad C_{probe, in} = C_{tip} + C_{att}
$$

where $R_{att}$ and $C_{att}$ are the attenuation-network resistor and capacitor. After compensation $R_{att} C_{att} = R_{in} C_{in}$, the divider is frequency-flat and the **input capacitance to the DUT** is approximately:

$$
C_{load} = C_{tip} \left( 1 + \frac{R_s}{R_{in}} \right) \approx C_{tip} \quad \text{(since } R_{in} = 10^{6}\ \Omega \text{)}
$$

So $C_{load} \approx C_{tip} = 10$ pF.

### Step 2: Compute the Thevenin drive resistance

The driver and source form a Thevenin source with $R_{th} = R_s = 25\ \Omega$ (assuming an ideal low-impedance CMOS output model; in practice $R_{th}$ is the on-resistance of the CMOS output FET).

### Step 3: Compute the RC time constant

$$
\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{load} = 25 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12} = 250 \times 10^{-12} = 250\ \text{ps}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the 10%–90% rise time

For a single-pole low-pass response, the 10%–90% rise time is:

$$
\tau_{r} = 2.2 \cdot \tau = 2.2 \cdot 250\ \text{ps} = 550\ \text{ps}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the root-sum-square (RSS) rise-time addition

If the original CMOS edge had $\tau_{r,DUT} = 100$ ps, the *measured* edge is:

$$
\tau_{r,meas} = \sqrt{\tau_{r,DUT}^{2} + \tau_{probe}^{2}} = \sqrt{100^{2} + 550^{2}} = \sqrt{10\,000 + 302\,500} = \sqrt{312\,500} = 559\ \text{ps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The probe has *increased* the apparent edge from 100 ps to 559 ps — a **5.6× distortion**. This is precisely why a **special fixture** (active probe with $C_{tip} < 0.5$ pF, or direct 50 Ω SMA launch) is mandatory for sub-nanosecond signals.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ground-Lead Inductance and Ring Frequency

**Given:** A passive probe with $C_{tip} = 10$ pF and a 5 cm pigtail ground lead. Compute the ringing frequency and decide if it falls within the signal spectrum.

### Step 1: Estimate the lead inductance

A straight round wire has an approximate internal + external inductance of $\approx 10$ nH/cm. For a 5 cm lead:

$$
L_g = 10\ \text{nH/cm} \cdot 5\ \text{cm} = 50\ \text{nH}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the resonant frequency

$$
f_{ring} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_g C_{tip}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{50 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12}}}
$$

Compute the radicand:

$$
L_g C_{tip} = 5 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{s}^{2}
$$

$$
\sqrt{L_g C_{tip}} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-19}} = 7.07 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{s}
$$

$$
f_{ring} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 7.07 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{1}{4.44 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 225\ \text{MHz}
$$

### Step 3: Determine if the ring is in-band

If the DUT has $\tau_r = 500$ ps, the knee frequency is:

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.5}{500 \times 10^{-12}} = 1\ \text{GHz}
$$

Since $f_{ring} = 225$ MHz is **well below** the knee frequency of 1 GHz, the ringing will appear as a clean overshoot on the oscilloscope — it is a **fixture artifact, not a real signal behavior**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently interpret observed ringing on an oscilloscope as a real signal-integrity problem (e.g., a reflection from a stub) when it is in fact **probe ground-lead resonance**. Always verify the ring frequency matches the formula $f_{ring} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_g C_{tip}}}$ before blaming the DUT.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Correct Fixture for a Given Edge Rate

| Edge Rate $\tau_r$ | Knee Frequency $f_{knee}$ | Recommended Fixture | Expected $C_{load}$ | Expected $L_{g,\text{eff}}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $> 5$ ns | $< 100$ MHz | Standard 10:1 passive + ground spring | 10–15 pF | 5–10 nH |
| $1$–$5$ ns | $100$–$500$ MHz | 10:1 passive + short ground blade | 8–12 pF | 2–5 nH |
| $200$ ps – $1$ ns | $0.5$–$2.5$ GHz | Active FET probe, 1–4 GHz | 0.4–0.8 pF | 0.2–0.5 nH |
| $50$ – $200$ ps | $2.5$–$10$ GHz | Coaxial pigtail + 50 Ω termination | 0.1–0.3 pF | 0.05 nH |
| $< 50$ ps | $> 10$ GHz | $2.92$ mm / $2.40$ mm launcher + TRL cal | $< 0.05$ pF | $< 0.02$ nH |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Probing-Fixture Chain

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end signal flow** from the Device Under Test (DUT) through the probing fixture, the interconnect, the measurement instrument, and back to the engineer via de-embedding. This is the conceptual model the KTU examiner expects a student to draw when describing a complete high-speed measurement setup.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["DUT ACTIVE OUTPUT STAGE 25 OHM DRIVE"]
    nodeB["TRANSMISSION LINE ON PCB 50 OHM MICROSTRIP L CRIT"]
    nodeC["PROBE CONTACT TIP COPLANAR GROUND CLUSTER"]
    nodeD["FIXTURE BODY 50 OHM CONTROLLED IMPEDANCE"]
    nodeE["CONNECTOR LAUNCHER SMA 2.92MM 2.40MM"]
    nodeF["COAXIAL CABLE LOSSLY FLEXIBLE RG405 OR SEMI RIGID"]
    nodeG["MEASUREMENT INSTRUMENT 50 OHM OSCILLOSCOPE OR VNA"]
    nodeH["CALIBRATION KIT SOLT OR TRL STANDARDS"]
    nodeI["DE EMBEDDING ALGORITHM INVERSE H OF FIXTURE"]
    nodeJ["TRUE DUT WAVEFORM RECOVERED"]
    nodeK["KTU VALUATION INSPECTOR COMPARES RECOVERED VS ACTUAL"]

    nodeA -- edge signal --> nodeB
    nodeB -- propagates --> nodeC
    nodeC -- capacitive coupled --> nodeD
    nodeD -- matched transfer --> nodeE
    nodeE -- coaxial mode --> nodeF
    nodeF -- delivered --> nodeG
    nodeG -- raw measured waveform --> nodeK
    nodeH -- reference data --> nodeI
    nodeG -- measured S parameters --> nodeI
    nodeD -- fixture S parameters --> nodeI
    nodeI -- corrected S parameters --> nodeJ
    nodeJ -- displays --> nodeK
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left-to-right chain** (nodes A → G) is the *forward measurement path*. Each transition between blocks is a potential **discontinuity** where reflections and loss are introduced.
- The **bottom-up chain** (nodes H, I, J) is the *post-processing chain* that mathematically removes the fixture's effect.
- The **KTU Valuation Inspector** (node K) compares the recovered waveform against the true DUT waveform — full marks are awarded only when the recovered and actual waveforms agree within the KTU tolerance band ($\pm 5\%$ amplitude, $\pm 3\%$ timing).

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Selecting a Special Probing Fixture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1["START SPECIFY EDGE RISE TIME TAU R"]
    nodeQ2{"IS TAU R GREATER THAN 1 NS"}
    nodeQ3["USE STANDARD 10 BY 1 PASSIVE PROBE WITH GROUND SPRING"]
    nodeQ4{"IS TAU R BETWEEN 200 PS AND 1 NS"}
    nodeQ5["USE ACTIVE FET PROBE 1 TO 4 GHZ BANDWIDTH"]
    nodeQ6{"IS TAU R BETWEEN 50 AND 200 PS"}
    nodeQ7["USE COAXIAL PIGTAIL LAUNCHER WITH 50 OHM TERMINATION"]
    nodeQ8{"IS TAU R LESS THAN 50 PS"}
    nodeQ9["USE 2.92 MM OR 2.40 MM LAUNCHER PERFORM TRL CALIBRATION ON COUPON"]
    nodeQ10["APPLY DE EMBEDDING ALGORITHM IN POST PROCESSING"]
    nodeQ11["VERIFY FIXTURE BANDWIDTH S21 GREATER THAN MINUS 3 DB AT 3RD HARMONIC"]

    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeQ4
    nodeQ4 -- YES --> nodeQ5
    nodeQ4 -- NO --> nodeQ6
    nodeQ6 -- YES --> nodeQ7
    nodeQ6 -- NO --> nodeQ8
    nodeQ8 -- YES --> nodeQ9
    nodeQ7 --> nodeQ10
    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ10
    nodeQ9 --> nodeQ10
    nodeQ10 --> nodeQ11
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the flowchart during the KTU exam:** Start at `START`, follow the *YES/NO* branches based on the value of $\tau_r$ given in the question, and end at the recommended fixture. Always show the **post-processing / de-embedding step** to score the 2–3 marks allocated to *best-practice workflow* in a 14-mark design problem.
