# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Telegrapher's Equations (from the Lumped Slice)

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider an infinitesimal segment $dx$ at position $x$ on the transmission line. Let the voltage across the line at position $x$ be $V(x,t)$ and at position $x + dx$ be $V(x+dx,t)$. The current entering the segment at $x$ is $I(x,t)$ and leaving at $x+dx$ is $I(x+dx,t)$.

The segment contains:

- A series impedance per unit length $Z = R + j\omega L$ (in phasor form) or $R + L\,\partial/\partial t$ (in time form).
- A shunt admittance per unit length $Y = G + j\omega C$ or $G + C\,\partial/\partial t$.

### 3.1.2 Applying KVL (Phasor Form)

The voltage drop across the series impedance $Z\,dx$ equals the difference between the input and output voltages:

$$V(x) - V(x+dx) = Z\,dx \cdot I(x)$$

Divide both sides by $dx$:

$$\frac{V(x+dx) - V(x)}{dx} = -Z\,I(x)$$

Take the limit as $dx \to 0$:

$$\frac{dV(x)}{dx} = -Z\,I(x) = -(R + j\omega L)\,I(x)$$

In time domain:

$$-\frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial x} = R\,I(x,t) + L\,\frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(Telegrapher's Eqn. 1)}$$

### 3.1.3 Applying KCL (Phasor Form)

The current leaving the node through the shunt admittance $Y\,dx$ equals the difference between the input and output currents:

$$I(x) - I(x+dx) = Y\,dx \cdot V(x+dx)$$

Dividing by $dx$ and taking the limit $dx \to 0$:

$$\frac{dI(x)}{dx} = -Y\,V(x) = -(G + j\omega C)\,V(x)$$

In time domain:

$$-\frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial x} = G\,V(x,t) + C\,\frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(Telegrapher's Eqn. 2)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Wave Equation for Voltage

### 3.2.1 Differentiate Telegrapher's Eqn. 1 with respect to $x$

$$\frac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} = -Z\,\frac{dI}{dx}$$

### 3.2.2 Substitute $\dfrac{dI}{dx} = -Y\,V$ from Eqn. 2

$$\frac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} = -Z \cdot (-Y\,V) = ZY\,V = \gamma^{2}\,V$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} - \gamma^{2} V = 0 \quad \text{...(Wave Equation in V)}$$

where

$$\gamma = \sqrt{ZY} = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The general solution of the ODE $\dfrac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} - \gamma^{2} V = 0$ is a linear combination of $e^{+\gamma x}$ and $e^{-\gamma x}$, which corresponds to the forward and backward waves described in §2.3.

## 3.3 Derivation of Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$

### 3.3.1 Voltage and Current Solutions

Let the general solution be:

$$V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x} + V_0^{-} e^{+\gamma x}$$

From Telegrapher's Eqn. 1:

$$I(x) = -\frac{1}{Z}\,\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{1}{Z}\left(-\gamma V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x} + \gamma V_0^{-} e^{+\gamma x}\right)$$

$$I(x) = \frac{\gamma}{Z}\left(V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x} - V_0^{-} e^{+\gamma x}\right)$$

### 3.3.2 Define the Characteristic Impedance

The characteristic impedance is defined as the ratio $V_0^{+}/I_0^{+}$ for the forward wave alone:

$$Z_0 = \frac{V_0^{+}}{V_0^{+}/Z_0} = \frac{Z}{\gamma} = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{ZY}} = \sqrt{\frac{Z}{Y}}$$

Substituting $Z = R + j\omega L$ and $Y = G + j\omega C$:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

### 3.3.3 Special Case — Lossless Line

For the lossless case, $R = 0$ and $G = 0$:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

and

$$\gamma = \sqrt{(j\omega L)(j\omega C)} = j\omega\sqrt{LC} = j\beta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 0,\ \beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$$

The phase velocity follows as:

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Infinite-Line Voltage Profile

Starting from the general solution:

$$V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x} + V_0^{-} e^{+\gamma x}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the physical requirement that the voltage must remain finite (bounded) as $x \to \infty$.

**Step 2:** Examine the term $V_0^{-} e^{+\gamma x}$. For a passive line, the real part of $\gamma$ is non-negative ($\alpha \ge 0$). Thus:

$$\left\vert e^{+\gamma x} \right\vert = e^{+\alpha x} \to \infty \quad \text{as} \quad x \to \infty$$

unless $V_0^{-} = 0$.

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$V_0^{-} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x}$$

Similarly, the current is:

$$I(x) = \frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0}\,e^{-\gamma x}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the input impedance at any point $x = \ell$:

$$Z_{in}(\ell) = \frac{V(\ell)}{I(\ell)} = \frac{V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma \ell}}{V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma \ell}/Z_0} = Z_0$$

This proves that the input impedance of an infinite line is **identically $Z_0$**, independent of position.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Lossless Infinite Line)

A PCB microstrip line has per-unit-length parameters:

$$L = 250\ \text{nH/m}, \quad C = 100\ \text{pF/m}, \quad R \approx 0, \quad G \approx 0$$

**Step 1 — Compute $Z_0$:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{250 \times 10^{-9}}{100 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute phase velocity at $f = 1$ GHz ($\omega = 2\pi \times 10^9$ rad/s):**

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(250 \times 10^{-9})(100 \times 10^{-12})}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-17}}}$$

$$v_p = \frac{1}{1.581 \times 10^{-8.5}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 2 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$$

(This is $\approx \dfrac{2}{3}c$, typical for FR-4 PCB microstrip.)

**Step 3 — Wavelength at 1 GHz:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{2 \times 10^{8}}{10^{9}} = 0.2\ \text{m} = 20\ \text{cm}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage at $x = 1$ m, given source $V_0^{+} = 5$ V:**

$$V(1\,\text{m}) = 5 \cdot e^{-j\beta (1)} = 5 \cdot e^{-j(2\pi \times 10^9)\sqrt{LC}\,(1)}$$

Compute $\beta$:

$$\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC} = (2\pi \times 10^{9})\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-17}} = (2\pi \times 10^{9})(5 \times 10^{-9}) = 10\pi\ \text{rad/m}$$

Thus at $x = 1$ m:

$$V(1\,\text{m}) = 5 \cdot e^{-j10\pi} = 5 \cdot (\cos 10\pi + j\sin 10\pi) = 5 \cdot (1 + j0) = 5\ \text{V}$$

(Phase wraps by 5 full cycles — back to the original phase. Magnitude preserved since the line is lossless.)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show the substitution step explicitly. Examiners award partial credit for the substitution even if the final numerical value is slightly off. Mention the units at every step.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Distributed Parameter Lumped Model

This diagram captures the equivalent circuit of a transmission line as an **infinite cascade of infinitesimal RLGC slices**, which is the foundation for deriving the infinite-line behavior.

```mermaid
graph LR
    source[Voltage Source Vg] --> r1[Internal Resistance Rg]
    r1 --> node1[Node x equals 0]
    node1 --> rSlice1[Series R dx]
    rSlice1 --> lSlice1[Series L dx]
    lSlice1 --> node2[Node x equals dx]
    node2 --> rSlice2[Series R dx]
    rSlice2 --> lSlice2[Series L dx]
    lSlice2 --> node3[Node x equals 2dx]
    node3 --> rSlice3[Series R dx]
    rSlice3 --> lSlice3[Series L dx]
    lSlice3 --> nodeN[Node x equals N dx]
    nodeN --> inf[Line Extends to Infinity]
    node2 --> cSlice1[Shunt C dx]
    cSlice1 --> gSlice1[Shunt G dx]
    gSlice1 --> gnd1[Return Path Ground]
    node3 --> cSlice2[Shunt C dx]
    cSlice2 --> gSlice2[Shunt G dx]
    gSlice2 --> gnd2[Return Path Ground]
    nodeN --> cSliceN[Shunt C dx]
    cSliceN --> gSliceN[Shunt G dx]
    gSliceN --> gndN[Return Path Ground]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `inf` node represents the conceptual infinite extension of the line. The signal propagates left-to-right; because the line never ends, there is no reflected wave traveling right-to-left.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Analytical Flow for Infinite-Line Voltage

This diagram maps the **logical/analytical flow** of how a sinusoidal source produces the infinite-line voltage solution.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sinusoidal Source Vg] --> B[Apply KVL to Lumped Slice dx]
    B --> C[Telegrapher Equation 1 dV by dx equals negative Z times I]
    C --> D[Apply KCL to Same Slice]
    D --> E[Telegrapher Equation 2 dI by dx equals negative Y times V]
    E --> F[Eliminate I by Cross Differentiation]
    F --> G[Wave Equation d2V by dx2 equals gamma squared V]
    G --> H[Define Gamma equals Square Root of ZY]
    H --> I[General Solution V of x equals V0 plus e power negative gamma x plus V0 minus e power positive gamma x]
    I --> J[Apply Infinite Line Boundary Condition]
    J --> K[Require V of x Bounded as x goes to Infinity]
    K --> L[Conclude V0 minus equals 0]
    L --> M[Final Form V of x equals V0 plus e power negative gamma x]
    M --> N[Compute Input Impedance Zin equals V by I equals Z0]
    N --> O[Zin is Position Independent and equals Z0]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical derivation flow** that examiners expect KTU students to reproduce in the 14-mark question. Memorize the sequence: KVL → KCL → Eliminate → Wave equation → Solve → Apply boundary condition → Conclude.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Interpretation of Forward Wave on Infinite Line

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Source at x equals 0] -->|V0 plus| start[Wavefront at x equals 0]
    start -->|travels| mid[Wavefront at x equals x1]
    mid -->|travels| far[Wavefront at x equals x2]
    far -->|travels| end[Wavefront at x approaches Infinity]
    mid -.->|"Attenuation factor e power negative alpha x1"| obs1[Observed Voltage V x1]
    far -.->|"Attenuation factor e power negative alpha x2"| obs2[Observed Voltage V x2]
    end -.->|"Zero reflection since line never ends"| refl[No Backward Wave Exists]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The amplitude decays as $e^{-\alpha x}$ (lossy) or stays constant (lossless), and the phase rotates as $e^{-j\beta x}$. The infinite line ensures the wave is "consumed" by the line itself and never returns to the source.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: Define an infinite uniform transmission line. Why is the input impedance of an infinite line always equal to its characteristic impedance, regardless of the position from which it is measured?**

**Model Answer:**

An infinite uniform transmission line is a transmission line that extends to infinity in one direction, with constant per-unit-length parameters ($R$, $L$, $G$, $C$) along its entire length. The term "uniform" implies no geometric or material discontinuities exist.

**Reason for $Z_{in} = Z_0$:**

For a sinusoidal steady-state excitation, the general voltage solution is:

$$V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x} + V_0^{-} e^{+\gamma x}$$

For an infinite line, the requirement that $V(x)$ remains finite as $x \to \infty$ forces the backward-wave coefficient to vanish: $V_0^{-} = 0$. Therefore:

$$V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x}, \quad I(x) = \frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma x}$$

The input impedance at any point $x = \ell$ is:

$$Z_{in}(\ell) = \frac{V(\ell)}{I(\ell)} = \frac{V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma \ell}}{V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma \ell}/Z_0} = Z_0$$

The exponential factors cancel exactly, leaving the position-independent characteristic impedance $Z_0$. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: For a lossless transmission line with $L = 200\ \text{nH/m}$ and $C = 80\ \text{pF/m}$, calculate the characteristic impedance and the phase velocity.**

**Model Answer:**

For a lossless line ($R = G = 0$):

**Step 1 — Characteristic impedance:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^{-9}}{80 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Phase velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(200 \times 10^{-9})(80 \times 10^{-12})}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{-17}}}$$

$$v_p = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-8.5}} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$$

Result: $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ and $v_p \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: Starting from the distributed parameter model of a uniform transmission line, derive the Telegrapher's equations. Show that for an infinite line, the voltage and current solutions reduce to a single forward-traveling wave, and derive the expression for the characteristic impedance. Compute $Z_0$ and $\gamma$ for a line with $R = 0.5\ \Omega/\text{m}$, $L = 200\ \text{nH/m}$, $G = 0.1\ \text{mS/m}$, $C = 100\ \text{pF/m}$ at $f = 1$ GHz.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Telegrapher's Equations and Infinite-Line Solutions (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Lumped slice model.**

Consider a small segment $dx$ of the line. The segment has:

- Series impedance $Z\,dx = (R + j\omega L)\,dx$
- Shunt admittance $Y\,dx = (G + j\omega C)\,dx$

**Step 2: Apply KVL.**

$$V(x) - V(x+dx) = Z\,dx \cdot I(x)$$

$$\Rightarrow -\frac{dV}{dx} = Z\,I = (R + j\omega L)\,I \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 3: Apply KCL.**

$$I(x) - I(x+dx) = Y\,dx \cdot V(x+dx)$$

$$\Rightarrow -\frac{dI}{dx} = Y\,V = (G + j\omega C)\,V \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 4: Eliminate $I$ to get the wave equation.**

Differentiate (1) w.r.t. $x$:

$$-\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = Z\,\frac{dI}{dx} = Z \cdot (-Y V) = -ZY\,V$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = \gamma^{2} V, \quad \text{where } \gamma = \sqrt{ZY} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 5: General solution.**

$$V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x} + V_0^{-} e^{+\gamma x}$$

**Step 6: Apply the infinite-line boundary condition.**

For $V(x)$ to remain bounded as $x \to \infty$, we require $V_0^{-} = 0$. Hence:

$$V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x}$$

From (1), the current is:

$$I(x) = -\frac{1}{Z}\,\frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{\gamma V_0^{+}}{Z}\,e^{-\gamma x} = \frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0}\,e^{-\gamma x}$$

**Step 7: Derive characteristic impedance.**

$$Z_0 = \frac{V(x)}{I(x)} = \frac{Z}{\gamma} = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{ZY}} = \sqrt{\frac{Z}{Y}} = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

This completes the derivation. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**

$$R = 0.5\ \Omega/\text{m}, \quad L = 200\ \text{nH/m}, \quad G = 0.1\ \text{mS/m}, \quad C = 100\ \text{pF/m}, \quad f = 1\ \text{GHz}$$

**Step 1: Compute $\omega L$ and $\omega C$.**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 10^{9}\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega L = 2\pi \times 10^{9} \times 200 \times 10^{-9} = 400\pi \approx 1256.6\ \Omega/\text{m}$$

$$\omega C = 2\pi \times 10^{9} \times 100 \times 10^{-12} = 2\pi \times 10^{-1} \approx 0.6283\ \text{S/m}$$

**Step 2: Compute $Z$ and $Y$.**

$$Z = R + j\omega L = 0.5 + j1256.6\ \Omega/\text{m}$$

$$Y = G + j\omega C = 0.0001 + j0.6283\ \text{S/m}$$

**Step 3: Compute $Z_0$.**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{0.5 + j1256.6}{0.0001 + j0.6283}}$$

Numerator magnitude: $\sqrt{0.5^2 + 1256.6^2} \approx 1256.6$
Denominator magnitude: $\sqrt{0.0001^2 + 0.6283^2} \approx 0.6283$

$$\vert Z_0 \vert \approx \sqrt{\frac{1256.6}{0.6283}} \approx \sqrt{2000} \approx 44.7\ \Omega$$

Argument of numerator: $\arctan(1256.6/0.5) \approx 89.977°$
Argument of denominator: $\arctan(0.6283/0.0001) \approx 89.991°$
Half-difference: $\approx -0.007° \approx 0$

$$Z_0 \approx 44.7\ \Omega \quad \text{(essentially resistive at 1 GHz)}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\gamma$.**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{ZY} = \sqrt{(0.5 + j1256.6)(0.0001 + j0.6283)}$$

Product: 

$$(0.5)(0.0001) + (0.5)(j0.6283) + (j1256.6)(0.0001) + (j1256.6)(j0.6283)$$

$$= 0.00005 + j0.31415 + j0.12566 - 789.55$$

$$= -789.55 + j0.4398$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{-789.55 + j0.4398} \approx j\sqrt{789.55} \approx j28.10\ \text{rad/m}$$

So $\alpha \approx 0.008\ \text{Np/m}$ and $\beta \approx 28.10\ \text{rad/m}$.

**Step 5: Physical interpretation.**

The line is nearly lossless at 1 GHz (since $\omega L \gg R$ and $\omega C \gg G$). The attenuation per meter is extremely small, and the line behaves essentially as a lossless line with $Z_0 \approx \sqrt{L/C} = 50\ \Omega$. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a) Derive the propagation constant $\gamma$ and characteristic impedance $Z_0$ for a general (lossy) uniform transmission line. What simplifications arise in the lossless case? (7 marks)**

**(b) For an infinite lossless line, show that the voltage profile $V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta x}$ and compute the wavelength and phase velocity for $L = 396\ \text{nH/m}$, $C = 99\ \text{pF/m}$ at $f = 500$ MHz. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Propagation Constant and Characteristic Impedance (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define the per-unit-length series impedance and shunt admittance.**

$$Z = R + j\omega L, \quad Y = G + j\omega C$$

**Step 2: Derive $\gamma$.**

The wave equation (derived in Section 3.2) is $\dfrac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = ZY\,V$. Comparing with $\dfrac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = \gamma^{2} V$:

$$\boxed{\gamma = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)} = \alpha + j\beta}$$

**Step 3: Derive $Z_0$.**

From the current expression $I(x) = \dfrac{\gamma}{Z}\,V(x)$, the ratio $V/I$ for the forward wave gives:

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}}$$

**Step 4: Lossless case ($R = 0$, $G = 0$).**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{(j\omega L)(j\omega C)} = j\omega\sqrt{LC} = j\beta$$

$$\Rightarrow \alpha = 0, \quad \beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$$

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \quad \text{(purely real)}$$

The lossless line has **zero attenuation** and a **real characteristic impedance**. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Voltage Profile and Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Voltage profile on infinite lossless line.**

With $\gamma = j\beta$ and $V_0^{-} = 0$:

$$V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta x}$$

This is a pure phase-shifted sinusoid — the magnitude is constant (no attenuation), and the phase rotates as $-j\beta x$ per unit length.

**Step 2: Compute $\beta$ at $f = 500$ MHz.**

$$\omega = 2\pi \times 500 \times 10^{6} = \pi \times 10^{9}\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC} = \pi \times 10^{9} \cdot \sqrt{(396 \times 10^{-9})(99 \times 10^{-12})}$$

$$LC = 396 \times 99 \times 10^{-21} = 39204 \times 10^{-21} = 3.9204 \times 10^{-17}$$

$$\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{3.9204 \times 10^{-17}} = 6.262 \times 10^{-8.5} \approx 1.976 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s/m}$$

$$\beta = \pi \times 10^{9} \times 1.976 \times 10^{-8} = \pi \times 19.76 \approx 62.07\ \text{rad/m}$$

**Step 3: Compute phase velocity.**

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{1.976 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 5.06 \times 10^{7}\ \text{m/s}$$

This is about $5.06 \times 10^{7}$ m/s, i.e., roughly $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of the speed of light (typical for some PCB materials).

**Step 4: Compute wavelength.**

$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi}{62.07} \approx 0.1012\ \text{m} = 10.12\ \text{cm}$$

**Step 5: Compute $Z_0$.**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{396 \times 10^{-9}}{99 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25\ \Omega$$

Summary: $Z_0 \approx 63.25\ \Omega$, $v_p \approx 5.06 \times 10^{7}$ m/s, $\lambda \approx 10.12$ cm, $\beta \approx 62.07$ rad/m. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes & Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the infinite-line boundary condition** ($V_0^{-} = 0$). Many students keep both exponentials in the final answer, which loses 2-3 marks immediately. Always state explicitly *why* the backward wave vanishes for an infinite line.
>
> 2. **Unit errors in per-unit-length parameters.** $L$ is in **H/m**, not H. $C$ is in **F/m**, not F. A single missing "/m" can cascade into a wrong $Z_0$ and lose marks.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\beta$.** $\alpha$ is the **real** part of $\gamma$ (attenuation, Np/m). $\beta$ is the **imaginary** part (phase, rad/m). State which is which at every step.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to take the square root of $Z$ by $Y$** in $Z_0 = \sqrt{Z/Y}$. A common typo: $Z_0 = Z/Y$ (no square root) — instant mark loss.
>
> 5. **Not justifying $V_0^{-} = 0$ from the boundedness condition.** Examiners expect: "Since $\alpha \ge 0$ for a passive line, $e^{+\gamma x}$ diverges as $x \to \infty$, requiring $V_0^{-} = 0$." A bald statement "$V_0^{-} = 0$" without justification gets partial credit only.
>
> 6. **Numerical computation slips in $\omega L$:** forgetting $2\pi$ when going from $f$ to $\omega$. Verify units: $\omega$ is in rad/s, $L$ in H/m, product in $\Omega$/m.
>
> 7. **Reporting $Z_0$ without specifying it is position-independent** on the infinite line. The whole point of the question is to highlight this property — always state it explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **infinite uniform transmission line** is the theoretical limit where the line extends without end, with constant per-unit-length $R$, $L$, $G$, $C$.
- The **lumped slice model** places a series $R\,dx$ and $L\,dx$ plus a shunt $G\,dx$ and $C\,dx$ at every infinitesimal segment.
- The **Telegrapher's Equations** are the two coupled PDEs (or ODEs in phasor form) governing $V(x,t)$ and $I(x,t)$:

  $$-\frac{dV}{dx} = (R + j\omega L)\,I, \quad -\frac{dI}{dx} = (G + j\omega C)\,V$$

- The **wave equation** is $\dfrac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = \gamma^{2} V$, with general solution $V(x) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma x} + V_0^{-} e^{+\gamma x}$.
- The **infinite-line boundary condition** ($V_0^{-} = 0$) is the central result — it eliminates the reflected wave.
- The **propagation constant** is $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$.
- The **characteristic impedance** is $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$.
- For **lossless** lines ($R = G = 0$): $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (real), $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$, $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$.
- **Input impedance of an infinite line equals $Z_0$ at every point** — this is the most important takeaway.
- Real-world significance: PCB trace matching, high-speed digital interconnect design, RF feed lines, on-chip global wires.
- Master the **derivation flow**: KVL → KCL → Eliminate $I$ → Wave equation → Solve → Boundary condition → Conclude.
- For KTU exams, **show the substitution steps** numerically; examiners reward working, not just answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideal Distortion Less Lossless Transmission Line, RC Transmission Line, Skin Effect, Proximity Effect, Dielectric Loss
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ideal Distortion Less Lossless Transmission Line, RC Transmission Line, Skin Effect, Proximity Effect, Dielectric Loss

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ideal Transmission Line Recap

A **transmission line** is a distributed parameter network characterized by four primary constants per unit length:

- **R** = Series resistance (Ω/m) — conductor loss
- **L** = Series inductance (H/m) — magnetic field storage
- **G** = Shunt conductance (S/m) — dielectric loss
- **C** = Shunt capacitance (F/m) — electric field storage

The Telegrapher's equations govern the voltage and current propagation:

$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -\left(R + j\omega L\right) I
$$

$$
\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = -\left(G + j\omega C\right) V
$$

Solving these yields the **propagation constant** $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ and **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In high-speed digital design, a transmission line is any interconnect whose physical length is comparable to (or greater than) $\lambda/10$ of the highest frequency component of the signal. Below this threshold, lumped-element analysis suffices.

## 1.2 The Ideal Distortionless Transmission Line

### Formal Definition

An **ideal distortionless transmission line** is one in which the signal waveform propagates without any change in shape, experiencing only a uniform (frequency-independent) attenuation and a linear phase shift. Mathematically, the attenuation constant $\alpha$ must be **independent of frequency** and the phase constant $\beta$ must be a **linear function of $\omega$**.

The classical **Heaviside Condition** defines when a line becomes distortionless:

$$
RC = LG
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When the Heaviside condition is satisfied, the line is distortionless. The signal shape is preserved (no dispersion), even though amplitude may decrease. This is the theoretical ideal for pulse transmission in telegraphy and broadband digital interconnects.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Stadium Wave" Crowd

Imagine spectators in a stadium performing "the wave." Now suppose:
- **People stand up slowly (low-frequency content)** — the wave moves through the crowd naturally.
- **People stand up very rapidly (high-frequency content)** — if the wave's speed depended on how fast people stand, the wavefront would smear out and distort.

A **distortionless line** is like a perfectly trained crowd where every person stands up at exactly the same *rate-proportional delay*, so the wave shape remains identical regardless of how fast the impulse occurs. The line merely delays and shrinks the wave uniformly — it does not reshape it.

## 1.3 The Ideal Lossless Transmission Line

A **lossless line** is the special case where $R = 0$ and $G = 0$ — no conductor loss, no dielectric loss. This gives:

- $\alpha = 0$ (no attenuation, ever)
- $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ (linear in $\omega$)
- $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (purely real and frequency-independent)

> [!NOTE]
> A lossless line is automatically **distortionless** because $\alpha = 0$ is trivially constant and $\beta$ is linear in $\omega$. This is why lossless models are the *first-order approximation* in nearly all high-speed digital signal integrity work.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Perfect Aquarium Hose

Think of a perfectly smooth, frictionless aquarium hose filled with ideal water. A water pressure pulse sent at one end emerges at the other end with the **exact same shape and amplitude** — only delayed in time. There is no energy lost to friction, no dispersion of the pulse. This is a lossless transmission line.

## 1.4 The RC Transmission Line

A **RC transmission line** describes interconnects where inductance effects are negligible ($L \to 0$) compared to the resistive–capacitive behavior. This model is highly relevant for **on-chip interconnects**, **heavily resistive PCB traces**, and certain **bond-wire geometries** at moderate-to-high frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On silicon ICs, the resistance per unit length of metal traces is significant (often 10–100 Ω/cm for narrow lines), and the capacitance per unit length is also high due to thin dielectrics. This makes the RC line model the dominant analytical tool for chip-level interconnect analysis.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Leaky Garden Hose

Imagine a very long, thin garden hose (high resistance to water flow) with many small holes along its length (distributed capacitance to ground). Water pressure entering one end does *not* arrive intact at the other — the pulse is smeared out, attenuated, and rounded. This "smearing" of sharp edges is exactly what an RC line does to a digital pulse, which is why RC delay is a critical metric in deep-submicron VLSI design.

## 1.5 Skin Effect

**Skin effect** is the phenomenon where alternating current (AC) in a conductor concentrates near the outer surface (skin) of the conductor rather than being uniformly distributed across its cross-section. This happens because the self-induced eddy currents oppose current flow in the interior.

The **skin depth** $\delta$ is defined as the depth at which the current density has decayed to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8%) of its value at the surface:

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> At **f = 1 GHz in copper**, the skin depth is only about **2.09 μm**. This means a typical PCB trace of 35 μm copper thickness behaves like a hollow tube at high frequencies — current effectively flows only in the top few microns.

## 1.6 Proximity Effect

The **proximity effect** is the phenomenon where the current distribution in one conductor is distorted by the magnetic field of an adjacent conductor carrying AC. Unlike skin effect (which is a self-induced phenomenon), proximity effect is **mutual** — it depends on the geometry and current direction of *neighboring* traces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In tightly coupled differential pairs or parallel bus lines, proximity effect can dominate over skin effect and increase the total AC resistance by **30% to 200%** depending on spacing. This is a major concern in high-density PCB and package design.

## 1.7 Dielectric Loss

**Dielectric loss** is the energy dissipated in the insulating (dielectric) material between conductors when subjected to an alternating electric field. It is caused by the finite conductivity and molecular polarization lag of real dielectric materials.

The loss is quantified by the **loss tangent**:

$$
\tan \delta_d = \frac{\sigma_d}{\omega \varepsilon}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> At **multi-GHz frequencies**, dielectric loss can become the **dominant loss mechanism** — exceeding conductor skin-effect loss. This is why low-loss PCB materials (e.g., Megtron 6, Rogers RO4350B) are essential for 25 Gbps+ SerDes channels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Transmission Line Theory Recap

For a line with primary constants $R, L, G, C$ per unit length, the propagation constant and characteristic impedance are:

$$
\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{\left(R + j\omega L\right)\left(G + j\omega C\right)}
$$

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}
$$

The **phase velocity** is $v_p = \omega/\beta$ and the **group velocity** is $v_g = d\omega/d\beta$.

## 2.2 The Lossless Line ($R = 0$, $G = 0$)

Substituting $R = G = 0$:

- $\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$ → $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$
- $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (real, frequency-independent)
- $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$
- No dispersion, no attenuation — *ideal propagation*

## 2.3 The Distortionless Line (Heaviside Condition)

The Heaviside condition $RC = LG$ ensures that the propagation constant simplifies to:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{RG}\left(1 + j\right) + j\omega\sqrt{LC}
$$

Wait — let's derive this more carefully. The full expression when $RC = LG$ is:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{RG} + j\omega\sqrt{LC}
$$

So:

- $\alpha = \sqrt{RG}$ (constant, frequency-independent)
- $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ (linear in $\omega$)
- $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C} = \sqrt{R/G}$ (real, frequency-independent)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A distortionless line has **constant amplitude attenuation** (independent of frequency) and **linear phase** (no group-delay dispersion). This is the engineering ideal — pulses retain their shape, just scaled and delayed.

## 2.4 The RC Transmission Line ($L \to 0$, $G \to 0$)

Setting $L = 0$ in the propagation constant:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega RC} = \sqrt{\omega RC} \cdot e^{j\pi/4} = \sqrt{\frac{\omega RC}{2}}\left(1 + j\right)
$$

Therefore:

- $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\omega RC/2}$ (both grow as $\sqrt{\omega}$)
- $Z_0 = \sqrt{R/(j\omega C)} = \frac{1}{1+j}\sqrt{R/(\omega C)}$
- Phase velocity: $v_p = \omega/\beta = \sqrt{2\omega/(RC)}$ (frequency-dependent!)

> [!WARNING]
> In an RC line, **$\alpha$ and $\beta$ are both proportional to $\sqrt{\omega}$**, which is *not* a linear relationship. This means **RC lines ARE dispersive and lossy** — they distort pulses. The classic step-response shows a slow, exponential rise governed by the **Elmore delay**: $\tau_d \approx 0.5 RC\ell^2$ for a line of length $\ell$.

### The Distributed RC Delay

For a distributed RC line of length $\ell$ with total resistance $R_{tot}$ and capacitance $C_{tot}$, the 50% propagation delay (Elmore delay) is:

$$
\tau_d = \frac{R_{tot} C_{tot}}{2} = \frac{R \ell \cdot C \ell}{2} = \frac{RC \ell^2}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **square-law dependence** on length that limits on-chip global interconnect performance. Doubling the wire length quadruples the delay! This is the fundamental scaling problem that drove the move from aluminum to copper interconnects and the introduction of repeaters.

## 2.5 Skin Effect — Detailed Mechanics

### Physical Origin

When AC flows in a conductor, the time-varying magnetic field inside the conductor induces eddy currents (per Faraday's law). By Lenz's law, these eddy currents **oppose** the main current in the interior and **reinforce** it at the surface. As frequency increases, this becomes more pronounced, and current is effectively excluded from the interior.

### Mathematical Development

For a round conductor of radius $r$ with $r \gg \delta$:

$$
R_{ac} = R_{dc} \cdot \frac{r}{2\delta}
$$

For a flat rectangular trace of width $w$ and thickness $t$ (when $t \gg \delta$):

$$
R_{ac,skin} = R_{dc} \cdot \frac{t}{2\delta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$, the AC resistance grows as $\sqrt{f}$. This is why broadband lossy channels (like backplane traces) exhibit a $\sqrt{f}$ loss profile characteristic.

## 2.6 Proximity Effect — Detailed Mechanics

### Physical Origin

When two conductors carry AC in close proximity, the magnetic field from one conductor induces eddy currents in the other. The current distribution in each conductor is then the superposition of the source current and the induced eddy currents:

- **Currents in the same direction (S-direction)**: Current concentrates on the **far sides** of both conductors.
- **Currents in opposite directions (O-direction, like differential pairs)**: Current concentrates on the **near sides** of the two conductors.

The O-direction coupling produces a **stronger proximity effect** and is responsible for the higher losses in tightly coupled differential pairs.

### Frequency Dependence

The proximity effect loss grows as $\sqrt{f}$ (similar to skin effect) but with a strong **geometry-dependent coefficient**. For two parallel round wires of radius $r$ with center-to-center spacing $d$ (in O-mode):

$$
\frac{R_{ac,prox}}{R_{dc}} \approx \frac{1}{1 - \left(\frac{2r}{d}\right)^2} \cdot \frac{d}{2\delta}
$$

For PCB stripline geometries, full expressions require numerical EM solvers (e.g., 2D/3D field solvers).

## 2.7 Dielectric Loss — Detailed Mechanics

### The Loss Tangent

Real dielectrics have a small but finite conductivity $\sigma_d$ and a complex permittivity:

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon' - j\varepsilon'' = \varepsilon'\left(1 - j\tan\delta_d\right)
$$

where $\tan\delta_d = \varepsilon''/\varepsilon' = \sigma_d/(\omega\varepsilon')$.

### Frequency-Dependent Loss

The shunt conductance per unit length is:

$$
G = \omega C \tan\delta_d
$$

This is **linear in frequency** (since $\sigma_d/(\omega\varepsilon) \cdot \omega C$ gives a constant when $\sigma_d$ is treated as material property, but for frequency-stable $\tan\delta_d$, $G \propto \omega$).

The attenuation due to dielectric loss is:

$$
\alpha_d = \frac{\omega}{2 c} \sqrt{\varepsilon_r} \cdot \tan\delta_d \quad \text{(Np/m)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dielectric loss grows **linearly with frequency**, while skin-effect loss grows as $\sqrt{f}$. Therefore, there is a **crossover frequency** above which dielectric loss dominates. For FR4 ($\tan\delta_d \approx 0.02$), this crossover is around 1–2 GHz for typical trace geometries. For low-loss materials like Rogers RO4350B ($\tan\delta_d \approx 0.003$), it shifts much higher.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Propagation constant | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$ | m$^{-1}$ | General line |
| Characteristic impedance | $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$ | Ω | General line |
| Lossless $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Ω | Purely real |
| Lossless $\beta$ | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ | rad/m | Linear in $\omega$ |
| Lossless $v_p$ | $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | m/s | = speed of signal |
| Heaviside condition | $RC = LG$ | — | Distortionless criterion |
| Distortionless $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \sqrt{RG}$ | Np/m | Frequency-independent |
| Distortionless $\beta$ | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ | rad/m | Linear in $\omega$ |
| RC line $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \sqrt{\omega RC/2}$ | Np/m | $\propto \sqrt{\omega}$ |
| RC line $\beta$ | $\beta = \sqrt{\omega RC/2}$ | rad/m | $\propto \sqrt{\omega}$ |
| RC Elmore delay | $\tau_d = RC\ell^2/2$ | s | 50% delay, distributed line |
| Skin depth | $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | m | Copper at 1 GHz: ≈ 2.09 μm |
| AC resistance (round) | $R_{ac} = R_{dc}\cdot r/(2\delta)$ | Ω | For $r \gg \delta$ |
| AC resistance (flat) | $R_{ac} = R_{dc}\cdot t/(2\delta)$ | Ω | For $t \gg \delta$ |
| Loss tangent | $\tan\delta_d = \sigma_d/(\omega\varepsilon)$ | — | Material property |
| Dielectric atten. | $\alpha_d = (\omega/c)\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\tan\delta_d$ | Np/m | Low-loss approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> In exam answers, **always state assumptions** (e.g., "assuming $r \gg \delta$", "assuming low-loss dielectric") before applying a formula. KTU examiners reward explicit reasoning.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lossless model**: Used for impedance-controlled routing of single-cycle signals below ~1 GHz; SI (signal integrity) simulation engines.
- **Distortionless model**: Historical relevance (loaded telephone lines used loading coils to approximate $RC = LG$); modern relevance in pulse-forming networks.
- **RC model**: Backbone of VLSI timing analysis tools (Synopsys PrimeTime, Cadence Tempus); determines clock tree delay.
- **Skin effect analysis**: Determines trace width requirements for power integrity and high-speed serial links.
- **Proximity effect analysis**: Critical for parallel bus design, memory bus routing, and high-current power distribution.
- **Dielectric loss analysis**: Material selection for high-frequency PCB, package substrate, and cable design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heaviside Condition for Distortionless Transmission

We start with the general propagation constant:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{\left(R + j\omega L\right)\left(G + j\omega C\right)}
$$

Expand the product:

$$
\gamma^2 = RG + j\omega RC + j\omega LG + (j\omega)^2 LC
$$

$$
\gamma^2 = (RG - \omega^2 LC) + j\omega(RC + LG)
$$

For the line to be **distortionless**, we require that the **real part of $\gamma^2$ be independent of $\omega$** and the **imaginary part be linear in $\omega$**. Let us set $RC = LG$ (Heaviside condition). Then:

$$
\gamma^2 = (RG - \omega^2 LC) + j\omega(2RC)
$$

But this is not yet in the form we want. Let us instead use the trick of writing:

$$
\gamma^2 = (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C) = RG\left(1 + \frac{j\omega L}{R}\right)\left(1 + \frac{j\omega C}{G}\right)
$$

Apply $LG = RC$ condition. Note that $L/R = C/G \equiv \tau$ (a time constant). Therefore:

$$
\gamma^2 = RG\left(1 + j\omega\tau\right)\left(1 + j\omega\tau\right) = RG(1 + j\omega\tau)^2
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{RG}\left(1 + j\omega\tau\right) = \sqrt{RG} + j\omega\sqrt{RG}\cdot\tau
$$

But $\sqrt{RG}\cdot\tau = \sqrt{RG}\cdot\sqrt{L/R}\cdot\sqrt{C/G} = \sqrt{LC}\cdot\sqrt{RG/G}\cdot\sqrt{G/R} = \sqrt{LC}$. Let us verify:

$$
\sqrt{RG}\cdot\sqrt{L/R} = \sqrt{GL} = \sqrt{LG}
$$

And $LG = RC$ gives $L = RC/G$, so $\sqrt{LG} = \sqrt{RC}$. So:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{RG} + j\omega\sqrt{LC}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\alpha = \sqrt{RG} \quad \text{(constant)}
$$

$$
\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC} \quad \text{(linear in }\omega\text{)}
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Characteristic Impedance Under Distortionless Condition

We have:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}
$$

Factor out $R$ and $G$:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R\left(1 + j\omega L/R\right)}{G\left(1 + j\omega C/G\right)}}
$$

Apply $L/R = C/G \equiv \tau$:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R}{G}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1 + j\omega\tau}{1 + j\omega\tau}} = \sqrt{\frac{R}{G}}
$$

This is purely real and **frequency-independent**. Also note that under $RC = LG$:

$$
\sqrt{R/G} = \sqrt{L/C}
$$

So the distortionless line has the same characteristic impedance as the lossless line with the same $L$ and $C$.

$\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: RC Line Propagation Constant

Set $L = 0$ in the general $\gamma$:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{R \cdot j\omega C} = \sqrt{j\omega RC}
$$

Use the identity $j = e^{j\pi/2}$, so $\sqrt{j} = e^{j\pi/4} = \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{\omega RC} \cdot e^{j\pi/4} = \sqrt{\omega RC} \cdot \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{\frac{\omega RC}{2}} + j\sqrt{\frac{\omega RC}{2}}
$$

So:

$$
\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\frac{\omega RC}{2}}
$$

The **phase velocity** is:

$$
v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{\omega}{\sqrt{\omega RC/2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2\omega}{RC}}
$$

This is **frequency-dependent** — confirming the dispersive (and hence distorting) nature of the RC line.

$\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation: Elmore Delay of a Distributed RC Line

Consider a uniform RC line of length $\ell$ with per-unit-length resistance $r$ and capacitance $c$, so total $R_{tot} = r\ell$ and $C_{tot} = c\ell$. We can model it as $N$ cascaded RC sections (each $\Delta x = \ell/N$ long), with $R_i = r\Delta x$ and $C_i = c\Delta x$, and apply the Elmore delay formula:

$$
\tau_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \sum_{k \le i} R_k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (c\Delta x)(i \cdot r\Delta x) = cr(\Delta x)^2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} i
$$

Using $\sum_{i=1}^{N} i = N(N+1)/2$ and $\Delta x = \ell/N$:

$$
\tau_D = cr \cdot \frac{\ell^2}{N^2} \cdot \frac{N(N+1)}{2} = \frac{cr\ell^2}{2} \cdot \frac{N+1}{N}
$$

As $N \to \infty$ (distributed limit):

$$
\tau_D = \frac{cr\ell^2}{2} = \frac{(r\ell)(c\ell)}{2} = \frac{R_{tot} C_{tot}}{2}
$$

This is the **Elmore delay** of a distributed RC line — a 50% step-response delay metric. The **quadratic dependence on $\ell$** is the central result: delay grows as length squared.

$\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Derivation: Skin Depth from Maxwell's Equations

Consider a semi-infinite conductor occupying $x > 0$ with a uniform tangential electric field $E_z$ applied at the surface. Inside the conductor, Maxwell's equations give:

$$
\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \mu\sigma \frac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}
$$

For a time-harmonic field $E_z(x) e^{j\omega t}$:

$$
\frac{d^2 E_z}{dx^2} = j\omega\mu\sigma \cdot E_z
$$

The solution that decays into the conductor is:

$$
E_z(x) = E_0 e^{-(1+j)x/\delta}
$$

where the **skin depth** is:

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu\sigma}}
$$

The current density follows the same spatial dependence, decaying to $1/e$ at depth $\delta$.

$\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Derivation: AC Resistance of a Round Conductor ($r \gg \delta$)

For a round wire of radius $r$ with uniform current density, the DC resistance is:

$$
R_{dc} = \frac{\ell}{\sigma \pi r^2}
$$

For AC at high frequency, current flows only in an annular ring of thickness $\delta$ near the surface (when $r \gg \delta$). The effective cross-sectional area is $A_{eff} \approx 2\pi r \delta$, so:

$$
R_{ac} \approx \frac{\ell}{\sigma \cdot 2\pi r \delta} = \frac{\ell}{\sigma \pi r^2} \cdot \frac{r}{2\delta} = R_{dc} \cdot \frac{r}{2\delta}
$$

Substituting $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}$:

$$
R_{ac} = R_{dc} \cdot \frac{r\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}}{2}
$$

So the AC resistance grows as $\sqrt{f}$ (for $r \gg \delta$).

$\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Numerical Example — KTU Standard Problem

**Problem**: A copper trace ($σ = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $μ = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m) is 35 μm thick and carries a 5 GHz signal.

**(a)** Calculate the skin depth at 5 GHz.

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot 5\times 10^9 \cdot 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot 5.8 \times 10^7}}
$$

Compute the denominator stepwise:

- $\pi \cdot 5 \times 10^9 = 1.571 \times 10^{10}$
- $4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.257 \times 10^{-6}$
- $5.8 \times 10^7$
- Product: $1.571 \times 10^{10} \cdot 1.257 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5.8 \times 10^7$

Step 1: $1.571 \times 10^{10} \cdot 1.257 \times 10^{-6} = 1.974 \times 10^{4}$

Step 2: $1.974 \times 10^{4} \cdot 5.8 \times 10^{7} = 1.145 \times 10^{12}$

Step 3: $\sqrt{1.145 \times 10^{12}} = 1.070 \times 10^{6}$

Therefore:

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{1.070 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.935 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

**(b)** Determine the ratio $R_{ac}/R_{dc}$ for this trace.

Since $t = 35 \,\mu\text{m} \gg \delta = 0.935 \,\mu\text{m}$:

$$
\frac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} = \frac{t}{2\delta} = \frac{35}{2 \cdot 0.935} = \frac{35}{1.87} \approx 18.7
$$

**Conclusion**: At 5 GHz, the AC resistance is **~18.7 times** the DC resistance! This is why high-speed channels have dramatic insertion loss and why wide, thin traces are used to push current capacity at high frequencies.

$\blacksquare$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an ideal distortionless transmission line. State the Heaviside condition. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer**:

An **ideal distortionless transmission line** is one in which a signal propagates without any change in waveform shape, undergoing only a uniform attenuation (constant with frequency) and a constant time delay (linear phase).

The **Heaviside condition** is:

$$
RC = LG
$$

When this condition is satisfied:
- The attenuation constant $\alpha = \sqrt{RG}$ is independent of frequency.
- The phase constant $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ is linear in $\omega$.
- The characteristic impedance $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ is real and frequency-independent.

Hence all frequency components of a signal travel with the same attenuation and same velocity, preserving the waveform shape.

**Tag**: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Set A]`

---

### Question 2: Define skin depth. Give its expression and significance in high-speed digital design. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer**:

The **skin depth** $\delta$ is the depth beneath the surface of a conductor at which the AC current density has decayed to $1/e$ (approximately 36.8%) of its value at the surface.

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

**Significance in high-speed digital design**:
1. At high frequencies, current is confined to a thin surface layer of thickness $\delta$, increasing the effective AC resistance.
2. The AC resistance grows as $\sqrt{f}$: $R_{ac} = R_{dc} \cdot r/(2\delta)$ for round conductors.
3. For copper at 1 GHz, $\delta \approx 2.09 \,\mu\text{m}$, much smaller than typical trace thicknesses — current effectively flows only near the surface.
4. This loss mechanism dominates insertion loss in PCB and package interconnects at multi-GHz data rates, directly limiting channel reach.

**Tag**: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**(a)** Derive the propagation constant and characteristic impedance of a **distortionless transmission line**, clearly stating the Heaviside condition. Show that under this condition, $\alpha$ is constant and $\beta$ is linear in $\omega$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For an RC transmission line ($L = 0$, $G = 0$), derive the expressions for $\alpha$, $\beta$, $Z_0$, and the phase velocity. Hence show that the RC line is dispersive. Comment on the step response and the Elmore delay. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a)** *Distortionless line derivation* — Refer to Section 3.1 and 3.2 above. The key steps are:

1. Write the general propagation constant: $\gamma = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$
2. Apply Heaviside condition $RC = LG$, which implies $L/R = C/G = \tau$
3. Factor: $\gamma^2 = RG(1+j\omega\tau)^2$
4. Take square root: $\gamma = \sqrt{RG}(1 + j\omega\tau) = \sqrt{RG} + j\omega\sqrt{RG}\tau$
5. Show $\sqrt{RG}\tau = \sqrt{LC}$, hence $\alpha = \sqrt{RG}$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$
6. Show $Z_0 = \sqrt{R/G} = \sqrt{L/C}$ (purely real)

**(b)** *RC line derivation* — Refer to Section 3.3 above.

1. Set $L = 0$: $\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega RC} = \sqrt{\omega RC/\sqrt{2}} \cdot (1+j)$
2. Therefore $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\omega RC/2}$
3. $Z_0 = \sqrt{R/(j\omega C)} = (1-j)\sqrt{R/(2\omega C)}$
4. $v_p = \omega/\beta = \sqrt{2\omega/(RC)}$ — **frequency-dependent** → dispersive
5. Elmore delay: $\tau_D = R_{tot}C_{tot}/2 = RC\ell^2/2$
6. Step response: exponential rise with time constant $\sim \tau_D$; sharp edges are rounded off, limiting bandwidth

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the **skin effect** in conductors. Derive the expression for skin depth and the AC resistance of a round conductor when $r \gg \delta$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **proximity effect** in parallel conductors. How does it differ from skin effect? Discuss its impact on differential pair and bus line design. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a)** *Skin Effect Derivation* — Refer to Section 3.5 and 3.6 above.

1. **Physical origin**: Time-varying magnetic field inside a conductor induces eddy currents that oppose the main current in the interior and reinforce it at the surface.
2. **Mathematical development**: From Maxwell's equation in a conductor: $\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \mu\sigma \partial \mathbf{E}/\partial t$
3. For time-harmonic field: $d^2E_z/dx^2 = j\omega\mu\sigma E_z$
4. Solution: $E_z(x) = E_0 e^{-(1+j)x/\delta}$ with $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu\sigma}$
5. **AC resistance for round conductor** ($r \gg \delta$): Current flows in annular ring of thickness $\delta$ near surface; effective area = $2\pi r\delta$.
6. $R_{ac} = \ell/(\sigma \cdot 2\pi r\delta) = R_{dc} \cdot r/(2\delta)$
7. Since $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$, $R_{ac} \propto \sqrt{f}$.

**(b)** *Proximity Effect* — Refer to Section 2.6 above.

1. **Definition**: Distortion of current distribution in one conductor due to the magnetic field of an adjacent AC-carrying conductor.
2. **Difference from skin effect**: Skin effect is *self-induced* (one conductor); proximity effect is *mutual* (depends on neighbors).
3. **Modes**:
   - Same-direction currents: current on far sides (weaker loss).
   - Opposite-direction currents: current on near sides (stronger loss, critical for differential pairs).
4. **Impact on design**:
   - Differential pairs: tight coupling increases loss; loose coupling preferred for low loss but worse common-mode rejection.
   - Bus lines: parallel traces in same direction accumulate proximity losses; spacing must be increased or trace width reduced.
5. **Mitigation**: increase conductor spacing, use lower-loss materials, reduce current density, use ground shielding.

**Tag for Q B**: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Set A]`

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing distortionless with lossless**: Students often write "lossless lines are distortionless, hence lossless = distortionless." This is true (lossless is a special case of distortionless), but the **reverse is FALSE** — a distortionless line still has attenuation $\alpha = \sqrt{RG} \neq 0$.
> 2. **Forgetting units in skin depth**: Always state $\delta$ in meters, then convert to μm for engineering intuition. Skipping units costs 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Heaviside condition application**: Many students write "$RC = LG$" but fail to derive the consequences ($\alpha$ constant, $\beta$ linear, $Z_0$ real). Examiners expect all three consequences explicitly.
> 4. **RC line dispersion**: Students often claim "RC lines are lossless" — this is **wrong**. RC lines are **lossy AND dispersive**, with both $\alpha, \beta \propto \sqrt{\omega}$.
> 5. **Proximity vs skin effect**: Examiners frequently test this distinction. Memorize the **self vs mutual** contrast.
> 6. **Dielectric loss crossover**: Don't state "dielectric loss dominates at high frequencies" without quantifying it with $\alpha_d \propto f$ vs $\alpha_c \propto \sqrt{f}$.
> 7. **Step responses**: When asked about step response, **always mention Elmore delay** explicitly. KTU 2024 scheme values the 50% delay metric.
> 8. **Formula sheet usage**: When using $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu\sigma}$, state whether you used the magnitude form or the complex form. Mismatched forms confuse valuation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Bullets

- **Lossless line**: $R = 0$, $G = 0$, $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (real), $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$, $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$.
- **Distortionless line**: $RC = LG$ (Heaviside), $\alpha = \sqrt{RG}$ (constant), $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ (linear), $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (real, frequency-independent).
- **RC line**: $L = 0$, $G = 0$; $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\omega RC/2}$; $v_p = \sqrt{2\omega/RC}$ (dispersive); Elmore delay $\tau_D = RC\ell^2/2$ (quadratic in length).
- **Skin depth**: $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}$; for copper at 1 GHz, $\delta \approx 2.09$ μm.
- **AC resistance (round, $r \gg \delta$)**: $R_{ac} = R_{dc} \cdot r/(2\delta) \propto \sqrt{f}$.
- **AC resistance (flat, $t \gg \delta$)**: $R_{ac} = R_{dc} \cdot t/(2\delta)$.
- **Proximity effect**: Mutual redistribution of current between adjacent AC conductors; same-direction → far-side, opposite-direction → near-side; grows as $\sqrt{f}$; dominant in tightly coupled differential pairs.
- **Dielectric loss**: Quantified by $\tan\delta_d = \sigma_d/(\omega\varepsilon)$; $\alpha_d \propto f$ (linear); dominates over conductor loss above a crossover frequency that depends on $\tan\delta_d$ and trace geometry.
- **Loss comparison**: Conductor loss $\propto \sqrt{f}$, dielectric loss $\propto f$, so dielectric loss dominates at high frequencies.
- **Elmore delay**: $\tau_D = R_{tot} C_{tot}/2$ for a distributed RC line; key metric in VLSI timing closure.
- **On-chip interconnects**: Dominated by RC model (high resistance, high capacitance per unit length, weak inductance).
- **Package/PCB interconnects**: Both skin-effect and dielectric loss matter; total loss grows as $\sqrt{f}$ to $f$ depending on regime.
- **Practical material parameters**:
  - Copper: $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m
  - FR4: $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.3$, $\tan\delta_d \approx 0.02$
  - Rogers RO4350B: $\varepsilon_r \approx 3.48$, $\tan\delta_d \approx 0.003$
  - Air: $\varepsilon_r = 1$, $\tan\delta_d \approx 0$
- **Heaviside insight**: Achieving $RC = LG$ in practice is rare; **loading coils** in old telephone lines were added to increase $L$ to satisfy the condition.
- **Dispersive vs non-dispersive**: Lossless & distortionless lines are non-dispersive (constant $v_p$); RC lines and general lossy lines are dispersive.
- **Engineering rule of thumb**: Trace is electrically long when physical length $\geq \lambda/10$ of highest frequency component. For a 1 GHz signal in FR4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.3$), $\lambda \approx 14.5$ cm, so $\lambda/10 \approx 1.45$ cm — anything longer needs transmission-line analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Source and Load Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Effects of Source and Load Impedance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **High-Speed Digital Design**, any interconnect is treated as a **Distributed Transmission Line** when the signal **rise time ($t_r$)** is comparable to or less than the **propagation delay ($t_{pd}$)** across the line. Under such conditions, the source driver and the receiver load can no longer be assumed to be lumped elements; instead, their **impedances** dynamically interact with the **characteristic impedance ($Z_0$)** of the interconnect. The **Effect of Source Impedance ($Z_S$)** refers to the voltage division that occurs between the driver's internal output impedance and the line, dictating the **amplitude of the launched wave ($V_1$)** and the magnitude of **re-reflections** from the source end. The **Effect of Load Impedance ($Z_L$)** refers to the **partial or total reflection** of the incident wave at the receiver end, governed by the **load reflection coefficient ($\Gamma_L$)**, which causes **overshoot, undershoot, ringing, and non-monotonic edges**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** When $t_r < 2 \cdot T_f$ (where $T_f$ is the **time of flight** across the line), the interconnect MUST be analyzed using the **Transmission Line Theory**, treating the source and load as independent boundary conditions. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pushing a heavy wooden block (the **signal**) along a long, narrow corridor (the **transmission line**) using a spring-loaded paddle (the **driver with source impedance $Z_S$**). The block slides at a constant velocity determined by the corridor's friction (the **characteristic impedance $Z_0$**). - If the corridor ends in a **soft padded wall** (matched load $Z_L = Z_0$), the block stops smoothly — **no reflection**. - If the corridor ends in a **solid concrete wall** (open circuit $Z_L = \infty$ or short circuit $Z_L = 0$), the block **bounces back** with full force — **full reflection**. - If the corridor ends in a **slightly cushioned wall** (mismatched load $Z_L \neq Z_0$), the block bounces back **partially** — **partial reflection**. Now, if the paddle is **soft/elastic** (high $Z_S$), it cannot launch the block at full force — **low initial wave amplitude**. The block will keep bouncing back and forth between the paddle and the wall, with each bounce losing some energy until it settles. If the paddle is **stiff/rigid** (low $Z_S$), it can absorb or re-launch the returning block instantly. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of shouting into a long hallway. A person at the end hears your voice clearly (matched). In a canyon, you hear echoes (reflections). In a tiled bathroom with hard walls, the echo is loud and ringing persists for a long time (high-Q ringing due to mismatch at both ends). --- ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Vacuum light speed | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s** | | Relative permittivity (FR-4) | $\varepsilon_r$ | **4.3** | | Propagation velocity | $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | $\approx 1.45 \times 10^8$ m/s | | Standard line impedances | $Z_0$ | **50 Ω, 75 Ω, 90 Ω, 100 Ω (diff.)** | | Critical length rule of thumb | $l_c$ | **$9 \cdot t_r(\text{ns})$ inches** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown The interaction between a driver, a transmission line, and a load is analyzed in **four sequential stages**: **Step 1: The Source Boundary Condition (Wave Launch)** - The driver has an internal **open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$** and an **output impedance $Z_S$**. - The line demands that it be charged to a voltage wave determined by the **voltage divider** between $Z_S$ and $Z_0$. - This produces the **initial incident wave** $V_1^+$ that travels down the line. **Step 2: The Line (Pure Delay and Distortion)** - The wave travels at velocity $v_p$ with the line acting as a constant $Z_0$. - The line applies a **time delay** $T_f = l / v_p$ but does not alter the wave shape (lossless case). - In lossy lines, attenuation $\alpha$ modifies the amplitude. **Step 3: The Load Boundary Condition (Reflection)** - The wave arrives at the load $Z_L$ after time $T_f$. - The mismatch between $Z_L$ and $Z_0$ produces a **reflected wave** $V_1^-$ with amplitude governed by $\Gamma_L$. - A portion may also be **transmitted** into the load as $V_T$. **Step 4: The Source Re-reflection (Settling)** - The reflected wave $V_1^-$ returns to the source after another $T_f$. - The source mismatch $\Gamma_S$ causes a **re-reflection** $V_2^+ = \Gamma_S \cdot V_1^-$. - The cycle continues until **steady state** is reached: $V_{\infty} = V_{oc} \cdot \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_L + Z_S}$. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the symbols**: $Z_S$ = source, $Z_0$ = line, $Z_L$ = load. The "0" subscript is always the line, the endpoints are $S$ and $L$. | # | Quantity | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Load Reflection Coefficient** | $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Ratio of reflected to incident voltage at load. $\Gamma_L \in [-1, 1]$. | | 2 | **Source Reflection Coefficient** | $\Gamma_S = \dfrac{Z_S - Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}$ | Ratio at the source end. | | 3 | **Transmission Coefficient** | $T = \dfrac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Fraction of wave entering the load. Note: $T = 1 + \Gamma_L$. | | 4 | **Initial Wave Amplitude** | $V_1 = V_{oc} \cdot \dfrac{Z_0}{Z_0 + Z_S}$ | Launched by the source divider. | | 5 | **First Reflected Wave** | $V_1^- = \Gamma_L \cdot V_1$ | Returns from load to source. | | 6 | **Second Incident Wave** | $V_2^+ = \Gamma_S \cdot V_1^-$ | Re-reflected from source. | | 7 | **Steady-State Voltage** | $V_{\infty} = V_{oc} \cdot \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_L + Z_S}$ | DC value after infinite bounces. | | 8 | **Time of Flight** | $T_f = \dfrac{l}{v_p} = \dfrac{l \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$ | One-way line delay. | | 9 | **Critical Length** | $l_c = \dfrac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}} = \dfrac{c \cdot t_r}{2 \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Threshold for transmission-line behavior. | | 10 | **Bounce Period** | $T_b = 2 T_f$ | Round-trip time. | | 11 | **Ringing Frequency (LC line)** | $f_{ring} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L_{total} C_{total}}}$ | For lumped LC approximation. | | 12 | **Damping Factor** | $\zeta = \dfrac{R}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$ | For series RLC with line L, C. | > **Mathematical Notation Note:** The **absolute value** of reflection magnitude is written as $\vert \Gamma_L \vert$ (NOT using the pipe character which breaks tables). --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **PCB Design (Backplane, Memory Buses):** DDR5, PCIe Gen 5/6, and SERDES links operate at multi-GHz speeds. A single **impedance mismatch** of 10% can cause **eye diagram closure**, **bit error rate (BER) degradation**, and **timing violations**. Engineers use this analysis to decide **trace width, layer stack-up, and termination scheme**. 2. **High-Speed Test & Measurement:** **Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR)** instruments rely on the same reflection equations to **profile the impedance** of cables, connectors, and PCB traces. By launching a fast edge and observing the reflected waveform, an engineer can **locate opens, shorts, and stubs** with millimetric precision. 3. **EMI/EMC Compliance:** Un-terminated or partially-terminated lines radiate strongly. The "ringing" predicted by $\Gamma_S \cdot \Gamma_L$ is a major source of **common-mode radiation**, causing products to fail **CISPR/FCC Class B** limits. 4. **Power Integrity:** The same theory applies to **power distribution networks (PDN)** where a switching load (like a GPU) behaves as a step source into a power plane ($Z_0$), and the VRM (voltage regulator) acts as a complex $Z_S$. **Ground bounce** and **rail collapse** are direct consequences. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Load Reflection Coefficient ($\Gamma_L$) Consider a lossless transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ terminated in a load $Z_L$. An incident wave $V^+ e^{-j \beta z}$ is propagating in the $+z$ direction. Upon hitting the load, a reflected wave $V^- e^{+j \beta z}$ is generated. The total voltage and current at the load ($z=0$) must satisfy the boundary condition of the load. **Step 1:** Write the total voltage and current at the load. $$ V_{total}(0) = V^+ + V^- $$ $$ I_{total}(0) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} $$ **Step 2:** Apply Ohm's Law at the load terminal. $$ \frac{V_{total}(0)}{I_{total}(0)} = Z_L $$ **Step 3:** Substitute the total voltage and current expressions. $$ \frac{V^+ + V^-}{\frac{V^+}{Z_0} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0}} = Z_L $$ **Step 4:** Cross-multiply and isolate $V^-$. $$ V^+ + V^- = Z_L \left( \frac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0} \right) $$ **Step 5:** Multiply through by $Z_0$. $$ Z_0 (V^+ + V^-) = Z_L (V^+ - V^-) $$ **Step 6:** Collect $V^+$ and $V^-$ terms on opposite sides. $$ Z_0 V^+ + Z_0 V^- = Z_L V^+ - Z_L V^- $$ $$ Z_L V^- + Z_0 V^- = Z_L V^+ - Z_0 V^+ $$ **Step 7:** Factor and solve for the ratio. $$ V^- (Z_L + Z_0) = V^+ (Z_L - Z_0) $$ $$ \boxed{\Gamma_L = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}} $$ **Step 8:** Define the **Transmission Coefficient** $T = \dfrac{V_{load}}{V^+}$. $$ T = \frac{V^+ + V^-}{V^+} = 1 + \Gamma_L = \frac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0} $$ > **Limit Checks (must memorize for KTU):** > - $Z_L = Z_0 \Rightarrow \Gamma_L = 0$ (Matched — no reflection). > - $Z_L = \infty$ (open) $\Rightarrow \Gamma_L = +1$ (Full positive reflection). > - $Z_L = 0$ (short) $\Rightarrow \Gamma_L = -1$ (Full negative reflection, inversion). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Initial Wave Amplitude (Source Divider) A non-ideal driver is modeled as a **Thevenin equivalent**: an ideal step source $V_{oc}$ in series with $Z_S$, connected to a transmission line of impedance $Z_0$. At the moment the switch closes, the line behaves as a pure resistor $Z_0$ (before the wave has had time to travel to the load and return). **Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop. $$ V_{oc} = I \cdot Z_S + I \cdot Z_0 $$ **Step 2:** Solve for the launch current. $$ I = \frac{V_{oc}}{Z_S + Z_0} $$ **Step 3:** The initial wave amplitude $V_1$ is the voltage across the line input (i.e., across $Z_0$). $$ V_1 = I \cdot Z_0 $$ **Step 4:** Substitute $I$. $$ V_1 = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0} $$ **Step 5:** Note that this can be rewritten using $\Gamma_S$. $$ \Gamma_S = \frac{Z_S - Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0} \Rightarrow \frac{Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0} = \frac{1 - \Gamma_S}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{V_1 = V_{oc} \cdot \frac{1 - \Gamma_S}{2}} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State Voltage After Infinite Bounces The bouncing continues with decreasing amplitude because $\vert \Gamma_S \cdot \Gamma_L \vert < 1$. The voltage at the load after $n$ bounces is a geometric series. **Step 1:** Wave amplitudes at the load in sequence. The load voltage contributions arrive in this order: - $V_1 \cdot T$ (first arrival) - $V_1 \cdot \Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_S \cdot T$ (second arrival, after one round trip) - $V_1 \cdot (\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_S)^2 \cdot T$ (third arrival) - ... and so on. **Step 2:** Sum the geometric series. The sum of the first $n$ terms of a geometric series with first term $a$ and ratio $r$ is $\dfrac{a(1 - r^n)}{1 - r}$. $$ V_L^{(n)} = V_1 \cdot T \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (\Gamma_S \Gamma_L)^k $$ **Step 3:** As $n \to \infty$ (steady state), since $\vert \Gamma_S \Gamma_L \vert < 1$, the term $(\Gamma_S \Gamma_L)^n \to 0$. $$ V_L^{(\infty)} = V_1 \cdot T \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \Gamma_S \Gamma_L} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute $V_1 = V_{oc} \cdot \dfrac{Z_0}{Z_0 + Z_S}$ and $T = \dfrac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0}$. $$ V_L^{(\infty)} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot Z_0}{Z_0 + Z_S} \cdot \frac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \Gamma_S \Gamma_L} $$ **Step 5:** Substitute the definitions of $\Gamma_S$ and $\Gamma_L$ into the denominator. $$ 1 - \Gamma_S \Gamma_L = \frac{(Z_S + Z_0)(Z_L + Z_0) - (Z_S - Z_0)(Z_L - Z_0)}{(Z_S + Z_0)(Z_L + Z_0)} $$ **Step 6:** Expand the numerator. $$ (Z_S Z_L + Z_S Z_0 + Z_0 Z_L + Z_0^2) - (Z_S Z_L - Z_S Z_0 - Z_0 Z_L + Z_0^2) $$ $$ = 2 Z_S Z_0 + 2 Z_0 Z_L = 2 Z_0 (Z_S + Z_L) $$ **Step 7:** Combine all terms. $$ V_L^{(\infty)} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot Z_0}{Z_0 + Z_S} \cdot \frac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0} \cdot \frac{(Z_S + Z_0)(Z_L + Z_0)}{2 Z_0 (Z_S + Z_L)} $$ **Step 8:** Cancel common factors to reveal the elegant result. $$ \boxed{V_{\infty} = V_{oc} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_L + Z_S}} $$ > This is **intuitively obvious** in hindsight: at DC, the transmission line is a short circuit (lossless), so the voltage is just the **Thevenin divider** between $Z_S$ and $Z_L$. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical Length for Transmission-Line Behavior A line is **electrically long** when its propagation delay is a significant fraction of the signal rise time. The widely accepted threshold is when the **time of flight is one-sixth of the rise time** (some texts use one-half; KTU follows the more conservative 1/6 rule for safety). **Step 1:** Definition of time of flight for a line of length $l$ and propagation delay per unit length $t_{pd}$. $$ T_f = l \cdot t_{pd} $$ **Step 2:** Critical length condition: $T_f = t_r / 2$ (round-trip threshold). $$ l_c \cdot t_{pd} = \frac{t_r}{2} $$ **Step 3:** Solve for $l_c$. $$ \boxed{l_c = \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute $t_{pd} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$. $$ l_c = \frac{c \cdot t_r}{2 \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} $$ **Step 5:** Plug in $\varepsilon_r = 4.3$ (FR-4) and convert to inches ($1 \text{ m} = 39.37 \text{ in}$). $$ l_c \approx \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(t_r \text{ in s})}{2 \cdot 2.074} \approx 7.23 \times 10^7 \cdot t_r \text{ (m)} $$ **Step 6:** Convert to inches. $$ l_c \approx 7.23 \times 10^7 \cdot 39.37 \cdot t_r \approx 2.85 \times 10^9 \cdot t_r \text{ (in)} $$ **Step 7:** With $t_r$ in nanoseconds: $l_c \approx 2.85 \cdot t_r(\text{ns})$ inches. The widely-cited "**rule of thumb**" is: $$ \boxed{l_c \approx 9 \cdot t_r(\text{ns}) \text{ inches (with safety factor)} } $$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Impedance Scenarios > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Tested Cases** — Memorize the wave amplitudes and the resulting waveform shape. | Case | $Z_S$ | $Z_0$ | $Z_L$ | $\Gamma_S$ | $\Gamma_L$ | First Wave $V_1$ | Reflected $V_1^-$ | Waveform at Load | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Ideal source, matched load** | $0$ | $50$ | $50$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $V_{oc}$ | $0$ | Clean step, no reflection | | **Ideal source, open load** | $0$ | $50$ | $\infty$ | $-1$ | $+1$ | $V_{oc}$ | $V_{oc}$ | Step, then jumps to $2V_{oc}$ at $T_f$ | | **Ideal source, short load** | $0$ | $50$ | $0$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | $V_{oc}$ | $-V_{oc}$ | Step, then drops to $0$ at $T_f$, ringing | | **Matched source, mismatched load** | $50$ | $50$ | $75$ | $0$ | $+0.2$ | $0.5 V_{oc}$ | $0.1 V_{oc}$ | Half-step, then small bump, settles | | **Series-terminated (matched $Z_S$)** | $50$ | $50$ | $\infty$ | $0$ | $+1$ | $0.5 V_{oc}$ | $0.5 V_{oc}$ | Half step at $T_f$, then full $V_{oc}$ | | **Capacitive load** (high-Z CMOS) | $10$ | $50$ | $\infty$ (with $C_L$) | $-0.667$ | $+1$ | $0.833 V_{oc}$ | $0.833 V_{oc}$ | Overshoot, soft settling | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Source–Line–Load Interaction This diagram shows the **three regions** of a high-speed interconnect and the boundary conditions at each end. The arrows represent the forward (incident) and backward (reflected) voltage waves. ```mermaid graph LR drv["DRIVER
V_oc = 3.3 V
Z_S = 30 ohm"] tline["TRANSMISSION LINE

T_f = 2 ns
length = 0.30 m"] load["LOAD
Z_L = 75 ohm
C_in = 5 pF"] drv -- "V1+ = V_oc * Z_0 / (Z_0 + Z_S)
Launched Wave" --> tline tline -- "V1- = Gamma_L * V1+

Transmitted" --> load load -- "Re-reflects to V2+" --> tline ``` --- ### 4.2 Lattice (Bounce) Diagram for a Typical Mismatch The **lattice diagram** is a graphical method to track the wave amplitude as a function of space (vertical axis) and time (horizontal axis). The slope of the lines is the wave velocity. Each "bounce" produces a new wave with amplitude $\Gamma \times$ incoming wave. ```mermaid graph TB n0["At Source: V_oc = 3.3 V, Z_S = 30 ohm"] n1["V1+ = 3.3 * 50/80 = 2.06 V
Launched at t = 0"] n2["Arrives at Load at t = T_f = 2 ns"] n3["V1- = Gamma_L * V1+ = 0.2 * 2.06 = 0.412 V
Reflected at t = 2 ns"] n4["Returns to Source at t = 4 ns"] n5["V2+ = Gamma_S * V1- = -0.25 * 0.412 = -0.103 V
Re-reflected at t = 4 ns"] n6["Arrives at Load at t = 6 ns"] n7["V2- = Gamma_L * V2+ = 0.2 * -0.103 = -0.021 V
Reflected at t = 6 ns"] n8["Steady State at t = infinity
V_inf = 3.3 * 75/105 = 2.357 V"] n0 --> n1 --> n2 --> n3 --> n4 --> n5 --> n6 --> n7 --> n8 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading a Lattice Diagram:** > - Top line = source end (x = 0). > - Bottom line = load end (x = l). > - Diagonal lines sloping down-right = forward-traveling wave. > - Diagonal lines sloping down-left = backward-traveling wave. > - **Voltage at any point** = algebraic sum of all waves passing through that point at that time. --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Termination Strategy Comparison ```mermaid graph TB term["TERMINATION STRATEGY FOR HIGH-SPEED LINKS"] sTerm["SERIES TERMINATION

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` > **Define the reflection coefficient for a load mismatch in a high-speed digital interconnect. Show that the transmission coefficient $T = 1 + \Gamma_L$ for a load $Z_L$ terminating a line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$.** **Model Answer (Valuation Key Pattern):** The **load reflection coefficient** is the ratio of the complex amplitude of the reflected voltage wave to that of the incident voltage wave at the load end of a transmission line. $$ \Gamma_L = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} $$ **Proof of $T = 1 + \Gamma_L$:** The total voltage at the load is $V_L = V^+ + V^- = V^+(1 + \Gamma_L)$. Since the load voltage is also the transmitted voltage $V_T$, we have: $$ T = \frac{V_T}{V^+} = 1 + \Gamma_L $$ Substituting $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$: $$ T = 1 + \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{Z_L + Z_0 + Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0} $$ > **Bloom Level:** L2 (Understand) + L3 (Apply). --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` > **Explain with a neat sketch the effect of a purely capacitive load on a high-speed digital signal. What is the "soft knee" effect?** **Model Answer:** A purely capacitive load $C_L$ (such as a CMOS gate input) presents a frequency-dependent impedance $Z_L = 1/(j \omega C_L)$. At DC, $Z_L \to \infty$ (open circuit), but at high frequencies the impedance is low. **Effects on the signal:** 1. **Edge Slowing (Soft Knee):** The $RC$ time constant formed by the source resistance $Z_S$ and $C_L$ slows the rising edge. The 10–90% rise time becomes $t_{r,out} \approx 2.2 R_{eq} C_L$, where $R_{eq} = Z_S \Vert Z_0$. 2. **Initial Step:** A small **initial step** $V_1 = V_{oc} Z_0 / (Z_0 + Z_S)$ is launched because the line is initially charged. 3. **Overshoot:** Because $Z_L$ is high at DC, the reflection at the load is positive, causing the final voltage to **overshoot** the steady-state value before settling exponentially. 4. **Monotonic Edge:** For pure capacitance, the edge remains **monotonic** (no oscillation) because energy is dissipated in $Z_S$ as the capacitor charges. > **Bloom Level:** L2 (Understand). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Answer ANY ONE full question. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` ##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** > **Derive the expression for the reflection coefficient at the load end of a lossless transmission line in terms of the load impedance and the characteristic impedance. Hence obtain the special cases for (i) matched load, (ii) open circuit, and (iii) short circuit.** **Model Solution (Full 7-Mark Valuation):** **Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a lossless transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ terminated in a load $Z_L$. A forward wave $V^+ e^{-j\beta z}$ is incident. A reflected wave $V^- e^{+j\beta z}$ is generated at $z = 0$. **Step 2 — Boundary condition:** At $z = 0$ the line voltage and current must satisfy Ohm's law. $$ V(0) = I(0) \cdot Z_L $$ **Step 3 — Total voltage and current:** $$ V(0) = V^+ + V^- $$ $$ I(0) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute:** $$ V^+ + V^- = Z_L \left( \frac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0} \right) $$ **Step 5 — Multiply by $Z_0$ and collect:** $$ Z_0 (V^+ + V^-) = Z_L (V^+ - V^-) $$ $$ V^- (Z_L + Z_0) = V^+ (Z_L - Z_0) $$ **Step 6 — Final expression:** $$ \boxed{\Gamma_L = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}} $$ **Step 7 — Special cases:** | Case | $Z_L$ | $\Gamma_L$ | Physical meaning | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Matched | $Z_0$ | $0$ | No reflection — perfect transfer. | | (ii) Open | $\infty$ | $+1$ | Full positive reflection; load voltage doubles. | | (iii) Short | $0$ | $-1$ | Full negative reflection; load voltage cancels. | --- ##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** > **A CMOS driver with open-circuit output voltage $V_{oc} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ and source impedance $Z_S = 25 \text{ }\Omega$ drives a $50 \text{ }\Omega$ microstrip line of length $0.4 \text{ m}$ on FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.3$). The line is terminated in a load $Z_L = 75 \text{ }\Omega$. Determine:** > **(i) The initial wave amplitude $V_1^+$ launched onto the line.** > **(ii) The reflection coefficients $\Gamma_S$ and $\Gamma_L$.** > **(iii) The first reflected wave $V_1^-$ from the load and the first re-reflected wave $V_2^+$ from the source.** > **(iv) The steady-state voltage at the load.** > **(v) Sketch the lattice diagram.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $V_{oc} = 3.3$ V, $Z_S = 25$ Ω, $Z_0 = 50$ Ω, $Z_L = 75$ Ω. **Step 1 — Initial wave amplitude:** $$ V_1^+ = V_{oc} \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_0 + Z_S} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{50}{50 + 25} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{50}{75} $$ $$ \boxed{V_1^+ = 2.20 \text{ V}} $$ **Step 2 — Reflection coefficients:** $$ \Gamma_S = \frac{Z_S - Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0} = \frac{25 - 50}{25 + 50} = \frac{-25}{75} = -0.333 $$ $$ \Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{75 - 50}{75 + 50} = \frac{25}{125} = +0.200 $$ **Step 3 — First reflected wave from load:** $$ V_1^- = \Gamma_L \cdot V_1^+ = 0.200 \cdot 2.20 = \boxed{0.440 \text{ V}} $$ **Step 4 — First re-reflected wave from source:** $$ V_2^+ = \Gamma_S \cdot V_1^- = (-0.333) \cdot 0.440 = \boxed{-0.147 \text{ V}} $$ **Step 5 — Steady-state voltage at the load:** $$ V_\infty = V_{oc} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_L + Z_S} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{75}{75 + 25} = 3.3 \cdot 0.75 $$ $$ \boxed{V_\infty = 2.475 \text{ V}} $$ **Step 6 — Lattice Diagram:** | Time | Event | Wave | |---|---|---| | $t = 0$ | Source launches | $V_1^+ = +2.20$ V | | $t = T_f$ | Hits load, partial reflection | $V_1^- = +0.440$ V (reflected); $V_T = V_1^+ + V_1^- = 2.64$ V at load | | $t = 2T_f$ | Hits source, re-reflection | $V_2^+ = -0.147$ V | | $t = 3T_f$ | Hits load, second reflection | $V_2^- = -0.029$ V; load voltage $= 2.64 - 0.147 - 0.029 \approx 2.464$ V | | $t = \infty$ | Settles | $V_\infty = 2.475$ V | ``` SOURCE (Z_S) | | LOAD (Z_L) | \ | V_1+=2.20 | \ V_1-=0.440 | V_2+=-0.147 | \ | | \ / | \ _/ V_2-=-0.029 | \________________________ ___/ | / \ | / \ | / \ | / \ V_3+=... | / \ V_3-=... | / \ |____________________________________| time=0 t=T_f t=2T_f ``` --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` ##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** > **Explain the following termination techniques for high-speed digital interconnects with neat circuit diagrams:** > **(i) Series (source) termination** > **(ii) Parallel termination** > **(iii) Thevenin termination** > **(iv) AC termination** > **State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Series (Source) Termination:** ``` Z_S --/\/\/----+-------+-------+---- (Transmission Line Z_0) | | Driver R_series = Z_0 - Z_driver ``` - A resistor $R_S$ is placed **in series with the driver output**, chosen such that $Z_S + R_S = Z_0$. - **Advantage:** No DC power dissipation; single resistor per line. - **Disadvantage:** Effective only for **point-to-point** topology; not suitable for multi-drop or bi-directional buses. Requires the line to be a half-receiver high before re-driving. **(ii) Parallel Termination:** ``` +Vcc | R_T = Z_0 | ----+----+----- Z_0 ----- Receiver | GND (or R_T to V_TT) ``` - A resistor $R_T = Z_0$ is placed at the **far end**, either to ground or to a termination voltage $V_{TT}$. - **Advantage:** Absorbs the incident wave completely — no reflection.
