This corresponds to the **Even-Mode** (or **Common-Mode**) excitation of the coupled line system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** Uniform Voltage Distribution is the symmetric excitation state of a coupled transmission line structure in which the per-unit-length charge and voltage on every signal conductor is equal, producing equal-magnitude, in-phase currents on all lines and a single return current on the reference conductor. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Highway Convoy** > Imagine a 4-lane highway where four cars (representing four coupled microstrip traces on a PCB) all travel at exactly the same speed, in the same direction, side-by-side. Because they are synchronized, no car "sees" its neighbour as a relative obstacle — the air gap (dielectric field) between them is calm and uniform. This is **uniform voltage distribution** in the electromagnetic world: each line carries the same wavefront, the **E-field** between adjacent traces is **symmetric**, and there is no differential-mode coupling or crosstalk noise. By contrast, if the cars travel in opposite directions, the air is violently disturbed — that is the **odd (differential) mode**. ## 1.3 Why It Matters in High-Speed Digital Design At multi-Gbps data rates (e.g., **5 Gbps USB 3.0**, **10 Gbps PCIe 3.0**, **25 Gbps SERDES**), PCB traces behave as **distributed transmission lines** rather than ideal lumped wires. When several such traces run in parallel for any appreciable length (≥ 1/10th of the signal rise-time spatial extent), they become **electromagnetically coupled**. The way voltage distributes itself across these coupled lines determines: - **Crosstalk magnitude** (NEXT / FEXT) - **Characteristic impedance** (Z₀) seen by each driver - **Mode conversion** between common-mode and differential-mode - **EMI/EMC radiation** levels The **uniform (even-mode)** case is critical because it represents the worst-case scenario for **common-mode noise**, **ground-bounce**, and **radiated emissions** — all hot favourites of KTU's signal-integrity examiners. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | | Relative permittivity (FR-4) | $\varepsilon_r$ | **4.2 – 4.5** | | Phase velocity in dielectric | $v_p$ | $c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0$ | **50 Ω** (single-ended), **100 Ω** (differential) | | Rise time | $t_r$ | **10 – 100 ps** (for GHz signals) | | Electrically long length | $l_{crit}$ | $l > t_r \cdot v_p / 2$ | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Multi-Conductor Transmission Line (MTL) Background A **symmetric 2-coupled microstrip** system consists of two signal traces (conductors 1 and 2) and a reference ground plane. Its behaviour is fully described by the **per-unit-length (p.u.l.) parameter matrices**: $$[L] = \begin{bmatrix} L_{11} & L_{12} \\ L_{21} & L_{22} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{H/m}$$ $$[C] = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} \\ C_{21} & C_{22} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{F/m}$$ > Where $L_{ii}$ = self-inductance, $L_{ij}$ ($i \ne j$) = mutual inductance, and similarly for $[C]$. For symmetric lines, $L_{11}=L_{22}$, $L_{12}=L_{21}$, and $C_{11}=C_{22}$, $C_{12}=C_{21}$. ## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Modes ### Mode 1 — Even Mode (Uniform Voltage Distribution) $V_1 = V_2$, currents $I_1 = I_2$ in the same direction. Voltage on every signal line is **identical**; one return current $2I_1$ flows in the ground plane. ### Mode 2 — Odd Mode (Differential Mode) $V_1 = -V_2$, currents $I_1 = -I_2$. Voltages are **equal and opposite** — the reference plane carries **no net current**. ## 2.3 Derivation of Modal Impedances The Telegrapher's equations for an MTL are: $$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}[V(x,t)] = -[L]\frac{\partial}{\partial t}[I(x,t)]$$ $$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}[I(x,t)] = -[C]\frac{\partial}{\partial t}[V(x,t)]$$ For the **even mode**, substituting $V_1 = V_2 = V_e$ and exploiting the symmetry of the $[L]$ and $[C]$ matrices: $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{L_{11} - L_{12}}{C_{11} + 2C_{12} - C_{12}}} = \sqrt{\frac{L_{11} - L_{12}}{C_{11} + C_{12}}}$$ For the **odd mode**: $$Z_{0o} = \sqrt{\frac{L_{11} + L_{12}}{C_{11}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** $Z_{0e} > Z_{0o}$ for tightly coupled lines. Even-mode impedance is **higher** because the effective capacitance per line to ground is reduced (charges are shared between lines, reducing the $C_{11}$ contribution). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Phase velocity | $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}$ | m/s | | 2 | Even-mode impedance | $Z_{0e} = \sqrt{(L_{11} - L_{12})/(C_{11} + C_{12})}$ | Ω | | 3 | Odd-mode impedance | $Z_{0o} = \sqrt{(L_{11} + L_{12})/C_{11}}$ | Ω | | 4 | Differential impedance | $Z_{diff} = 2 Z_{0o}$ | Ω | | 5 | Common-mode impedance | $Z_{cm} = Z_{0e}/2$ | Ω | | 6 | Effective permittivity (microstrip) | $\varepsilon_{eff} = (\varepsilon_r + 1)/2 + (\varepsilon_r - 1)/2 \cdot (1+12h/W)^{-1/2}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Coupling coefficient | $k = (Z_{0e} - Z_{0o})/(Z_{0e} + Z_{0o})$ | $0 \le k \le 1$ | | 8 | Far-end crosstalk (FEXT) | $V_{FEXT} = k \cdot V_{0} \cdot (t_{10\%-90\%})/(2T_f)$ | V | | 9 | LC propagation delay | $t_{pd} = \sqrt{LC}$ | s/m | | 10 | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ | dimensionless | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Uniform voltage distribution analysis is indispensable in: - **DDR memory bus design** (parallel data/address/clock lines) - **USB, HDMI, DisplayPort, PCIe** differential channels - **SERDES link modelling** (28–112 Gbps PAM4) - **Backplane design** (multi-gigabit backplanes, e.g., ATCA, AdvancedTCA) - **EMI compliance testing** where common-mode currents dominate radiation - **High-speed probe station measurements** (TDR/TDT characterisation) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Even-Mode Impedance $Z_{0e}$ for Symmetric Coupled Microstrips **Starting point:** For a lossless, symmetric 2-line coupled system, the per-unit-length inductance and capacitance matrices are symmetric: $$[L] = L_0 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & k_L \\ k_L & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [C] = C_0 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -k_C \\ -k_C & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 1 — Define modal voltages and currents.** Even mode demands $V_1 = V_2 = V_e$ and $I_1 = I_2 = I_e$. Substitute into the per-line Telegrapher's equation: $$\frac{\partial V_e}{\partial x} = -L_0(1 + k_L) \frac{\partial I_e}{\partial t}$$ $$\frac{\partial I_e}{\partial x} = -C_0(1 - k_C) \frac{\partial V_e}{\partial t}$$ **Step 2 — Identify effective per-unit-length parameters.** From the substitution above, the effective inductance and capacitance for the even mode are: $$L_e = L_0(1 + k_L)$$ $$C_e = C_0(1 - k_C)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the definition of characteristic impedance.** For any single transmission-line mode, $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$. Therefore: $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{L_e}{C_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{L_0(1 + k_L)}{C_0(1 - k_C)}}$$ **Step 4 — Express in matrix-element form.** Recognising that $L_{11} = L_0$, $L_{12} = L_0 k_L$, $C_{11} = C_0$, and $C_{12} = -C_0 k_C$: $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{L_{11} + L_{12}}{C_{11} - C_{12}}}$$ **Step 5 — Cross-check with the dual formulation.** Using the duality relation $[L][C] = \mu\varepsilon [I]$ (for TEM/quasi-TEM lines), one can show equivalently: $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{L_{11} - L_{12}}{C_{11} + C_{12}}}$$ Both forms are equivalent; the second is more frequently used in textbooks (e.g., Johnson & Graham, "High-Speed Digital Design") because it matches the KTU Board convention. ## 3.2 Derivation: Common-Mode Impedance of the Pair **Step 1 — Common-mode voltage definition.** $V_{cm} = (V_1 + V_2)/2$. For uniform distribution $V_1 = V_2 = V_e$, so $V_{cm} = V_e$. **Step 2 — Common-mode current definition.** $I_{cm} = (I_1 + I_2)/2 = I_e$ (for even mode). **Step 3 — Voltage between the two lines.** Since $V_1 = V_2$, the differential voltage $V_{diff} = V_1 - V_2 = 0$. **Step 4 — Compute the common-mode impedance as the voltage between both lines and ground divided by the total current returning through ground.** $$Z_{cm} = \frac{V_{cm}}{I_{cm}} = \frac{V_e}{2 I_e} = \frac{Z_{0e}}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Physical interpretation.** In the common (even) mode, the two lines appear as a **single wide conductor** in parallel. The total return current $2I_e$ flows through the ground plane. Hence the impedance to ground is **half** the per-line even-mode impedance. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A symmetric coupled microstrip on FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.4$) has p.u.l. parameters $L_{11} = 380$ nH/m, $L_{12} = 60$ nH/m, $C_{11} = 120$ pF/m, $C_{12} = -8$ pF/m. Calculate $Z_{0e}$, $Z_{0o}$, and the common-mode impedance. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute $Z_{0e}$ using $Z_{0e} = \sqrt{(L_{11} - L_{12})/(C_{11} + C_{12})}$: $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{(380 - 60) \times 10^{-9}}{(120 + (-8)) \times 10^{-12}}}$$ $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{320 \times 10^{-9}}{112 \times 10^{-12}}}$$ $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{2857.14} \approx 53.45 \ \Omega$$ **Step 2:** Compute $Z_{0o}$ using $Z_{0o} = \sqrt{(L_{11} + L_{12})/C_{11}}$: $$Z_{0o} = \sqrt{\frac{(380 + 60) \times 10^{-9}}{120 \times 10^{-12}}}$$ $$Z_{0o} = \sqrt{\frac{440 \times 10^{-9}}{120 \times 10^{-12}}}$$ $$Z_{0o} = \sqrt{3666.67} \approx 60.55 \ \Omega$$ **Step 3:** Compute common-mode impedance: $$Z_{cm} = \frac{Z_{0e}}{2} = \frac{53.45}{2} \approx 26.73 \ \Omega$$ **Step 4:** Verification — coupling coefficient: $$k = \frac{Z_{0e} - Z_{0o}}{Z_{0e} + Z_{0o}} = \frac{53.45 - 60.55}{53.45 + 60.55} = \frac{-7.10}{114.00} \approx -0.0623$$ The negative sign indicates a sign convention artefact; the magnitude $\vert k \vert = 0.062$ represents a **6.2% coupling** — typical for moderately spaced microstrip traces. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check:** $Z_{0e} < Z_{0o}$ in microstrip because the even mode "sees" more return current flowing in the ground, reducing its impedance. (In stripline the opposite holds.) The 6.2% coupling magnitude is well within the acceptable range for high-speed buses. ## 3.4 Cross-Modal Energy Conservation A general excitation $V_1, V_2$ can be decomposed: $$V_1 = V_e + V_o, \quad V_2 = V_e - V_o$$ where $V_e = (V_1 + V_2)/2$ (uniform component) and $V_o = (V_1 - V_2)/2$ (differential component). This **modal decomposition** is the foundation of KTU's cross-talk analysis. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Coupled Microstrip Cross-Section (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Conductor 1
Trace Width W] --> B[Dielectric Substrate
Height h, eps_r] C[Conductor 2
Trace Width W] --> B B --> D[Reference Ground Plane
Solid Copper] E[Spacing S between traces] --> A E --> C F[Input Signal V1 on Line 1] --> A G[Input Signal V2 on Line 2] --> C H[Mode Selector] --> F H --> G I[Even Mode V1=V2] --> H J[Odd Mode V1=-V2] --> H ``` ## 4.2 Modal Decomposition Flow ```mermaid graph LR A[Arbitrary Excitation V1, V2] --> B[Modal Decomposition
Matrix T] B --> C[Even Mode Component
Ve = V1+V2 over 2] B --> D[Odd Mode Component
Vo = V1-V2 over 2] C --> E[Propagation at Ze
Velocity ve] D --> F[Propagation at Zo
Velocity vo] E --> G[Recombination at Load] F --> G G --> H[Observed Voltages V1, V2 at Receiver] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MTL Solver ```mermaid graph TD A[Physical Geometry Input
W, S, h, eps_r, t] --> B[2D Field Solver
or Analytic Approx] B --> C[p.u.l. L Matrix] B --> D[p.u.l. C Matrix] C --> E[Modal Decomposition
Eigenanalysis of L times C] D --> E E --> F[Modal Velocities vd, ve] E --> G[Modal Impedances Zo, Ze] F --> H[Time Domain Spice W-element] G --> H H --> I[Near End and Far End Crosstalk] H --> J[Eye Diagram Analysis] H --> K[Common Mode Radiation Estimate] ``` ## 4.4 Voltage Standing Pattern Under Uniform Excitation ```mermaid graph LR A[Source
Vg = 1V, Zg = Zo_e] --> B[Coupled Line Length L] B --> C[Load Termination
Z_L = Zo_e matched] subgraph Uniform_Distribution D[Line 1 Voltage = V_e] E[Line 2 Voltage = V_e] F[Return Current 2 I_e in Ground] end B --> Uniform_Distribution ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** When the system is driven in the uniform (even) mode with matched terminations ($Z_L = Z_{0e}$), the voltage on both traces is identical at every point $x$ along the line, and the return current in the ground plane is exactly twice the per-line current. No standing waves form because impedance continuity is maintained. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define uniform voltage distribution in a multi-conductor transmission line. Mention the modal impedance expression for this condition.** **Model Answer:** Uniform voltage distribution is the **even-mode** excitation of a coupled transmission line system in which all signal conductors are at the same potential with respect to the reference conductor, and the currents on all lines are equal and in-phase. The modal characteristic impedance is: $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{L_{11} - L_{12}}{C_{11} + C_{12}}}$$ The corresponding common-mode impedance of the pair is $Z_{cm} = Z_{0e}/2$. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **State the difference between even-mode and odd-mode excitations in a symmetric two-line coupled microstrip.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Even Mode (Uniform) | Odd Mode (Differential) | |---|---|---| | Voltage relation | $V_1 = V_2$ | $V_1 = -V_2$ | | Current relation | $I_1 = I_2$ | $I_1 = -I_2$ | | Return current in ground | $2I_1$ (non-zero) | $0$ (perfect cancellation) | | Impedance (microstrip) | $Z_{0e}$ (lower) | $Z_{0o}$ (higher) | | Application | Common-mode noise, EMI | High-speed differential signalling | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **even-mode characteristic impedance** $Z_{0e}$ of a symmetric two-conductor coupled microstrip line in terms of per-unit-length inductance and capacitance matrix elements. **Model Solution:** Start with the Telegrapher's equations for a 2-line lossless MTL: $$\frac{\partial [V]}{\partial x} = -[L]\frac{\partial [I]}{\partial t}, \quad \frac{\partial [I]}{\partial x} = -[C]\frac{\partial [V]}{\partial t}$$ For a symmetric line: $L_{11} = L_{22} \equiv L_s$, $L_{12} = L_{21} \equiv L_m$, $C_{11} = C_{22} \equiv C_s$, $C_{12} = C_{21} \equiv C_m$. For even mode, impose $V_1 = V_2 = V_e$ and $I_1 = I_2 = I_e$. Substituting into the first line of the matrix equation: $$\frac{\partial V_e}{\partial x} = -(L_s + L_m)\frac{\partial I_e}{\partial t}$$ $$\frac{\partial I_e}{\partial x} = -(C_s + 2C_m)\frac{\partial V_e}{\partial t}$$ Define effective parameters $L_e = L_s + L_m$ and $C_e = C_s + 2C_m$. The phase velocity is $v_e = 1/\sqrt{L_e C_e}$ and: $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{L_e}{C_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{L_s + L_m}{C_s + 2C_m}}$$ Using the duality $[L][C] = \mu\varepsilon [I]$, one can re-express: $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{L_s - L_m}{C_s + C_m}}$$ Both are equivalent under TEM conditions. **(b) [7 Marks]** A symmetric coupled microstrip has $L_{11} = 400$ nH/m, $L_{12} = 50$ nH/m, $C_{11} = 110$ pF/m, and $C_{12} = -5$ pF/m. Calculate (i) $Z_{0e}$, (ii) $Z_{0o}$, (iii) $Z_{cm}$, and (iv) the coupling coefficient $k$. Comment on whether the coupling is acceptable for a 5 Gbps bus. **Model Solution:** **Step (i):** $Z_{0e}$ calculation: $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{(400 - 50) \times 10^{-9}}{(110 + (-5)) \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{\frac{350 \times 10^{-9}}{105 \times 10^{-12}}}$$ $$Z_{0e} = \sqrt{3333.33} \approx 57.74 \ \Omega$$ **Step (ii):** $Z_{0o}$ calculation: $$Z_{0o} = \sqrt{\frac{(400 + 50) \times 10^{-9}}{110 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{\frac{450 \times 10^{-9}}{110 \times 10^{-12}}}$$ $$Z_{0o} = \sqrt{4090.91} \approx 63.96 \ \Omega$$ **Step (iii):** $Z_{cm}$: $$Z_{cm} = \frac{Z_{0e}}{2} = \frac{57.74}{2} \approx 28.87 \ \Omega$$ **Step (iv):** Coupling coefficient: $$k = \frac{Z_{0e} - Z_{0o}}{Z_{0e} + Z_{0o}} = \frac{57.74 - 63.96}{57.74 + 63.96} = \frac{-6.22}{121.70} \approx -0.051$$ Magnitude $\vert k \vert = 0.051$ or **5.1%**. **Comment:** For 5 Gbps, acceptable coupling is generally $<10\%$. The 5.1% coupling is well within limits, indicating the trace spacing is adequate. However, if the spacing is reduced, $k$ will increase and crosstalk will become problematic. Designers typically target $\vert k \vert \le 0.05$ for high-speed buses. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a suitable diagram the **modal decomposition** of voltages in a two-conductor coupled transmission line. Derive the expressions for even-mode and odd-mode components. **Model Solution:** The total voltage on each line can be written as the sum of modal components: $$V_1(x,t) = V_e(x,t) + V_o(x,t)$$ $$V_2(x,t) = V_e(x,t) - V_o(x,t)$$ Solving simultaneously: $$V_e = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \quad \text{(Even/Common Mode)}$$ $$V_o = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2} \quad \text{(Odd/Differential Mode)}$$ **Diagram interpretation:** In a symmetric 2-line system, an arbitrary excitation can always be decomposed into the superposition of an even-mode wave and an odd-mode wave. Each mode propagates independently with its own velocity and characteristic impedance. The boundary conditions (source/load) determine the modal weights, and the lines recombine to produce the observed $V_1, V_2$ at any point. **Physical reasoning:** Because the system is linear and time-invariant, superposition applies. The symmetric (even) part of the excitation excites only the even mode; the antisymmetric (odd) part excites only the odd mode. Cross-mode coupling occurs only through asymmetry in the terminations (e.g., skew between the two lines or unmatched loads). **(b) [7 Marks]** For a 50 Ω characteristic impedance differential pair with $Z_{0e} = 55 \ \Omega$, $Z_{0o} = 45 \ \Omega$, and a common-mode excitation $V_1 = V_2 = 1$ V, calculate the per-line voltage and current at the load when the lines are (i) terminated in $Z_{0e}$ and (ii) left open-circuited at the far end. Comment on the practical implications. **Model Solution:** **Case (i): Matched termination $Z_L = Z_{0e} = 55$ Ω** The system is impedance-matched, so no reflection occurs. The voltage and current propagate without distortion. $$V_{1,\text{load}} = V_2,\text{load} = 1 \text{ V}$$ $$I_{1,\text{load}} = I_{2,\text{load}} = \frac{V_e}{Z_{0e}} = \frac{1}{55} \approx 18.18 \text{ mA}$$ **Case (ii): Open-circuited far end** The reflection coefficient in even mode is: $$\Gamma_e = \frac{Z_L - Z_{0e}}{Z_L + Z_{0e}} = \frac{\infty - 55}{\infty + 55} = +1$$ The voltage at the open end doubles (full positive reflection): $$V_{1,\text{open}} = V_2,\text{open} = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ V}$$ The current at the open end is zero (open circuit boundary condition). **Practical implications:** - In high-speed design, an unmatched even-mode termination causes **common-mode voltage doubling** at the open end, leading to **EMI radiation** and possible **driver over-voltage stress**. - For **USB 2.0/3.0, HDMI, PCIe**, designers use **common-mode chokes** and **AC-coupling capacitors** to manage this reflected common-mode energy. - If $V_{cm}$ reflections build up, they can convert back to differential mode at asymmetries, **degrading the eye diagram** and increasing **bit error rate (BER)**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Examiner's Eye):** > 1. **Confusing $Z_{0e}$ and $Z_{0o}$ signs:** Many students write $Z_{0e} > Z_{0o}$ for microstrip without realising the *opposite* is true for microstrip — the sign depends on whether the dielectric is symmetric (stripline) or asymmetric (microstrip). For stripline, $Z_{0e} > Z_{0o}$; for microstrip, $Z_{0e} < Z_{0o}$. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** The common-mode impedance of a pair is $Z_{0e}/2$, **not** $Z_{0e}$. Students routinely lose 1 mark here. > 3. **Mixing up $C_{11} + C_{12}$ vs $C_{11} - C_{12}$:** The correct even-mode capacitance depends on whether you use $Z_{0e} = \sqrt{(L_s - L_m)/(C_s + C_m)}$ or $Z_{0e} = \sqrt{(L_s + L_m)/(C_s + 2C_m)}$. Both are correct *only* under the duality relation — examiners check this. > 4. **Units:** Per-unit-length $L$ is in **H/m** and $C$ in **F/m**. Frequently, students use nH and pF without converting. Always convert: $L(\text{H/m}) = L(\text{nH/m}) \times 10^{-9}$. > 5. **Coupling coefficient sign:** $k$ can be negative depending on convention. Quote $\vert k \vert$ when reporting. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Uniform Voltage Distribution = Even Mode** of an MTL. - All signal conductors at **identical potential**; currents equal and in-phase. - Return current flows entirely in the **ground plane** ($2I_e$). - **Even-mode impedance:** $Z_{0e} = \sqrt{(L_{11} - L_{12})/(C_{11} + C_{12})}$ (microstrip form). - **Odd-mode impedance:** $Z_{0o} = \sqrt{(L_{11} + L_{12})/C_{11}}$. - **Differential impedance:** $Z_{diff} = 2Z_{0o}$ (100 Ω typical for USB/HDMI). - **Common-mode impedance of pair:** $Z_{cm} = Z_{0e}/2$. - **Coupling coefficient:** $k = (Z_{0e} - Z_{0o})/(Z_{0e} + Z_{0o})$. - Microstrip: $Z_{0e} < Z_{0o}$. Stripline: $Z_{0e} > Z_{0o}$. - **Modal decomposition:** $V_e = (V_1 + V_2)/2$, $V_o = (V_1 - V_2)/2$. - **Reflection at open end (even mode):** $\Gamma_e = +1$ → voltage doubles. - **Phase velocity:** $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}$. - **Engineering rule:** for ≥ 5 Gbps, target $\vert k \vert \le 0.05$ and use **grounded coplanar waveguide (GCPW)** if coupling must be tightly controlled. - **Pitfall:** Always convert L (nH → H) and C (pF → F) before computing impedances. - **Application hotspot:** DDR, USB, HDMI, PCIe, SERDES — all depend on accurate uniform/differential mode analysis.

topics.title: Distribution Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distribution Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distribution Problems** in high-speed digital design refer to the engineering challenges that arise when a critical signal — most commonly a **clock signal**, but also strobe, enable, or reset — must be delivered from a single source to multiple loads (flip-flops, latches, ASICs, memory) on a printed circuit board (PCB) or across a multi-board system, while preserving tight **timing margins** in the presence of **transmission-line effects**, **mismatched impedances**, and **path-length differences**.

In the language of the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PEECT526 – High Speed Digital Design**, Module 4 treats the *clock* as the most demanding signal on a board because it is the only signal that switches *every* output simultaneously. Any imperfection in the way the clock is **distributed** becomes a direct limit on the maximum achievable data rate of the system. The principal quantities of interest are:

- **Clock Skew** $\Delta t_{skew}$ — spatial (point-to-point) difference in arrival times of the same clock edge at different loads.
- **Clock Jitter** $\Delta t_{jitter}$ — temporal (cycle-to-cycle / period) variation of a clock edge at a *single* load.
- **Timing Budget / Margin** $T_{margin}$ — the slack that remains after subtracting skew, jitter, setup, and hold requirements from the clock period $T_{clk}$.
- **Clock Distribution Topology** — the physical routing structure (H-tree, star, balanced tree, clock buffer tree) used to fan the clock out from its source to all loads.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Water-Pipeline Analogy:** Imagine a water reservoir (the clock generator) that must feed exactly the same pressure pulse, at exactly the same instant, to ten sprinklers in a garden. If the pipes are of different lengths, water reaches the far sprinklers later than the near ones — this is **skew**. If the pump itself delivers each pulse at slightly different intervals due to a worn-out valve — that is **jitter**. The plumber's job (the digital designer's job) is to lay the pipes so that every sprinkler opens and closes at *the same moment*, and to choose a pump that is steady and reliable.

In a high-speed digital board the "reservoir" is a **crystal oscillator / PLL**, the "pipes" are **PCB traces** acting as transmission lines, and the "sprinklers" are the **clock pins of every flip-flop and ASIC** on the board. The faster the system runs (the higher $f_{clk}$), the smaller the available time window per clock cycle, and the more damaging even a few **picoseconds of skew or jitter** become.

## 1.3 Why Distribution Problems Exist

Three physical realities conspire to make distribution a problem at high speeds:

1. **Finite propagation velocity** of signals on PCB traces — typically $v \approx c/2 \approx 6 \text{ in/ns}$ (≈ **150 mm/ns**) for a FR-4 microstrip. A 1 cm difference in trace length translates to ≈ **67 ps** of arrival-time difference.
2. **Dispersion and rise-time degradation** due to skin-effect resistance, dielectric loss (tan δ), and via stubs at multi-GHz frequencies.
3. **Impedance discontinuities** at the clock source, the buffer stages, the vias, the connectors, and the receiver die-pad, which cause **reflections** and **ringing** that disturb edge timing.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb (Howard Johnson):** When the trace length exceeds $\dfrac{t_r}{6 \cdot t_{pd}}$ — i.e., when the trace is electrically "long" — it must be treated as a **transmission line**, and termination becomes mandatory. For $t_r = 1\,\text{ns}$ and $t_{pd} = 150\,\text{ps/in}$, this critical length is ≈ **1.1 inch (≈ 2.8 cm)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Clock Distribution

Every distribution problem is a balance between three competing goals:

| Pillar | Engineering Goal | Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Skew Minimization** | All loads see the same edge at the same time | $\Delta t_{skew}$ in ps |
| **Jitter Minimization** | Each edge is precisely periodic at one load | $\Delta t_{jitter}$ in ps (rms / pk-pk) |
| **Signal Integrity** | Edges are clean (no ringing, no reflection) | $V_{overshoot}, V_{undershoot}, t_{r}, t_{f}$ |

## 2.2 Skew — Formal Definition and Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clock Skew** $\Delta t_{skew}$ is the difference in arrival time of the *same* clock edge at *two different* loads on the board.

$$\Delta t_{skew} = \mid t_{arrival,\,L1} - t_{arrival,\,L2} \mid$$

**Sources of Skew:**

1. **Trace-length mismatch** $\Delta L$ → contributes $\Delta t = \Delta L \cdot t_{pd}$.
2. **Mismatch in load capacitance** (different fan-out at each branch) → different RC delays.
3. **Mismatch in driver strength** if multiple buffers are used.
4. **Via stubs** — each via adds ≈ 1–2 pH + ≈ 0.3–0.5 pF, which distorts the rising edge and shifts the threshold crossing time.
5. **Process / temperature / voltage (PVT) variation** between identical clock buffers.

**Skew budget** for a synchronous system of period $T_{clk}$:

$$T_{clk} \geq t_{setup} + t_{hold} + t_{co} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter} + t_{margin}$$

Rearranging for the maximum allowable data rate:

$$f_{clk,\,max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{co} + t_{setup} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter} + t_{margin}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why skew matters:** Consider a 200 MHz system ($T_{clk} = 5\,\text{ns}$). A 100 ps skew consumes only 2 % of the period. The same 100 ps skew in a 1 GHz system ($T_{clk} = 1\,\text{ns}$) consumes 10 %. Skew is a *constant* timing tax that grows in importance linearly with frequency.

## 2.3 Jitter — Formal Definition and Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clock Jitter** is the short-term variation of a clock edge from its ideal position in time, measured at a *single* load. It is purely a **temporal** phenomenon, not a spatial one.

**Cycle-to-Cycle Jitter** $J_{cc}$:
$$J_{cc} = \mid T_{n+1} - T_{n} \mid$$

**Period Jitter** $J_{per}$:
$$J_{per} = \mid T_{n} - T_{nominal} \mid$$

**Phase Jitter** $J_{\phi}(f)$ — spectral density (SSB phase noise $L(f)$) integrated over an offset-frequency band:

$$J_{\phi,\,rms} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_0}\sqrt{2 \int_{f_1}^{f_2} 10^{L(f)/10}\, df}$$

**Time-Interval-Error (TIE) Jitter** $J_{TIE}$ — deviation of each edge from a recovered ideal clock.

> [!NOTE]
> **In a KTU answer, always specify which jitter you mean.** Examiners distinguish sharply between period, cycle-to-cycle, and phase jitter.

### 2.3.1 The Jitter Hierarchy (Practical PCB)

| Layer | Typical Jitter Contribution |
|---|---|
| Crystal / oscillator | 1 – 5 ps RMS |
| PLL / DLL | 5 – 30 ps RMS |
| Clock buffer (1-to-N fan-out) | 1 – 10 ps additive jitter |
| PCB trace (matched length ±1 mm) | ≈ 7 ps deterministic |
| Receiver pin / package | 1 – 5 ps |
| **Total at the die pad** | **~15 – 50 ps RMS** |

Jitter adds in **RMS** (random) and in **peak** (deterministic) — never in linear sum.

$$J_{total,\,RMS} = \sqrt{J_1^2 + J_2^2 + J_3^2 + \cdots}$$

$$J_{total,\,pk-pk} \approx J_{total,\,RMS} \times \text{BER multiplier} \approx 7 \times J_{total,\,RMS} \;\;(10^{-12}\text{ BER})$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Propagation delay on trace | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\,eff}}}{c} \cdot L$ | s | $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | Trace-length → time | $\Delta t = \Delta L \cdot t_{pd}$ | s | $\Delta L$ in m, $t_{pd}$ in s/m |
| 3 | Skew | $\Delta t_{skew} = \vert t_1 - t_2 \vert$ | s | Spatial (between two loads) |
| 4 | Critical length | $L_{crit} = \dfrac{t_r}{6\, t_{pd}}$ | m | Above this, treat as transmission line |
| 5 | Max clock freq (skew-limited) | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{co}+t_{su}+t_{skew}+t_{jit}}$ | Hz | Conservative design |
| 6 | RMS jitter sum | $J_{tot} = \sqrt{\sum J_i^2}$ | s | Random / uncorrelated |
| 7 | Peak-to-peak (Gaussian) | $J_{pk-pk} \approx 7\, J_{rms}$ | s | At $10^{-12}$ BER |
| 8 | Phase jitter from $L(f)$ | $J_{\phi} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \int 10^{L(f)/10}\, df}}{2\pi f_0}$ | s rad | Single-sideband phase noise |
| 9 | Characteristic impedance (microstrip) | $Z_0 = \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r+1.41}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t}\right)$ | $\Omega$ | $h$ = dielectric height |
| 10 | Timing margin | $T_m = T_{clk} - (t_{co} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jit} + t_{hold})$ | s | Must be ≥ 0 |
| 11 | Fan-out delay (lumped) | $t_{d} = 0.69 R_{out} C_L$ | s | First-order approximation |
| 12 | Edge rate after lossy line | $t_{r,\,out} = \sqrt{t_{r,\,in}^2 + (k \cdot L)^2}$ | s | $k \approx 0.5$–$1$ ps/mm at 5 GHz |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX delimiter reminder:** In the KTU exam script, write $\vert \cdot \vert$ (or *mod*) instead of the typewriter $\mid \cdot \mid$ in plain text. Never embed $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Clock distribution is the limiting factor in:

- **DDR5/LPDDR5x memory interfaces** where byte-level skew must be < 5 ps.
- **112 Gbps SerDes** channels where the recovered clock's RMS jitter must be < 150 fs.
- **AI accelerator ASICs** (GPU, TPU) where 500+ flip-flops share one clock domain with picosecond-level budgets.
- **FPGA fabric clocks** that traverse programmable interconnect with deterministic but non-zero delay.

> [!TIP]
> The same theory applies to **strobe distribution** in source-synchronous buses, **reset and power-good distribution**, and even **synchronizer chains**. Once you internalize the *budget equation*, every distribution problem is a special case of the same trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Skew-to-Length Conversion

**Problem restated:** A clock edge travels on a microstrip whose $t_{pd} = 6 \,\text{ps/mm}$. Two loads $L_1$ and $L_2$ are reached by traces of length $100.0\,\text{mm}$ and $104.5\,\text{mm}$ respectively. Find the skew.

**Step 1 — Identify the *delay* at each load.**

$$t_{L1} = L_1 \cdot t_{pd} = 100.0\,\text{mm} \times 6\,\text{ps/mm} = 600.0\,\text{ps}$$

$$t_{L2} = L_2 \cdot t_{pd} = 104.5\,\text{mm} \times 6\,\text{ps/mm} = 627.0\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the difference (this *is* the skew).**

$$\Delta t_{skew} = \vert t_{L1} - t_{L2} \vert = \vert 600.0 - 627.0 \vert = 27.0\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 3 — Sanity-check the dominant source.**

$$\Delta t_{skew} = \Delta L \cdot t_{pd} = 4.5\,\text{mm} \times 6\,\text{ps/mm} = 27\,\text{ps} \quad\checkmark$$

**Engineering insight:** A 4.5 mm length mismatch (about the size of a fingernail) produces 27 ps of skew. At 1 GHz, this alone consumes ≈ 2.7 % of the period *only for skew*. This is why **matched-length routing** is the very first rule of high-speed clock layout.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Operating Frequency with Skew + Jitter

**Given (a KTU-style problem):**
- Clock-to-output delay: $t_{co} = 0.30\,\text{ns}$
- Setup time of receiving FF: $t_{su} = 0.20\,\text{ns}$
- Hold time: $t_{hold} = 0.10\,\text{ns}$
- Total skew (PCB + buffer mismatch): $t_{skew} = 50\,\text{ps} = 0.05\,\text{ns}$
- Total RMS jitter: $t_{jitter,RMS} = 25\,\text{ps}$, peak-to-peak budgeted at $7 \sigma$: $t_{jitter,pk-pk} = 7 \times 25 = 175\,\text{ps} = 0.175\,\text{ns}$
- Required timing margin: $t_{margin} = 0.10\,\text{ns}$

**Step 1 — Write the budget equation.**

$$T_{clk} \geq t_{co} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter,pk-pk} + t_{margin}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numbers.**

$$T_{clk} \geq 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.05 + 0.175 + 0.10\,\text{ns} = 0.825\,\text{ns}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ceiling (the next "round" period) and convert to frequency.**

$$T_{clk} = 1.0\,\text{ns} \;\Rightarrow\; f_{clk,\,max} = \dfrac{1}{1.0\times 10^{-9}} = 1.0\,\text{GHz}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the *ideal* no-skew, no-jitter case.**

$$f_{ideal} = \dfrac{1}{0.30 + 0.20 + 0.10} = \dfrac{1}{0.60\,\text{ns}} \approx 1.67\,\text{GHz}$$

**Step 5 — Quantify the loss.**

$$\text{Loss} = \dfrac{1.67 - 1.00}{1.67} = 40\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **40 % of the theoretical bandwidth is consumed by distribution imperfections.** This single calculation is the *most important number* in any clock-distribution design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — H-Tree Skew Minimization

**Concept:** An **H-tree** is a recursive, symmetric, fractal routing topology that equalizes trace length from the source to *every* leaf by construction.

**Geometry:** At level 1, the trunk splits into 2 arms of length $L/2$. Each arm splits into 2 sub-arms of length $L/4$, and so on. After $k$ levels, there are $2^k$ leaves, all at the *same* total path length $L$.

**Total path length from root to any leaf:**

$$L_{total} = \dfrac{L}{2} + \dfrac{L}{4} + \dfrac{L}{8} + \cdots + \dfrac{L}{2^k} = L\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^k}\right)$$

**Number of leaves:**

$$N = 2^k$$

**Skew of an ideal H-tree** (geometry only, no driver mismatch):

$$\Delta t_{skew,\,H-tree} = 0 \quad \text{(by symmetry)}$$

**Skew budget under realistic perturbations** (PVT, dielectric variation, length-tuning tolerance $\delta L$):

$$\Delta t_{skew,\,real} = \delta L \cdot t_{pd} \cdot N_{layers} \cdot \alpha_{PVT}$$

where $\alpha_{PVT} \approx 1.2$ is a typical PVT derating factor.

**Numerical example:** $N_{layers} = 5$, $\delta L = 0.5\,\text{mm}$, $t_{pd} = 6\,\text{ps/mm}$, $\alpha_{PVT} = 1.2$.

$$\Delta t_{skew} = 0.5 \times 6 \times 5 \times 1.2 = 18\,\text{ps}$$

This is the practical, achievable skew on a well-designed H-tree at gigabit rates.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — RMS Jitter Combination

**Given:** Three independent jitter sources, each 10 ps RMS. Find the total RMS jitter.

**Step 1 — Apply the quadratic (RSS) sum rule.**

$$J_{tot} = \sqrt{J_1^2 + J_2^2 + J_3^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$J_{tot} = \sqrt{10^2 + 10^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{300} = 17.32\,\text{ps RMS}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to peak-to-peak (BER $10^{-12}$).**

$$J_{pk-pk} \approx 7 \times 17.32 = 121.2\,\text{ps}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with naïve arithmetic sum.**

$$J_{arith} = 10 + 10 + 10 = 30\,\text{ps RMS} \;\Rightarrow\; 210\,\text{ps pk-pk}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The naïve arithmetic sum is wrong by a factor of 1.7×.** Examiners specifically look for the **RSS rule** when adding random jitters. Lose a mark if you write $J_{tot} = J_1 + J_2 + J_3$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Distribution Topology

| Topology | Skew | Jitter | Routing Cost | Power | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **H-Tree** | Excellent (≈ 0 by design) | Low | High (length-matching) | Moderate | Multi-GHz ASIC, FPGA fabric |
| **Star (1-to-N point-to-point)** | Excellent if matched | Lowest | Very high | Moderate | 4–8 loads, low density |
| **Balanced Clock Buffer Tree** | Good | Adds buffer jitter | Lowest | Higher (more buffers) | Mid-range PCB (DDR, busses) |
| **Daisy Chain** | Poor (skew = $N \cdot t_{pd}$) | Lowest | Lowest | Lowest | < 100 MHz, non-critical |
| **PLL-driven mesh** | Excellent | Depends on PLL | Highest | High | 100+ loads, large ASICs |

## 3.6 Worked Example (Full KTU-style Problem)

> **Problem (KTU-style 14-mark):** A 1 GHz digital system uses a clock that must be distributed to 8 loads through an H-tree. Each leaf branch has $t_{pd} = 6\,\text{ps/mm}$. The total trace length from the source to each load is 150 mm. The clock buffer contributes 8 ps additive jitter RMS. The oscillator contributes 3 ps RMS. The PCB has matched length to within ±1 mm. The receiver adds 2 ps RMS. The serialiser has $t_{co} = 0.20\,\text{ns}$ and the deserialiser has $t_{su} = 0.15\,\text{ns}$. Calculate: (a) the skew, (b) the total jitter (RMS and pk-pk), (c) the timing margin.

### (a) Skew

$$\Delta t_{skew} = \delta L \cdot t_{pd} = 1\,\text{mm} \times 6\,\text{ps/mm} = 6\,\text{ps}$$

### (b) Jitter

$$J_{tot,RMS} = \sqrt{3^2 + 8^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{9 + 64 + 4} = \sqrt{77} \approx 8.77\,\text{ps RMS}$$

$$J_{tot,pk-pk} \approx 7 \times 8.77 = 61.4\,\text{ps pk-pk}$$

### (c) Timing Margin

$$T_{margin} = T_{clk} - (t_{co} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter,pk-pk})$$

$$T_{margin} = 1000 - (200 + 150 + 6 + 61.4)\,\text{ps} = 582.6\,\text{ps}$$

The system has a comfortable **582 ps of margin** — about 58 % of the clock period — making it a robust design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 H-Tree Clock Distribution Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC["Clock Source\nCrystal / PLL"]:::src
    SPL1["Splitter\nLevel 1"]:::buf
    SPL2L["Splitter\nLevel 2 Left"]:::buf
    SPL2R["Splitter\nLevel 2 Right"]:::buf
    SPL3LL["Splitter L3 LL"]:::buf
    SPL3LR["Splitter L3 LR"]:::buf
    SPL3RL["Splitter L3 RL"]:::buf
    SPL3RR["Splitter L3 RR"]:::buf
    L1["Load FF 1"]:::leaf
    L2["Load FF 2"]:::leaf
    L3["Load FF 3"]:::leaf
    L4["Load FF 4"]:::leaf
    L5["Load FF 5"]:::leaf
    L6["Load FF 6"]:::leaf
    L7["Load FF 7"]:::leaf
    L8["Load FF 8"]:::leaf

    SRC --> SPL1
    SPL1 --> SPL2L
    SPL1 --> SPL2R
    SPL2L --> SPL3LL
    SPL2L --> SPL3LR
    SPL2R --> SPL3RL
    SPL2R --> SPL3RR
    SPL3LL --> L1
    SPL3LL --> L2
    SPL3LR --> L3
    SPL3LR --> L4
    SPL3RL --> L5
    SPL3RL --> L6
    SPL3RR --> L7
    SPL3RR --> L8

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef buf fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The clock is fanned out in three binary splits (1 → 2 → 4 → 8). Every leaf is at the *same total path length* from the source by geometric construction. This is the mathematical reason that **H-trees are inherently low-skew**.

## 4.2 Star Distribution Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC["Clock Source"]:::src
    BUF["1-to-N Buffer"]:::buf
    L1["Load 1"]:::leaf
    L2["Load 2"]:::leaf
    L3["Load 3"]:::leaf
    L4["Load 4"]:::leaf
    L5["Load 5"]:::leaf
    L6["Load 6"]:::leaf

    SRC --> BUF
    BUF --> L1
    BUF --> L2
    BUF --> L3
    BUF --> L4
    BUF --> L5
    BUF --> L6

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef buf fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a **star**, all loads emanate from a single central point. Skew is the *trace-length mismatch* between the buffer output and each load. Star is excellent for 4–8 loads but breaks down for high-fan-out designs because every trace has to be length-tuned manually.

## 4.3 Daisy-Chain Distribution (The Anti-Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Clock Source"]:::src
    BUF1["Buffer"]:::buf
    BUF2["Buffer"]:::buf
    BUF3["Buffer"]:::buf
    L1["Load 1"]:::leaf
    L2["Load 2"]:::leaf
    L3["Load 3"]:::leaf
    L4["Load 4"]:::leaf

    SRC --> BUF1 --> BUF2 --> BUF3
    BUF1 --> L1
    BUF2 --> L2
    BUF3 --> L3
    BUF3 --> L4

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef buf fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

> [!WARNING]
> The **daisy chain** introduces cumulative skew. Load 4 sees the clock after the cumulative delay of three buffers. With three 50-ps buffers, the worst-case skew is ≈ **150 ps** — unacceptable above 500 MHz. This topology is restricted to slow, non-timing-critical signals.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Clock Distribution Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source["Signal Source"]
        XTL["Crystal\nOscillator"]:::src
        PLL["PLL\nMultiplier"]:::buf
    end

    subgraph Distribution["Distribution Network"]
        DRV["Clock\nDriver"]:::buf
        NET["Matched-Length\nPCB Traces"]:::buf
        TERM["Termination\n50 Ohm"]:::buf
    end

    subgraph Loads["Receiver Loads"]
        FF1["FF / Latch 1"]:::leaf
        FF2["FF / Latch 2"]:::leaf
        FF3["FF / Latch 3"]:::leaf
        FFM["FF / Latch M"]:::leaf
    end

    XTL --> PLL --> DRV --> NET --> TERM
    NET --> FF1
    NET --> FF2
    NET --> FF3
    NET --> FFM

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef buf fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level interpretation:** The **crystal** provides the fundamental reference (1–50 MHz). The **PLL** multiplies it up to the system clock (e.g., 1 GHz). The **clock driver** provides the fan-out current to charge all the load capacitances. The **matched-length traces** equalize path delays. The **series or parallel termination** damps reflections. Each block contributes to the total jitter budget in a quantifiable way.

## 4.5 Skew and Jitter — Visual Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDX["Ideal Clock"]
        ID1["Edge 1: t = 0.000 ns"]:::leaf
        ID2["Edge 2: t = 1.000 ns"]:::leaf
        ID3["Edge 3: t = 2.000 ns"]:::leaf
    end
    subgraph SK["Skew at Load B"]
        SK1["Edge 1: t = 0.000 ns"]:::buf
        SK2["Edge 2: t = 1.020 ns"]:::buf
        SK3["Edge 3: t = 2.040 ns"]:::buf
    end
    subgraph JT["Jitter at Load A"]
        JT1["Edge 1: t = 0.000 ns"]:::leaf
        JT2["Edge 2: t = 0.985 ns"]:::leaf
        JT3["Edge 3: t = 2.015 ns"]:::leaf
    end
    IDX -.- SK
    IDX -.- JT

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef buf fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Skew** shifts the *entire* edge train uniformly (a constant time offset). **Jitter** makes each edge arrive at a *slightly different* time. The two are independent and must be added (skew linearly, jitter in RMS) to the timing budget.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEECT526]`

**Q.** Define *clock skew* and *clock jitter*. How do they differ from each other in terms of their measurement location and physical origin?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Clock Skew** is the *spatial* difference in arrival time of the *same* clock edge at *two different* loads on the board. It originates from **trace-length mismatch**, **load-capacitance mismatch**, and **PVT variations between identical buffers** (1 mark).
- **Clock Jitter** is the *temporal* variation of a clock edge at a *single* load, from one cycle to the next. It originates from **PLL phase noise**, **supply noise modulating buffer delay**, **thermal noise**, and **crosstalk** (1 mark).
- **Key difference:** Skew is measured *between two loads* and is mostly *deterministic* (and correctable by length matching); jitter is measured *at one load* and is mostly *random* (and correctable only by better components) (1 mark).

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEECT526]`

**Q.** What is an H-tree clock distribution? Why is it preferred for high-speed multi-load clock distribution?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- An **H-tree** is a recursive binary-branching routing structure in which the clock source sits at the *root* of an "H", and at each level the trace splits into two equal-length arms, recursively (1 mark).
- It is preferred because, by geometric construction, **every leaf is at the same total path length** from the source. This makes the *intrinsic* skew equal to **zero**, leaving only the much smaller PVT and length-tuning tolerance skew (1 mark).
- The structure scales to $2^k$ loads with a regular, repeatable layout, and is well-suited to **ASIC, FPGA, and multi-DDR clock distribution at gigabit speeds** (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEECT526 Module 4]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **timing budget** of a synchronous clocked system including skew, jitter, setup, hold, and clock-to-output delays. State the units and identify each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 500 MHz clock ($T_{clk} = 2.0$ ns) is distributed to 16 loads via an H-tree on an FR-4 board. Each microstrip segment has $t_{pd} = 6 \,\text{ps/mm}$. The maximum length mismatch between any two paths is **±2 mm**. The clock buffer has an additive RMS jitter of **10 ps**, the PLL adds **15 ps RMS**, the receiver adds **5 ps RMS**. Compute: (i) the geometric skew, (ii) the total RMS jitter, (iii) the total pk-pk jitter at BER $10^{-12}$, (iv) the achievable timing margin if $t_{co} = 0.30$ ns and $t_{su} = 0.20$ ns. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The fundamental constraint of a synchronous system is that the data launched by the launching flip-flop on the *current* clock edge must be captured by the receiving flip-flop on the *next* clock edge. The available time is one clock period $T_{clk}$. The time consumed by various delays and uncertainties must be subtracted. The **timing budget** is therefore:

$$T_{clk} \geq t_{co} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter,pk-pk} + t_{margin}$$

(2 marks for stating the equation, 1 mark each for identifying the following four terms:)

- $t_{co}$ — **clock-to-output delay** of the launching FF, in **ns** — the time from the active clock edge to valid data at the output.
- $t_{su}$ — **setup time** of the receiving FF, in **ns** — the time data must be stable *before* the receiving clock edge.
- $t_{skew}$ — **clock skew**, in **ps**, the worst-case spatial difference between the launching and receiving clock edges.
- $t_{jitter,pk-pk}$ — **peak-to-peak clock jitter**, in **ps**, the worst-case cycle-to-cycle variation of either clock edge.
- $t_{margin}$ — **safety margin**, in **ps**, the guard band left over for unforeseen effects.

Rearranging for the *achievable* maximum frequency (1 mark):

$$f_{clk,\,max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{co} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter,pk-pk} + t_{margin}}$$

This single equation captures the *entire* essence of clock distribution design: every picosecond of skew and jitter directly subtracts from the data rate. (1 mark for the engineering conclusion.)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Geometric skew (1 mark):**

The maximum path-length mismatch is $\pm 2\,\text{mm}$, so the worst-case differential length is:

$$\Delta L = 4\,\text{mm}$$

$$\Delta t_{skew} = \Delta L \cdot t_{pd} = 4 \times 6 = 24\,\text{ps}$$

**(ii) Total RMS jitter (2 marks):**

Using the root-sum-square (RSS) rule for uncorrelated random jitters:

$$J_{tot,RMS} = \sqrt{10^2 + 15^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{100 + 225 + 25} = \sqrt{350} = 18.71\,\text{ps RMS}$$

**(iii) Peak-to-peak jitter at BER $10^{-12}$ (1 mark):**

For a Gaussian-distributed random jitter, the peak-to-peak at BER $10^{-12}$ is approximately $7\sigma$:

$$J_{tot,pk-pk} = 7 \times 18.71 = 131.0\,\text{ps pk-pk}$$

**(iv) Achievable timing margin (3 marks):**

$$T_{margin} = T_{clk} - (t_{co} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter,pk-pk})$$

$$T_{margin} = 2000\,\text{ps} - (300 + 200 + 24 + 131)\,\text{ps}$$

$$T_{margin} = 2000 - 655 = 1345\,\text{ps} = 1.345\,\text{ns}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comment on the result:** The margin is **67 % of the clock period** — a very healthy design. If, however, the designer had chosen a daisy-chain distribution, an additional 200 ps of skew would have pushed the margin below 30 %, an unsafe design.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEECT526 Module 4]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the **H-tree clock distribution topology**. Show mathematically that *every* leaf sees the same path length from the source. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clock distribution network on a high-speed board uses **three identical clock buffers** cascaded in a daisy chain. Each buffer contributes 25 ps of propagation delay and 5 ps of additive RMS jitter. The PCB trace between buffers has $t_{pd} = 6\,\text{ps/mm}$ and is 50 mm long. The clock must be delivered to four loads, with the last load being 50 mm beyond the third buffer. Calculate: (i) the **skew** between the first and last load, (ii) the **total RMS jitter** at the last load, (iii) the **maximum clock frequency** if $t_{co} = 0.30$ ns, $t_{su} = 0.20$ ns, and a 10 % timing margin is required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

An H-tree is constructed recursively. At the first level, the source trace of length $L$ splits into two equal arms of length $L/2$. Each arm, in turn, splits into two equal sub-arms of length $L/4$, and so on for $k$ levels, producing $2^k$ leaves. (2 marks for diagram + verbal description, 1 mark for the node-identifier `SRC/SPL1/...` shown below.)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC["Clock Source"]:::src
    A1["L/2 branch"]:::buf
    A2["L/2 branch"]:::buf
    B1["L/4 branch"]:::buf
    B2["L/4 branch"]:::buf
    B3["L/4 branch"]:::buf
    B4["L/4 branch"]:::buf
    Lf1["Load"]:::leaf
    Lf2["Load"]:::leaf
    Lf3["Load"]:::leaf
    Lf4["Load"]:::leaf
    SRC --> A1 --> B1
    SRC --> A1 --> B2
    SRC --> A2 --> B3
    SRC --> A2 --> B4
    B1 --> Lf1
    B1 --> Lf2
    B2 --> Lf3
    B2 --> Lf4

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef buf fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

**Mathematical proof of equal path length (3 marks):**

The total path from source to any leaf is the sum of segment lengths along the unique route:

$$L_{total} = \dfrac{L}{2} + \dfrac{L}{4} + \dfrac{L}{8} + \cdots + \dfrac{L}{2^k}$$

This is a finite geometric series with first term $a_1 = L/2$ and ratio $r = 1/2$, summed over $k$ terms:

$$L_{total} = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1/2)^k}{1 - 1/2} = L\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^k}\right)$$

Since $L$ and $k$ are the same for every leaf, $L_{total}$ is **identical for all $2^k$ leaves**, which implies *zero geometric skew*. (1 mark for the engineering interpretation.)

**Engineering conclusion (1 mark):** This is the mathematical reason the H-tree is the topology of choice for clock distribution in high-speed ASICs and FPGAs.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Skew between first and last load (2 marks):**

The first load sees the clock after the first buffer's delay (assuming it taps off the input of BUF1, otherwise after BUF1):

$$t_{L1} = 25\,\text{ps (buffer)} + 50\,\text{mm} \times 6\,\text{ps/mm} + 0 = 25 + 300 + 0 = 325\,\text{ps from source}$$

The last load sees the clock after the cumulative delays of BUF1, BUF2, BUF3, plus the trace between each, plus the final 50 mm trace:

$$t_{Llast} = 3 \times 25 + 2 \times 300 + 300 = 75 + 600 + 300 = 975\,\text{ps from source}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta t_{skew} = 975 - 325 = 650\,\text{ps}}$$

**(ii) Total RMS jitter at the last load (2 marks):**

Each of the three buffers adds 5 ps RMS jitter *independently*. Using RSS:

$$J_{tot,RMS} = \sqrt{5^2 + 5^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{75} = 8.66\,\text{ps RMS}$$

**(iii) Maximum clock frequency (3 marks):**

Convert to pk-pk (BER $10^{-12}$):

$$J_{pk-pk} = 7 \times 8.66 = 60.6\,\text{ps}$$

Apply the timing budget with a 10 % margin on the period:

$$T_{margin} = 0.10 \cdot T_{clk} \;\Rightarrow\; T_{clk} = 1.111\,(t_{co} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter})$$

$$0.9\,T_{clk} = t_{co} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter,pk-pk} = 300 + 200 + 650 + 60.6 = 1210.6\,\text{ps}$$

$$T_{clk} = \dfrac{1210.6}{0.9} = 1345.1\,\text{ps} = 1.345\,\text{ns}$$

$$\boxed{f_{clk,\,max} = \dfrac{1}{1.345\,\text{ns}} \approx 743\,\text{MHz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Daisy-Chain Dangers:**
> 1. Students often **forget to multiply** the trace $t_{pd}$ by the *number of* segments, not just one. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. Students frequently **add the jitters arithmetically** ($5+5+5 = 15$ ps) instead of using RSS ($\sqrt{75} = 8.66$ ps). Lose 1 mark.
> 3. Students often **omit the 7σ conversion** between RMS and pk-pk, leading to an unrealistically small $f_{max}$. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. Students forget to **subtract the 10 % margin** from the available period. Lose 1 mark.
> 5. Students ignore the **hold-time** constraint. Although not asked here, on a real exam, hold-time is checked against the *minimum* path delay and is independent of $T_{clk}$. Including a hold-time check is a bonus mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Distribution Problems**

- ☐ **Clock Skew** = *spatial*; **Clock Jitter** = *temporal*. They are **not the same thing**. (Most-frequently-asked definition.)
- ☐ The **master timing equation** is $T_{clk} \geq t_{co} + t_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter,pk-pk} + t_{margin}$. Memorize it.
- ☐ $t_{pd} \approx 6$–$7\,\text{ps/mm}$ for FR-4 microstrip; $t_{pd} \approx 7$–$8\,\text{ps/mm}$ for stripline. Use 6 for quick hand-calculations.
- ☐ **Critical length** $L_{crit} = t_r / (6\, t_{pd})$. Above this, treat as a transmission line.
- ☐ **Length-mismatch → skew** is the simplest and most common design lever: every **1 mm of mismatch = 6 ps of skew** on FR-4.
- ☐ **Random jitters add in RSS** (root-sum-square). **Deterministic jitters add in peak**.
- ☐ $J_{pk-pk} \approx 7 \cdot J_{RMS}$ at BER $10^{-12}$. The "×7 rule".
- ☐ **H-tree** = zero geometric skew by design. Best for $N = 2^k$ loads.
- ☐ **Star** = excellent for 4–8 loads with central buffer. Costly to route.
- ☐ **Daisy chain** = **forbidden** at gigabit speeds. Skew grows linearly with depth.
- ☐ **Buffer tree** = practical compromise. Each buffer adds ≈ 1–10 ps RMS jitter and 25–100 ps delay.
- ☐ **Termination** ($R_s$ series or $R_t$ parallel, both = $Z_0$ typically 50 Ω) prevents ringing that *itself* creates timing noise.
- ☐ **Hold time** is checked against the *shortest* path, **setup time** against the *longest* path. They are *not* symmetric constraints.
- ☐ In KTU 14-mark problems, *always* state **units** in every numerical answer (ps, ns, mm, m). Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark.
- ☐ In KTU 14-mark problems, *always* show the **RSS arithmetic** explicitly: $\sqrt{10^2 + 15^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{350}$, not just the final 18.7 ps.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** When you see a "distribution" question in the KTU exam, write down the **timing budget equation first** (1 mark), **list the numerical values with units** (1 mark), and **show every substitution step** (remaining marks). This pattern alone typically secures **≥ 12 of 14 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choosing a Bypass Capacitor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Choosing a Bypass Capacitor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bypass Capacitor** (also referred to as a **Decoupling Capacitor** in KTU high-speed digital design literature) is a passive energy-storage component placed in close physical proximity to the power pins of an active integrated circuit (IC). Its primary function is to provide a low-impedance local source of charge that can supply transient switching currents demanded by the IC, thereby *bypassing* (short-circuiting) high-frequency noise on the power distribution network (PDN) to ground. The capacitor effectively isolates the IC from the inductance of the PCB power/ground planes and the bulk supply, ensuring the rail-to-rail voltage at the die remains within the **noise tolerance budget** (typically **5%–10%** of the nominal supply voltage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The bypass capacitor is *not* primarily an energy storage device at DC — at DC, the supply rail is maintained by the voltage regulator. The bypass capacitor is a **high-frequency impedance controller** for the PDN. The KTU examiner expects students to explicitly state that the goal is to keep the **Power Distribution Network Impedance (Z_PDN) below the Target Impedance (Z_target)** across the entire frequency band of interest.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Tower" Analogy — Plain English Explanation**
>
> Imagine your home is connected to a municipal water supply. When you suddenly open multiple taps simultaneously (a *transient load*), the pressure at the taps momentarily drops because the main pipe is narrow and long, and water cannot travel fast enough from the reservoir. To fix this, plumbers install a **small overhead water tank** near your house. Even though the main pipe is far away, the local tank immediately releases stored water, keeping tap pressure steady.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Municipal Reservoir** ↔ Bulk voltage regulator / power supply
> - **Long Narrow Pipe** ↔ Inductive PCB trace / plane pair
> - **Overhead Tank** ↔ Bypass Capacitor
> - **Sudden Tap Opening** ↔ Simultaneous gate switching inside an IC
> - **Stable Tap Pressure** ↔ Stable V_DD at the IC die
>
> The bypass capacitor is the *local water tank* — it does not generate water, it only *stores and releases* it on demand. The "size" of the tank (capacitance) determines how long it can sustain the demand, while the "pipe diameter connecting the tank" (ESL/ESR of capacitor + via inductance) determines how *fast* it can respond.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in KTU Boards

- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- Relative permittivity of common PCB FR-4: **εᵣ ≈ 4.3 (at 1 MHz)**, drops to **~3.8** above **1 GHz**
- Standard logic supply: **V_DD = 3.3 V, 2.5 V, 1.8 V, 1.2 V**
- Typical noise tolerance: **±5 %** (sometimes ±2.5 % for high-speed SerDes)
- Standard capacitor packages (imperial): **0402, 0603, 0805, 1206**
- Common MLCC dielectrics: **C0G/NP0, X7R, X5R, Y5V, Z5U**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Origin of the Bypass Capacitor's Necessity

When an IC core switches its internal logic gates, it draws a sudden burst of current (di/dt transient). The PCB power/ground planes, bond wires, package pins, and vias all possess **parasitic inductance (L)** and **resistance (R)**. Applying the fundamental V = L (di/dt) relation, this inductance converts the fast current step into a voltage spike — a *supply droop* or *ground bounce* — which corrupts logic levels and causes timing jitter. The bypass capacitor counteracts this by providing a low-impedance, *locally resonant* charge reservoir.

### 2.2 The Non-Ideal Capacitor Model

A real bypass capacitor is **NOT** a pure capacitance. It must be modelled as a series RLC network:

- **C** = Nominal capacitance (energy storage element)
- **ESR (Equivalent Series Resistance)** = Loss component from leads, plates, and dielectric. Typical MLCC ESR is in **milliohms**.
- **ESL (Equivalent Series Inductance)** = Parasitic inductance from package geometry, traces, and vias. Typical values: **0.5 nH – 10 nH** depending on package size.

The complete impedance equation of a real capacitor:

$$
Z(j\omega) = ESR + j\omega \cdot ESL + \frac{1}{j\omega \cdot C}
$$

$$
Z(j\omega) = ESR + j\left(\omega \cdot ESL - \frac{1}{\omega \cdot C}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Choosing" is a Multi-Dimensional Problem:** Choosing a bypass capacitor in KTU 2024 scheme is not about picking the *largest* µF. It is a trade-off across **six orthogonal axes**: (1) Capacitance, (2) ESL, (3) ESR, (4) Dielectric type / DC-bias derating, (5) Voltage rating, and (6) Physical package size (which sets ESL). The examiner will award marks for *justifying* the choice against each axis.

### 2.3 The Target Impedance — The Foundation of Selection

The single most important design target in bypass capacitor selection is the **Target Impedance (Z_target)**. It is defined as the maximum allowable impedance of the PDN at any frequency.

$$
Z_{target} = \frac{V_{DD} \times \text{ripple tolerance}}{I_{transient}}
$$

Where:
- **V_DD** = Nominal supply voltage (Volts)
- **Ripple tolerance** = Fractional allowed noise (e.g., 0.05 for 5 %)
- **I_transient** = Maximum simultaneous switching current (Amps)

**Design Rule:** For the PDN to remain functional, the impedance offered by the *parallel combination* of all bypass capacitors + planes + bulk must satisfy:

$$
Z_{PDN}(f) \leq Z_{target} \quad \forall f \in [f_{low}, f_{high}]
$$

### 2.4 Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)

At the frequency where the inductive reactance equals the capacitive reactance, the capacitor behaves as a pure resistance equal to its ESR. This is the **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)**.

$$
f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C}}
$$

At f < f_SRF: capacitor is *capacitive* (Z decreases with frequency).
At f = f_SRF: |Z| minimum = ESR.
At f > f_SRF: capacitor is *inductive* (Z increases with frequency).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume a 100 nF capacitor "decouples" up to 1 GHz. In reality, due to ESL (~1 nH in a 0603 package), the SRF is around **~16 MHz**. Above this, the component is *inductive* and stops being a capacitor. This is why **multiple capacitor values are placed in parallel** — to extend effective decoupling across decades of frequency.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Expression | Units | Typical Range / KTU Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Target Impedance | $Z_{target} = V_{DD} \cdot \text{tol} / I_{transient}$ | Ω | 0.01 Ω – 1 Ω |
| 2 | Capacitor Impedance | $Z = ESR + j(\omega L - 1/\omega C)$ | Ω | Function of f |
| 3 | Self-Resonant Frequency | $f_{SRF} = 1 / (2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ | Hz | kHz – GHz |
| 4 | Energy Stored | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Joules | µJ – mJ |
| 5 | Charge Delivered per Cycle | $\Delta Q = I_{avg} \cdot \Delta t_{sw}$ | Coulombs | nC – µC |
| 6 | Voltage Droop (Capacitor-limited) | $\Delta V = I_{transient} \cdot \Delta t / C$ | Volts | mV range |
| 7 | Anti-Resonance Peak Frequency | $f_{anti} \approx \sqrt{f_{SRF1} \cdot f_{SRF2}}$ | Hz | Geometric mean of two SRFs |
| 8 | Required # of Caps (parallel) | $N = Z_{cap,min} / Z_{target}$ | dimensionless | 1 – 50 |
| 9 | Inductance of a Via (approx) | $L_{via} \approx 5 \text{ nH/mm}$ | nH/mm | Geometry-dependent |
| 10 | Plane Capacitance (per unit area) | $C_{plane} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | F | pF – nF range |

> **Note:** The vertical bar symbol has been intentionally replaced with `\vert` or kept as `j` notation in the table to avoid markdown pipe conflicts.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In commercial PCB design (Intel, AMD, NVIDIA, Qualcomm reference boards), choosing bypass capacitors is a multi-million-dollar industry concern. A poorly decoupled **PCIe Gen5** SerDes running at **32 GT/s** will exhibit Bit-Error-Rate (BER) explosions, eye-diagram closure, and link training failure. **Automotive ECU** designers must derate X7R capacitors for **DC-bias loss** (a 100 nF 0402 X7R at 5 V may behave as 50 nF). **Aerospace** designs prohibit use of Class-II/III dielectrics due to piezo-electric vibration sensitivity. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly expects students to identify the application context.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Self-Resonant Frequency of a Bypass Capacitor

**Given:** A real capacitor with C = 100 nF, ESL = 1 nH (typical 0603 MLCC).

**Step 1 —** Write the complex impedance of the series RLC model.

$$
Z(j\omega) = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C}
$$

**Step 2 —** Combine the reactive terms over a common denominator.

$$
Z(j\omega) = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)
$$

**Step 3 —** The Self-Resonant Frequency is defined as the frequency where the net reactance equals zero. Set the imaginary part to zero.

$$
\omega_{SRF} L - \frac{1}{\omega_{SRF} C} = 0
$$

**Step 4 —** Solve for ω_SRF algebraically.

$$
\omega_{SRF} L = \frac{1}{\omega_{SRF} C}
$$

$$
\omega_{SRF}^{2} = \frac{1}{L C}
$$

$$
\omega_{SRF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}
$$

**Step 5 —** Convert from angular frequency to ordinary frequency using ω = 2πf.

$$
2\pi f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
$$

$$
f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}
$$

**Step 6 —** Substitute numerical values.

$$
f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(1 \times 10^{-9})(100 \times 10^{-9})}}
$$

$$
f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-16}}}
$$

$$
f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
f_{SRF} \approx 15.92 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Conclusion:** This 100 nF / 0603 capacitor is *only* effective as a bypass capacitor below ~16 MHz. Above this, it behaves as an inductor and fails its function.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Computing the Required Number of Bypass Capacitors in Parallel

**Given:** A digital ASIC with V_DD = 1.8 V, ±5 % ripple tolerance, maximum simultaneous switching current I_transient = 2 A. The chosen capacitor has Z_cap,min = 0.2 Ω at its SRF.

**Step 1 —** Compute the Target Impedance.

$$
Z_{target} = \frac{V_{DD} \times \text{tolerance}}{I_{transient}}
$$

$$
Z_{target} = \frac{1.8 \times 0.05}{2}
$$

$$
Z_{target} = \frac{0.09}{2}
$$

$$
Z_{target} = 0.045 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the number of capacitors required in parallel. Capacitors in parallel combine as conductances, so impedance scales as **1/N**.

$$
Z_{parallel} = \frac{Z_{cap,min}}{N} \leq Z_{target}
$$

**Step 3 —** Solve for N.

$$
N \geq \frac{Z_{cap,min}}{Z_{target}}
$$

$$
N \geq \frac{0.2}{0.045}
$$

$$
N \geq 4.44
$$

**Step 4 —** Round up to the next integer (you cannot place 0.44 of a capacitor).

$$
N = 5 \text{ capacitors}
$$

**Step 5 —** Verify the resulting PDN impedance.

$$
Z_{PDN} = \frac{0.2}{5} = 0.04 \text{ Ω} \leq 0.045 \text{ Ω} \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Charge-Required Sizing for a Single Switching Event

**Given:** An IC core draws I_avg = 500 mA during a switching event of duration Δt = 2 ns. Available rail droop budget ΔV_max = 90 mV.

**Step 1 —** Express the charge requirement.

$$
\Delta Q = I_{avg} \times \Delta t
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute values.

$$
\Delta Q = (0.5) \times (2 \times 10^{-9})
$$

$$
\Delta Q = 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C} = 1 \text{ nC}
$$

**Step 3 —** Relate charge to capacitance using Q = C·V.

$$
C_{min} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta V_{max}}
$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute values.

$$
C_{min} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{90 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
C_{min} = 1.11 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F}
$$

$$
C_{min} \approx 11.1 \text{ nF}
$$

**Step 5 —** Design decision: choose the next standard EIA value above 11.1 nF, which is **15 nF** or **22 nF**. To include margin, select **22 nF**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Capacitor Selection Decision Table

| Selection Axis | Question to Ask | Effect of Increasing | Typical KTU-Recommended Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitance (C) | How much charge must be stored? | Lower frequency coverage | Bulk: 10 µF – 100 µF electrolytic |
| ESL | What is the highest frequency? | Lowers SRF, limits high-freq | Smaller package (0402 < 0603) |
| ESR | Is damping required? | Higher ESR = more damping | Low-ESR MLCC for high-freq |
| Dielectric | Stability needed? | Y5V = unstable; C0G = stable | X7R (general), C0G (precision) |
| Voltage Rating | V_DD margin required? | Larger packages | 2× V_DD minimum |
| Package | ESL minimization? | Bigger → more ESL | 0402 / 0201 for >100 MHz |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bypass Capacitor in the PDN

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the high-level flow of current from the voltage regulator module (VRM) to the IC die, with the bypass capacitor acting as a *local current source* for high-frequency transients.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VRM Voltage Regulator] -->|Low-freq DC supply, high inductance| B[PCB Power Plane Pair]
    B -->|Parasitic L and R| C[IC Package Pin and Bondwire]
    C --> D[IC Internal Die VDD Rail]
    E[Bulk Bypass Capacitor 10uF to 100uF] -->|Low-freq decoupling, f less than 1 MHz| B
    F[Mid-freq MLCC 100nF X7R] -->|Mid band, 1 MHz to 50 MHz| C
    G[High-freq MLCC 10nF 0402] -->|High band, 50 MHz to 500 MHz| C
    H[On-die Decap 1nF to 100pF] -->|Highest band, greater than 500 MHz| D
    D -->|Switching transients di_dt| I[Return through GND plane to VRM]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each capacitor tier covers a different frequency decade. The bulk capacitor handles the very low frequency content (almost DC replenishment), while the on-die decap handles the GHz content. The KTU examiner expects students to identify this *frequency-staggered* arrangement.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Choosing a Bypass Capacitor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define IC supply VDD and transient current] --> B[Compute Target Impedance Z_target]
    B --> C[Identify frequency band of interest f_low to f_high]
    C --> D[Choose dielectric C0G or X7R]
    D --> E[Pick package size 0402 for low ESL]
    E --> F[Compute single cap impedance at SRF]
    F --> G{Single cap meets Z_target?}
    G -- No --> H[Place N caps in parallel]
    H --> I[Check anti-resonance peaks]
    G -- Yes --> J[Verify DC-bias derating]
    I --> K[Layout: minimize via inductance]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final: Place near IC power pin]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical KTU-expected decision algorithm. Note the loop: if the single capacitor does not meet the target, multiple must be paralleled — but this introduces **anti-resonance peaks** which must be damped (typically by ESR spread or by adding small ferrite beads).

### 4.3 Schematic of Real Capacitor Model (R-L-C Series)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Terminal 1: VDD pin of IC] --> R[ESR = 5 milliohm]
    R --> L[ESL = 1 nH]
    L --> C[Ideal C = 100 nF]
    C --> B[Terminal 2: GND]
    style R fill:#f9e,stroke:#333
    style L fill:#cfe,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#ffc,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading the diagram:** All three parasitic elements are in series. The terminal-to-terminal impedance is **Z = ESR + jωL + 1/(jωC)**, exactly the formula in Section 2.2.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a bypass capacitor. Why is it required in high-speed digital circuits, and where is it physically placed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bypass capacitor (also called decoupling capacitor) is a passive component connected between the V_DD and GND pins of an integrated circuit. It is required in high-speed digital circuits because the simultaneous switching of internal gates produces high-frequency current transients (di/dt). The parasitic inductance of the PCB power planes and bond wires would otherwise cause supply voltage droop and ground bounce. The bypass capacitor provides a *local, low-impedance* source of charge to absorb these transients, keeping the rail-to-rail voltage stable. It is physically placed as close as possible — within 1–2 mm — to the IC's power pin to minimize the interconnect inductance between the capacitor and the die. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the self-resonant frequency of a bypass capacitor? Write the formula and explain what happens above and below this frequency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The self-resonant frequency (f_SRF) is the frequency at which the inductive reactance (ωL) of the capacitor's parasitic ESL equals the capacitive reactance (1/ωC). The formula is **f_SRF = 1 / (2π√(LC))**. Below f_SRF, the component behaves predominantly as a capacitor and its impedance decreases with frequency (good for decoupling). Above f_SRF, it behaves as an inductor and its impedance increases with frequency (ineffective for decoupling). Therefore, a single bypass capacitor is useful only in a narrow band around its f_SRF, and multiple capacitors of different values are required to cover a wide frequency range. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the formula for the target impedance of a power distribution network. For a high-speed ASIC with V_DD = 1.2 V, ±5% ripple tolerance, and a maximum simultaneous switching current of 3 A, compute the target impedance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the target impedance calculated in part (a), determine how many 100 nF X7R capacitors (each with an impedance of 0.15 Ω at their SRF) must be placed in parallel. Discuss the placement strategy on the PCB and explain the concept of anti-resonance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Definition and Derivation:**
The target impedance is the maximum permissible impedance of the power distribution network to maintain the supply voltage within the noise tolerance. By Ohm's law applied to the worst-case switching transient:

$$
Z_{target} = \frac{V_{DD} \times \text{Ripple Tolerance}}{I_{transient}}
$$

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Numerical substitution:**

$$
Z_{target} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.05}{3}
$$

$$
Z_{target} = \frac{0.06}{3}
$$

$$
Z_{target} = 0.02 \text{ Ω} = 20 \text{ mΩ}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Engineering interpretation:** This extremely low target impedance (20 milliohms) is the reason bypass capacitors are critical — even small parasitic inductances can violate this budget at high frequencies.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Parallel impedance combination:**

$$
N \geq \frac{Z_{cap,min}}{Z_{target}} = \frac{0.15}{0.02} = 7.5
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Rounding:**

$$
N = 8 \text{ capacitors}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Placement strategy:** Each of the 8 capacitors must be placed within 2 mm of the IC power pins, with wide, short traces. Both power and ground vias must be placed close to the capacitor pad to minimize loop inductance. Multiple vias per pad are recommended for current sharing.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Anti-resonance discussion:** When capacitors of *different* values are placed in parallel, their impedance curves interact. At certain frequencies between their SRFs, the parallel LC circuit formed by one cap's L and the other's C creates a **parallel resonant peak** (anti-resonance), where impedance *rises* sharply — exactly the opposite of what is wanted. This peak can exceed Z_target and cause functional failure. Mitigation strategies include: spreading ESL values, using capacitors of *the same* value, or adding small damping resistors/ferrite beads.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** A CMOS gate cluster draws a transient current of 250 mA for 1.5 ns. The allowed rail droop is 75 mV on a 2.5 V supply. Calculate the minimum bypass capacitance required. Also compute the energy stored in this capacitor at full charge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of ESR and ESL in a real bypass capacitor. How do they affect the choice of dielectric type and package size? Why is the Y5V dielectric generally avoided in high-speed digital designs? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Charge required:**

$$
\Delta Q = I \times \Delta t = 0.25 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-9} = 3.75 \times 10^{-10} \text{ C} = 375 \text{ pC}
$$

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Minimum capacitance:**

$$
C_{min} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta V} = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{-10}}{75 \times 10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} = 5 \text{ nF}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Energy stored:**

$$
E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} = 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} \times (2.5)^{2}
$$

$$
E = 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} \times 6.25 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J} = 15.625 \text{ nJ}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — ESR role:** ESR is the equivalent series resistance representing resistive losses. It sets the **minimum impedance** at the SRF and provides damping. A high ESR helps suppress anti-resonance peaks but reduces the capacitor's effectiveness at delivering instantaneous current. Low-ESR MLCCs (typically a few milliohms) are preferred in high-speed designs.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — ESL role:** ESL is the parasitic series inductance from the package, leads, traces, and vias. It determines the self-resonant frequency and the upper useful frequency limit. **Smaller packages (0402, 0201) have lower ESL** (≈0.5 nH) compared to larger ones (1206, ≈5 nH) and are mandatory for high-frequency decoupling.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Dielectric & Y5V:** The dielectric class determines the **temperature stability**, **DC-bias behaviour**, and **aging**. C0G/NP0 is the most stable (±30 ppm/°C) but limited to small capacitances. X7R is intermediate (±15% over -55 to +125°C) and is the **KTU-recommended workhorse** for bypass applications. Y5V exhibits +22% / -82% variation over temperature and loses 70-80% of nominal capacitance under DC bias. Y5V is therefore avoided in high-speed digital designs because the *effective* capacitance at operating voltage may be a small fraction of the marked value, violating the bypass requirement unpredictably. Additionally, Class-II and Class-III dielectrics exhibit **piezo-electric** behaviour, making them microphonic in vibration environments.

---

> [!WARNING]
