<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define timing margin in a clock distribution system. How is it computed for the setup constraint of a flip-flop pipeline stage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Timing margin (or slack) is the residual time difference between the *required* arrival of data at a flip-flop input and its *actual* arrival, guaranteeing reliable sampling.

For the **setup constraint**:

$$T_{su\_margin} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{su} - t_{skew} - t_{jitter}$$

A positive value indicates a **passing** design; a negative value indicates a **setup violation** requiring either clock period extension, logic optimization, or skew/jitter reduction.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — formula; 1 mark — physical interpretation.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Why does clock skew affect setup and hold margins in opposite ways? Illustrate with one sentence per case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Clock skew introduces a relative shift between launch and capture edges. **For setup**, if the capture edge arrives *later* than the launch edge, data has *more* time to settle — this is **helpful skew**. **For hold**, if the capture edge arrives *later*, the data launched in the *current* cycle has *more* time to corrupt the still-valid data being held — this is **harmful skew**, threatening a hold violation.

Hence, the same physical skew that benefits setup can simultaneously degrade hold, demanding careful balance by the clock-tree designer.

*(1.5 marks — setup case; 1.5 marks — hold case.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-level internal choice. Two fully independent question options are provided below. Examiner picks ONE.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive the setup and hold timing margin equations for a generic launch-to-capture flip-flop pipeline, clearly stating all assumptions. Include the role of clock skew and jitter. (7 marks)**

**(b) A synchronous circuit operates at $T_{clk} = 400$ ps. The launch-to-capture path has $T_{cq} = 60$ ps, $T_{comb} = 150$ ps, $T_{su} = 35$ ps, $T_{h} = 25$ ps. The clock tree has a worst-case skew of 20 ps and jitter of 12 ps. Compute the setup margin, hold margin, and maximum operating frequency. (7 marks)**

#### 🟢 Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the pipeline and reference times:**
Consider two flip-flops $FF_1$ (launch) and $FF_2$ (capture) connected via combinational logic. Let the global clock period be $T_{clk}$. Let $t_{launch}$ and $t_{capture}$ be the times at which the clock edge reaches $FF_1$ and $FF_2$ respectively.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Setup derivation:**
Data leaves $FF_1$ at time $t_{launch} + T_{cq}$ and arrives at the D-pin of $FF_2$ at:

$$T_{arrival} = t_{launch} + T_{cq} + T_{comb}$$

The capturing edge arrives at $t_{capture}$. For a non-violating setup:

$$T_{arrival} \le t_{capture} - T_{su}$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$t_{launch} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} \le t_{capture}$$

Letting $\Delta t_{skew} = t_{capture} - t_{launch}$ and including jitter $t_{jitter}$:

$$T_{clk} \ge T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter}$$

Hence the **setup margin**:

$$T_{su\_margin} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{su} - t_{skew} - t_{jitter}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Hold derivation:**
Hold uses the *same* clock edge. Data must not transition in the window $[t_{capture} - T_{h}, \, t_{capture}]$:

$$T_{cq} + T_{comb} \ge T_{h} - \Delta t_{skew}$$

The **hold margin**:

$$T_{h\_margin} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} - T_{h} - t_{skew}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Assumptions and role of skew/jitter:**
- *Single-cycle path assumption.*
- *Worst-case skew direction used (adversarial).*
- *Skew is *spatial* (deterministic + systematic) and *jitter is *temporal* (random, cycle-to-cycle).*
- *Skew enters as a constant offset; jitter enters as a peak-to-peak reduction in the effective period.*
- *No intentional clock gating or multi-cycle paths.*

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Physical interpretation:**
Setup margin governs **maximum frequency** ($f_{max}$). Hold margin governs **short-path protection** and is independent of $T_{clk}$. Both must be positive across all PVT corners for the design to be sign-off clean.

---

#### 🟢 Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify parameters:**

$$T_{clk} = 400 \text{ ps}, \quad T_{cq} = 60 \text{ ps}, \quad T_{comb} = 150 \text{ ps}$$

$$T_{su} = 35 \text{ ps}, \quad T_{h} = 25 \text{ ps}, \quad t_{skew} = 20 \text{ ps}, \quad t_{jitter} = 12 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute setup margin:**

$$T_{su\_margin} = 400 - 60 - 150 - 35 - 20 - 12 = 123 \text{ ps}$$

Since $T_{su\_margin} > 0$, the setup constraint is **satisfied**.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute hold margin:**

$$T_{h\_margin} = 60 + 150 - 25 - 20 = 165 \text{ ps}$$

Since $T_{h\_margin} > 0$, the hold constraint is **satisfied**.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute $f_{max}$:**

$$T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + t_{skew} + t_{jitter}$$

$$T_{clk,min} = 60 + 150 + 35 + 20 + 12 = 277 \text{ ps}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \frac{1}{277 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_{max} \approx 3.610 \text{ GHz}$$

**Conclusion:** The design operates correctly at the 2.5 GHz target with healthy 123 ps setup slack and 165 ps hold slack, and could in principle be pushed to ~3.61 GHz.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the H-tree and mesh clock distribution architectures. Compare them on the basis of skew, robustness, power, and routing complexity. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a clock distribution network, the budget is $T_{clk} = 600$ ps. The total clock-to-Q and combinational delay in the critical path is 280 ps, and the setup requirement of the destination flop is 50 ps. If 20 ps is allocated to skew and 15 ps to jitter, what is the residual timing margin? If the skew is doubled, what happens to the margin? Comment. (7 marks)**

#### 🟢 Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1 (1 mark) — H-tree description:**
The H-tree is a recursively symmetric binary branching structure. From the root, the clock splits into two equal-length branches; each branch splits into two more, and so on. Because the geometric path length from the root to every leaf is **identical**, the *intrinsic* spatial skew is theoretically **zero**, modulo process mismatch.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Mesh description:**
A clock mesh is a regular grid of orthogonal clock wires driven by multiple buffer points. The high connectivity creates many redundant paths, making it extremely robust to localized wire delay variation, defects, and supply noise.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Comparison table:**

| Parameter | H-Tree | Mesh |
|---|---|---|
| Skew | Very low (symmetric) | Very low (high redundancy) |
| Robustness | Moderate (single point of failure per branch) | High (multi-driver redundancy) |
| Power | Lower (predictable switching) | Higher (large capacitive load) |
| Routing | Dense, area-intensive at leaf | Very dense, area-intensive |
| Suitability | High-performance CPUs, regular blocks | Server-class chips, large macros |

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Engineering trade-off commentary:**
H-tree is preferred when **predictability and low power** dominate, and when the floorplan is regular. Mesh is preferred when **robustness against OCV and voltage droop** is paramount — at the cost of significant dynamic power due to the large mesh capacitance. Modern sub-7nm designs often use **hybrid spine + mesh** structures.

---

#### 🟢 Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify parameters:**

$$T_{clk} = 600 \text{ ps}, \quad T_{cq} + T_{comb} = 280 \text{ ps}$$

$$T_{su} = 50 \text{ ps}, \quad t_{skew} = 20 \text{ ps}, \quad t_{jitter} = 15 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute initial margin:**

$$T_{margin} = 600 - 280 - 50 - 20 - 15 = 235 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute margin with doubled skew:**

$$t_{skew,new} = 2 \times 20 = 40 \text{ ps}$$

$$T_{margin,new} = 600 - 280 - 50 - 40 - 15 = 215 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Comment on the impact:**

$$\Delta T_{margin} = 215 - 235 = -20 \text{ ps}$$

Doubling the skew **degrades the margin by exactly 20 ps**, which is the amount of additional skew introduced. This linear, one-to-one sensitivity is a direct consequence of the setup equation's structure. It confirms the well-known KTU principle that **skew reduction has a 1:1 impact on timing margin** for setup — making clock-tree balancing one of the most powerful optimization levers in high-speed design.

If skew were to increase further (say, to 240 ps), the margin would become **negative**, and the design would fail timing — requiring either clock period extension, pipeline restructuring, or physical-design-driven skew minimization.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Exam Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting to include jitter** in the setup margin equation — costs ~2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing skew and jitter** — skew is *spatial* (between flops), jitter is *temporal* (cycle-to-cycle at the same flop).
> 3. **Wrong direction of skew in hold** — students often use helpful skew where harmful skew should be used.
> 4. **Unit conversion errors** — always convert GHz to ps via $T = 1/f$.
> 5. **Mixing up $T_{su}$ and $T_h$** — setup closes the *trailing* window, hold closes the *leading* window relative to the capture edge.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — KTU examiners explicitly award marks for clearly stated "single-cycle path" and "worst-case PVT" assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Clock Distribution: Timing Margin):**

- ⏰ Timing margin = **required time − actual arrival time**.
- ⏰ Setup margin governs $f_{max}$; Hold margin is independent of $T_{clk}$.
- ⏰ Skew and jitter are the **two primary eroders** of timing margin.
- ⏰ Skew direction has **opposite effects** on setup vs. hold.
- ⏰ H-tree gives low skew; mesh gives high robustness.
- ⏰ Jitter enters as a **subtractive** term in the setup equation.
- ⏰ Useful (helpful) skew can be **deliberately engineered** to borrow time between adjacent pipeline stages.
- ⏰ In nanometer CMOS, the timing budget is dominated by **variation**, not nominal delay.
- ⏰ OCV derates ($d_{late} > 1$, $d_{early} < 1$) augment the basic margin for sign-off.
- ⏰ Clock gating adds **gating skew** — must be included in the margin budget.
- ⏰ Always verify **both** setup and hold — they are independent constraints, not complements.
- ⏰ In KTU derivations, **explicitly state** all variables and physical units (ps or ns).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clock Skew
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Clock Skew

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clock Skew** is defined as the *maximum difference in the arrival times of the clock signal at any two sequentially-adjacent flip-flops (or sequential elements) in a synchronous digital circuit, measured at their respective clock input pins.*

Mathematically, if two flip-flops **FF₁** and **FF₂** receive the same clock edge at instants $t_{c1}$ and $t_{c2}$ respectively, then:

$$\delta_{skew} \;=\; t_{c2} \;-\; t_{c1}$$

Clock skew is a **spatial (deterministic)** phenomenon — it is the difference in propagation delay along different paths of the clock distribution network (CDN).

### 1.2 Related Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> - **Clock Jitter** ($\Delta t_{jitter}$): Temporal variation of a single clock edge from its ideal position, measured *at the same point* over many cycles. Unit: **picoseconds (ps)** or **femtoseconds (fs)**.
> - **Clock Skew** ($\delta$): Spatial difference of the *same* clock edge observed at *different points* in the chip.
> - **Skew + Jitter = Total Clock Uncertainty** in modern timing analysis.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Relay Race Baton Handoff

Imagine a relay race where two runners, A and B, must exchange a baton. Both runners are supposed to start running *at the same whistle*. But because the sound takes time to travel, runner B (further away) hears the whistle a little **later** than runner A. That tiny delay is exactly **clock skew**.

If the baton is thrown too early (because B is still reacting), it is dropped — analogous to a **hold-time violation**. If the baton takes too long to reach B (because A was held up), the next whistle blows before B is ready — analogous to a **setup-time violation**.

The fix? A proper starting mechanism (like synchronized speakers at equal distance), which corresponds to an **H-tree clock distribution** in VLSI design.

### 1.4 Why Clock Skew Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Clock skew directly limits the **maximum operating frequency** ($f_{max}$) of any synchronous system. A poorly designed clock network can reduce a chip's clock speed by **20–40%**, even with identical logic.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Modern CMOS) |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Skew | $\delta_{skew}$ | **10 – 100 ps** |
| Clock Jitter | $t_{jitter}$ | **1 – 50 ps** |
| Clock Period | $T_{clk}$ | **0.5 – 10 ns** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Clock Skew

1. **Positive Skew** ($\delta > 0$): The receiving (capturing) flip-flop gets the clock **later** than the launching flip-flop.
   - **Effect:** *Helps* the setup-time constraint; *Hurts* the hold-time constraint.

2. **Negative Skew** ($\delta < 0$): The receiving flip-flop gets the clock **earlier** than the launching flip-flop.
   - **Effect:** *Hurts* the setup-time constraint; *Helps* the hold-time constraint.

3. **Zero Skew** ($\delta = 0$): All flip-flops receive the clock edge at exactly the same instant — the **ideal** case.

### 2.2 Sources / Causes of Clock Skew

> [!NOTE]
> Sources are broadly grouped into three categories:

- **Geometric / Routing Causes**
  - Unequal wire lengths in the clock tree.
  - Asymmetric branching (different number of buffers per path).
  - Different via counts per path.

- **Electrical / Process Causes**
  - Variations in buffer drive strength across die.
  - Mismatched load capacitances at sink points.
  - Process, Voltage, Temperature (PVT) variations.

- **Environmental Causes**
  - Localised hot spots causing slow transistors.
  - Power supply noise (IR drop) modulating buffer delay.
  - Substrate / coupling noise.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following are the only equations you must memorize for clock skew problems in the KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = t_{c2} - t_{c1}$ | Basic skew definition (capturing FF minus launching FF) | s |
| 2 | $T_{clk} \ge T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta$ | **Setup-time constraint with skew** | s |
| 3 | $T_{cq} + T_{comb} \ge T_{h} + \delta$ | **Hold-time constraint with skew** | s |
| 4 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta_{max}}$ | Maximum operating frequency | Hz |
| 5 | $\delta_{max} = T_{cq,min} + T_{comb,min} - T_{h}$ | Maximum allowable positive skew (hold limited) | s |
| 6 | $\delta_{min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - T_{clk}$ | Minimum allowable (most negative) skew | s |
| 7 | $t_{wire} = 0.5 \cdot R_{pu} \cdot C_{L}$ | First-order buffer-to-load wire delay (Elmore, lumped) | s |
| 8 | $T_{clk} \ge T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + t_{jitter}$ | Setup constraint including jitter (combined with skew) | s |
| 9 | $\delta_{total} = \sqrt{\delta_{random}^{2} + \delta_{systematic}^{2}}$ | Statistical combination of skew components | s |

> **Key Insight:** Equations (2) and (3) are the **two most-tested formulas** in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Note that increasing the *clock period* $T_{clk}$ compensates for **negative skew**, but **positive skew** can *only* be tamed by reducing $\delta$ itself.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprocessors:** Skew budgets as tight as **±15 ps** are allocated across a die in sub-7 nm designs (Apple M-series, AMD Zen).
- **DDR Memory Interfaces:** Write-leveling and read-leveling algorithms compensate skew in real time using delay-locked loops (DLLs).
- **FPGA Designs:** Vendor tools (Xilinx, Intel) automatically perform *clock tree synthesis (CTS)* to minimise $\delta$ to within **~50 ps**.
- **SerDes Links:** Multi-GHz serial links use phase interpolators to actively cancel residual skew on a per-bit basis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Setup-Time Constraint with Clock Skew

**System under analysis:** A launching flip-flop **FF₁** drives combinational logic that feeds a capturing flip-flop **FF₂**. Both FFs are clocked by the same global clock, but the clock arrives at the two FFs at instants $t_{c1}$ and $t_{c2}$ respectively.

**Step 1: Time at which the launching FF releases new data**

The clock edge arrives at FF₁ at $t = t_{c1}$. After the **clock-to-Q delay** ($T_{cq}$), valid data appears at FF₁'s output:

$$t_{data,launch} = t_{c1} + T_{cq}$$

**Step 2: Time at which data arrives at FF₂'s input**

The data propagates through the combinational logic block with delay $T_{comb}$:

$$t_{data,arrival} = t_{c1} + T_{cq} + T_{comb}$$

**Step 3: Time of the next capturing clock edge at FF₂**

The clock period is $T_{clk}$. The next rising edge of the same clock waveform at FF₂ occurs at:

$$t_{capture,edge} = t_{c2} + T_{clk}$$

**Step 4: Apply the setup-time requirement**

For FF₂ to reliably latch the data, the data must arrive **at least $T_{su}$ before** the capturing clock edge:

$$t_{data,arrival} + T_{su} \;\le\; t_{capture,edge}$$

Substituting:

$$t_{c1} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} \;\le\; t_{c2} + T_{clk}$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to expose the clock period**

$$T_{clk} \;\ge\; T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + (t_{c1} - t_{c2})$$

Recognising that $t_{c1} - t_{c2} = -\delta$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{clk} \;\ge\; T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} \;-\; \delta\,}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $\delta > 0$ (positive skew, FF₂ clock arrives later), the right-hand side shrinks — **the circuit is easier to meet setup**. Good for high speed.
- If $\delta < 0$ (negative skew), the right-hand side grows — **setup margin worsens**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Hold-Time Constraint with Clock Skew

**Step 1: Data from the *same* clock edge must not corrupt the new capture**

The data launched at $t_{c1}$ arrives at FF₂'s input at $t_{c1} + T_{cq} + T_{comb}$. The capturing clock edge at FF₂ occurs at $t_{c2}$.

**Step 2: Apply the hold-time requirement**

The new data must arrive at FF₂ *no earlier than* $T_{h}$ after the capturing clock edge:

$$t_{data,arrival} \;\ge\; t_{c2} + T_{h}$$

Substituting:

$$t_{c1} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} \;\ge\; t_{c2} + T_{h}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange**

$$T_{cq} + T_{comb} \;\ge\; (t_{c2} - t_{c1}) + T_{h}$$

Since $\delta = t_{c2} - t_{c1}$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{cq} + T_{comb} \;\ge\; T_{h} \;+\; \delta\,}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Positive skew ($\delta > 0$) **tightens** the hold constraint — the data races too quickly toward FF₂. *This is the well-known hold-time failure caused by excessive positive clock skew.*
- Negative skew *relaxes* the hold constraint.

---

### 3.3 Maximum Operating Frequency from Setup Constraint

Starting from Eq. (2), the minimum possible clock period is:

$$T_{clk,\,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta$$

The maximum frequency is therefore:

$$\boxed{\,f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta_{max}}\,}$$

Note carefully: only the **most positive** value of $\delta$ that *all* paths simultaneously experience enters this equation.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> A 4-stage pipelined processor has the following parameters:
> $T_{cq} = 0.30$ ns, $T_{comb} = 1.20$ ns, $T_{su} = 0.20$ ns, $T_{h} = 0.10$ ns.
> **(a)** Find the maximum clock skew that can be tolerated at 500 MHz operation.
> **(b)** What is $f_{max}$ if the actual measured skew is $\delta = +0.15$ ns?

**Solution:**

Clock period at 500 MHz:

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{6}} = 2.0 \text{ ns}$$

**Part (a) — Setup-limited skew:**

From $T_{clk} \ge T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta$:

$$2.0 \;\ge\; 0.30 + 1.20 + 0.20 - \delta$$

$$\delta \;\ge\; 1.70 - 2.0 \;=\; -0.30 \text{ ns}$$

So the **most negative** skew permitted is $-0.30$ ns (i.e., the capturing FF must not receive the clock more than 0.30 ns *early*).

**Hold-limited skew (positive):**

$$T_{cq} + T_{comb} \ge T_{h} + \delta$$
$$0.30 + 1.20 \ge 0.10 + \delta$$
$$\delta \;\le\; 1.40 \text{ ns}$$

So the **most positive** skew permitted is $+1.40$ ns.

Therefore the allowable skew range is:

$$-0.30 \text{ ns} \;\le\; \delta \;\le\; +1.40 \text{ ns}$$

**Part (b) — $f_{max}$ for $\delta = +0.15$ ns:**

$$T_{clk,min} = 0.30 + 1.20 + 0.20 - 0.15 = 1.55 \text{ ns}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{1.55 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 645.16 \text{ MHz}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Skew Type | Sign of $\delta$ | Setup Margin | Hold Margin | Can Reduce $f_{max}$? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zero | 0 | Baseline | Baseline | No (reference) |
| Positive | + | Increased | Decreased | No (often helps) |
| Negative | – | Decreased | Increased | **Yes** |
| Excessive Positive | + (large) | Increased | **VIOLATED** | Indirectly (chip fails) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 H-Tree Clock Distribution (Zero-Skew Topology)

The H-tree is the most common zero-skew clock distribution network. The clock source sits at the centre of the chip; symmetric H-shaped wires run to all four quadrants, then to sub-quadrants, recursively. Because every path has *identical electrical length*, the skew approaches zero.

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Clock Source at Die Centre] --> sp[Central Split Point]
    sp --> a1[Branch A - North]
    sp --> a2[Branch B - South]
    a1 --> ff1[FF1 - North West]
    a1 --> ff2[FF2 - North East]
    a2 --> ff3[FF3 - South West]
    a2 --> ff4[FF4 - South East]
    style src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style sp fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style a1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style a2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ff1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ff2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ff3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ff4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every path from `src` to a leaf FF traverses exactly *one* central split and *one* directional branch — so all four electrical path lengths are equal by construction.

---

### 4.2 Skew Sources — Concept Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    sk[Clock Skew] --> G[Geometric]
    sk --> P[Process / Electrical]
    sk --> E[Environmental]
    G --> G1[Unequal wire length]
    G --> G2[Asymmetric buffer tree]
    G --> G3[Via count mismatch]
    P --> P1[Buffer drive variation]
    P --> P2[Load capacitance mismatch]
    P --> P3[PVT spread]
    E --> E1[Temperature gradient]
    E --> E2[IR drop / supply noise]
    E --> E3[Substrate coupling]
    style sk fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Positive vs Negative Skew — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph POS[Positive Skew Case]
        Pclk1[Clock at FF1 - t0] --> Pff1[FF1 launches data]
        Pclk2[Clock at FF2 - t0 + delta] --> Pff2[FF2 captures data late]
        Pff1 --> Pff2
    end
    subgraph NEG[Negative Skew Case]
        Nclk1[Clock at FF1 - t0] --> Nff1[FF1 launches data]
        Nclk2[Clock at FF2 - t0 minus delta] --> Nff2[FF2 captures data early]
        Nff1 --> Nff2
    end
    style POS fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400
    style NEG fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000
```

---

### 4.4 Clock Distribution with Skew Compensation Block

For more advanced designs (e.g., phase-locked loops and delay-locked loops), the architecture includes an active skew-compensation block.

```mermaid
graph TD
    pll[PLL / DLL Reference] --> comp[Skew Compensation Unit]
    comp --> mux[Clock Mux Tree]
    mux --> fb1[Feedback Tap from FF1]
    mux --> fb2[Feedback Tap from FF2]
    fb1 --> comp
    fb2 --> comp
    comp --> drv[Matched Clock Drivers]
    drv --> sink1[FF1 Sink]
    drv --> sink2[FF2 Sink]
    drv --> sink3[FF3 Sink]
    drv --> sink4[FF4 Sink]
    style pll fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style comp fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style drv fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> The **feedback taps** route a copy of the local clock back to the compensation unit, which then *adjusts* each branch's delay so that all sinks see the same edge — actively enforcing zero skew.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*Define clock skew. How is it different from clock jitter? Why is clock skew a critical parameter in high-speed digital design?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Clock skew** is the difference in arrival times of the same clock edge at two (or more) flip-flops in a synchronous circuit, mathematically expressed as $\delta = t_{c2} - t_{c1}$.
>
> **Difference from clock jitter:**
>
| Property | Clock Skew | Clock Jitter |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Spatial (point-to-point) | Temporal (cycle-to-cycle) |
| Measured at | Different sinks, same edge | Same sink, different edges |
| Cause | Path-length / load mismatch | Power noise, PLL error |
| Compensation | Clock tree, H-tree, DLL | PLL filtering |
>
> Clock skew is critical because it **directly limits the maximum operating frequency** through the setup-time equation and can cause **hold-time failures** if excessive positive skew exists. In multi-GHz designs, even tens of picoseconds of skew can cause timing violations. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Explain positive and negative clock skew. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

> - **Positive Skew ($\delta > 0$):** The capturing flip-flop receives the clock *later* than the launching flip-flop.
>   - *Advantage:* Relaxes the setup-time constraint — allows higher $f_{max}$.
>   - *Disadvantage:* Tightens the hold-time constraint — may cause hold violation.
>
> - **Negative Skew ($\delta < 0$):** The capturing flip-flop receives the clock *earlier* than the launching flip-flop.
>   - *Advantage:* Relaxes the hold-time constraint.
>   - *Disadvantage:* Tightens the setup-time constraint — reduces $f_{max}$.
>
> Designers usually prefer *small positive skew* in pipelines for setup relief. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **H-tree** method of clock distribution. Why is it called a *zero-skew* topology? Mention two practical limitations of the H-tree.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A digital subsystem has $T_{cq} = 0.4$ ns, $T_{comb} = 1.8$ ns, $T_{su} = 0.2$ ns, and $T_{h} = 0.1$ ns. If the clock skew across the chip is measured to be $\delta = +0.25$ ns, calculate:
   (i) the maximum operating frequency,
   (ii) whether the hold-time constraint is satisfied.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**Part (a) — H-Tree Clock Distribution:**

The H-tree is a symmetric, recursive clock distribution network in which the clock source is placed at the *geometric centre* of the die, and equal-length wires branch outward in an "H" pattern.

**Working principle:**
1. The clock source feeds a central split point.
2. From the split point, four equal-length branches extend toward the four quadrants of the die.
3. At the end of each branch, the wire splits again into two sub-branches of equal length, each terminating at a flip-flop or local clock buffer.
4. By symmetry, the *electrical path length* from the source to every endpoint is *identical*.

> Because all paths have the same delay, the clock edge arrives simultaneously at every sink, giving $\delta = 0$ — hence the name **zero-skew topology**.

**Diagram:**

```
              +-------+-------+
              |       |       |
              |       |       |
        +-----+-------+-------+-----+
        |                 |         |
        |                 |         |
   +----+----+       +----+----+    |
   |         |       |         |    |
  FF1       FF2     FF3       FF4
   ^         ^       ^         ^
   +----+----+-------+---------+--+
        |                 |
     (equal length traces from source)
```

**Two practical limitations:**
1. **Routing congestion:** Real chips have blockages (macros, memory arrays) that prevent the perfectly symmetric H layout. Designers must detour wires, which slightly unbalances the path lengths.
2. **Power / buffer mismatch:** The end points of different branches have different load capacitances (number of flip-flops per region), causing residual skew even with equal-length wires.

**(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for principle, 2 for limitations)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $T_{cq} = 0.4$ ns, $T_{comb} = 1.8$ ns, $T_{su} = 0.2$ ns, $T_{h} = 0.1$ ns, $\delta = +0.25$ ns.

**(i) Maximum operating frequency:**

Minimum clock period from setup constraint:

$$T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta$$

Substituting:

$$T_{clk,min} = 0.4 + 1.8 + 0.2 - 0.25 = 2.15 \text{ ns}$$

Maximum frequency:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \frac{1}{2.15 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 465.12 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Hold-time check:**

$$T_{cq} + T_{comb} \;\ge\; T_{h} + \delta$$

$$0.4 + 1.8 \;\ge\; 0.1 + 0.25$$

$$2.2 \;\ge\; 0.35 \;\; \text{(TRUE)}$$

The hold-time constraint is **satisfied** with a large margin of 1.85 ns.

**(7 marks — 3 for setup derivation, 2 for $f_{max}$, 2 for hold check)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative — KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **major sources of clock skew** in a synchronous VLSI circuit. Explain how each source contributes to the overall skew budget.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A high-speed pipelined multiplier has the following timing parameters: $T_{cq} = 0.20$ ns, $T_{comb} = 0.80$ ns, $T_{su} = 0.15$ ns, $T_{h} = 0.10$ ns. The chip is required to operate at **800 MHz**.
   (i) Find the maximum allowable clock skew.
   (ii) If the actual measured skew is $\delta = -0.20$ ns, does the design work? Justify with both setup and hold checks.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**Part (a) — Sources of Clock Skew:**

| # | Source | Contribution Type | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wire length mismatch** in clock routing | Systematic (deterministic) | 20 – 100 ps |
| 2 | **Buffer / inverter delay variation** between branches | Systematic + Random | 10 – 50 ps |
| 3 | **Load capacitance mismatch** at sinks (different number of FFs per region) | Systematic | 5 – 30 ps |
| 4 | **Process variation** (L, W, $t_{ox}$ spread) | Random | 10 – 60 ps |
| 5 | **Temperature gradients** across die | Systematic (slow drift) | 5 – 25 ps |
| 6 | **Supply voltage (IR) drop** modulating buffer delay | Dynamic | 5 – 40 ps |
| 7 | **Coupling / substrate noise** | Dynamic (jitter-like) | 1 – 10 ps |

The **total skew** is the worst-case sum (or statistical combination via Eq. 9) of all contributions.

**(7 marks — 3 for systematic, 2 for random, 2 for dynamic sources)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $T_{cq} = 0.20$ ns, $T_{comb} = 0.80$ ns, $T_{su} = 0.15$ ns, $T_{h} = 0.10$ ns, $f_{req} = 800$ MHz.

Clock period:

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{800 \times 10^{6}} = 1.25 \text{ ns}$$

**(i) Maximum allowable clock skew range:**

*Setup-limited (lower bound on $\delta$):*

$$T_{clk} \ge T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta$$
$$1.25 \ge 0.20 + 0.80 + 0.15 - \delta$$
$$\delta \ge 1.15 - 1.25 = -0.10 \text{ ns}$$

*Hold-limited (upper bound on $\delta$):*

$$T_{cq} + T_{comb} \ge T_{h} + \delta$$
$$0.20 + 0.80 \ge 0.10 + \delta$$
$$\delta \le 0.90 \text{ ns}$$

Therefore, the allowable skew range is:

$$-0.10 \text{ ns} \;\le\; \delta \;\le\; +0.90 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Check with actual $\delta = -0.20$ ns:**

*Setup check:*

$$T_{clk,req} = 0.20 + 0.80 + 0.15 - (-0.20) = 1.35 \text{ ns}$$

But $T_{clk,actual} = 1.25$ ns. Since $1.25 < 1.35$, **the setup-time constraint is violated**.

*Hold check (for completeness):*

$$0.20 + 0.80 \ge 0.10 + (-0.20) \;\Rightarrow\; 1.00 \ge -0.10 \;\; \text{(TRUE)}$$

Hold is satisfied, but **setup fails** — therefore the design **does NOT work at 800 MHz** with $\delta = -0.20$ ns. The negative skew leaves insufficient timing margin for the combinational logic to settle before the next clock edge.

**(7 marks — 2 for $T_{clk}$, 2 for range, 3 for the violation check)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Sign confusion in $\delta$:** A shocking number of students write the *setup equation* with $\delta$ *added* instead of *subtracted*. Always remember:
>    $T_{clk} \ge T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta$ **(subtract skew)**
>
> 2. **Mixing skew with jitter:** Jitter and skew are *not the same* — the KTU examiner will deduct marks if you use them interchangeably. Jitter is *temporal* and skew is *spatial*.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the hold check:** Many students solve only the setup equation and stop. The hold-time constraint is a *separate* equation that must always be verified.
>
> 4. **Unit mistakes:** $f_{max}$ in MHz requires $T_{clk}$ in $\mu$s. Use $1 / T_{clk}$ with consistent SI units.
>
> 5. **Skew range vs single value:** A KTU question may ask "what is the maximum skew?" — if both setup and hold constraints are violated by the same skew value, the answer is the *intersection* of the two safe ranges, not just one.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-know for KTU 2024 ESE)**
>
> - Clock skew $\delta$ is **spatial**; clock jitter $t_{jitter}$ is **temporal**. Do not confuse.
> - The two *must-memorize* equations are:
>   (i) $T_{clk} \ge T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta$ (setup)
>   (ii) $T_{cq} + T_{comb} \ge T_{h} + \delta$ (hold)
> - Positive skew $\Rightarrow$ helps setup, **hurts hold**.
> - Negative skew $\Rightarrow$ hurts setup, **helps hold**.
> - $f_{max} = 1 \,/\, (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} - \delta_{max})$.
> - Sources of skew: wire mismatch, buffer delay variation, load mismatch, PVT spread, IR drop, noise.
> - H-tree, clock tree synthesis (CTS), PLLs, and DLLs are the standard skew-reduction techniques.
> - Always express answers in **consistent SI units** (ns for $T_{clk}$, GHz for $f_{max}$).
> - "Maximum allowable skew" is a *range*; check **both** setup-limited and hold-limited bounds.
> - Modern sub-nm designs target $|\delta| < 25$ ps across the entire die.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Low Impedance Drivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using Low Impedance Drivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In high-speed digital design, a **Low Impedance Driver** is defined as a digital output buffer whose effective output impedance ($Z_{driver}$) is intentionally designed to be **substantially lower** than the characteristic impedance ($Z_0$) of the driven transmission line, such that the ratio $Z_{driver} \ll Z_0$. These drivers are deployed as part of **source-series termination** or **active damping** strategies to suppress transmission-line reflections, damp ringing on the line, and minimize overshoot/undershoot at the receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The term "low impedance" is *relative*. A driver is considered low impedance in a given system only if its Thevenin output impedance is at most one-third to one-half of $Z_0$ (typical guideline: $Z_{driver} \leq Z_0/3$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Fire Hose and Nozzle

Imagine a powerful fire hydrant (the **driver**) pushing water through a long, thin garden hose (the **transmission line**). When the water hits a closed nozzle (a **high-impedance receiver**), it bounces back violently, creating a pressure surge that travels backward and shakes the entire hose. This is exactly what happens in a digital line: voltage waves reflect off impedance discontinuities and create **ringing, overshoot, and double-clocking** at the receiver.

Now, install a **heavy, low-resistance pressure regulator** at the hydrant end. Even if the water reflects from the nozzle, the regulator absorbs the returning surge by presenting a near-short path to ground. The reflections die out quickly because the regulator "sinks" their energy instead of bouncing them back. That regulator is your **low-impedance driver** — it acts as an *energy-absorbing boundary* rather than a *signal-isolating one*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Characteristic Impedance of PCB trace (FR-4 microstrip):** typically **$Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$**
- **CMOS driver output impedance (standard):** **$Z_{driver} = 10 \text{ to } 25 \, \Omega$** in modern sub-100 nm processes
- **TTL driver output impedance:** **$Z_{driver} \approx 30 \text{ to } 60 \, \Omega$**
- **Speed of signal propagation in FR-4:** **$v_p \approx 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$** (roughly half the speed of light in vacuum)
- **Receiver input impedance (CMOS):** **$Z_{in} \approx 1 \text{ M}\Omega$** (essentially open circuit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Low Impedance" Matters in KTU Examinations:** Whenever a question mentions *ringing*, *overshoot*, *multiple crossings of a logic threshold*, or *double clocking* in high-speed links, the examiner is implicitly pointing toward low-impedance driver solutions. Always recall $Z_{driver} \ll Z_0$ as the operational rule.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Reflections in a Digital Line

When a digital driver launches a step edge into a transmission line, two simultaneous events occur at the **receiver** end (assuming $Z_{receiver} \neq Z_0$):

1. A **forward-traveling wave** ($V^+$) propagates from driver to receiver.
2. A **reflected wave** ($V^-$) is generated at the receiver discontinuity and travels back to the driver.

The **reflection coefficient** at any boundary is given by:

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_{load} - Z_0}{Z_{load} + Z_0}$$

### 2.1.1 The Three Canonical Termination Scenarios

| Scenario | Termination Condition | Reflection Coefficient $\Gamma$ | Effect on Signal |
|----------|----------------------|--------------------------------|------------------|
| Open-circuit receiver | $Z_{load} \to \infty$ | $\Gamma = +1$ | Full positive reflection, doubles the edge amplitude at receiver |
| Short-circuit receiver | $Z_{load} \to 0$ | $\Gamma = -1$ | Full negative reflection, inverts the edge at receiver |
| Matched receiver | $Z_{load} = Z_0$ | $\Gamma = 0$ | No reflection, clean signal, no ringing |
| **Low-Z driver at source** | **$Z_{driver} \ll Z_0$** | **$\Gamma \approx -1$** | **Reflected wave is absorbed (damped) at the source end** |

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the low-impedance driver produces **$\Gamma \approx -1$** at the source boundary. A negative reflection inverts and cancels the original overshoot, which is precisely the damping mechanism we need.

## 2.2 How a Low-Impedance Driver Damps Ringing

A transmission line behaves like a distributed LC ladder. When excited, the natural response is a damped sinusoid. The **damping factor** of this response is governed by the **series resistance** of the driver and the line.

Consider a line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$, total capacitance $C_L$, driven by a source of impedance $Z_s$ and terminated by a high-impedance load $Z_L \to \infty$. The system can be approximated as a second-order RLC with:

- **Effective inductance:** $L_{eff} \approx Z_0 \cdot t_d$ (where $t_d$ is the one-way delay)
- **Effective capacitance:** $C_{eff} = C_L$
- **Damping resistance:** $R = Z_s + Z_0$

The **quality factor** $Q$ of this RLC is:

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L_{eff}}{C_{eff}}}$$

**Lower $Q$ → more damped → less ringing.** Since $R = Z_s + Z_0$, reducing $Z_s$ (using a low-impedance driver) increases $R$ and therefore **reduces $Q$**, which directly suppresses ringing.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L_0}{C_0}}$ | $L_0$: per-unit-length inductance, $C_0$: per-unit-length capacitance | Compute characteristic impedance of a trace |
| 2 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | $Z_L$: load impedance | Compute reflection coefficient at any boundary |
| 3 | $V_{reflected} = \Gamma \cdot V_{incident}$ | Scalar relation | Find reflected wave amplitude |
| 4 | $V_{received} = V_{incident}(1 + \Gamma)$ | Voltage at the boundary | Actual voltage observed at receiver |
| 5 | $t_d = \dfrac{l \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}{c}$ | $l$: trace length, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | One-way propagation delay |
| 6 | $f_{ring} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L_{eff} C_{eff}}}$ | Natural ringing frequency | Predict resonance frequency on the line |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L_{eff}}{C_{eff}}}$ | $R = Z_s + Z_0$ | Quantify ringing severity |
| 8 | $V(t) = V_{final}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $\tau = R C_{total}$ | Charging response at receiver |
| 9 | Critical length: $l_{crit} = \dfrac{t_{rise}}{2 t_d \text{ per unit length}}$ | $t_{rise}$: 10–90% rise time | When to treat interconnect as a transmission line |
| 10 | $Z_{driver} \leq Z_0 / 3$ | Design rule of thumb | Criterion for "low impedance" classification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Low-impedance drivers are the *de-facto* termination method in **DDR memory interfaces (DDR3/DDR4/DDR5),** **high-speed serial links (PCIe, SATA, USB 3.x),** and **LVDS drivers** because they are tunable through on-die calibration and avoid the cost of external termination resistors.

## 2.4 Power vs. Signal Integrity Trade-off

A low-impedance driver, by definition, draws more DC current when driving a logic level. The static current consumption is:

$$I_{DC} = \frac{V_{DD}}{Z_{driver} + Z_{load}}$$

For a 3.3 V CMOS driver with $Z_{driver} = 10 \, \Omega$ driving an open receiver ($Z_L \to \infty$):

$$I_{DC} = \frac{3.3}{10 + \infty} \approx 0 \text{ A (open load case)}$$

But during a switching event, the charging current into the line capacitance can momentarily reach:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_{DD}}{Z_{driver} + Z_0} = \frac{3.3}{10 + 50} = 55 \text{ mA}$$

This is the **fundamental trade-off** examiners love to test: *low-impedance drivers improve signal integrity at the cost of switching power and ground-bounce noise.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reflection Coefficient at a Low-Impedance Source

**Given:**
- A transmission line with characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$
- A driver with output impedance $Z_s = 10 \, \Omega$ (low-impedance)
- A receiver with input impedance $Z_L \to \infty$ (open circuit, e.g., CMOS gate)

**Find:** Reflection coefficient at the source end and the resulting damped waveform.

**Step 1 — Initial forward wave launched at the source.**

When the driver switches from low to high, it presents a voltage divider between $Z_s$ and $Z_0$:

$$V^+ = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_s + Z_0} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{50}{10 + 50} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{5}{6}$$

**Step 2 — Wave reaches the receiver and reflects.**

At the open-circuit receiver, the reflection coefficient is:

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \lim_{Z_L \to \infty} \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = +1$$

Therefore the reflected wave amplitude is:

$$V^- = \Gamma_L \cdot V^+ = +1 \cdot V^+ = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{5}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Reflected wave returns to the source and re-reflects.**

At the source boundary, the reflection coefficient is:

$$\Gamma_S = \frac{Z_s - Z_0}{Z_s + Z_0} = \frac{10 - 50}{10 + 50} = \frac{-40}{60} = -\frac{2}{3}$$

This is the key result: **$\Gamma_S = -2/3 \approx -0.67$**, which is a strong **negative** value.

**Step 4 — Total voltage at the receiver after the second wave arrival.**

The first wave arrives with $V^+ = (5/6) V_{DD}$. The reflected wave from the source arrives after a round-trip delay $2 t_d$, adding:

$$V_{add} = \Gamma_S \cdot V^- = -\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{5}{6} V_{DD} = -\frac{5}{9} V_{DD}$$

**Step 5 — Total steady-state voltage at the receiver.**

$$V_{receiver} = V^+ + V_{add} = \frac{5}{6} V_{DD} - \frac{5}{9} V_{DD} = \frac{15 - 10}{18} V_{DD} = \frac{5}{18} V_{DD}$$

**Step 6 — After many reflections (geometric series).**

The full settling voltage is the sum of an infinite geometric series:

$$V_{final} = V^+ \cdot \left(1 + \Gamma_S \Gamma_L + (\Gamma_S \Gamma_L)^2 + \cdots \right) = V^+ \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \Gamma_S \Gamma_L}$$

With $\Gamma_S \Gamma_L = (-2/3)(+1) = -2/3$:

$$V_{final} = \frac{5}{6} V_{DD} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (-2/3)} = \frac{5}{6} V_{DD} \cdot \frac{1}{5/3} = \frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{3}{5} V_{DD} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DD}$$

This matches the expected divider: $V_{DD} \cdot Z_0 / (Z_s + Z_0) = V_{DD} \cdot 50 / 60$, but only **after** the source has fully charged the line through repeated reflections. The receiver, however, initially sees a *large overshoot* of $(5/6) V_{DD}$, which is the ringing that low-impedance drivers must dampen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above derivation is **the single most commonly asked derivation** in KTU board papers on this topic. Memorize the geometric-series form of $V_{final}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Damping Time Constant

**Given:** Low-impedance driver $Z_s = 10 \, \Omega$, line $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$, total line capacitance $C_L = 20$ pF, $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, initial receiver voltage $V_{receiver}(0) = 2.75$ V (from $5/6 \cdot V_{DD}$).

**Step 1 — Equivalent RC discharge path.**

After the first reflection, the receiver is overcharged and must discharge back toward $V_{final} = 1.65$ V. The discharge current flows back through $Z_0$ into the low-impedance source $Z_s$, with the path resistance:

$$R_{path} = Z_0 + Z_s = 50 + 10 = 60 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time constant.**

$$\tau = R_{path} \cdot C_L = 60 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-12} = 1.2 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Overshoot amplitude.**

$$\Delta V = V_{receiver}(0) - V_{final} = 2.75 - 1.65 = 1.10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Damped settling response.**

$$V(t) = V_{final} + \Delta V \cdot e^{-t/\tau} = 1.65 + 1.10 \cdot e^{-t/1.2\text{ns}}$$

At $t = \tau = 1.2$ ns, the overshoot has decayed to $1.10 \cdot e^{-1} \approx 0.405$ V. At $t = 5\tau = 6$ ns, the overshoot is down to $1.10 \cdot e^{-5} \approx 7$ mV — negligible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Designers typically require the line to settle to within **5%** of its final value before the next edge arrives, which corresponds to about **$3\tau$** of settling time. This sets the maximum data rate for a given line length.

## 3.3 Comparison: Standard vs. Low-Impedance Driver

| Parameter | Standard CMOS Driver ($Z_s = 50 \, \Omega$) | Low-Z Driver ($Z_s = 10 \, \Omega$) |
|-----------|---------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Source reflection coefficient $\Gamma_S$ | $0$ (matched) | $-2/3$ (negative) |
| Receiver overshoot | None on first edge, but slow rise | Large initial overshoot, then damped |
| Ringing severity | Minimal (matched) | Heavily damped |
| Switching current | $V_{DD} / (50 + 50) = 33$ mA | $V_{DD} / (10 + 50) = 55$ mA |
| Power dissipation per switch | $0.5 \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Slightly higher due to $I^2R$ loss |
| Use case | Short traces (< $l_{crit}$) | Long high-speed traces, buses |
| Termination cost | May need external resistor | Internal, tunable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Low-Impedance Driver System

The diagram below illustrates the **signal flow architecture** of a low-impedance driver feeding a transmission line, including the reflection paths. This is the conceptual equivalent of a physical circuit schematic for the KTU 2024 scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CMOS Driver with Low Z Output Zs = 10 ohm] -->|Launches Forward Wave V+| B[Transmission Line Z0 = 50 ohm Length l]
    B -->|Travels in t_d seconds| C[Receiver Load ZL = High Z Open Circuit]
    C -->|Reflects Gamma L equals plus 1| D[Reflected Wave V- returns to source]
    D -->|Hits Source Boundary| E[Source Reflection Gamma S equals minus 2 by 3]
    E -->|Absorbs Energy and Damps Overshoot| F[Overshoot suppressed within 3 tau]
    F -->|Steady State| G[Vfinal equals VDD times Z0 over Zs plus Z0]
```

## 4.2 Waveform Behaviour: Open-Load Receiver with Low-Z Driver

The following flowchart maps out the **time-domain waveform evolution** at the receiver node as the forward and reflected waves superimpose.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[T equals 0 Driver switches high] --> T1[T equals t_d First wave arrives: V = 5 by 6 VDD overshoot]
    T1 --> T2[T equals 2 t_d Reflected wave from source arrives with Gamma S equals minus 2 by 3]
    T2 --> T3[Net voltage drops toward Vfinal = 0.5 VDD]
    T3 --> T4[T equals 3 t_d Second reflection cycle attenuated by Gamma S squared]
    T4 --> T5[Exponential decay envelope with tau equals R path C line]
    T5 --> T6[T greater than 5 tau Steady state reached, no ringing]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Use a Low-Impedance Driver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: High Speed Digital Link Design] --> Q1{Is trace length greater than l_crit?}
    Q1 -->|No| A1[Use standard CMOS driver, treat interconnect as lumped]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is receiver CMOS high impedance open load?}
    Q2 -->|No matched load| A2[Use series termination at source Zs equals Z0]
    Q2 -->|Yes open load| Q3{Is ringing overshoot a problem?}
    Q3 -->|No| A3[Use moderate impedance driver Zs equals Z0 by 2]
    Q3 -->|Yes| A4[Use low impedance driver Zs less than Z0 by 3]
    A4 --> Q4{Multiple receivers bus topology?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| A5[Add additional damping resistors at each tap]
    Q4 -->|No point to point| A6[Implement source series termination with Zs equals Z0 minus 5 ohm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Viva:** The block diagrams above are typically drawn as physical schematics in textbooks. Since this is a notes document, the **functional architecture** form is used to map the same interactions between the driver, line, load, and reflection paths.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> Define a *low-impedance driver*. State **two** key benefits of using a low-impedance driver in a high-speed digital link.

**Model Answer:**

A low-impedance driver is a digital output buffer whose Thevenin equivalent output impedance $Z_s$ is **substantially smaller** than the characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of the driven interconnect, satisfying the design rule $Z_s \leq Z_0 / 3$.

**Two key benefits:**

1. **Reflection damping at the source boundary:** A low $Z_s$ produces a strongly negative source reflection coefficient $\Gamma_S = (Z_s - Z_0)/(Z_s + Z_0) \approx -1$. Returning reflected waves from the receiver are re-reflected with inverted polarity, actively cancelling overshoot and ringing on the line.

2. **Tunable on-die termination (ODT):** Modern CMOS processes integrate programmable low-Z drivers (e.g., DDR4 POD drivers) that eliminate the need for bulky external termination resistors, saving PCB area and reducing cost.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> A driver with $Z_s = 15 \, \Omega$ launches a step into a $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$ line terminated in an open circuit. Calculate the **source reflection coefficient** and comment on whether the driver qualifies as "low impedance."

**Model Answer:**

$$\Gamma_S = \frac{Z_s - Z_0}{Z_s + Z_0} = \frac{15 - 50}{15 + 50} = \frac{-35}{65} = -0.538$$

**Check low-impedance criterion:**

$$\frac{Z_s}{Z_0} = \frac{15}{50} = 0.30 \leq \frac{1}{3} = 0.333$$

**Conclusion:** Since $Z_s / Z_0 = 0.30 \leq 0.33$, the driver **does qualify** as a low-impedance driver. The strongly negative $\Gamma_S = -0.538$ confirms its damping behaviour at the source boundary.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** In multi-reflection problems, students often:
> 1. Forget to convert $\Gamma_S \Gamma_L$ into a product before applying the geometric series.
> 2. Use the wrong sign when adding reflected waves (reflected waves are *algebraically* added, not *subtracted*).
> 3. Confuse $V_{final}$ with $V_{received}$ — the former is the steady-state DC value, the latter is the transient peak.
> Avoid these mistakes to secure full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> A CMOS driver with $Z_s = 10 \, \Omega$ drives a 30 cm PCB microstrip line ($Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$, $t_d$ per unit length $= 7$ ns/m) terminated in a high-impedance CMOS receiver ($Z_L \to \infty$). $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V. Determine:
> (i) The forward wave voltage launched at the source.
> (ii) The reflected wave voltage at the receiver.
> (iii) The total receiver voltage at $t = t_d$.
> (iv) The one-way propagation delay $t_d$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Forward wave voltage:**

$$V^+ = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_s + Z_0} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{50}{10 + 50} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{5}{6} = 2.75 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Reflected wave at the receiver:**

$$\Gamma_L = \lim_{Z_L \to \infty} \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = +1$$

$$V^- = \Gamma_L \cdot V^+ = +1 \cdot 2.75 = 2.75 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Total receiver voltage at $t = t_d$:**

$$V_{received}(t_d) = V^+ \cdot (1 + \Gamma_L) = 2.75 \cdot (1 + 1) = 5.50 \text{ V}$$

Note: This is the **peak overshoot** voltage, which is **1.67 times $V_{DD}$** — a serious signal-integrity violation that justifies a low-Z driver to damp subsequent reflections.

**(iv) One-way propagation delay:**

$$t_d = l \cdot t_{d,\text{per unit}} = 0.30 \text{ m} \cdot 7 \text{ ns/m} = 2.1 \text{ ns}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> Continuing from Part (a), calculate:
> (i) The source reflection coefficient $\Gamma_S$.
> (ii) The final steady-state voltage $V_{final}$ at the receiver after all reflections have settled.
> (iii) The percentage overshoot at the first edge.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Source reflection coefficient:**

$$\Gamma_S = \frac{Z_s - Z_0}{Z_s + Z_0} = \frac{10 - 50}{10 + 50} = -\frac{2}{3} \approx -0.667$$

**(ii) Final steady-state voltage:**

$$V_{final} = V^+ \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \Gamma_S \Gamma_L} = 2.75 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (-2/3)(1)} = 2.75 \cdot \frac{1}{5/3} = 2.75 \cdot 0.6 = 1.65 \text{ V}$$

This matches the intuitive result: $V_{final} = V_{DD}/2 = 3.3/2 = 1.65$ V.

**(iii) Percentage overshoot:**

$$\% \text{Overshoot} = \frac{V_{peak} - V_{final}}{V_{final}} \cdot 100\% = \frac{5.50 - 1.65}{1.65} \cdot 100\% \approx 233\%$$

This dramatic overshoot demonstrates *why* a low-impedance driver must be paired with a properly damped receiver or a clamping diode network.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> A long backplane trace has $Z_0 = 65 \, \Omega$ and is driven by a low-impedance driver with $Z_s = 20 \, \Omega$. The receiver end is connected to a CMOS ASIC with $Z_L = 10 \, k\Omega$ (approximately open). If the line is 50 cm long and $t_d$ per unit length is 6.5 ns/m:
> (i) Identify whether the driver is "low impedance" and justify your answer.
> (ii) Compute the initial forward wave amplitude for a 2.5 V driver supply.
> (iii) Calculate the receiver voltage at first arrival and steady-state.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Low-impedance verification:**

$$\frac{Z_s}{Z_0} = \frac{20}{65} \approx 0.308 \leq 0.333$$

Yes, the driver qualifies as low impedance. Its $\Gamma_S$ will be strongly negative, providing source-side damping.

**(ii) Initial forward wave amplitude:**

$$V^+ = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_s + Z_0} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{65}{20 + 65} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{65}{85} = 1.912 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Receiver voltage at first arrival:**

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{10000 - 65}{10000 + 65} \approx +0.987 \approx +1$$

$$V_{receiver}(t_d) = V^+ \cdot (1 + \Gamma_L) \approx 1.912 \cdot 2 = 3.823 \text{ V}$$

This is **1.53 times $V_{DD}$**, again showing a major overshoot.

**Steady-state voltage:**

$$\Gamma_S = \frac{20 - 65}{20 + 65} = \frac{-45}{85} = -0.529$$

$$V_{final} = 1.912 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (-0.529)(0.987)} = 1.912 \cdot \frac{1}{1.522} = 1.256 \text{ V}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> For the same backplane system as Part (a), determine:
> (i) The settling time required to bring the receiver voltage within 5% of $V_{final}$ if the total line capacitance is 15 pF.
> (ii) Comment on the **power vs. signal-integrity trade-off** in selecting a low-impedance driver for this backplane.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Settling time calculation:**

The path resistance for the discharge is:

$$R_{path} = Z_s + Z_0 = 20 + 65 = 85 \, \Omega$$

The discharge time constant is:

$$\tau = R_{path} \cdot C_L = 85 \cdot 15 \times 10^{-12} = 1.275 \text{ ns}$$

For 5% settling, we need $3\tau$:

$$t_{settle} = 3 \tau = 3 \cdot 1.275 = 3.825 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Power vs. Signal Integrity Trade-off:**

Switching peak current per edge:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_{DD}}{Z_s + Z_0} = \frac{2.5}{85} = 29.4 \text{ mA}$$

For a 500 Mbps data rate (2 transitions per bit = 1 Gbps edge rate):

$$P_{switch} \approx C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f = 15 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2.5^2 \cdot 5 \times 10^8 = 46.9 \text{ mW per line}$$

**Comment:** Lowering $Z_s$ further (e.g., to $5 \, \Omega$) would reduce ringing $Q$-factor by half but increase $I_{peak}$ to $35.7$ mA and power dissipation to about 58 mW — a **25% increase in power for marginal SI gain**. In backplane design, the optimal $Z_s$ is found by sweeping it in simulation to minimize the **eye-diagram jitter** at the receiver, subject to a power budget.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Using Low Impedance Drivers**

- **Definition:** A driver with $Z_{driver} \ll Z_0$, satisfying $Z_{driver} \leq Z_0/3$.
- **Key formula 1:** Source reflection coefficient $\Gamma_S = (Z_s - Z_0)/(Z_s + Z_0) \approx -1$ when $Z_s \ll Z_0$.
- **Key formula 2:** Steady-state voltage after infinite reflections $V_{final} = V^+ / (1 - \Gamma_S \Gamma_L)$.
- **Key formula 3:** Peak receiver overshoot $V_{peak} = V^+ (1 + \Gamma_L) = 2V_{DD} \cdot Z_0 / (Z_s + Z_0)$ for open-load.
- **Key formula 4:** Settling time constant $\tau = (Z_s + Z_0) \cdot C_L$.
- **Key formula 5:** Quality factor $Q = (1/R) \sqrt{L_{eff}/C_{eff}}$ — reduce $Q$ to damp ringing.
- **Three core effects of low-Z drivers:** (i) Source-side damping of reflections, (ii) Higher switching current draw, (iii) Tunable impedance for multi-standard I/O.
- **Real-world examples:** DDR3/4/5 (POD drivers), LVDS, PCIe, SATA, USB 3.x.
- **Trade-off axis:** Signal integrity ($\downarrow$ ringing) **vs.** Power consumption ($\uparrow I_{peak}$) **vs.** Ground bounce ($\uparrow$ SSO noise).
- **Geometric series form** is the most tested derivation in KTU board papers — memorize it.
- **Always verify** $Z_s / Z_0 \leq 1/3$ before declaring a driver "low impedance" in a numerical problem.
- **Open-circuit termination** ($\Gamma_L = +1$) is the worst case — always use it for conservative overshoot estimates.
- **Matched termination** ($\Gamma_L = 0$) eliminates reflections at the receiver but causes full-amplitude $V^+$ propagation loss.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"How does a low-impedance driver improve signal integrity?"*, the model answer must include **all three**: (1) negative $\Gamma_S$, (2) reduced Q-factor, and (3) shorter settling time $\tau$. Skipping any one will cost you 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Low Impedance Distribution Lines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using Low Impedance Distribution Lines

# Using Low Impedance Distribution Lines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Low Impedance Distribution Line** in high-speed digital design is a printed transmission line (microstrip, stripline, or coplanar waveguide) intentionally designed with a characteristic impedance **Z₀** substantially lower than the typical **50 Ω** standard — commonly in the range of **Z₀ ≈ 8 Ω to 33 Ω**. By keeping the characteristic impedance low, the system trades off logic-swing amplitude for superior immunity to reflections, ringing, switching noise (ΔI-noise / ground bounce), and crosstalk. The fundamental design intent is to make the driver behave as a **low-impedance voltage source** relative to the line, so that the receiving end always sees a stable, well-defined voltage independent of the far-end termination mismatch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A Low Impedance Distribution Line is one whose **characteristic impedance Z₀ is deliberately chosen much smaller than the driver output impedance** and often much smaller than the standard 50 Ω reference. This technique, formalized in the works of Johnson & Graham and Howard W. Johnson, is used to guarantee that any impedance discontinuity on the line (connector, branch, stub) produces only a small percentage reflection that the receiving gate can tolerate.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **fire hose connected to a small garden tap**:

- A **high-impedance line (e.g., 50 Ω or higher)** is like a narrow pipe — any kink, leak, or branch fitting causes pressure waves to bounce violently back, and the pressure at the nozzle fluctuates wildly.
- A **low-impedance line (e.g., 10 Ω)** is like a fat, stiff fire hose with a high-pressure pumping station feeding it. Even if someone steps on the hose (a discontinuity), the flow rate at the nozzle hardly changes because the source is so much "stronger" than the disturbance.

In electronics, a low Z₀ means a **stiff, low-impedance source** dominates the line, and small discontinuities (stubs, vias, connectors) become electrically invisible — a hugely desirable property when you have **multi-drop buses**, **DIMM memory arrays**, or **backplane topologies** where physical discontinuities are unavoidable.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Metrics**
> - **Free-space impedance:** Z₀(freespace) = **376.730313461 Ω ≈ 377 Ω**
> - **Typical PCB dielectric constant:** εᵣ = **3.5 – 4.5 (FR-4)**
> - **Targeted low Z₀ range:** **8 Ω to 33 Ω**
> - **Standard reference Z₀:** **50 Ω** (kept as fallback for measurement equipment)
> - **Signal speed on FR-4:** v ≈ **6 inch/ns** (≈ 1.5 × 10⁸ m/s)
> - **Edge rate considered "high-speed":** tᵣ < **2 × t_pd** of the interconnect

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Why is a low impedance line preferred for a clock distribution network?"* — Answer: Because clocks are multi-drop, fan-out heavy, and absolutely intolerant of jitter from reflections.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Why Low Z₀ Helps

The complete design argument rests on the **reflection coefficient** at a load discontinuity. For a line of characteristic impedance Z₀ terminated in an impedance Z_L:

$$\rho_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

The *magnitude* of the voltage swing caused by a reflection is proportional to the *ratio* of the discontinuity to the source/line impedance. The smaller Z₀ is, the smaller the reflection voltage becomes for a given absolute mismatch.

> **Example:** A stub of impedance **Z_stub = 50 Ω** tapping a main line of **Z₀ = 50 Ω** creates a reflection of
> $$\rho = \frac{25 - 50}{25 + 50} = -\frac{1}{3} \approx -33\%$$
> If instead the main line is **Z₀ = 10 Ω**, the same stub now sees an effective load of
> $$\rho = \frac{50 - 10}{50 + 10} = \frac{40}{60} = +66.7\%\text{ (of stub current)}$$
> but the *voltage disturbance on the main line* is governed by the divider: the **±66.7 %** is divided by the source-to-line impedance ratio, yielding only a small **±6.7 %** voltage perturbation on the main line.

### 2.2 Driver, Line, and Receiver Impedance Triangle

A correct high-speed design must satisfy the inequality:

$$Z_{\text{driver}} \ll Z_0 \ll Z_{\text{receiver}}$$

- **Driver (source):** Should be a low-impedance switch (open-drain FET with R_on ≈ 5–10 Ω).
- **Line:** Z₀ chosen small (e.g., 10 Ω) — the "low impedance" the topic is named for.
- **Receiver:** High input impedance CMOS gate (≫ 1 kΩ, ideally infinite).

This creates an **asymmetric reflection environment**:
- At the **receiver (open circuit)**: $\rho = \frac{\infty - Z_0}{\infty + Z_0} = +1$ → full positive reflection (always).
- At the **driver (low R_on)**: $\rho = \frac{Z_0 - R_{\text{on}}}{Z_0 + R_{\text{on}}} \approx +1$ if R_on ≪ Z₀; or $\rho \approx -1$ if R_on ≫ Z₀.

The "sweet spot" is when **R_on ≈ Z₀**, giving a matched source and zero reflection at the driver end.

### 2.3 Voltage Transfer and Terminations

The voltage at the receiver, when the line is driven from impedance Z_S and terminated at the receiver end in Z_L, after infinite reflections (steady state) is:

$$V_{\text{receiver}} = V_{\text{open}} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_S + Z_L} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L}$$

where

$$\rho_S = \frac{Z_S - Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}, \quad \rho_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

### 2.4 ΔI Noise / Switching Noise (Ground Bounce)

Ground bounce voltage is given by:

$$V_{\text{bounce}} = L_{\text{pin}} \cdot \frac{dI}{dt}$$

With many outputs switching simultaneously, the effective dI/dt grows. Lowering Z₀ reduces the current swing per line because

$$I = \frac{V_{\text{swing}}}{Z_0}$$

So a **halving of Z₀ doubles the current** — which seems bad — but the noise is also spread across more capacitance and the resulting ground bounce per unit current is lower when the system is properly designed. (The real benefit comes from terminations and topology, not Z₀ alone.)

### 2.5 Crosstalk Reduction

Crosstalk coefficient between two adjacent microstrips of height *h* above ground and separation *s* is roughly:

$$K_{\text{next}} \approx \frac{1}{1 + 2 \cdot (s/h)^2}$$

A lower Z₀ line has a **wider trace** for the same substrate geometry, which *reduces s/h* and so *increases* crosstalk. **This is the main penalty** of low Z₀ — trade-off must be evaluated.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Per-unit-length LC model — defines characteristic impedance of any lossless line |
| 2 | $\rho = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Reflection coefficient at a load — used to compute ringing & overshoot |
| 3 | $T = 1 + \rho = \dfrac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Transmission coefficient at load boundary |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Signal propagation velocity on the line |
| 5 | $V_{\text{rcvr}} = V_{\text{oc}} \cdot \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_S + Z_L} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L}$ | Steady-state receiver voltage (lattice/bounce diagram result) |
| 6 | $V_{\text{bounce}} = L_{\text{pin}} \cdot dI/dt$ | Ground-bounce / ΔI-noise estimate |
| 7 | $I = V_{\text{swing}} / Z_0$ | Current per line; ↓Z₀ → ↑I |
| 8 | $t_{\text{flight}} = \ell / v$ | Time for edge to traverse line length $\ell$ |
| 9 | $f_{\text{knee}} = 0.5 / t_{r}$ | Frequency above which transmission-line behavior is mandatory |
| 10 | $\rho_S \rho_L < 1$ | Bounded-response condition (Barkhausen-style stability) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Watchpoint:** Whenever you see the phrase *"bounce diagram"*, the formula $V_{\text{rcvr}} = V_{\text{oc}} \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_S + Z_L} \dfrac{1}{1-\rho_S\rho_L}$ is expected. Memorize it cold.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Low impedance distribution lines are the **de-facto industry standard** for:
1. **DDR memory buses** (DDR3/4/5 use Z₀ ≈ 40–50 Ω with carefully controlled multi-drop topology).
2. **Clock distribution trees** in ASICs, FPGAs, and backplanes.
3. **PCI Express, SATA, SERDES links** — though these are 85/100 Ω differential.
4. **High-fanout busses** in industrial control and automotive ECUs.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Receiver Voltage with Mismatched Ends

We will rigorously derive the receiver voltage after infinite reflections. This is the **lattice (bounce) diagram closed-form** that KTU Part-B questions almost always demand.

**Step 1 — Initial Wave Generation**
When the driver switch closes at t = 0, an incident wave of amplitude V_i is launched onto the line:

$$V_i = V_{\text{oc}} \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}$$

**Step 2 — First Arrival at Receiver**
The wave travels time $t_{\text{fd}}$ to the receiver end. The incident voltage at the receiver, *before* any reflection, is V_i.

**Step 3 — Reflection at Receiver**
The receiver presents Z_L. Using the boundary condition, the reflected wave amplitude is:

$$V_{r,1} = \rho_L \cdot V_i, \quad \rho_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

The voltage seen at the receiver end of the line (KVL: incident + reflected) is:

$$V_{\text{at rcvr}} = V_i + V_{r,1} = V_i (1 + \rho_L)$$

**Step 4 — Reflection from Source**
The reflected wave V_{r,1} returns to the source. The source-end reflection coefficient is:

$$\rho_S = \frac{Z_S - Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}$$

The new wave re-launched back to the receiver has amplitude:

$$V_{i,2} = \rho_S \cdot V_{r,1} = \rho_S \rho_L \cdot V_i$$

**Step 5 — Infinite Geometric Series**
The total voltage at the receiver is the sum of all incident waves plus their first reflections at the receiver:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{rcvr}} &= V_i (1 + \rho_L) + V_{i,2}(1 + \rho_L) + V_{i,3}(1 + \rho_L) + \dots \\
&= V_i (1 + \rho_L) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (\rho_S \rho_L)^n \\
&= V_i (1 + \rho_L) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L}
\end{aligned}$$

(valid for $\vert \rho_S \rho_L \vert < 1$, the bounded-response condition.)

**Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{rcvr}} &= V_{\text{oc}} \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0} \cdot (1 + \rho_L) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L} \\
&= V_{\text{oc}} \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0} \cdot \frac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L} \\
&= V_{\text{oc}} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_S + Z_L} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **classic closed-form** used in every KTU exam answer for transmission-line bounce problems.

### 3.2 Derivation: Why Low Z₀ Attenuates Stub-Induced Disturbance

Consider a main line of Z₀ driving a stub of impedance Z_stub, length << λ/4. The stub appears as a shunt impedance Z_stub at the junction.

**Step 1 — Equivalent Shunt at Junction**
The reflection coefficient at the junction, looking from the main line into the parallel combination of through-line (Z₀) and stub (Z_stub):

$$Z_{\text{par}} = \frac{Z_0 \cdot Z_{\text{stub}}}{Z_0 + Z_{\text{stub}}}$$

**Step 2 — Reflection Coefficient at Junction**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{junction}} &= \frac{Z_{\text{par}} - Z_0}{Z_{\text{par}} + Z_0} \\
&= \frac{\frac{Z_0 Z_{\text{stub}}}{Z_0 + Z_{\text{stub}}} - Z_0}{\frac{Z_0 Z_{\text{stub}}}{Z_0 + Z_{\text{stub}}} + Z_0} \\
&= \frac{Z_{\text{stub}} - (Z_0 + Z_{\text{stub}})}{Z_{\text{stub}} + (Z_0 + Z_{\text{stub}})} \\
&= \frac{-Z_0}{Z_0 + 2 Z_{\text{stub}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of Reflection Voltage**
The reflected voltage (as a fraction of incident) is:

$$\vert \rho_{\text{junction}} \vert = \frac{Z_0}{Z_0 + 2 Z_{\text{stub}}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
- If Z₀ = 50 Ω and Z_stub = 50 Ω: $\vert \rho \vert = 50 / 150 = 33\%$.
- If Z₀ = 10 Ω and Z_stub = 50 Ω: $\vert \rho \vert = 10 / 110 \approx 9\%$.

Thus, a 5× reduction in Z₀ reduces the stub-induced reflection by **~3.7×**, validating the "low impedance line is forgiving of discontinuities" claim.

### 3.3 Derivation: Current & ΔI-Noise Trade-off

The current per switching line is:

$$I_{\text{line}} = \frac{V_{\text{swing}}}{Z_0}$$

For N lines switching simultaneously, the *simultaneous* current change is bounded by the package pin inductance:

$$V_{\text{bounce}} = L_{\text{pin}} \cdot \frac{dI}{dt} \approx L_{\text{pin}} \cdot N \cdot \frac{I_{\text{line}}}{t_r}$$

The *fractional* voltage disturbance at the receiver is:

$$\frac{V_{\text{bounce}}}{V_{\text{swing}}} = \frac{N \cdot L_{\text{pin}}}{t_r \cdot Z_0}$$

Lower Z₀ **increases the denominator**, *reducing* the fractional noise — this is the rigorous justification for "low Z₀ = low noise" used in KTU 2024 Module 4.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 5 cm long microstrip on FR-4 has Z₀ = 10 Ω, t_pd = 0.7 ns/m. Source impedance Z_S = 5 Ω, receiver is high-Z (Z_L = ∞). A 2 V step is launched. Find the steady-state voltage at the receiver and the time at which it settles to within 5 % of final value.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Reflection Coefficients**

$$\rho_S = \frac{5 - 10}{5 + 10} = -\frac{1}{3}, \quad \rho_L = \frac{\infty - 10}{\infty + 10} = +1$$

**Step 2 — Steady-State Voltage**

$$V_{\text{rcvr}} = V_{\text{oc}} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_S + Z_L} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L} = 2 \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (-\tfrac{1}{3})(1)} = \frac{2}{1 + \tfrac{1}{3}} = 1.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Time of Flight**

$$t_{\text{fd}} = (5 \text{ cm})(0.7 \text{ ns/m}) = 0.05 \text{ m} \times 0.7 \text{ ns/m} = 0.035 \text{ ns} = 35 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 4 — Round-Trip Time**

$$t_{\text{rt}} = 2 t_{\text{fd}} = 70 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 5 — Bounce Diagram (First 4 arrivals)**
- t = 35 ps: V = 2 · (10/15) · 1 = **1.333 V**
- t = 70 ps: V = 1.333 + (2·(10/15)·1)·(ρ_S) = 1.333 + 1.333·(-1/3) = **0.889 V**
- t = 105 ps: V = 0.889 + 1.333·(ρ_S ρ_L) = 0.889 + 1.333·(-1/3) = **0.444 V**... [wait, this is wrong direction; let me redo carefully]

Let me redo the bounce diagram correctly with **incident** wave magnitudes:
- V_incident = V_oc · Z₀/(Z_S+Z₀) = 2·10/15 = 1.333 V

| t (ps) | Event | V at receiver |
|---|---|---|
| 35 | First arrival of +1.333 V | 1.333 V |
| 70 | Reflection from source returns (ρ_S = −1/3): new V = 1.333·(−1/3) = −0.444 V | 1.333 − 0.444 = 0.889 V |
| 105 | Source reflection arrives again: 1.333·(1/9) = 0.148 V | 0.889 + 0.148 = 1.037 V |
| 140 | Next: 1.333·(−1/27) = −0.049 V | 1.037 − 0.049 = 0.988 V |
| ∞ | Converges to | **1.500 V** ✓ |

**Step 6 — Settling to within 5 %** (i.e., within 0.075 V of 1.5 V)
This occurs at t ≈ 140 ps (within 0.012 V). So settling time ≈ **0.14 ns** for a 5 cm line.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Low-Z Distribution Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    DRV[Driver Stage - Low Zs] --> ML[Main Distribution Line - Low Z0 approx 10 to 33 Ohm]
    ML --> S1[Stub Branch 1 - Loads]
    ML --> S2[Stub Branch 2 - Loads]
    ML --> S3[Stub Branch 3 - Loads]
    ML --> S4[Stub Branch 4 - Loads]
    S1 --> R1[Receiver Gate - High Zin]
    S2 --> R2[Receiver Gate - High Zin]
    S3 --> R3[Receiver Gate - High Zin]
    S4 --> R4[Receiver Gate - High Zin]
    ML --> TERM[Far End Termination - Optional Thevenin or AC]
    DRV --> GND[Return Path - Low Inductance Plane]
    TERM --> GND
    R1 --> GND
    R2 --> GND
    R3 --> GND
    R4 --> GND
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Driver** has low Z_S (≈ 5 Ω) — depicted as a strong, stiff source.
- The **Main Line** is the low-Z₀ backbone carrying the signal.
- **Stubs** tap the main line to multiple receivers; they are the discontinuities that *would* cause severe reflections on a 50 Ω line, but are tolerable on a 10 Ω line.
- The **far-end termination** may be omitted (relying on receiver's high-Z to give ρ = +1) or added as a Thevenin pair for extra cleanliness.
- The **return path** (ground/power plane) is emphasized — it must have low inductance or the whole low-Z₀ benefit is lost.

### 4.2 Bounce Diagram (Lattice Diagram) of Reflections

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0["t=0 at Driver: V=Vs"] --> T1["t=tfd at Rcvr: V=Vi"]
    T1 --> T2["t=2tfd at Drv: V=Vi rhoL"]
    T2 --> T3["t=3tfd at Rcvr: V=Vi rhoL rhoS"]
    T3 --> T4["t=4tfd at Drv: V=Vi rhoL2 rhoS"]
    T4 --> T5["t=5tfd at Rcvr: V=Vi rhoL2 rhoS2"]
    T5 --> TINF["t=infinity: Vss = Vi 1 plus rhoL over 1 minus rhoS rhoL"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each horizontal step represents one round-trip of the wave between driver and receiver.
- The voltage at each "kink" of the lattice is the cumulative sum of all incident waves arriving at that node up to that time.
- The lattice converges to the steady-state value calculated in §3.1.

### 4.3 Termination Topology Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Choose Z0 for the bus] --> B{Fan-out and topology?}
    B -->|Single receiver, point-to-point| C[Use series termination at source]
    B -->|Multi-drop bus| D[Use low Z0 line with parallel end termination]
    B -->|Daisy chain| E[Use distributed series termination]
    B -->|High-speed differential| F[Use 100 ohm diff with matched termination]
    D --> G{Receiver count high?}
    G -->|Yes, more than 5| H[Accept DC power loss - Thevenin OK]
    G -->|No, less than 5| I[Use AC termination - capacitor to ground]
```
