## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "low impedance distribution line" as used in high-speed digital design. Why is it preferred for clock distribution networks?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A low-impedance distribution line is a transmission line whose characteristic impedance Z₀ is deliberately chosen to be much smaller than the standard 50 Ω — typically in the range **8 Ω to 33 Ω** — such that the driver's output impedance is much smaller than Z₀ and the receiver's input impedance is much larger than Z₀.

It is preferred for clock distribution because:
1. Clock networks are **multi-drop / high fan-out**, so discontinuities (stubs, vias) are unavoidable.
2. A low Z₀ makes the **source dominate** the reflections, so the perturbation caused by any single stub is small.
3. The resulting clock has **low jitter and low ringing**, which is critical for timing accuracy.

*(Word count target: ~70 words; expected time: 5 min)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State and explain the significance of the reflection coefficient formula $\rho = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ in the context of low impedance lines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$\rho = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

This is the **reflection coefficient** at a load boundary, giving the ratio of the reflected wave amplitude to the incident wave amplitude. Its significance for low-impedance lines is:
- When Z₀ ≪ Z_L (low Z₀, high Z receiver), $\rho \to +1$ (open-circuit-like reflection at receiver).
- When Z₀ ≫ Z_S (low Z₀, low Z driver), $\rho_S \to -1$ at the driver.
- The product $\rho_S \rho_L$ determines the **ringing amplitude** at the receiver. A small |Z₀| makes the system robust to load variations because the *percentage* mismatch seen by a stub is reduced.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a low-impedance clock distribution network. List **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of using Z₀ = 10 Ω versus Z₀ = 50 Ω. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a low-impedance line of Z₀ = 15 Ω, source impedance Z_S = 5 Ω, and open-circuit receiver (Z_L → ∞), compute:
1. The reflection coefficients at both ends.
2. The steady-state receiver voltage for a 3.3 V step.
3. The receiver voltage at the first, second, and third arrival.
4. The total settling time to within 2 % of final value. Take t_fd = 50 ps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

*Architecture (block diagram — refer to §4.1):*
A low-impedance clock distribution network consists of a **low-impedance driver** (Z_S ≈ 5–10 Ω) feeding a **main backbone line** (Z₀ ≈ 8–33 Ω) from which multiple **stubs** branch out to **receiver gates** (high-Z). Optionally, a **far-end termination** (Thevenin or AC) is placed at the bus end. A **low-inductance ground/power plane** provides the return path.

*Advantages of Z₀ = 10 Ω vs 50 Ω:*
1. **Stub immunity:** A 50 Ω stub on a 10 Ω main line causes ~9 % reflection vs ~33 % on a 50 Ω line.
2. **Lower ringing/overshoot** at the receivers because $\rho_S \rho_L$ is dominated by the source-end reflection, which is automatically controlled by the low Z_S.

*Disadvantages:*
1. **Higher drive current** per line: I = V_swing / Z₀ is 5× larger, increasing power dissipation.
2. **Increased crosstalk** because lower Z₀ traces are wider and closer to neighbors, raising the mutual capacitance coupling coefficient.

**(b) [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Reflection Coefficients*

$$\rho_S = \frac{5 - 15}{5 + 15} = -\frac{10}{20} = -0.5, \quad \rho_L = \frac{\infty - 15}{\infty + 15} = +1$$

*Step 2 — Steady-State Voltage*

$$V_{\text{rcvr,ss}} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_S + Z_L} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L} = 3.3 \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (-0.5)(1)} = \frac{3.3}{1.5} = 2.2 \text{ V}$$

*Step 3 — Initial Incident Wave*

$$V_i = 3.3 \cdot \frac{15}{5+15} = 3.3 \cdot 0.75 = 2.475 \text{ V}$$

| Arrival | Time (ps) | V at receiver (V) |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | 50 | 2.475 |
| 2nd | 150 | 2.475 + 2.475·(−0.5) = 1.238 |
| 3rd | 250 | 1.238 + 2.475·(0.25) = 1.857 |

*Step 4 — Settling within 2 %* of 2.2 V means within 0.044 V.
Required: $\vert V_n - 2.2 \vert < 0.044$
Using $V_n = 2.2 (1 - (-0.5)^n)$ we get $(-0.5)^n < 0.02$, so $n \geq 6$.
Settling time = $6 \times t_{\text{rt}} = 6 \times 100 \text{ ps} = 600 \text{ ps}$ (≈ 0.6 ns).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive, starting from the fundamental reflection coefficient, the closed-form expression for the steady-state voltage at the receiver of a mismatched transmission line with source impedance Z_S and load impedance Z_L. Clearly show the geometric series summation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A backplane bus uses a Z₀ = 20 Ω line driven by a 2.5 V CMOS driver (Z_S = 5 Ω). The far end is open. Compute:
1. The steady-state high-level voltage at the far end.
2. The maximum overshoot as a percentage of the steady-state value.
3. The time to settle within 5 % of final value, given t_fd = 200 ps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]** — See §3.1 above for the complete derivation. Recap key steps:

1. Initial incident wave: $V_i = V_{oc} \dfrac{Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}$
2. First arrival at receiver: $V_1 = V_i (1 + \rho_L)$
3. Source re-reflection: $V_2 = V_{i,2}(1 + \rho_L) = V_i \rho_S \rho_L (1 + \rho_L)$
4. Geometric series: $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (\rho_S \rho_L)^n = \dfrac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L}$
5. Final result: $V_{\text{rcvr}} = V_{\text{oc}} \cdot \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_S + Z_L} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L}$

**(b) [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Reflection Coefficients*

$$\rho_S = \frac{5 - 20}{5 + 20} = -0.6, \quad \rho_L = +1$$

*Step 2 — Steady-State Voltage*

$$V_{\text{ss}} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (-0.6)(1)} = \frac{2.5}{1.6} = 1.5625 \text{ V}$$

*Step 3 — Maximum Overshoot*
The first arrival (at t = 200 ps) is:

$$V_{\text{peak}} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{20}{25} = 2.0 \text{ V}$$

Percentage overshoot: $\dfrac{2.0 - 1.5625}{1.5625} \times 100\% = 28\%$

*Step 4 — Settling within 5 %* (within 0.078 V of 1.5625 V)
$V_n = 1.5625 (1 - (-0.6)^n)$; require $\vert (-0.6)^n \vert < 0.05$
$(-0.6)^4 = 0.1296$ — not yet
$(-0.6)^5 = -0.0778$ — magnitude 0.0778 > 0.05 — not yet
$(-0.6)^6 = 0.0467$ — < 0.05 ✓
Settling time = $6 \times 2 \times 200 \text{ ps} = 2.4 \text{ ns}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the steady-state formula derivation** — many students state the formula but cannot derive it; KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests derivation ability (Bloom's Level 4 — *Analyze*).
> 2. **Sign errors in $\rho_S$** — the formula is $(Z_S - Z_0)/(Z_S + Z_0)$, NOT $(Z_0 - Z_S)/(Z_S + Z_0)$. A sign flip changes the entire overshoot direction. KTU answer keys explicitly mark this.
> 3. **Mixing up t_fd and t_rt** — round-trip time is **twice** the one-way flight time. Common 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Omitting the bounded-response condition** $\vert \rho_S \rho_L \vert < 1$ — if violated, the series diverges and the analysis is invalid. Always state this.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (V vs mV, ns vs ps). KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 mark per unit error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 / Low-Z Distribution Lines**

- **Definition:** A low-Z line has Z₀ = 8–33 Ω, much lower than 50 Ω.
- **Why?** Driver Z_S ≪ Z₀ ≪ Receiver Z_L; source dominates reflections.
- **Reflection coefficient formula:** $\rho = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ — *memorize verbatim*.
- **Steady-state receiver voltage:** $V_{\text{rcvr}} = V_{\text{oc}} \cdot \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_S + Z_L} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - \rho_S \rho_L}$.
- **Bounded-response condition:** $\vert \rho_S \rho_L \vert < 1$ — *always* state this when using the closed form.
- **Round-trip time:** $t_{\text{rt}} = 2 t_{\text{fd}} = 2 \ell / v$.
- **Stub disturbance rule of thumb:** A 50 Ω stub on a 10 Ω line causes ~9 % reflection vs ~33 % on a 50 Ω line.
- **ΔI-noise (ground bounce):** $V_{\text{bounce}} = L_{\text{pin}} \cdot dI/dt$; lowering Z₀ reduces the *fractional* bounce.
- **Crosstalk penalty:** Lower Z₀ ⇒ wider traces ⇒ higher mutual coupling; check K_next before committing to Z₀.
- **Termination choice:** Series at source for point-to-point; parallel at far end for multi-drop; AC termination for low-power multi-drop.
- **Free-space impedance:** **377 Ω** — useful for sanity checks in antenna problems (KTU sometimes asks).
- **Industry context:** DDR buses, backplanes, FPGA clock trees all use variants of low-Z design.
- **Exam tip:** Always draw the **bounce (lattice) diagram** when solving a transmission-line problem — it earns easy process marks even if the arithmetic is wrong.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Memory Mnemonic — "DLDS-R"**: **D**river (low Z) **D**rives **L**ine (low Z₀) **S**erves **R**eceivers (high Z). This is the *only* impedance ordering that makes low-Z distribution work.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delay Adjustments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Delay Adjustments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Delay Adjustments — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In high-speed digital design, **Delay Adjustment** refers to the deliberate insertion, removal, or programmability of propagation delay along a signal or clock path so that the **time-of-arrival (TOA)** of multiple signals at a destination node satisfies a specified timing relationship (e.g., alignment, phase offset, or skew budget).

The KTU 2024 Scheme frames Delay Adjustment as a **timing-margin engineering discipline** that addresses three fundamental problems:

1. **Skew correction** — making two nominally identical paths produce equal delays.
2. **Phase shifting** — generating a known, controllable phase difference between two reference edges.
3. **Latency equalization** — matching delays across widely different fanouts and load capacitances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (PEECT526 · Module 4):** Delay adjustments are a prerequisite for clock distribution, source-synchronous interfaces, DDR memory strobe alignment, time-interleaved ADCs, and time-to-digital converters. Every GHz-class digital system contains at least one explicit delay-adjustment block.

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standard across KTU-evaluated high-speed design problems:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Propagation delay | $t_{pd}$ | **ps**, **ns** |
| Skew | $\Delta t_{skew}$ | **ps** |
| Jitter (cycle-to-cycle) | $t_{j,c2c}$ | **ps RMS** |
| Unit delay | $\tau$ | **ps** |
| Buffer fan-out | $f$ | dimensionless |
| Tap resolution | $\Delta t_{tap}$ | **ps** |
| Tuning range | $T_{tune}$ | **ns** |
| Phase margin | $\phi_{m}$ | **°** or **rad** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marathon Relay" Intuition

Imagine 8 runners starting a relay race at the *same physical line*, but their tracks are different lengths (curved inner tracks vs. long outer tracks). If all runners ran at the *same* speed, the one on the longest track would hand off the baton **much later**, breaking the team's rhythm.

**Delay Adjustment is the act of deliberately slowing down the runners on shorter tracks** (by adding sand, hurdles, or detours) so that **all 8 runners hand off the baton at exactly the same instant**.

In CMOS, the "tracks" are the *metal wires and transistor chains*; the "slowing-down mechanism" is a *delay line, buffer chain, or programmable phase interpolator*. The "baton" is the clock edge or strobe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Delay adjustment does **not** change the data content; it only changes *when* the data is sampled or latched. This is the *temporal* dimension of signal integrity.

## 1.4 Why Delay Adjustment is Non-Trivial in Modern CMOS

At sub-**100 ps** clock periods (≥ 10 GHz), a **1 mm** of unmatched trace introduces ≈ **6–7 ps** of delay (assuming $t_{pd,trace} \approx 6\,\text{ps/mm}$ for FR-4 / standard CMOS BEOL). Hence, *uncorrected* physical mismatches alone can consume **5–10 %** of the clock period. Delay adjustment circuits correct this residual.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Delay Adjustment Techniques

A KTU 2024 answer should classify delay adjustment along **four** orthogonal axes:

### A. By Tunability

1. **Fixed (Passive) Delays** — Set once at design time. No real-time control.
   - Wire meandering
   - Tapered buffer chains
   - Inverter chains
   - RC delay lines
2. **Variable (Active / Programmable) Delays** — Controlled at runtime by a digital code or analog bias.
   - Current-starved inverters
   - Shunt-capacitor arrays
   - Varactor-loaded delay lines
   - Phase interpolators
   - DLL/PLL-based deskew

### B. By Granularity

1. **Coarse delay** — Large steps (≥ 1 inverter delay ≈ 30–100 ps).
2. **Fine delay** — Sub-gate-delay steps (≤ 10 ps), often Vernier-based.

### C. By Signal Domain

1. **Clock-path delays** (clock distribution trees, deskew circuits).
2. **Data-path delays** (source-synchronous strobe alignment).
3. **Control-path delays** (reset, scan-enable skew matching).

### D. By Physical Mechanism

1. **RC charging** (dominant in 180–28 nm CMOS).
2. **LC transmission-line propagation** (GHz+ SerDes, mm-wave).
3. **Charge-redistribution** (switched-capacitor delay lines).
4. **Saturated velocity propagation** (on-chip transmission lines).

## 2.2 Operating Principles of Each Mechanism

### 2.2.1 Inverter-Chain Fixed Delay
Each CMOS inverter introduces a propagation delay $t_{p,inv}$ that depends on load capacitance $C_L$ and effective ON-current $I_{on}$:

$$
t_{p,inv} \;\approx\; \frac{C_L \, V_{DD}}{2 \, I_{on}} \;+\; \tau_{int}
$$

where $\tau_{int}$ is the intrinsic parasitic delay (~30 % of total in modern nodes).

An N-stage chain has total delay:

$$
t_{d,chain} \;=\; N \, t_{p,inv}
$$

### 2.2.2 Buffer Insertion (Repowering) Delay
Inserting buffers at intervals prevents quadratic RC growth. After $k$ buffer insertions on a long wire:

$$
t_{d,buffered} \;\approx\; k \left( t_{p,buf} + 0.4 \, R_{wire}C_{wire,segment} \right)
$$

The optimum number of buffers (Bakoglu's metric) is:

$$
N_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.4 \, R_{int} \, C_L}{0.7 \, R_0 \, C_{int}}}
$$

where $R_{int}, C_{int}$ are the inverter's intrinsic output resistance and input capacitance.

### 2.2.3 Current-Starved Inverter (CSI) — *Programmable Delay*
A PMOS/NMOS pair acts as a current source, *starving* the inverter's charging current. The effective delay is:

$$
t_{d,CSI} \;=\; \frac{C_L \, V_{sw}}{I_{bias}}
$$

where $V_{sw}$ is the switching threshold voltage and $I_{bias}$ is set by an external digital-to-analog converter (DAC). Range can span **3–5×** of nominal delay.

### 2.2.4 Shunt-Capacitor (Digitally Controlled) Delay
Adding switchable MOS capacitors $C_{add}$ at the output node of an inverter:

$$
t_{d,shunt} \;=\; 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, (C_{int} + n \, C_{add})
$$

where $n$ is the digital control word (number of capacitors engaged). Resolution:

$$
\Delta t_{tap} \;=\; 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_{add}
$$

### 2.2.5 LC Transmission Line Delay
A controlled-impedance line of length $l$ has delay:

$$
t_{d,TL} \;=\; l \, \sqrt{L \, C}
$$

For a 50 Ω microstrip on a typical CMOS BEOL with $\varepsilon_r \approx 4$:

$$
t_{d,TL} \;\approx\; 6{-}7 \, \text{ps/mm}
$$

### 2.2.6 Vernier Delay Line (Sub-Gate-Delay Resolution)
Two parallel delay lines with slightly different per-stage delays $t_1$ and $t_2$ ($t_1 > t_2$). The differential delay per stage is:

$$
\Delta t_{vernier} \;=\; t_1 - t_2
$$

After N matched stages, the time between the two output edges is:

$$
t_{vernier,N} \;=\; N \, (t_1 - t_2)
$$

Resolution is **independent** of absolute gate delay — a major KTU-favorite insight.

### 2.2.7 Phase Interpolator (PI)
Two clocks of phase $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ are weighted-summed by currents $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$
\phi_{out} \;=\; \phi_1 + \frac{I_2}{I_1 + I_2} (\phi_2 - \phi_1)
$$

Resolution is set by current-DAC linearity, often **≪ 1 ps** in 28 nm CMOS.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inverter intrinsic delay | $t_{p0} = 0.69 \, R_{eq} C_{int}$ | Self-loaded inverter |
| 2 | Inverter loaded delay | $t_{p} = t_{p0}(1 + C_L/C_{int})$ | Fan-out $f = C_L/C_{int}$ |
| 3 | Optimum stage ratio | $f_{opt} = \sqrt{C_{out}/C_{in}} = e$ | Logical effort rule |
| 4 | Optimum stage count | $N_{opt} = \ln(f_{total})/\ln(f_{opt})$ | Minimum total delay |
| 5 | Minimum path delay | $t_{p,min} = N_{opt} \, t_{p,opt}$ | Logical effort optimum |
| 6 | Elmore delay (RC tree) | $t_D = \sum_k R_{ik} \, C_k$ | Sum over all capacitors |
| 7 | N-stage RC ladder | $t_D = RC \cdot N(N+1)/2$ | Equal R, C per stage |
| 8 | Current-starved delay | $t_{d,CSI} = C_L V_{sw}/I_{bias}$ | $I_{bias}$ programmable |
| 9 | Shunt-cap tap delay | $\Delta t_{tap} = 0.69 R_{eq} C_{add}$ | 1 LSB of digital control |
| 10 | Vernier step | $\Delta t = t_1 - t_2$ | Sub-gate resolution |
| 11 | Phase interpolator | $\phi_{out} = (I_1 \phi_1 + I_2 \phi_2)/(I_1 + I_2)$ | Linear weighting |
| 12 | LC line delay | $t_d = l \sqrt{LC}$ | Lossless assumption |
| 13 | LC characteristic Z | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | 50 Ω typical |
| 14 | Skew definition | $t_{skew} = t_{max} - t_{min}$ | Across matched paths |
| 15 | DLL steady-state | $\phi_{out} - \phi_{in} = 0$ modulo $T_{clk}$ | Lock condition |
| 16 | Shunt-peaked BW | $f_{-3dB} \approx 1.6/(2\pi R_L C)$ | $L = 0.4 R_L^2 C$ |
| 17 | Wire delay per mm | $t_{d,wire} \approx \sqrt{LC}$ ps/mm | 6–7 ps/mm typical |
| 18 | Inverter chain total | $t_d = N t_p$ | Linear sum |
| 19 | Buffer insertion gain | $\Delta t = k(t_{buf} + 0.4 R_w C_w)$ | k buffer insertions |
| 20 | Duty-cycle correction | $DC = 50 \% \pm \Delta t/T_{clk}$ | After DCC block |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the **logical-effort** result (rows 3–5) when asked about *optimum* buffering. Use **Elmore delay** when asked about *first-order estimation of RC tree delay* — the KTU textbook answer.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Delay adjustment circuits are the **backbone of every high-speed serial-link receiver, DRAM interface, and clock-distribution network**:

- **DDR5/LPDDR5x memory controllers**: use delay-locked loops (DLLs) to align DQS to CK.
- **PCIe Gen 5/6 receivers**: per-lane deskew blocks.
- **Time-Interleaved ADCs**: per-channel delay adjustment corrects aperture-time mismatch.
- **Source-synchronous buses** (e.g., parallel display links): match strobe to data across 16–32 lanes.
- **Clock-and-Data Recovery (CDR) circuits**: phase interpolators at the heart of every CDR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Elmore Delay of a Uniform N-Stage RC Ladder

This is a **classic KTU derivation** and appears in almost every Module-4 past paper. We derive the 50 %-propagation delay from the input of the ladder to the output of the $N^{th}$ node.

**Setup:** N identical stages, each with series resistance $R$ and shunt capacitance $C$ to ground. The ladder is driven by an ideal step $V_{DD}$ at the input.

### Step 1 — Capacitor charging currents

The Thevenin resistance seen by the $k^{th}$ capacitor $C_k$ (counted from the *output* end inward) is the sum of all resistors between $C_k$ and the source:

$$
R_{th,k} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N-k+1} R \;=\; (N - k + 1) \, R
$$

> *Logic:* Current to $C_k$ must flow through every resistor *upstream* of it. The farther $C_k$ is from the source, the more R it sees.

### Step 2 — Apply Elmore's theorem

Elmore's first-moment delay (≈ propagation delay for a step input) is:

$$
t_{D} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} R_{th,k} \, C_k
$$

Since each stage has identical $C$:

$$
t_{D} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} (N - k + 1) \, R \, C
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute index $m = N - k + 1$

As $k$ runs from $1$ to $N$, $m$ runs from $N$ down to $1$:

$$
t_{D} \;=\; R \, C \sum_{m=1}^{N} m
$$

### Step 4 — Closed-form summation

$$
\sum_{m=1}^{N} m \;=\; \frac{N(N+1)}{2}
$$

Hence:

$$
t_{D} \;=\; R \, C \, \frac{N(N+1)}{2}
$$

> *Verification:* For $N=1$, $t_D = RC$ ✓ (single RC). For $N=2$, $t_D = 3RC$ ✓ (sum of $(2R)C_1 + (R)C_2$).

### Step 5 — Asymptotic scaling

For large $N$:

$$
t_{D} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{2} R \, C \, N^2
$$

**Quadratic growth with $N$** is precisely why long RC wires are *impractical* and **buffer insertion** is mandatory. The KTU follow-up question: *"How many buffers should be inserted for minimum delay?"* is answered in §3.2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Logical-Effort Optimum Buffering

**Setup:** A long wire of total resistance $R_w$ and total capacitance $C_w$ is to be driven by a chain of $N$ identical inverters, each of input capacitance $C_{in}$, output resistance $R_{inv}$, and self-load $C_{out}$.

### Step 1 — Per-stage electrical effort

The load seen by each stage is the wire-segment RC plus the next stage's input capacitance. Define the per-stage fan-out (electrical effort):

$$
f \;=\; \frac{C_{out,segment}}{C_{in}}
$$

The total path effort is $F = C_w / C_{in}$ and the path logical effort is $G = 1$ (inverter chain).

### Step 2 — Total path delay (Logical Effort)

$$
t_p \;=\; N \left( t_{p0} + t_{p0} \, f \right) \;=\; t_{p0} \, N (1 + f)
$$

But $F = f^N$ → $f = F^{1/N}$. Therefore:

$$
t_p \;=\; t_{p0} \, N \left( 1 + F^{1/N} \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Minimize with respect to $N$

Differentiate with respect to $N$ and set to zero. Use $\partial F^{1/N}/\partial N = -F^{1/N} \ln F / N^2$:

$$
\frac{\partial t_p}{\partial N} \;=\; t_{p0} \left[ (1 + F^{1/N}) - \frac{F^{1/N} \ln F}{N} \right] \;=\; 0
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
F^{1/N} (\ln F - N) \;=\; N
$$

### Step 4 — Solve the transcendental equation (qualitative result)

The optimum occurs at:

$$
f_{opt} \;=\; e \;\approx\; 2.718
$$

> *Intuition:* Each stage should fan out by exactly $e$. This is a **KTU golden result** — committing it to memory is essential.

### Step 5 — Optimum stage count

$$
N_{opt} \;=\; \frac{\ln(F)}{\ln(e)} \;=\; \ln \left( \frac{C_w}{C_{in}} \right)
$$

### Step 6 — Minimum achievable delay

$$
t_{p,min} \;=\; t_{p0} \, N_{opt} \, (1 + e) \;\approx\; 3.7 \, t_{p0} \, \ln \left( \frac{C_w}{C_{in}} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This logarithmic dependence on wire length is why **proper buffering yields nearly constant delay** per unit wire, and why unbuffered wires scale **quadratically** (from §3.1). The *ratio* is the entire reason delay adjustment exists.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vernier Delay-Line Resolution

**Setup:** A coarse line of N stages each with delay $t_1$ runs in parallel with a fine line of N stages each with delay $t_2$, where $t_1 > t_2$. Both start simultaneously. After N stages, the *leading* line has progressed by $N t_1$ and the *trailing* line by $N t_2$. The trailing line is **ahead** by $\Delta T$ relative to leading? — careful, we define the *time difference* between the two output edges.

### Step 1 — Per-stage differential delay

$$
\Delta t_{stage} \;=\; t_1 - t_2
$$

### Step 2 — Output-edge time difference

The fine line, being *faster*, completes N stages earlier by:

$$
\Delta T_{N} \;=\; N (t_1 - t_2)
$$

### Step 3 — Resolution (smallest step)

One stage of the Vernier line has resolution:

$$
\Delta t_{vernier} \;=\; t_1 - t_2
$$

### Step 4 — Total tuning range

$$
T_{tune} \;=\; N_{max} \, (t_1 - t_2)
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style)

Suppose $t_1 = 80$ ps, $t_2 = 70$ ps, and $N = 64$:

$$
\Delta t_{vernier} \;=\; 10 \, \text{ps}
$$

$$
T_{tune} \;=\; 64 \times 10 \;=\; 640 \, \text{ps}
$$

> *Insight:* A 10 ps resolution is *finer* than a single 28 nm inverter delay (~30 ps). This is the entire motivation for Vernier architectures in **time-to-digital converters (TDCs)** — a frequent KTU sub-question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — DLL Lock Condition and Acquisition Range

A Delay-Locked Loop (DLL) is the canonical *closed-loop* delay adjustment circuit.

### Step 1 — Block diagram and signals

A reference clock $CK_{ref}$ enters a **Voltage-Controlled Delay Line (VCDL)** producing $CK_{delayed}$. A **Phase Detector (PD)** compares $CK_{delayed}$ against $CK_{ref}$ and drives a **charge pump** that adjusts the VCDL control voltage $V_c$.

### Step 2 — Steady-state lock condition

At lock:

$$
t_{d,VCDL}(V_c) \;=\; N \, T_{clk}
$$

for some integer $N$. The DLL forces *one* $CK_{ref}$ period to be absorbed by $N$ delay stages.

### Step 3 — Linearized VCDL model

$$
t_d(V_c) \;=\; t_{d0} - K_{VCDL} \, V_c
$$

where $K_{VCDL}$ is the VCDL gain (ps/V). Substituting:

$$
t_{d0} - K_{VCDL} V_{c,lock} \;=\; N T_{clk}
$$

### Step 4 — Acquisition range

$$
T_{acq} \;=\; t_{d,max} - t_{d,min} \;=\; K_{VCDL} (V_{c,max} - V_{c,min})
$$

Outside this range, the DLL cannot lock; a **false-lock** occurs at $N \pm 1$.

### Step 5 — Phase margin and loop bandwidth

The closed-loop bandwidth is:

$$
f_{BW} \;=\; \frac{I_{cp} \, K_{VCDL}}{2 \pi \, C_{loop}}
$$

where $I_{cp}$ is the charge-pump current and $C_{loop}$ is the loop-filter capacitance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A DLL has a **finite** acquisition range equal to one VCDL delay, unlike a PLL which can lock to any multiple. This is a frequently asked conceptual question.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Delay-Adjustment Method

| Application | Required Resolution | Required Range | Recommended Topology | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Clock distribution deskew | 5–20 ps | 100–500 ps | DLL + buffer chain | Closed-loop, process-tracking |
| DDR5 DQS-to-CK alignment | 5–10 ps | full clock period | Phase Interpolator in PLL | Wide range, sub-gate resolution |
| TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter) | < 10 ps | ns-scale | Vernier Delay Line | Sub-gate, linear in N |
| Source-sync data strobe | 1 UI | 1 UI | PI + fine Vernier | One-bit-period deskew |
| Reset-tree de-skew | 50–200 ps | per-branch | Tapered buffer chain | Static, low power |
| Reset deassertion alignment | ns-scale | ns-scale | Coarse + fine delay line | Two-step coarse-then-fine |
| ADC aperture correction | sub-ps | tens of ps | Analog PI in SiGe/BiCMOS | Continuous tuning |
| On-chip test scan skew | 50–200 ps | 1–10 ns | Shunt-cap DAC | Simple, low area |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Closed-Loop Delay Adjustment (DLL Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Clock CKref] --> B[Phase Detector PD]
    C[VCDL Output CKdelayed] --> B
    B --> D[Charge Pump CP]
    D --> E[Loop Filter Cloop]
    E --> F[Control Voltage Vc]
    F --> G[Voltage Controlled Delay Line VCDL]
    A --> G
    G --> C
    H[Digital Code] -. Optional Override .-> G
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    style E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style G fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style H fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a6b,color:#000
```

**Read this diagram as a feedback control loop:**

1. The **Phase Detector** compares the reference clock edge with the VCDL output.
2. The **Charge Pump** converts the phase error into a current.
3. The **Loop Filter** integrates this current onto $C_{loop}$, producing the analog control voltage $V_c$.
4. $V_c$ tunes the **VCDL** delay so that the output is brought into lock with the input.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Aid:** Arrows represent signal flow; the loop is *closed* (output feeds back to the PD), making this a **closed-loop** delay adjustment — a key distinction from *open-loop* shunt-cap or buffer chains.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Vernier Delay Line Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Start Pulse] --> B[Coarse Line Stage 1]
    B --> C[Coarse Line Stage 2]
    C --> D[Coarse Line Stage N]
    A --> E[Fine Line Stage 1]
    E --> F[Fine Line Stage 2]
    F --> G[Fine Line Stage N]
    D --> H[Time Difference Output]
    G --> H
    I[Coarse Control Word CW] -.-> B
    I -.-> C
    J[Fine Control Word FW] -.-> E
    J -.-> F
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style B fill:#fad7ac,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#000
    style C fill:#fad7ac,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#000
    style D fill:#fad7ac,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#000
    style E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    style F fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    style G fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    style H fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    style I fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a6b,color:#000
    style J fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a6b,color:#000
```

**Operational sequence:**

1. A *start pulse* enters **both** delay lines simultaneously.
2. The *coarse* line (slower, $t_1$) and the *fine* line (faster, $t_2$) propagate in parallel.
3. The time difference between the two arrivals at the end equals $N (t_1 - t_2)$.
4. A digital control word selects *how many stages* each line actually traverses, producing a programmable total delay.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Phase Interpolator (PI) Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Clock Phase Phi1] --> M1[Current Weighting I1]
    B[Clock Phase Phi2] --> M2[Current Weighting I2]
    M1 --> S[Summing Node]
    M2 --> S
    S --> O[Interpolated Output Phase Phiout]
    D[DAC Code] -.-> M1
    D -.-> M2
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style M1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style S fill:#fad7ac,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#000
    style O fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    style D fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a6b,color:#000
```

**Read this as a weighted current summer:** The DAC-generated currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ steer the relative contribution of two quadrature (or adjacent-phase) clocks. The output is a *continuous* phase between $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ — ideal for sub-gate-delay resolution in serial-link CDRs.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Coarse-Fine Delay Adjustment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Edge] --> COARSE[Coarse Delay 1 UI steps]
    COARSE --> FINE[Fine Delay sub UI steps]
    FINE --> OUT[Aligned Output Edge]
    CTRLC[Coarse Code] -.-> COARSE
    CTRLF[Fine Code] -.-> FINE
    FB[Feedback from Destination] -.-> CTRLC
    FB -.-> CTRLF
    style IN fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style COARSE fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    style FINE fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style CTRLC fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a6b,color:#000
    style CTRLF fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a6b,color:#000
    style FB fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
```

**Why this matters for KTU:** Real delay adjustment is **never** a single stage. A coarse block handles a full unit interval (UI), and a fine block refines within the UI. The feedback path ensures lock is maintained against PVT variations.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Schematic — Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Delay Adjustment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPEN[Open Loop Adjustment]
        O1[Digital Code] --> O2[Shunt Cap Array or CSI]
        O2 --> O3[Output Delay]
    end
    subgraph CLOSED[Closed Loop Adjustment]
        C1[Reference] --> C2[Phase Detector]
        C3[VCDL Output] --> C2
        C2 --> C4[Charge Pump]
        C4 --> C5[Loop Filter]
        C5 --> C6[VCDL]
        C1 --> C6
        C6 --> C3
    end
    style O1 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a6b,color:#000
    style O2 fill:#fad7ac,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#000
    style O3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style C4 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    style C5 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    style C6 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-loop** blocks (left) are *fast, simple, and area-efficient* but suffer from PVT drift. **Closed-loop** blocks (right) *track* PVT variations at the cost of complexity and a feedback stability constraint. The choice is application-driven and is a recurring KTU design question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define delay adjustment and list any four techniques used to achieve it in high-speed digital circuits. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Delay adjustment is the deliberate control of propagation delay along a signal path to achieve a desired timing relationship between multiple signals, typically clock and data, or between parallel signal paths.

Four common techniques are:

1. **Inverter/buffer chain** — fixed coarse delay, scalable in steps of $t_{p,inv}$.
2. **Current-starved inverter (CSI)** — analog-controlled delay via $I_{bias}$.
3. **Shunt-capacitor array** — digitally controlled $C_{add}$ modulates RC delay.
4. **Phase interpolator (PI)** — weighted current summer producing sub-UI phase shifts.
5. *Vernier delay line* / *DLL / PLL* — any one of these may be added as a fifth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation cue (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark; Four techniques with one-line descriptions = 0.5 mark each = 2 marks. Total 3.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Elmore's delay expression for a uniform N-stage RC ladder and explain why long on-chip wires need buffering. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

For a uniform RC ladder with N identical stages of resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$:

$$
t_{D,Elmore} \;=\; R \, C \, \frac{N(N+1)}{2} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{2} R C N^2 \quad \text{(for large N)}
$$

**Why buffering is needed:** Delay grows **quadratically** with the number of stages (or wire length). Inserting buffers (inverters) at intermediate points *isolates* downstream capacitance from upstream resistance, breaking the quadratic growth. With optimal buffering (fan-out $= e$), the delay scales **logarithmically** with the total fan-out, achieving nearly constant per-mm delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation cue:** Formula = 1.5 marks; Quadratic vs logarithmic scaling explanation = 1.5 marks.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> *KTU ESE pattern: Attempt any ONE full question. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Delay-Locked Loop (DLL) used for clock deskew. Derive the steady-state lock condition and explain the concept of false locking. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Reproduce the Mermaid diagram in §4.1, or hand-draw VCDL → PD → CP → LF → back to VCDL.)

**Operation:**

A DLL aligns a *delayed* version of a reference clock to the *next cycle* of the same reference. The Phase Detector (PD) generates an UP or DOWN pulse whose width is proportional to the time difference between the VCDL output and the reference edge. The Charge Pump (CP) sources or sinks current into the Loop Filter, which integrates it onto $C_{loop}$, producing a control voltage $V_c$. $V_c$ tunes the per-stage delay of the VCDL. The loop is stable when $V_c$ settles such that the VCDL delay equals exactly one (or more) reference period.

**Lock condition:**

At lock, no net current flows in the loop filter:

$$
t_{d,VCDL}(V_{c,lock}) \;=\; N \cdot T_{clk}, \quad N \in \mathbb{Z}^+
$$

The VCDL delay is linearly related to its control voltage:

$$
t_d(V_c) \;=\; t_{d0} - K_{VCDL} V_c
$$

Equating:

$$
t_{d0} - K_{VCDL} V_{c,lock} \;=\; N T_{clk}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad V_{c,lock} \;=\; \frac{t_{d0} - N T_{clk}}{K_{VCDL}}
$$

**Acquisition range:**

$$
T_{acq} \;=\; t_{d,max} - t_{d,min} \;=\; K_{VCDL} (V_{c,max} - V_{c,min})
$$

**False lock:** If the VCDL's *minimum* delay $t_{d,min} > T_{clk}$, the DLL cannot lock to $N=1$ and may instead lock at $N=2$ or higher — but with the *wrong phase relationship*, because the VCDL output edge may not coincide with the next reference edge. This is called **harmonic locking** or **false lock**. Prevention requires that:

$$
t_{d,min} < T_{clk} \quad \text{and} \quad t_{d,max} > T_{clk}
$$

and a frequency-detect / lock-acquisition circuit to bring $V_c$ into the correct range at startup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation breakdown (7 marks):** Block diagram (1) + Operation (2) + Lock condition derivation (2) + False lock explanation (2).

---

**(b) Explain the working of a Vernier delay line. A Vernier TDC uses two delay lines with per-stage delays of 80 ps and 65 ps. Determine (i) the resolution, (ii) the total measurable time interval if 32 stages are used, and (iii) the number of stages required to cover a 2 ns range with the same resolution. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of Vernier Delay Line:**

A Vernier delay line uses *two* matched delay lines whose per-stage delays are *deliberately* offset by a small amount. The slower line has per-stage delay $t_1$, the faster line $t_2$, with $t_1 > t_2$. A "start" pulse is launched into the slower line, and a "stop" pulse into the faster line. The pulses propagate, and the faster pulse *catches up* to the slower pulse by an amount $\Delta t = t_1 - t_2$ per stage traversed. The number of stages needed for the catch-up to equal the input time interval is the TDC output code.

**Resolution:** $\Delta t = t_1 - t_2 = 80 - 65 = \mathbf{15 \, ps}$

**Total measurable interval for 32 stages:**

$$
T_{max} \;=\; N \cdot \Delta t \;=\; 32 \times 15 \;=\; \mathbf{480 \, ps}
$$

**Stages required for 2 ns range:**

$$
N \;=\; \frac{T_{target}}{\Delta t} \;=\; \frac{2000 \, \text{ps}}{15 \, \text{ps}} \;\approx\; 134 \, \text{stages}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation breakdown (7 marks):** Working explanation (2) + Part (i) (1.5) + Part (ii) (1.5) + Part (iii) (2).

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) Derive the logical-effort-based expression for the optimum number of buffer stages for driving a large load capacitance $C_L$ from a minimum-sized inverter. Comment on the minimum total delay. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup:** A chain of $N$ identical inverters drives a load $C_L$ from a source $C_{in}$. Each inverter has intrinsic delay $t_{p0}$ and self-load $C_{out}$. The fan-out (electrical effort) of each stage is $f = C_{out,segment}/C_{in}$, with total path effort $F = f^N = C_L/C_{in}$.

**Step 1 — Per-stage delay:**

$$
t_{p,stage} \;=\; t_{p0} (1 + f)
$$

**Step 2 — Total path delay:**

$$
t_p \;=\; N \cdot t_{p0}(1 + f) \;=\; t_{p0} \, N \left( 1 + F^{1/N} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $N$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial t_p}{\partial N} \;=\; t_{p0} \left[ 1 + F^{1/N} - \frac{F^{1/N} \ln F}{N} \right] \;=\; 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
F^{1/N} \left( \frac{\ln F}{N} - 1 \right) \;=\; 1
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the optimum condition $f = e$:**

It can be shown that the minimum delay occurs at:

$$
f_{opt} \;=\; e \;\approx\; 2.718
$$

Hence:

$$
N_{opt} \;=\; \frac{\ln(C_L/C_{in})}{\ln(e)} \;=\; \ln \left( \frac{C_L}{C_{in}} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum total delay:**

$$
t_{p,min} \;=\; t_{p0} \cdot N_{opt} \cdot (1 + e) \;\approx\; 3.72 \, t_{p0} \cdot \ln \left( \frac{C_L}{C_{in}} \right)
$$

**Comment:** The minimum delay grows **logarithmically** with the load ratio, not quadratically. This is a dramatic improvement over an *unbuffered* wire (cf. §3.1, which is $O(N^2)$). The "fan-out-of-4" approximation ($f=4$ instead of $e$) is widely used in practice and adds only ~5 % delay penalty with simpler stage counts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation breakdown (7 marks):** Setup (1) + Total delay expression (1.5) + Differentiation (1.5) + $N_{opt}$ and $t_{p,min}$ (1.5) + Comment (1.5).

---

**(b) With the aid of a current-starved inverter (CSI) circuit diagram, explain how analog voltage control of delay is achieved. A CSI has a load capacitance of 50 fF, switching threshold of 0.5 V, and a bias current of 20 µA. Compute the propagation delay. If the bias current is reduced to 5 µA, what is the new delay and the delay tuning range? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**CSI Circuit Diagram (textual):**

```
       VDD
        |
        |  +---- PMOS M1 (current source) ----+
        |  |                                    |
        |  +---- PMOS M2 (switching) ----+     |
        |       |                         |     |
   Vin --+--|--- +-- Node_out --- C_L ---+-- GND
           |  |                         |
           |  +---- NMOS M3 (switching) +
           |  |
           |  +---- NMOS M4 (current source) --+
                                                |
                                               V_bias
```

- M1, M4 are **current sources** biased by $V_{bias}$ (or a DAC-generated voltage). They set the maximum current that can charge/discharge the output node.
- M2, M3 are the **switching pair** driven by $V_{in}$.
- The output capacitor $C_L$ is charged/discharged through the *current sources*, hence the name "current-starved."

**Delay expression:** During a transition, $C_L$ is charged (or discharged) at constant current $I_{bias}$ from the rail to the switching threshold $V_{sw}$:

$$
t_{d,CSI} \;=\; \frac{C_L \, V_{sw}}{I_{bias}}
$$

**Numerical — Case 1 ($I_{bias} = 20 \, \mu A$):**

$$
t_{d1} \;=\; \frac{50 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.5}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; \frac{25 \times 10^{-15}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.25 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{s}
$$

$$
t_{d1} \;=\; \mathbf{1.25 \, ns}
$$

**Numerical — Case 2 ($I_{bias} = 5 \, \mu A$):**

$$
t_{d2} \;=\; \frac{50 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.5}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 5.0 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{s} \;=\; \mathbf{5.0 \, ns}
$$

**Tuning range:**

$$
T_{tune} \;=\; t_{d2} - t_{d1} \;=\; 5.0 - 1.25 \;=\; \mathbf{3.75 \, ns}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation breakdown (7 marks):** Circuit description (2) + Delay formula (1) + Case 1 (1.5) + Case 2 (1.5) + Tuning range (1).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Scheme Mistakes — Delay Adjustments**
> 1. **Confusing DLL with PLL.** A DLL locks to *one* period of the reference (no integration in the VCDL); a PLL can lock to a *multiplied* frequency. Marks lost when "DLL can multiply frequency" is asserted.
> 2. **Quoting logical effort without the $+1$ term.** The full delay is $t_{p0} N(1 + f)$; many students drop the "1" and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Treating Vernier line resolution as one *stage* delay.** The resolution is the *difference* $t_1 - t_2$, not $t_1$ or $t_2$ themselves. This is a 3-mark trap in numerical problems.
> 4. **Mixing up "skew" and "jitter."** Skew is *spatial* (path-to-path DC difference); jitter is *temporal* (cycle-to-cycle random variation). KTU questions explicitly test this distinction.
> 5. **Forgetting units in current-starved inverter delay.** $C_L V_{sw}/I_{bias}$ yields seconds; students must convert to ps/ns.
> 6. **Assuming phase interpolators are linear by default.** The current-DAC nonlinearity (mismatch) limits the achievable resolution; mention this for full marks.
> 7. **Ignoring PVT in open-loop delay.** Open-loop blocks (shunt cap, CSI) drift with PVT; this is *the* motivation for closed-loop DLLs.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Delay adjustment** is the controlled modulation of propagation delay for *timing alignment* — not data integrity.
- **Elmore delay** of a uniform N-stage RC ladder: $t_D = R C \cdot N(N+1)/2$ — *quadratic* growth motivates buffering.
- **Logical-effoptimum**: each stage should fan-out by $e$ (≈ 2.718); $N_{opt} = \ln(C_L/C_{in})$; minimum delay $\approx 3.7 \, t_{p0} \, \ln(C_L/C_{in})$.
- **Current-starved inverter**: $t_{d,CSI} = C_L V_{sw}/I_{bias}$ — *continuous*, PVT-sensitive analog control.
- **Shunt-capacitor delay**: $t_{d,shunt} = 0.69 R_{eq}(C_{int} + nC_{add})$ — *digital*, monotonic control.
- **Vernier line resolution** = $t_1 - t_2$, *not* either $t_1$ or $t_2$; total range = $N(t_1 - t_2)$.
- **Phase interpolator**: $\phi_{out} = (I_1 \phi_1 + I_2 \phi_2)/(I_1 + I_2)$ — sub-UI continuous phase shifts.
- **DLL steady-state**: $t_{d,VCDL}(V_c) = N T_{clk}$; acquisition range is *one* VCDL delay; false lock is harmonic locking at wrong $N$.
- **PLL vs DLL**: PLL multiplies frequency and integrates phase (VCO); DLL does not multiply and uses VCDL.
- **Open-loop** (CSI, shunt cap, buffer chain) = fast + simple + PVT-sensitive. **Closed-loop** (DLL, PLL + PI) = slower + complex + PVT-tracking.
- **Skew** = spatial, *DC* path mismatch. **Jitter** = temporal, *random* edge variation.
- **LC transmission-line delay**: $t_d = l\sqrt{LC} \approx 6{-}7$ ps/mm in standard BEOL.
- **Coarse-fine architecture** is the standard for high-resolution, wide-range delay adjustment.
- **Real-world applications**: DDR5 DQS alignment, PCIe receiver deskew, TDC, time-interleaved ADC aperture correction, source-synchronous strobe alignment.
- **KTU 2024 hot topics**: Vernier TDC numerical problems; DLL false-lock analysis; logical-effort optimization; CSI delay formula with numerical substitution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Differential Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Distribution** is the high-speed signal-routing methodology in which information is transmitted as the **voltage difference** between two tightly-coupled transmission lines (a *differential pair*) rather than as a single-ended voltage measured against a fixed reference. The two traces, conventionally designated **P (True)** and **N (Complement)**, carry equal-magnitude, opposite-polarity waveforms, while the receiver detects only the *difference* $V_{DIFF} = V_P - V_N$.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the term encompasses the **physical design**, **modal analysis** (odd/even modes), **impedance control**, **return-path management**, **skew budgeting**, **termination topology**, and **mixed-mode S-parameter characterization** of the coupled pair.

## 1.2 Physical Cross-Section of an Edge-Coupled Differential Pair

The canonical implementation on a multilayer PCB consists of two parallel stripline or microstrip traces of equal width $w$, separated laterally by a spacing $s$, running above a continuous reference plane at a height $h$, with a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$.

$$ Z_{DIFF} = 2 \, Z_{ODD} \quad ; \quad Z_{CM} = \frac{Z_{EVEN}}{2} $$

where **$Z_{ODD}$** is the **odd-mode characteristic impedance** (each trace referenced to the *other* trace, opposing current flow) and **$Z_{EVEN}$** is the **even-mode characteristic impedance** (each trace referenced to *ground*, in-phase current flow).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Tug-of-War Rope

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine two equally matched teams pulling on opposite ends of a single rope, while a third observer stands on a *floating platform* (the reference plane). The outcome (who wins) is determined **only by the difference in pull**, not by the absolute strength of each team or the position of the platform. A sudden gust of wind that moves the platform affects *both teams equally* and is therefore **invisible to the observer**. Differential distribution works identically: the receiver ignores any disturbance that hits both lines equally (common-mode noise) and reacts only to the difference (the signal).

**Real-world standard metrics (must be memorized):**
- **USB 2.0/3.0:** $Z_{DIFF} = 90 \, \Omega \pm 10\%$
- **HDMI / DisplayPort:** $Z_{DIFF} = 100 \, \Omega \pm 10\%$
- **SATA / PCIe Gen 3:** $Z_{DIFF} = 85 \, \Omega \pm 10\%$
- **Ethernet 1000BASE-T:** $Z_{DIFF} = 100 \, \Omega \pm 5\%$
- **Rise-time budget skew:** $\Delta t_{skew} \le 0.1 \times t_r$

## 1.4 Why Use Differential Distribution? — The Five Pillars

| Pillar | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Common-Mode Rejection** (CMR) | Noise coupled equally to both traces is rejected by the receiver's differential amplifier |
| **Reduced EMI Radiation** | Opposing currents produce a $1/r^2$ far-field falloff (quadratic), versus $1/r$ for single-ended |
| **Higher Signal Integrity** | Logic swing is doubled ($2 \times V_{swing}$) for the same supply rail |
| **Reference-Plane Independence** | Receiver no longer needs a quiet, low-inductance ground to interpret logic levels |
| **Return-Current Self-Containment** | The two return currents cancel *within the pair*, minimizing ground-bounce and SSN |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Coupled-Line Physical Model

A differential pair is mathematically modeled as a **two-port coupled transmission-line system** with per-unit-length matrices:

$$ \mathbf{Z} = j\omega \begin{bmatrix} L & L_m \\ L_m & L \end{bmatrix} \quad , \quad \mathbf{Y} = j\omega \begin{bmatrix} C & -C_m \\ -C_m & C \end{bmatrix} $$

where $L$ is the self-inductance per unit length, $L_m$ the mutual inductance, $C$ the total self-capacitance (to ground + to the partner trace), and $C_m$ the mutual capacitance to the partner trace.

> [!NOTE]
> The **sign convention** is critical: $C_m$ is *negative* in the admittance matrix because current leaving node 1 via the mutual capacitor enters node 2 — a *180°* phase inversion relative to the self-capacitance. The same sign logic gives $L_m > 0$ for the magnetic coupling.

## 2.2 Modal Decomposition — Odd and Even Modes

The symmetric matrix pair $\mathbf{Z}$, $\mathbf{Y}$ is diagonalized by two orthogonal eigenvectors, yielding the **natural modes of propagation**:

| Mode | Eigenvector Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Odd Mode (Differential)** | $V_P = -V_N, \, I_P = -I_N$ | Currents flow *oppositely*; the inter-trace gap acts as a virtual return path |
| **Even Mode (Common)** | $V_P = V_N, \, I_P = I_N$ | Currents flow *in phase*; both traces return current through the reference plane |

## 2.3 Deriving the Modal Characteristic Impedances

Substituting the modal conditions into the telegrapher equations yields:

$$ \boxed{\, Z_{ODD} = \sqrt{\frac{L - L_m}{C + C_m}} \,} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{\, Z_{EVEN} = \sqrt{\frac{L + L_m}{C - C_m}} \,} $$

The corresponding phase velocities are generally *not equal* in inhomogeneous media (e.g., microstrip), which causes the differential signal to progressively develop a common-mode component — a phenomenon quantified by the **mode-conversion parameter** $S_{CD21}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU ESE Module 4 and are routinely tested in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_{DIFF} = 2 \, Z_{ODD}$ | Differential impedance seen between P and N | Termination, receiver design |
| 2 | $Z_{CM} = Z_{EVEN}/2$ | Common-mode impedance to ground | Common-mode choke design |
| 3 | $k = \dfrac{Z_{EVEN} - Z_{ODD}}{Z_{EVEN} + Z_{ODD}}$ | Coupling coefficient, $-1 < k < 1$ | Quantifies electromagnetic coupling |
| 4 | $Z_{0,iso} = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Single-line impedance *without* coupling | Reference value for design |
| 5 | $Z_{ODD} = \dfrac{Z_{0,iso}}{\sqrt{1 + k}}$ | Odd-mode impedance in homogeneous medium (stripline) | PCB stackup design |
| 6 | $Z_{EVEN} = \dfrac{Z_{0,iso}}{\sqrt{1 - k}}$ | Even-mode impedance in homogeneous medium | PCB stackup design |
| 7 | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}$ | Phase velocity on the trace | Skew budget, time-of-flight |
| 8 | $\Delta t_{skew} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{v_p}$ | Intra-pair skew | Length-matching requirement |
| 9 | $L_{match} \le 0.1 \, t_r \, v_p$ | Maximum length mismatch allowed | Design rule |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dV_{DIFF}}{dt} = \dfrac{dV_P}{dt} - \dfrac{dV_N}{dt}$ | Differential signal derivative | Receiver threshold detection |
| 11 | $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \!\left( \dfrac{A_{DM}}{A_{CM}} \right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | Receiver selection |
| 12 | $S_{CD21} \; (\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \!\left( \dfrac{V_{CM,out}}{V_{DM,in}} \right)$ | Differential-to-common mode conversion | Channel compliance |

**Boundary conditions / operating limits:**
- $0 \le k \le 1$ (always non-negative for passive, lossless lines)
- $Z_{EVEN} > Z_{0,iso} > Z_{ODD}$ (always true for edge-coupled symmetric pair)
- $Z_{DIFF} \approx 2 Z_{0,iso}$ for **loose coupling** ($s/w > 2$); $Z_{DIFF} < 2 Z_{0,iso}$ for **tight coupling**
- $\varepsilon_{r,eff,ODD} \ne \varepsilon_{r,eff,EVEN}$ in **microstrip** → mode velocity mismatch (a primary source of common-mode conversion)
- $\varepsilon_{r,eff,ODD} = \varepsilon_{r,eff,EVEN}$ in **stripline** → mode velocity matched (no skew accumulation)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Differential distribution is the *de-facto* physical-layer choice for every modern multi-gigabit serial standard: **PCIe, USB 3.x, SATA, HDMI, 1000BASE-T/10GBASE-T Ethernet, Serial RapidIO, JESD204B/C (ADC links), MIPI D-PHY/C-PHY, and InfiniBand**. The ITRS roadmap identifies *signal integrity of differential channels* as a top-five design challenge below the 28 nm node because return-loss, mode-conversion, and crosstalk budgets tighten by ~3 dB per process generation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Modal Impedances from the Coupled Telegrapher Equation

We begin from the 2-line per-unit-length model and derive the odd-mode and even-mode characteristic impedances in full.

**Step 1 — Write the generalized telegrapher's equations for two coupled lines.**

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} $$

For a **symmetric, lossless, reciprocal** pair: $Z_{11} = Z_{22} = j\omega L$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = j\omega L_m$, $Y_{11} = Y_{22} = j\omega C$, $Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -j\omega C_m$.

**Step 2 — Apply the odd-mode eigenvector** $V_2 = -V_1$, $I_2 = -I_1$. This reduces the four-equation system to a single-line equation:

$$ \frac{\partial V_1}{\partial x} = -j\omega(L - L_m)\,I_1 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial I_1}{\partial x} = -j\omega(C + C_m)\,V_1 $$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the first equation with respect to $x$ and substitute the second:**

$$ \frac{\partial^{2} V_1}{\partial x^{2}} = -j\omega(L - L_m)\frac{\partial I_1}{\partial x} = -\omega^{2}(L - L_m)(C + C_m)\,V_1 $$

**Step 4 — Recognize the standard wave-equation form** $\partial^{2}V/\partial x^{2} = \gamma^{2} V$ with:

$$ \gamma_{ODD}^{2} = -\omega^{2}(L - L_m)(C + C_m) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \gamma_{ODD} = j\omega\sqrt{(L - L_m)(C + C_m)} $$

**Step 5 — Divide the $V$–$I$ relation to obtain the characteristic impedance:**

$$ Z_{ODD} = \frac{V_1}{I_1}\bigg|_{x\,=0} = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega(L - L_m)}{j\omega(C + C_m)}} $$

$$ \boxed{\, Z_{ODD} = \sqrt{\frac{L - L_m}{C + C_m}} \,} \quad \text{(Result A-1)} $$

**Step 6 — Repeat Steps 2–5 for the even-mode eigenvector** $V_2 = V_1$, $I_2 = I_1$:

$$ \boxed{\, Z_{EVEN} = \sqrt{\frac{L + L_m}{C - C_m}} \,} \quad \text{(Result A-2)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The **sign flip in the capacitance term** ($C + C_m$ vs. $C - C_m$) is the formal origin of the asymmetry $Z_{EVEN} \ne 2 Z_{ODD}$. It comes directly from the fact that odd-mode currents *add* to charge the inter-trace gap, whereas even-mode currents *subtract* in that gap.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Coupling Coefficient and Its Range

We prove that the coupling coefficient $k = (Z_{EVEN} - Z_{ODD})/(Z_{EVEN} + Z_{ODD})$ lies strictly in $[0, 1]$ for physical, lossless lines.

**Step 1 — Express $Z_{EVEN}$ and $Z_{ODD}$ in terms of $L, L_m, C, C_m$:**

$$ Z_{EVEN} = \sqrt{\frac{L+L_m}{C-C_m}} \quad ; \quad Z_{ODD} = \sqrt{\frac{L-L_m}{C+C_m}} $$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio $Z_{EVEN} / Z_{ODD}$:**

$$ \frac{Z_{EVEN}}{Z_{ODD}} = \sqrt{\frac{(L+L_m)(C+C_m)}{(L-L_m)(C-C_m)}} $$

**Step 3 — Define $k_m = L_m/L$ and $k_c = C_m/C$. Substitute and simplify the radical numerator and denominator:**

$$ \frac{Z_{EVEN}}{Z_{ODD}} = \sqrt{\frac{(1 + k_m)(1 + k_c)}{(1 - k_m)(1 - k_c)}} $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $k_m$ and $k_c$ separately using the classical TEM relationship** $L_m/L = C_m/C \equiv k$ (valid in any homogeneous medium; approximately true in microstrip). Substituting $k_m = k_c = k$:

$$ \frac{Z_{EVEN}}{Z_{ODD}} = \frac{1 + k}{1 - k} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k = \frac{Z_{EVEN}/Z_{ODD} - 1}{Z_{EVEN}/Z_{ODD} + 1} = \frac{Z_{EVEN} - Z_{ODD}}{Z_{EVEN} + Z_{ODD}} $$

$$ \boxed{\, k = \frac{Z_{EVEN} - Z_{ODD}}{Z_{EVEN} + Z_{ODD}} \in [0, 1] \,} \quad \text{(Result B-1)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence conditions:** $0 \le L_m \le L$ and $0 \le C_m \le C$. Physically, mutual inductance never exceeds self-inductance (no negative energy storage) and the inter-trace capacitance cannot exceed the per-line capacitance to ground and partner combined. These inequalities force $k \in [0, 1]$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Skew Budget for a 5 Gbps Differential Link

**Given:** $t_r = 50 \, \text{ps}$ (20–80% rise time), $v_p = 1.5 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$ (typical for FR-4, $\varepsilon_{r,eff} \approx 4$), link length $L_{total} = 200 \, \text{mm}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the unit interval (UI):**

$$ UI = \frac{1}{f_{baud}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{9}} = 200 \, \text{ps} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the empirical rule of thumb** $\Delta t_{skew} \le 0.1 \, t_r$:

$$ \Delta t_{skew}^{max} = 0.1 \times 50 \, \text{ps} = 5 \, \text{ps} $$

**Step 3 — Convert time-skew budget to length-mismatch budget:**

$$ \Delta L_{max} = \Delta t_{skew}^{max} \times v_p = 5 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.5 \times 10^{8} = 0.75 \, \text{mm} $$

**Step 4 — Express as a fraction of $t_r$ propagated distance:**

$$ d_{rise} = v_p \times t_r = 1.5 \times 10^{8} \times 50 \times 10^{-12} = 7.5 \, \text{mm} $$

$$ \frac{\Delta L_{max}}{d_{rise}} = \frac{0.75}{7.5} = 0.10 \;(10\%) $$

**Step 5 — Length-matching specification for PCB layout:**

$$ \boxed{\, \Delta L \le 0.75 \, \text{mm} \;\; \text{(about 30 mil)} \;\; \text{for this 5 Gbps link} \,} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** At 10 Gbps the same link demands $\Delta L_{max} \le 0.375 \, \text{mm}$ (15 mil). Industry practice tightens to 5 mil at 10 Gbps and to 3 mil at 25 Gbps (OIF CEI-25G-LR). This is why **automatic length-tuning** (serpentine routing) is mandatory in modern PCB CAD tools.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Loose vs Tight Coupling

| Design Parameter | Loose Coupling ($s/w \ge 2$) | Tight Coupling ($s/w \le 1$) | Preferred For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Impedance $Z_{DIFF}$ | $\approx 2 Z_{0,iso}$ | $< 2 Z_{0,iso}$ | Depends on standard |
| Field containment | Poor — fields reach far | Excellent — fields confined | EMI-sensitive designs |
| Mode-velocity matching | Less matched | Better matched | High-data-rate serial |
| Crosstalk immunity | Weaker | Stronger | Dense routing |
| Manufacturing tolerance | Easier (large $s$) | Tighter (small $s$) | Cost-sensitive PCB fab |
| Connector compatibility | Good (pin pitch $\ge$ 0.8 mm) | Poor (needs BGA/via-array) | Backplane vs chip-to-chip |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Coupling Coefficient & Differential Impedance

**Given:** Edge-coupled microstrip pair on FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.3$), $w = 0.2 \, \text{mm}$, $s = 0.15 \, \text{mm}$, $h = 0.2 \, \text{mm}$, $t = 35 \, \mu m$. Field-solver reports $Z_{ODD} = 48.2 \, \Omega$ and $Z_{EVEN} = 65.3 \, \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Coupling coefficient:**

$$ k = \frac{65.3 - 48.2}{65.3 + 48.2} = \frac{17.1}{113.5} = 0.1506 $$

**Step 2 — Differential impedance:**

$$ Z_{DIFF} = 2 \times 48.2 = 96.4 \, \Omega \;\;(\text{within 100 }\Omega \text{ USB spec}) $$

**Step 3 — Common-mode impedance:**

$$ Z_{CM} = \frac{65.3}{2} = 32.65 \, \Omega $$

**Step 4 — Single-line isolated impedance (back-calculation):**

$$ Z_{0,iso} = \sqrt{Z_{ODD} \cdot Z_{EVEN}} = \sqrt{48.2 \times 65.3} = \sqrt{3147.46} = 56.10 \, \Omega $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Because $Z_{0,iso} = 56.1 \, \Omega < 60 \, \Omega$, the trace is electrically wide for its height — consistent with the chosen $w/h = 1$ geometry. Tightening the spacing $s$ from 0.15 mm to 0.10 mm would increase $k$ to roughly 0.22 and drop $Z_{DIFF}$ to ~89 $\Omega$, requiring a width reduction to $w = 0.17 \, \text{mm}$ to compensate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams below are **block-level functional architecture** representations of the physical structures discussed. They show signal-flow and connectivity rather than physical artwork (which is not renderable in Mermaid).

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Differential Distribution Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[TRANSMITTER END]
        TXC[DIGITAL LOGIC\nCONTROL]
        TXD[DIFFERENTIAL\nDRIVER\nBUFFER]
    end

    subgraph B[PCB CHANNEL]
        PT[TRACE P\nTOPOLOGY]
        NT[TRACE N\nTOPOLOGY]
        RP[REFERENCE\nPLANE]
        CP[INTERPAIR\nCOUPLING\nCAPACITANCE AND INDUCTANCE]
    end

    subgraph C[RECEIVER END]
        TMR[TERMINATION\nNETWORK]
        RXR[DIFFERENTIAL\nRECEIVER\nCOMPARATOR]
    end

    subgraph D[NOISE SOURCES]
        XTK[NEAR END\nCROSSTALK]
        EMI[EMI AND\nREFLECTION]
        VCM[COMMON MODE\nVOLTAGE]
    end

    TXC --> TXD
    TXD -- DIFFERENTIAL OUT P --> PT
    TXD -- DIFFERENTIAL OUT N --> NT
    PT --- CP
    NT --- CP
    RP -. RETURN PATH .-> PT
    RP -. RETURN PATH .-> NT
    PT --> TMR
    NT --> TMR
    TMR --> RXR
    XTK -.-> PT
    XTK -.-> NT
    EMI -.-> PT
    EMI -.-> NT
    VCM -.-> RXR
```

## 4.2 Modal Decomposition State Diagram (Odd / Even)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[DIFFERENTIAL OR\nCOMMON MODE SOURCE] --> DM{WHICH MODE\nEXCITED}
    DM -- P AND N OPPOSITE --> OD[ODD MODE\nVIRTUAL GROUND\nBETWEEN TRACES]
    DM -- P AND N IN PHASE --> EV[EVEN MODE\nBOTH TRACES REFERENCE GROUND]
    OD --> ZO[ZODD PER LINE\nZDIFF = 2 ZODD\nBETWEEN P AND N]
    EV --> ZE[ZEVEN PER LINE\nZCM = ZEVEN OVER 2\nTO GROUND]
    ZO --> OUTA[USEFUL SIGNAL\nPROPAGATES]
    ZE --> OUTB[NOISE AND\nCOMMON MODE]
    OUTA --> RXOK[RECEIVED CLEANLY]
    OUTB --> RXBAD[CONVERTED TO\nDIFFERENTIAL BY\nSKEW AND\nDISCONTINUITY]
    RXBAD --> LOSS[EYE MARGIN LOSS\nAND EMI RADIATION]
```

## 4.3 Termination Topology Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{WHAT IS THE\nDRIVER TYPE}
    Q1 -- CURRENT MODE LVDS --> T1[TERMINATE ACROSS PAIR\n100 OHM TO RECEIVER\nNEAR END]
    Q1 -- VOLTAGE MODE CMOS --> Q2{IS THE LINK\nSHORT LESS THAN\nLAMBDA OVER 10}
    Q2 -- YES --> T2[NO TERMINATION\nREFLECTIONS NEGLIGIBLE]
    Q2 -- NO --> T3[DIFFERENTIAL\nSERIES OR\nPARALLEL\nTERMINATION]
    T1 --> OUTD[FREE TO USE\n100 OHM DIFF\nBETWEEN P AND N]
    T2 --> OUTD
    T3 --> OUTD
    Q1 -- CML OR LVPECL --> T4[PARALLEL 50 OHM\nTO VTERM ON\nEACH LINE TO GROUND]
    T4 --> OUTE[ZDIFF = 100 OHM\nZCM = 25 OHM\nAT TERMINATION]
    OUTD --> DONE[DESIGN COMPLETE]
    OUTE --> DONE
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define differential signaling. List any four advantages it offers over single-ended signaling for high-speed links.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 + 2 split):**
Differential signaling transmits information as the *difference* $V_{DIFF} = V_P - V_N$ between two complementary traces, rather than as a single-ended voltage to ground. Advantages: (i) **common-mode rejection** — noise coupled equally to both lines is rejected by the differential receiver; (ii) **reduced EMI** — opposing currents give a $1/r^{2}$ far-field falloff; (iii) **doubled signal swing** for the same supply rail; (iv) **return-current self-containment** within the pair, minimizing ground bounce; (v) tolerance to **reference-plane discontinuities**; (vi) **half the supply voltage** needed per driver output.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is intra-pair skew? Why is it a critical design constraint in differential distribution?*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 + 2 split):**
Intra-pair skew is the *time difference* between the arrival of the P-edge and the N-edge of a differential signal at the receiver, caused by length mismatch or asymmetric routing. It is critical because (i) skew directly converts a portion of the desired differential signal into **common-mode noise**, reducing the differential amplitude seen by the receiver; (ii) any residual skew appears at the receiver as **jitter**, shrinking the eye diagram; (iii) the generated common-mode component can cause **EMI radiation** and saturate the receiver's common-mode range; (iv) it increases **$S_{CD21}$ mode-conversion loss**, violating channel compliance specs. Rule of thumb: $\Delta t_{skew} \le 0.1 \, t_r$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — 14-Mark Choice Pair

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Coupled-Line Theory, Modal Analysis, and Coupling Coefficient*
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Starting from the per-unit-length impedance and admittance matrices of a symmetric, lossless, two-line coupled system, derive the expressions for the odd-mode and even-mode characteristic impedances $Z_{ODD}$ and $Z_{EVEN}$. State all assumptions clearly.
>
> **(b) [7 marks)** An edge-coupled microstrip differential pair on FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.3$, $h = 0.2 \, \text{mm}$, $t = 35 \, \mu m$) has trace width $w = 0.15 \, \text{mm}$ and edge-to-edge spacing $s = 0.10 \, \text{mm}$. A 2-D field solver reports $Z_{ODD} = 45.8 \, \Omega$ and $Z_{EVEN} = 60.1 \, \Omega$. Compute the coupling coefficient $k$, the differential impedance $Z_{DIFF}$, the common-mode impedance $Z_{CM}$, and the isolated single-line impedance $Z_{0,iso}$. Comment on whether this pair meets the 100 $\Omega$ USB-3.0 specification.

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Coupled-line model [1 mark]:** Define per-unit-length matrices $\mathbf{Z} = j\omega[[L, L_m], [L_m, L]]$ and $\mathbf{Y} = j\omega[[C, -C_m], [-C_m, C]]$ with assumptions: (i) lossless, (ii) symmetric, (iii) reciprocal, (iv) homogeneous dielectric OR quasi-TEM approximation.

2. **Telegrapher equations [1 mark]:** Write the four coupled partial-differential equations for $V_1, V_2, I_1, I_2$ as a function of $x$.

3. **Odd-mode eigenvector [1 mark]:** Apply $V_2 = -V_1$, $I_2 = -I_1$ to collapse the 4-equation system to a single line with effective parameters $L_{eff} = L - L_m$ and $C_{eff} = C + C_m$.

4. **Derive wave equation [1 mark]:** Combine $\partial V_1/\partial x = -j\omega(L-L_m)I_1$ and $\partial I_1/\partial x = -j\omega(C+C_m)V_1$ to obtain $\partial^2 V_1/\partial x^2 = -\omega^2(L-L_m)(C+C_m)V_1$.

5. **Extract $Z_{ODD}$ [1 mark]:** $Z_{ODD} = \sqrt{(L-L_m)/(C+C_m)}$.

6. **Even-mode analogous derivation [1 mark]:** Apply $V_2 = V_1$, $I_2 = I_1$ to obtain $Z_{EVEN} = \sqrt{(L+L_m)/(C-C_m)}$.

7. **Physical interpretation and sign discussion [1 mark]:** Explain why $Z_{EVEN} > Z_{ODD}$ (mutual inductance adds for even, subtracts for odd; mutual capacitance subtracts for even, adds for odd) and why this implies $Z_{DIFF} < 2Z_{0,iso}$ for tight coupling.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Coupling coefficient $k$ [2 marks]:**

$$ k = \frac{Z_{EVEN} - Z_{ODD}}{Z_{EVEN} + Z_{ODD}} = \frac{60.1 - 45.8}{60.1 + 45.8} = \frac{14.3}{105.9} = 0.1350 $$

**Step 2 — Differential impedance $Z_{DIFF}$ [2 marks]:**

$$ Z_{DIFF} = 2 \times Z_{ODD} = 2 \times 45.8 = 91.6 \, \Omega $$

**Step 3 — Common-mode impedance $Z_{CM}$ [1.5 marks]:**

$$ Z_{CM} = \frac{Z_{EVEN}}{2} = \frac{60.1}{2} = 30.05 \, \Omega $$

**Step 4 — Isolated single-line impedance $Z_{0,iso}$ [1.5 marks]:**

$$ Z_{0,iso} = \sqrt{Z_{ODD} \cdot Z_{EVEN}} = \sqrt{45.8 \times 60.1} = \sqrt{2752.58} = 52.46 \, \Omega $$

**Step 5 — Compliance check (USB 3.0 requires $Z_{DIFF} = 90 \, \Omega \pm 10\%$, i.e., $81 \, \Omega$ to $99 \, \Omega$):**

$$ 91.6 \, \Omega \in [81, 99] \, \Omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{PASS} $$

**Engineering commentary:** The computed $Z_{DIFF} = 91.6 \, \Omega$ lies within the USB-3.0 envelope. The coupling coefficient $k = 0.135$ is moderate — typical for edge-coupled microstrip with $s/w = 0.67$. The relatively low $Z_{0,iso} = 52.5 \, \Omega$ indicates the trace is electrically wide; further tightening of $s$ would reduce $Z_{DIFF}$ further into the 90 $\Omega$ spec but would compromise manufacturing yield and crosstalk tolerance.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Skew Budget, Length-Matching, and Differential Channel Compliance*
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Define intra-pair skew. Derive the relationship between physical length mismatch $\Delta L$, dielectric effective permittivity $\varepsilon_{r,eff}$, and time-domain skew $\Delta t_{skew}$ in a differential pair. A 10 Gbps serial link on FR-4 ($\varepsilon_{r,eff} = 3.8$) has a driver with 35 ps 20–80% rise time. Compute the maximum length mismatch that can be tolerated under the rule-of-thumb $\Delta t_{skew} \le 0.1 \, t_r$.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the four most common termination schemes used in differential distribution (source-series, receiver-parallel, AC-coupled receiver-parallel, and differential-T) with the help of a labelled functional description. For each, state the typical application, the IO standard it pairs with, and the value of termination resistor(s) used.

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Skew derivation and calculation (7 marks):**

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** Intra-pair skew is the arrival-time difference at the receiver between the P-edge and the N-edge of a single bit transition. Origin: physical length mismatch $\Delta L$ on the PCB OR dielectric asymmetry.

2. **Phase velocity [1 mark]:** For a TEM/quasi-TEM line, $v_p = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

3. **Skew formula [1 mark]:**

$$ \Delta t_{skew} = \frac{\Delta L}{v_p} = \frac{\Delta L \, \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}{c} $$

4. **Rise time given:** $t_r = 35$ ps, so allowed skew [1 mark]: $\Delta t_{skew}^{max} = 0.1 \times 35 = 3.5$ ps.

5. **Compute $v_p$ [1 mark]:** $v_p = 3 \times 10^{8} / \sqrt{3.8} = 1.539 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

6. **Compute $\Delta L_{max}$ [1 mark]:** $\Delta L_{max} = 3.5 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.539 \times 10^{8} = 0.5386$ mm $\approx$ **21 mil**.

7. **Engineering interpretation [1 mark]:** The PCB layout must constrain intra-pair mismatch to within ~0.5 mm or 21 mil; in practice, designers tighten to 5–10 mil to leave margin for dielectric dispersion, fiber-weave effect, and via-length asymmetry.

**(b) Termination schemes (7 marks):**

| # | Scheme | Topology | Application | Termination Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Source-Series (Series-Damping)** | Resistor $R_s$ in series with each driver output, placed *near* the driver | LVDS, CML, multi-drop buses, ringing suppression | $R_s = 10$–$33 \, \Omega$ |
| 2 | **Receiver-Parallel (DC-Coupled)** | $R_{term} = Z_{DIFF}$ placed between P and N at the receiver | Point-to-point current-mode links, LVDS-PECL interfaces | $R_{term} = 100 \, \Omega$ |
| 3 | **AC-Coupled Receiver-Parallel** | $C_{AC}$ in series with each line, then $R_{term}$ between them at receiver | AC-coupled standards (SATA, USB-SS, PCIe, HDMI) where DC bias shifts must be blocked | $C_{AC} = 0.1 \, \mu F$ (frequency-dependent), $R_{term} = 100 \, \Omega$ |
| 4 | **Differential-T (Pi-/T-Network)** | $R_T$ from each line to $V_{TT}$ bias, then optional series $R_s$ to control impedance | SSTL, HSTL DDR memory interfaces, push-pull voltage drivers | $R_T = 50 \, \Omega$ each to $V_{TT}$ (so pair sees 100 $\Omega$ differential) |

For each scheme the student must state (i) the *purpose* (impedance match, common-mode bias, AC blocking), (ii) the *standard* (LVDS / PCIe / DDR / HDMI), and (iii) the *typical resistance values* — these are the three sub-marks distributed within each row of the 7-mark allocation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss pitfalls in Differential Distribution questions (verified by KTU 2019–2024 valuation audits):**
> 1. **Sign error in $Z_{ODD}$ / $Z_{EVEN}$ derivation** — Forgetting the *sign* of $C_m$ in the $C - C_m$ term of the even-mode denominator. Result: $Z_{EVEN}$ comes out *smaller* than $Z_{ODD}$, which is physically impossible. **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing $Z_{DIFF}$ with $Z_{ODD}$** — Writing $Z_{DIFF} = Z_{ODD}$ instead of $Z_{DIFF} = 2 Z_{ODD}$. The factor of 2 arises because $Z_{ODD}$ is the *per-line* impedance to the virtual return between the traces. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 3. **Unit error in skew calculation** — Using $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and $\Delta L$ in cm without conversion. **Penalty: −0.5 to −1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting the "$\le 0.1 \, t_r$" rule** — Substituting 0.5 or 1.0 as the proportionality constant. The KTU marking scheme specifically awards 1 mark for stating the rule correctly. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 5. **Mixing microstrip and stripline formulas** — Applying stripline (homogeneous) closed-form equations to a microstrip (inhomogeneous) geometry yields 10–25% error. Always quote the appropriate formula with the effective permittivity $\varepsilon_{r,eff}$ specified. **Penalty: −0.5 to −1 mark.**
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer** — Even when the numerical value is correct, the answer is *incomplete* without a unit ($\Omega$, ps, mm). **Penalty: −0.5 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Differential signal** = $V_P - V_N$; **common-mode signal** = $(V_P + V_N)/2$.
- [x] **Two natural modes** of a coupled pair: **Odd** ($V_P = -V_N$) and **Even** ($V_P = V_N$).
- [x] $Z_{DIFF} = 2 Z_{ODD}$ — the fundamental differential-impedance relation.
- [x] $Z_{CM} = Z_{EVEN}/2$ — the fundamental common-mode-impedance relation.
- [x] $Z_{EVEN} > Z_{ODD}$ always; $Z_{0,iso} = \sqrt{Z_{ODD} Z_{EVEN}}$.
- [x] Coupling coefficient $k = (Z_{EVEN} - Z_{ODD})/(Z_{EVEN} + Z_{ODD})$ is in $[0, 1]$.
- [x] **Loose coupling** ($s/w \ge 2$): $Z_{DIFF} \approx 2 Z_{0,iso}$; **tight coupling** ($s/w \le 1$): $Z_{DIFF} < 2 Z_{0,iso}$.
- [x] **Stripline** is homogeneous → odd and even modes have *equal* phase velocity → no skew accumulation.
- [x] **Microstrip** is inhomogeneous → modes have *unequal* phase velocity → mode conversion over distance.
- [x] Intra-pair skew budget: $\Delta t_{skew} \le 0.1 \times t_r$.
- [x] Length-mismatch budget: $\Delta L_{max} = 0.1 \, t_r \, v_p = 0.1 \, t_r \, c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}$.
- [x] Standard target impedances: USB 90 $\Omega$, HDMI/DisplayPort 100 $\Omega$, PCIe/SATA 85 $\Omega$, Ethernet 100 $\Omega$.
- [x] **Differential advantages:** CMR, EMI reduction, doubled swing, return-current containment, plane-noise immunity.
- [x] **Differential disadvantages:** 2× trace count, length matching overhead, more connector pins, AC-coupling capacitor cost.
- [x] **Mode-conversion loss** $S_{CD21}$ quantifies how much differential signal is undesirably converted to common mode by asymmetry.
- [x] **CMRR** quantifies the receiver's ability to reject common-mode voltage; given in dB.
- [x] Termination must match $Z_{DIFF}$ to avoid reflections; placement at the *receiver* (parallel) or *driver* (series) depends on the IO standard.
- [x] **Reference-plane discontinuity** under a differential pair causes a local $Z_{DIFF}$ excursion; place ground stitching vias at $\lambda/20$ spacing.
- [x] **Fiber-weave effect** in FR-4 can introduce $\pm 5$–$10$ ps of skew at 10 Gbps — use spread-glass or low-Dk/low-Df materials.
- [x] **Connector pinout rule:** always assign differential pairs to *adjacent* pins; never split across columns.
- [x] **Via design rule:** use *symmetric* via geometry for P and N — identical antipad, identical barrel length — to avoid via-induced skew.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
