<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Duty Cycle (DC)** of a periodic clock signal is defined as the ratio of the duration for which the signal remains in its **active (HIGH / logic-1)** state during one full period, expressed as a **percentage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duty Cycle (DC)**: A first-order figure-of-merit for any periodic digital waveform that quantifies the *temporal asymmetry* between the HIGH phase and the LOW phase of the signal. In KTU 2024 scheme, duty cycle is a key timing parameter in **source-synchronous interfaces, DDR memory subsystems, clock distribution networks, and PLL/DLL outputs.**

Mathematically, the most fundamental form is:

$$
DC = \left(\frac{t_{H}}{T}\right) \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- **$t_H$** = HIGH-time (also called pulse width, PW or $t_{ON}$) — measured at the **50% voltage threshold crossing**.
- **$T$** = Signal period = $t_H + t_L$ (the sum of HIGH-time and LOW-time).
- **$f$** = Repetition frequency = $1/T$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Traffic Light Analogy:** Imagine a traffic signal at a busy intersection that runs on a fixed 60-second cycle. If the **green light** (representing the clock HIGH phase) stays on for 30 seconds, then the duty cycle is **50%** — the green and red durations are equal. If the green stays on for only 18 seconds, then the duty cycle is **30%** — the intersection spends most of its time in red (LOW). Now imagine a thousand such intersections all along a highway, and they must all "pulse green" at exactly the right fraction of the second to allow synchronized traffic to flow. That is *exactly* what happens in a high-speed digital chip — the clock "green light" must pulse with precisely controlled duty cycle so that every flip-flop, latch, and I/O cell captures data at the correct instant.

A **second analogy** — the **heartbeat of a digital system:**
- A *resting adult heart* has roughly a **50% duty cycle** — the systolic (pumping) phase and diastolic (resting) phase are comparable.
- A *stressed heart under tachycardia* has a high duty cycle — the muscle pumps almost constantly.
- Likewise, a clock signal with a **50% duty cycle** is the "healthy resting heartbeat" preferred by synchronous logic, while a heavily distorted duty cycle is a "cardiac arrhythmia" of the digital domain.

## 1.3 Why Duty Cycle Matters — The KTU High-Yield Perspective

| Context | Importance of Duty Cycle |
|---|---|
| **Synchronous Logic** | 50% DC provides equal setup and hold timing margins. |
| **DDR / DDR2/3/4/5 Memory** | Data is sampled on both edges; DC must be 45%–55% for valid strobe centering. |
| **Source-Synchronous Links** (e.g., SERDES, LVDS) | DC drift causes inter-symbol interference and ISI. |
| **PLL / DLL Output Buffers** | DC distortion is a primary specification for clock generator ICs. |
| **PWM Power Control** | DC intentionally varied from 0% to 100% to control power delivery. |
| **EMI / Spectrum** | DC = 50% eliminates even-order harmonics, reducing radiated emissions. |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Nominal Duty Cycle in high-speed digital design = 50%** (often called a "**symmetric clock**").
> **Acceptable DC tolerance** for most modern high-speed interfaces: **±5%** (i.e., 45%–55%).
> **DC measurement reference level** = **50% of $V_{DD}$** (the midpoint of the logic swing).
> **Critical threshold:** $V_{IH}$ and $V_{IL}$ define when a receiver "sees" the signal as HIGH or LOW — duty cycle is measured between the 50% crossings to remove input-threshold dependence.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Logical Breakdown — Operational Concept

A clock signal is fundamentally a **periodic, two-level (binary) waveform**. Even though it is conceptually simple, *four hidden timing parameters* completely characterize it:

1. **Period ($T$)** — total repetition time.
2. **High time ($t_H$)** — pulse width.
3. **Low time ($t_L$)** — interval between pulses.
4. **Rise time ($t_r$) and Fall time ($t_f$)** — transition durations (affect DC indirectly via threshold crossings).

Duty cycle is *derived* from parameters (2) and (1). It is therefore a **derived metric**, not an independent quantity.

### Step-by-step logical deconstruction:

- **Step 1 — Define the period.** $T$ is the time between two consecutive identical edges (e.g., rising-edge to rising-edge).
- **Step 2 — Define pulse width.** $t_H$ is measured between the **50% crossings** of the rising edge and the **50% crossing** of the falling edge.
- **Step 3 — Compute the LOW time.** $t_L = T - t_H$.
- **Step 4 — Form the ratio.** $DC = t_H / T$. Multiplied by 100% to express as a percentage.
- **Step 5 — Sanity-check.** A symmetric 50% DC means $t_H = t_L = T/2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why measure at the 50% point and not at the 10% or 90% levels?** The 50% threshold is *insensitive* to amplitude noise and to the exact values of $V_{IH}$ / $V_{IL}$. This makes the duty cycle definition *receiver-independent* — any compliant receiver will measure the same value.

## 2.2 Sub-Categorization of Duty Cycle Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Duty Cycle** | $DC_{static}$ | Measured value of $t_H / T$ at a single instant (DC offset). |
| **Duty Cycle Distortion (DCD)** | $\Delta DC$ | Deviation from nominal: $DC_{actual} - DC_{nominal}$. |
| **Duty Cycle Jitter** | $J_{DC}$ | Time-varying component of DC fluctuation (often expressed as ps peak-to-peak). |
| **Cycle-to-Cycle DC Variation** | $\Delta DC_{c2c}$ | Change in duty cycle between two adjacent periods. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are the **canonical KTU 2024 PEECT526 module-equations**. Master this table — it covers >80% of examination numericals.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DC = \dfrac{t_H}{T} \times 100\%$ | Fundamental duty cycle definition | % |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Period from frequency | s |
| 3 | $t_L = T - t_H$ | LOW time | s |
| 4 | $DC = t_H \cdot f \times 100\%$ | Duty cycle via frequency | % |
| 5 | $t_H = \dfrac{DC}{100\%} \cdot T$ | Pulse-width from DC | s |
| 6 | $DCD = DC_{actual} - DC_{ideal}$ | Duty-cycle distortion | % |
| 7 | $\Delta t_H = (DC_{actual} - 50\%) \cdot T$ | DC deviation converted to time-skew | s |
| 8 | $a_n = \dfrac{2A}{n\pi}\sin(n\pi D)$ | n-th Fourier coefficient (rectangular pulse train) | V |
| 9 | $a_0 = A \cdot D$ | DC Fourier component | V |
| 10 | $\text{Even harmonics} \rightarrow 0 \iff D = 0.5$ | 50% DC eliminates even harmonics | — |

**Critical Table Note on notation:** Where absolute magnitude or vertical bars are needed, **never use the pipe symbol** `|` inside markdown rows. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX, for example $\vert a_n \vert$ instead of $|a_n|$.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

In **modern processor and ASIC design**, duty cycle is treated as a **first-class timing constraint**, not a "nice-to-have." Specific applications:

- **DDR4/DDR5 memory interfaces** — The memory controller generates a bidirectional DQS (Data Strobe) signal whose duty cycle must be tightly controlled so that the **read and write capture windows** are balanced. A 5% DC error at 3.2 GT/s (period = 312.5 ps) translates to a **15.6 ps skew** between the two edges — easily large enough to violate setup/hold margins.
- **Clock Distribution Networks (CDN)** — Multi-drop H-trees, clock meshes, and zero-delay buffers all introduce DC distortion due to asymmetric loading, mismatched transistor strengths, and inter-symbol interference.
- **Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs)** — The Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) inside a PLL must produce a symmetric waveform; DC distortion is a major spur mechanism.
- **EMI compliance** — A 50% duty cycle clock has its spectral energy concentrated in **odd harmonics only**, with sharp nulls at even harmonics. Distorting DC to, say, 48% reintroduces even harmonics and may push the design over **FCC / CISPR Class-B EMI limits**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From First Principles: Duty Cycle via Period and Frequency

**Starting principle:** A periodic binary clock toggles between $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) and $0$ V (LOW). Let $t_H$ be the duration of HIGH and $t_L$ be the duration of LOW within one period.

**Step 1.** By definition of period:

$$
T = t_H + t_L
$$

**Step 2.** The reciprocal relationship between period and frequency:

$$
f = \frac{1}{T}
$$

**Step 3.** Solve for the LOW time explicitly:

$$
t_L = T - t_H
$$

**Step 4.** Take the ratio of HIGH-time to total period:

$$
DC = \frac{t_H}{T}
$$

**Step 5.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $f = 1/T$ to introduce frequency:

$$
DC = t_H \cdot \frac{1}{T} = t_H \cdot f
$$

**Step 6.** Express as a percentage:

$$
DC\,[\%] = t_H \cdot f \times 100\%
$$

**Step 7.** Solve for pulse width $t_H$ given DC and $f$:

$$
t_H = \frac{DC}{100\%} \cdot \frac{1}{f}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** Step 6 normalizes the dimensionless ratio into a percentage. Step 7 inverts the relationship so that *given* a duty cycle specification, one can compute the *minimum HIGH pulse width* needed for a downstream flip-flop to register the clock reliably.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fourier Series of a Rectangular Pulse Train with Arbitrary Duty Cycle

This is a **flagship KTU 2024 derivation**. It establishes *why* a 50% duty cycle is spectrally special.

**Setup:** Consider an infinite periodic train of rectangular pulses with amplitude $A$, period $T$, and pulse width $\tau = D \cdot T$ where $D$ is the duty cycle (as a fraction, not a percentage).

$$
x(t) = \begin{cases} A, & 0 \leq t < \tau \\ 0, & \tau \leq t < T \end{cases}
$$

**Step 1 — DC component (n = 0).** The average value of one period:

$$
a_0 = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x(t)\,dt = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{\tau} A\,dt = \frac{A\tau}{T} = A \cdot D
$$

> **Logic Note:** Physically, the time-average voltage of a pulse train is *literally* the area under one pulse divided by the period. This is why a lower duty cycle produces a smaller DC offset — a key principle in PWM audio amplifiers and class-D power stages.

**Step 2 — Cosine coefficients (n ≥ 1):**

$$
a_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt = \frac{2A}{T}\int_{0}^{\tau}\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt
$$

where $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
a_n = \frac{2A}{T} \cdot \left[\frac{\sin(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0}\right]_{0}^{\tau} = \frac{2A}{T} \cdot \frac{\sin(n\omega_0 \tau)}{n\omega_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T$ and $\tau = DT$:**

$$
a_n = \frac{2A}{T} \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi n D)}{2\pi n / T} = \frac{2A}{n\pi}\sin(n\pi D)
$$

**Step 5 — Sine coefficients.** By symmetry, since $x(t)$ is an even function about its midpoint (when shifted), the sine coefficients vanish:

$$
b_n = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Full Fourier series representation:**

$$
x(t) = A\cdot D + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{2A}{n\pi}\sin(n\pi D)\cos(n\omega_0 t)
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $D = 0.5$ (50% duty cycle):**

$$
\sin(n\pi \cdot 0.5) = \sin\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right)
$$

This term evaluates to:

- $n=1$: $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$ → harmonic present
- $n=2$: $\sin(\pi) = 0$ → **harmonic zeroed**
- $n=3$: $\sin(3\pi/2) = -1$ → harmonic present (with phase flip)
- $n=4$: $\sin(2\pi) = 0$ → **harmonic zeroed**
- $n=5$: $\sin(5\pi/2) = 1$ → harmonic present

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical result:** When $D = 0.5$, the sine factor $\sin(n\pi/2)$ is zero for every *even* $n$ and $\pm 1$ for every *odd* $n$. Therefore, **a 50% duty cycle eliminates all even-order harmonics**, leaving only the fundamental and odd harmonics. This is the spectral signature that makes symmetric clocks so desirable in EMI-sensitive applications.

**Step 8 — Generalization.** For *arbitrary* $D \neq 0.5$, even harmonics are *non-zero*, meaning the clock spectrum contains energy at frequencies $2f_0, 4f_0, 6f_0, \ldots$ This energy couples into adjacent channels and signal traces, raising the noise floor and potentially violating regulatory limits.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conversion of DC Distortion to Time Skew

**Problem context:** Given a clock at frequency $f$ with a measured duty cycle of $DC_{actual}$, compute the equivalent timing error (in seconds) relative to the ideal 50% symmetric clock.

**Step 1.** The *ideal* pulse width at 50% DC:

$$
t_{H,\text{ideal}} = 0.5 \cdot T = \frac{0.5}{f}
$$

**Step 2.** The *actual* pulse width:

$$
t_{H,\text{actual}} = \frac{DC_{actual}}{100\%} \cdot T
$$

**Step 3.** The timing error (difference):

$$
\Delta t_H = t_{H,\text{actual}} - t_{H,\text{ideal}} = \left(\frac{DC_{actual}}{100\%} - 0.5\right) \cdot T
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $T = 1/f$:

$$
\Delta t_H = \left(\frac{DC_{actual}}{100\%} - 0.5\right) \cdot \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.** A clock has $f = 2.5$ GHz and $DC_{actual} = 45\%$. Find $\Delta t_H$:

- Period: $T = 1/2.5 \times 10^9 = 4 \times 10^{-10}$ s $= 400$ ps
- $\Delta t_H = (0.45 - 0.50) \cdot 400$ ps $= (-0.05) \cdot 400$ ps $= -20$ ps

> **Interpretation:** The HIGH pulse is 20 ps *shorter* than ideal. This 20 ps asymmetry shifts every rising edge by 10 ps and every falling edge by 10 ps relative to the symmetric reference — enough to corrupt setup timing in a high-speed flip-flop with sub-50 ps setup margin.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Clock Duty Cycle — Functional Block Architecture Flow

The following **Mermaid functional architecture diagram** traces the flow of a clock signal from generation through duty-cycle measurement and correction. This is the conceptual model KTU examiners expect students to draw in Part B answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[XtalOsc Source] --> B[PLL Frequency Multiplier]
    B --> C[Clock Distribution Buffer Tree]
    C --> D[DC Measurement Block 50 percent Threshold]
    D --> E{DC within 45 to 55 percent}
    E -- Yes --> F[Clock Output to Load Flip Flops]
    E -- No --> G[Duty Cycle Corrector DCC]
    G --> H[Feedback to Buffer Tree Trim]
    H --> D
    F --> I[Synchronous Logic Block]
    F --> J[DDR Memory Strobe]
    F --> K[SERDES Transceiver]
    D --> L[DC Distortion Monitor DCD Register]
    L --> M[EMI Compliance Spectrum Check]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **crystal oscillator** provides a low-frequency, low-jitter reference.
- The **PLL** multiplies the frequency to the target GHz range.
- The **clock distribution buffer tree** fans the clock out to multiple loads.
- The **DC measurement block** extracts the 50%-crossing timing of each period.
- The **decision diamond** checks whether DC is within the spec window (typically 45%–55%).
- If **out-of-spec**, the signal is routed through a **Duty Cycle Corrector (DCC)** — a closed-loop circuit that adjusts the pulse width using a delay-line or feedback inverter chain.
- The corrected clock drives **synchronous logic**, **DDR strobes**, and **SERDES** blocks.
- A **DCD register** continuously logs the deviation for **margin testing** and **EMI spectrum verification**.

## 4.2 Duty Cycle Anatomy — Timing Parameter Breakdown

The following diagram shows the *internal anatomy* of one clock period, identifying every timing parameter referenced in the formulas above.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Rising Edge 50 percent Crossing] --> H[HIGH Time tH]
    H --> P2[Falling Edge 50 percent Crossing]
    P2 --> L[LOW Time tL]
    L --> P3[Next Rising Edge]
    P3 --> T[Period T = tH + tL]
    P1 --> TR[Rise Time tr]
    P2 --> TF[Fall Time tf]
```

**Key visual takeaway:** Duty cycle is *not* measured at the absolute peak or the absolute zero — it is measured **between the 50% voltage crossings** on both the rising and falling edges. This is the universal KTU convention.

## 4.3 Source-of-Duty-Cycle-Distortion Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Duty Cycle Distortion Sources] --> S1[Asymmetric Buffer Transistor Sizing]
    SRC --> S2[Load Mismatch in H Tree]
    SRC --> S3[Power Supply Noise VDD Droop]
    SRC --> S4[Inter Symbol Interference ISI]
    SRC --> S5[Channel Loss Dispersion]
    SRC --> S6[Substrate Noise Coupling]
    S1 --> OUT[DCD at Receiver]
    S2 --> OUT
    S3 --> OUT
    S4 --> OUT
    S5 --> OUT
    S6 --> OUT
    OUT --> FIX[Mitigation Duty Cycle Corrector]
    FIX --> M1[Matched Buffer Sizing]
    FIX --> M2[Active Feedback Loop]
    FIX --> M3[PI Power Supply Decoupling]
    FIX --> M4[Pre Emphasis on Channel]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define duty cycle of a clock signal. Why is a 50% duty cycle specifically preferred in high-speed synchronous digital design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Duty cycle (DC) of a periodic clock signal is defined as the percentage ratio of the duration for which the signal remains in the HIGH (logic-1) state during one complete period. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
DC = \left(\frac{t_H}{T}\right) \times 100\%
$$

where $t_H$ is the high-time (pulse width) and $T$ is the period of the clock signal.

**Reason for 50% preference (1.5 marks):** A 50% duty cycle is preferred in high-speed synchronous digital design because:
1. It provides **equal timing margins for rising and falling edges**, ensuring symmetric setup and hold windows for flip-flops triggered on either edge.
2. It **eliminates all even-order harmonics** in the clock spectrum ($\sin(n\pi \cdot 0.5) = 0$ for even $n$), reducing EMI radiation.
3. It enables **clean dual-edge sampling** required by DDR memory interfaces and double-data-rate SERDES links.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is Duty Cycle Distortion (DCD)? Mention any two sources of DCD in a clock distribution network.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Duty Cycle Distortion (DCD) is the deviation of the actual duty cycle of a clock signal from its nominal (ideal) value. It is quantitatively defined as:

$$
DCD = DC_{actual} - DC_{nominal}
$$

A common nominal target is 50%, so any deviation from this symmetric value constitutes DCD.

**Two sources (1.5 marks):**

1. **Asymmetric transistor sizing in clock buffers:** The pull-up PMOS and pull-down NMOS transistors in the output buffer rarely have perfectly matched drive strengths. This causes the rising and falling transition rates to differ, leading to different 50%-crossing intervals on the rising vs. falling edges.

2. **Load mismatch in the clock distribution tree:** An H-tree or clock mesh is designed assuming equal capacitive loading at every leaf, but routing congestion, vias, and nearby signal traces create asymmetric loads, slowing one edge more than the other.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake in Part A:**
> Students frequently write "DCD is when the clock is not 50%" without specifying *deviation from nominal*. KTU examiners expect the precise **deviation language** ($DCD = DC_{actual} - DC_{nominal}$). Vague definitions lose 0.5–1 mark immediately.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Module Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

#### (a) Derive the Fourier series representation of a rectangular pulse train with arbitrary duty cycle $D$. Using this derivation, prove that a 50% duty cycle eliminates all even-order harmonics. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the waveform:**
Consider a periodic rectangular pulse train with period $T$, amplitude $A$, and pulse width $\tau = DT$ where $D \in (0,1)$. The signal $x(t)$ equals $A$ during $[0, \tau)$ and $0$ during $[\tau, T)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute the DC component $a_0$:**

$$
a_0 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x(t)\,dt = \frac{A\tau}{T} = A\cdot D
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute the cosine coefficients $a_n$ for $n \geq 1$:**

$$
a_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^\tau A\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt = \frac{2A}{T}\left[\frac{\sin(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0}\right]_0^\tau = \frac{2A}{n\pi}\sin(n\pi D)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Note that sine coefficients vanish:** $b_n = 0$ by symmetry.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Final Fourier series:**

$$
x(t) = A\cdot D + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{2A}{n\pi}\sin(n\pi D)\cos(n\omega_0 t)
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Prove the 50% property:** Substitute $D = 0.5$:

$$
\sin\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right) = \begin{cases} 0, & n = 2, 4, 6, \ldots \text{(even)} \\ \pm 1, & n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots \text{(odd)} \end{cases}
$$

Since $\sin(n\pi/2) = 0$ for every even $n$, **the coefficients $a_n$ vanish for all even harmonics**. Therefore, the spectrum of a 50% duty cycle clock contains only the DC component and the odd harmonics — even harmonics are completely suppressed. ∎

#### (b) A clock signal has frequency $f = 2.5$ GHz and a measured high-time of $t_H = 180$ ps. Calculate: **(i)** the period, **(ii)** the duty cycle in percent, **(iii)** the low-time, and **(iv)** the duty cycle distortion expressed in picoseconds relative to the 50% ideal. Discuss the implications for a DDR4 memory interface. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Period (1 mark):**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Hz}} = 4 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s} = 400\,\text{ps}
$$

**(ii) Duty cycle (1.5 marks):**

$$
DC = \frac{t_H}{T}\times 100\% = \frac{180\,\text{ps}}{400\,\text{ps}}\times 100\% = 45\%
$$

**(iii) Low time (1 mark):**

$$
t_L = T - t_H = 400\,\text{ps} - 180\,\text{ps} = 220\,\text{ps}
$$

**(iv) DCD expressed as timing skew (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta t_H = (DC_{actual} - 50\%)\cdot T = (0.45 - 0.50)\times 400\,\text{ps} = -20\,\text{ps}
$$

The HIGH pulse is 20 ps **shorter** than ideal; equivalently the LOW pulse is 20 ps **longer**. This 20 ps asymmetry translates to a 10 ps shift on each edge relative to the symmetric reference.

**DDR4 Implications (1.5 marks):**
- DDR4 memory uses bidirectional Data Strobe (DQS) signals with **dual-edge sampling** — both rising and falling edges of DQS are used to capture data.
- A 20 ps asymmetry between the two edges shifts the **read and write capture windows** asymmetrically, reducing the effective eye-opening.
- At 3.2 GT/s DDR4-3200, the unit interval (UI) is 312.5 ps. A 20 ps DC error is **~6.4% of the UI**, large enough to potentially violate the tDQSQ (DQS-to-DQ skew) specification.
- **Mitigation:** Use a duty cycle corrector (DCC) in the memory controller PHY, or a precision PLL with symmetric VCO design.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) Explain Duty Cycle Distortion (DCD) in clock distribution networks. Discuss four major sources of DCD and three standard mitigation techniques. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** DCD is the deviation of the actual duty cycle from the nominal 50% (symmetric) value. Quantitatively, $DCD = DC_{actual} - 50\%$. In high-speed clock distribution networks (CDNs), DCD accumulates along the buffer chain and tree branches.

**Four major sources (4 marks):**

1. **Asymmetric buffer drive strength:** The PMOS pull-up and NMOS pull-down transistors in CMOS clock buffers have different carrier mobilities ($\mu_p \ll \mu_n$). Designers compensate by sizing the PMOS ~2× larger than NMOS, but any residual mismatch causes one edge to be faster than the other.

2. **Power supply noise ($V_{DD}$ droop):** When many switching circuits simultaneously draw current, $V_{DD}$ sags momentarily. The PMOS device slows down (because $V_{GS}$ is reduced), making the rising edge slower than the falling edge. This creates **cycle-to-cycle DCD variation** correlated with switching activity.

3. **Inter-symbol interference (ISI) and channel dispersion:** A long PCB trace or on-chip interconnect acts as a low-pass filter. The finite bandwidth slows one transition more than the previous one, causing the pulse width to drift depending on the **data pattern** of adjacent clock cycles.

4. **Load mismatch in the clock tree:** An H-tree is designed for symmetry, but routing congestion, vias, and proximity to signal traces create unequal capacitive loads. Branches with higher load have slower edges, shifting the 50%-crossing time.

**Three mitigation techniques (2 marks):**

1. **Duty Cycle Corrector (DCC):** A feedback loop that uses a delay-locked loop (DLL) or analog integrator to detect DC error and adjust a digitally-controlled delay line until DC = 50%.

2. **Symmetric buffer design with matched transistor sizing and active feedback biasing** of the NMOS/PMOS current sources.

3. **Power supply decoupling and de-coupling capacitors** at every clock buffer, combined with **on-die decoupling capacitance**, to suppress $V_{DD}$ droop-induced DCD.

#### (b) A clock buffer output has a duty cycle of 45% at $f = 1$ GHz. Calculate: **(i)** $t_H$, **(ii)** $t_L$, **(iii)** the DC error in picoseconds, and **(iv)** the time-shift on each clock edge relative to a perfect 50% reference. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Period (1 mark):**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{9}} = 1\,\text{ns} = 1000\,\text{ps}
$$

**(ii) High-time $t_H$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
t_H = \frac{DC}{100\%}\cdot T = 0.45 \times 1000\,\text{ps} = 450\,\text{ps}
$$

**(iii) Low-time $t_L$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
t_L = T - t_H = 1000\,\text{ps} - 450\,\text{ps} = 550\,\text{ps}
$$

**(iv) DC error in picoseconds (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta t_H = (DC_{actual} - 50\%)\cdot T = (0.45 - 0.50) \times 1000\,\text{ps} = -50\,\text{ps}
$$

The HIGH pulse is 50 ps shorter than ideal; equivalently the LOW pulse is 50 ps longer.

**(v) Time-shift per edge (1.5 marks):** Since the 50 ps *total* asymmetry is distributed across one rising edge and one falling edge, each individual edge shifts by approximately:

$$
\Delta t_{edge} = \frac{\vert \Delta t_H \vert}{2} = \frac{50\,\text{ps}}{2} = 25\,\text{ps}
$$

**Why critical for high-speed sampling (1 mark):**
At 1 GHz, the unit interval is 1000 ps. A 25 ps edge shift is 2.5% of UI. In a high-speed ADC or DDR interface where the setup/hold window is typically only 50–100 ps, a 25 ps DC-induced shift can consume 25–50% of the available timing margin. For sub-GHz interfaces this is tolerable, but for **multi-GHz SERDES, DDR5, and PCIe Gen5/6**, this would be catastrophic — typically DC tolerances of **±2% or better** are mandated.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Duty Cycle questions (PEECT526):**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Mixing MHz with ns, or GHz with ps. Always verify: $1\,\text{ns} \leftrightarrow 1\,\text{GHz}^{-1}$. At 2.5 GHz, $T = 400$ ps, NOT 400 ns.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the ×100% conversion:** Students compute $DC = t_H/T = 0.45$ but write the answer as "0.45" instead of "45%". KTU examiners mark this wrong. *Always* include the percent symbol when the formula has it.
>
> 3. **Confusing DC with frequency:** DC is a *ratio* (dimensionless or %), while frequency has units of Hz. They are fundamentally different quantities — duty cycle tells you *what fraction* of the cycle is HIGH, not *how often* the cycle repeats.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Fourier derivation step-by-step:** In 7-mark questions, KTU examiners want to see the integration steps explicitly written out. Writing only the final formula $a_n = (2A/n\pi)\sin(n\pi D)$ without showing the integral evaluation loses 3–4 marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the 50% spectral property:** Many students complete the Fourier derivation but forget to *explicitly* substitute $D = 0.5$ and evaluate $\sin(n\pi/2)$ to show the even harmonics vanish. KTU examiners specifically look for this substitution as the climax of the proof.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Clock Signal Duty Cycle:**

- [ ] **DC definition:** $DC = (t_H / T) \times 100\%$ — measured at 50% voltage crossings.
- [ ] **Period–frequency relation:** $T = 1/f$, $t_L = T - t_H$.
- [ ] **Nominal value:** Standard high-speed clocks target **DC = 50%**, ±5% tolerance for most interfaces.
- [ ] **50% is spectrally special:** Eliminates **all even-order harmonics**; only DC + odd harmonics remain.
- [ ] **Fourier series:** $x(t) = AD + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{2A}{n\pi}\sin(n\pi D)\cos(n\omega_0 t)$, with $b_n = 0$.
- [ ] **Even-harmonic suppression condition:** $\sin(n\pi/2) = 0 \iff n$ even.
- [ ] **DCD definition:** $\Delta DC = DC_{actual} - DC_{nominal}$; expressed in % or in time-skew (ps).
- [ ] **DCD to time conversion:** $\Delta t_H = (DC_{actual} - 50\%) \cdot T$.
- [ ] **Major sources of DCD:** Asymmetric buffer transistors, load mismatch, $V_{DD}$ noise, ISI, channel dispersion.
- [ ] **Mitigation:** Duty Cycle Correctors (DCC), matched buffer sizing, supply decoupling, pre-emphasis.
- [ ] **DDR / SERDES impact:** DC error of a few percent translates to many ps of edge skew — often exceeds setup/hold margin.
- [ ] **EMI link:** Distorted DC reintroduces even harmonics → couples into adjacent traces → potential FCC/CISPR failure.
- [ ] **PWM note:** In *power electronics*, DC is intentionally varied from 0–100% — but in *clock distribution*, DC is held fixed near 50%.
- [ ] **Measurement convention:** Always use the **50% voltage threshold**, never $V_{IH}$/$V_{IL}$, for receiver-independent DC measurement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decoupling Clock Receivers from the Clock Bus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT526 - HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decoupling Clock Receivers from the Clock Bus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Decoupling clock receivers from the clock bus** refers to the set of design techniques employed to electrically isolate the input stage of clock-receiving devices (e.g., flip-flops, PLLs, clock buffers, ASICs) from the main clock distribution network (the *clock bus*) using **controlled-impedance termination networks**, **series isolation elements**, and **stub-cancellation structures** (T-coils).

The objective is to suppress the loading effect of the receiver's parasitic input capacitance on the transmission-line characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of the clock distribution medium, thereby preventing **reflections**, **ringing**, **overshoot/undershoot**, and **ground/power bounce** that would otherwise corrupt the clock edges at the receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEECT526 Module 4):** This topic directly satisfies the Course Outcome **CO4 – "Design high-speed clock distribution networks with controlled impedance, termination, and signal-integrity aware methodologies."**

Mathematically, the receiver is treated as a **shunt load** with input impedance $Z_{RX}(f)$, and the clock bus is modelled as a **lossy/lossless transmission line** with characteristic impedance $Z_0$. The decoupling network must ensure that the **input reflection coefficient** $\Gamma_{in}(f)$ seen by the bus remains bounded within the operating frequency range.

$$\Gamma_{in}(f) = \frac{Z_{RX}(f) - Z_0}{Z_{RX}(f) + Z_0}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Intuition

Imagine the clock bus as a **long garden hose** carrying water pulses (the clock edges) from a tap (the clock driver) to multiple spray nozzles (the clock receivers).

- If a nozzle has a **wide opening**, it acts as a sudden "bottleneck" — water splashes back up the hose. This is what an un-terminated, high-capacitance CMOS input does to a fast clock edge — it **reflects energy back**.
- If we attach a **small flow-restrictor (resistor) right at the nozzle**, or a **pressure-absorber at the tap**, the pulses arrive cleanly, the backflow is killed, and every nozzle gets a tidy pulse.

In electrical terms:
- The **hose** = controlled-impedance PCB trace / stripline / microstrip.
- The **spray nozzle** = CMOS clock receiver with its input capacitance $C_{in}$ (typically **2 pF to 5 pF**).
- The **flow-restrictor at the tap** = **series (source) termination resistor** $R_S = Z_0$.
- The **pressure-absorber at the nozzle** = **parallel / AC / Thevenin / T-coil termination**.

## 1.3 Why Decoupling is Non-Negotiable in High-Speed Design

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important reason: **A clock edge is the heartbeat of a synchronous system. If the heartbeat is dirty, every flip-flop in the system misfires.**

The "dirty" symptoms that decoupling cures are:

| Symptom | Physical Origin | Effect on System |
|---|---|---|
| **Ringing** | $L$-$C$ resonance between trace inductance and $C_{in}$ | Multiple false clock edges, double-clocking |
| **Overshoot / Undershoot** | Reflected wave from open-circuited stub | Forward-biasing of ESD diodes, latch-up risk |
| **Ground Bounce** | Simultaneous switching of many receivers | $V_{OL}$ rises above threshold, false logic |
| **ISI (Inter-Symbol Interference)** | Ringing from previous bit contaminates next bit | Setup/hold time violations in flip-flops |
| **Skew** | Different propagation delay through different branches | Race conditions, metastability |

The standard safe zone for the input clock edge rate is typically **3× to 5× faster than the system clock period** (e.g., for a **100 MHz** clock ($T = 10\text{ ns}$), the edge must be settled within **2–3 ns**).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8\text{ m/s}$
- Relative permittivity of standard FR-4 PCB: $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.2$ to $4.5$
- Signal velocity in FR-4: $v_p \approx 1.5 \times 10^8\text{ m/s} \;(6\text{ in/ns})$
- Typical controlled trace impedance: $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ (single-ended), $100\ \Omega$ (differential)
- Typical CMOS clock input capacitance: $C_{in} = 2\text{–}5\text{ pF}$
- Maximum stub length for $>\mathbf{1\text{ GHz}}$ operation: $< 5\text{ mm}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Model — Clock Bus as a Multi-Loaded Transmission Line

When a clock buffer drives $N$ receivers distributed along a trace, the structure becomes a **multi-conductor loaded transmission line**. Each receiver is a *shunt parasitic* at its tap-off point, and the tap-off trace (the "stub") has its own characteristic impedance $Z_{stub}$ and length $l_{stub}$.

The receiver input is modelled as a parallel combination of:
- A **gate capacitance** $C_{in}$ (dominant at high frequency)
- An **ESD diode network** (non-linear, clamps to $V_{DD}/GND$)
- A **resistive input** $R_{in}$ (very high, often $> 10^9\ \Omega$ for CMOS)

$$Z_{RX}(f) = \frac{R_{in}}{1 + j2\pi f R_{in} C_{in}} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{j2\pi f C_{in}} \quad \text{for } f \gg \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in} C_{in}}$$

At clock frequencies of $100\text{ MHz}$ to $1\text{ GHz}$, the receiver essentially looks **purely capacitive**.

## 2.2 The Decoupling Toolkit — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Series (Source) Termination

**WHY it works:** A resistor $R_S = Z_0 - Z_{driver}$ is placed in series at the source. When the incident wave reaches the (high-impedance) far end, it reflects with $\Gamma = +1$, but the reflection is fully absorbed at the source end because $R_S + Z_{driver} = Z_0$ (matched).

**HOW to apply:**
1. Place $R_S$ as close as possible to the clock driver's output pin.
2. Use $R_S = Z_0 - R_{out,driver}$ (e.g., $33\ \Omega$ if $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ and $R_{out} = 17\ \Omega$).
3. Use this for **point-to-point** clocks and **daisy-chained** receiver chains.
4. **Disadvantage:** Half of the signal swing is dropped across $R_S$ at the source → clock edge at the receiver is delayed by $2 \times t_{pd}$ (round-trip).

### 2.2.2 Parallel (End) Termination

**WHY it works:** A resistor $R_T = Z_0$ is placed at the far end, in parallel with the receiver. The matched load absorbs the incident wave — no reflection.

**HOW to apply:**
1. Place $R_T$ at the *last* receiver on the bus.
2. Use $R_T = Z_0$ (typically $50\ \Omega$ for single-ended, $100\ \Omega$ for differential).
3. **Drawback:** Constant DC current flows through $R_T$ → wastes power, distorts $V_{OL}/V_{OH}$ levels.

### 2.2.3 Thevenin (Split) Termination

**WHY it works:** A divider of $R_1$ (to $V_{TT}$) and $R_2$ (to $GND$) gives:
$$Z_{Thev} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$
Setting $R_1 = R_2 = 2 Z_0$ gives $Z_{Thev} = Z_0$ and biases the line at $V_{TT} = V_{DD}/2$.

**HOW to apply:** Use when you need a clean DC bias and matched AC termination. Common in **GTL**, **HSTL**, and **SSTL** clock interfaces.

### 2.2.4 AC (RC) Termination — *The Clock Designer's Favourite*

**WHY it works:** A series $R$–$C$ network blocks DC but provides AC matching at the clock frequency. The capacitor $C_T$ is chosen so that $X_{C_T} \ll Z_0$ at the fundamental, i.e., $C_T > 5/(2\pi f Z_0)$.

**HOW to apply:**
1. $R_T = Z_0$, $C_T$ large enough to be a short at $f_{clk}$.
2. **No DC power wasted** — best for low-power CMOS clock trees.
3. Cannot bias a floating line, so the receiver must be self-biasing (most modern CMOS clock inputs are).

### 2.2.5 T-Coil (Stub-Cancellation) Termination

**WHY it works:** A T-coil is a pair of **magnetically-coupled inductors** (mutual inductance $M \approx k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$, $k \approx 0.7$ nominal) that presents a *broadband constant impedance* equal to $\sqrt{L/C}$ over more than a decade of frequency, *cancelling* the pole created by $C_{in}$.

**HOW to apply:**
1. Insert the T-coil **in series** between the bus and the receiver pin.
2. Choose $L$ such that the resonant pole of $L$ and $C_{in}$ is *above* the highest harmonic of interest.
3. Used in **DDR3/DDR4/DDR5**, **SerDes**, and **GHz-range ASIC** clock inputs.

### 2.2.6 Differential Termination (LVDS / CML / LVPECL)

For differential clocks (PCIe, USB 3.x, SATA, SerDes, Ethernet), a $100\ \Omega$ resistor is placed **between the two differential lines** at the receiver. This matches the differential mode impedance $Z_{diff} = 2 Z_0$ and suppresses common-mode noise.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations cover 80% of KTU 2024 numerical problems on clock distribution.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Reflection coefficient at load | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0} = 1 + \Gamma$ | Transmission coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}$ | Signal velocity on PCB trace | m/s |
| 4 | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{l}{v_p} \approx 6.6\text{ ps/mm}$ | Propagation delay (FR-4) | ps/mm |
| 5 | $Z_0^{microstrip} = \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r+1.41}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t}\right)$ | Microstrip impedance | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $Z_0^{stripline} = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1.8 b}{0.8 w + t}\right)$ | Stripline impedance | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $Z_0^{coax} = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ | Coaxial cable impedance | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $f_{c,stub} = \dfrac{c}{4\,l_{stub}\,\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}$ | Stub cutoff frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $R_{S,opt} = Z_0 - R_{out,driver}$ | Series source termination | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | $R_{T,opt} = Z_0$ | Parallel/AC termination | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | $C_T > \dfrac{5}{2\pi f_{clk} Z_0}$ | AC-termination capacitor | F |
| 12 | $Z_{T\text{-}coil} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C_{in}}}$ | T-coil characteristic impedance | $\Omega$ |
| 13 | $t_{r,min} > 2\,t_{pd}$ | Condition for transmission-line behaviour | s |
| 14 | $\delta_{skew} = l\,\Delta\varepsilon_r / c$ | Skew between paired traces | s |
| 15 | $V_{reflected} = \Gamma \cdot V_{incident}$ | Reflected voltage amplitude | V |
| 16 | $L_{loop} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\!\left[\text{ln}(d/r) + 0.25\right]$ | Partial self-inductance per unit length | H/m |
| 17 | $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_S C_{in}}$ | Receiver input low-pass cutoff | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> All vertical bars `|` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) in the table above have been replaced with `\vert` or reformulated to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. KTU examiners often mark down tables that render incorrectly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DDR memory interfaces:** DDR4/DDR5 use **fly-by topology** with on-die termination (ODT) plus external AC termination, eliminating stubs entirely.
- **Server backplanes:** Source-series termination is the default for clock distribution across FR-4 backplanes up to **25–40 cm**.
- **Telecom line cards:** T-coils are routinely used on **CML/LVDS** clock inputs to maintain $S_{11} < -10\text{ dB}$ up to $12.5\text{ GHz}$.
- **FPGA clock networks:** Xilinx, Intel, and Lattice FPGAs expose internal programmable termination (e.g., `IN_TERM = UNTUNED_SPLIT_50`) that is essentially a digitally-controlled Thevenin network.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reflection Coefficient at a Capacitive Clock Receiver

**Given:**
- A clock bus with characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$.
- A receiver with input capacitance $C_{in} = 3\text{ pF}$.
- Clock fundamental $f_{clk} = 500\text{ MHz}$ (3rd harmonic $= 1.5\text{ GHz}$).

**Find:** the reflection coefficient magnitude $\vert \Gamma \vert$ at the 3rd harmonic, and the impact of adding an AC-termination network.

**Step 1 — Receiver input impedance at the frequency of interest:**

$$Z_{RX}(f) = \frac{1}{j 2 \pi f C_{in}}$$

Substituting $f = 1.5 \times 10^9\text{ Hz}$ and $C_{in} = 3 \times 10^{-12}\text{ F}$:

$$Z_{RX} = \frac{1}{j 2 \pi (1.5 \times 10^9)(3 \times 10^{-12})}$$

$$Z_{RX} = \frac{1}{j 2 \pi \cdot 4.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Z_{RX} = \frac{1}{j 0.02827}$$

$$Z_{RX} = -j\,35.37\ \Omega$$

**Interpretation:** The receiver looks like a *negative imaginary* impedance of $35.37\ \Omega$ at 1.5 GHz — i.e., a strong capacitive short.

**Step 2 — Compute the reflection coefficient:**

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_{RX} - Z_0}{Z_{RX} + Z_0} = \frac{-j35.37 - 50}{-j35.37 + 50}$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$-j35.37 - 50 = -50 - j35.37$$

**Compute denominator:**

$$-j35.37 + 50 = 50 - j35.37$$

**Compute the magnitude-squared ratio** (since the magnitude of a complex fraction equals the ratio of magnitudes):

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{\sqrt{50^2 + 35.37^2}}{\sqrt{50^2 + 35.37^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2500 + 1251.05}}{\sqrt{2500 + 1251.05}} = 1$$

**Wait — that cannot be the literal answer.** The magnitude of $\Gamma$ for *any* purely reactive $Z_{RX}$ is exactly $1$, because reactive loads conserve energy (no dissipation). The crucial quantity is the *phase* and the *voltage* at the receiver.

**Step 3 — Compute the phase of $\Gamma$:**

$$\angle \Gamma = \angle(-50 - j35.37) - \angle(50 - j35.37)$$

$$\angle(-50 - j35.37) = -180° + \arctan(35.37/50) = -180° + 35.26° = -144.74°$$

$$\angle(50 - j35.37) = -\arctan(35.37/50) = -35.26°$$

$$\angle \Gamma = -144.74° - (-35.26°) = -109.48°$$

**Step 4 — Compute the voltage transmission coefficient $T$:**

$$T = 1 + \Gamma = 1 + (\cos(-109.48°) + j\sin(-109.48°))$$

$$\cos(-109.48°) = -0.3329,\quad \sin(-109.48°) = -0.9429$$

$$T = 1 + (-0.3329) + j(-0.9429) = 0.6671 - j0.9429$$

$$\vert T \vert = \sqrt{0.6671^2 + 0.9429^2} = \sqrt{0.4450 + 0.8891} = \sqrt{1.3341} = 1.1550$$

**Step 5 — Add AC termination $R_T = 50\ \Omega$ in parallel with $C_{in} = 3\text{ pF}$:**

The combined impedance becomes:

$$Z_{load} = R_T \parallel \frac{1}{j2\pi f C_{in}} = \frac{R_T \cdot \dfrac{1}{j2\pi f C_{in}}}{R_T + \dfrac{1}{j2\pi f C_{in}}}$$

$$Z_{load} = \frac{R_T}{1 + j2\pi f R_T C_{in}}$$

Substituting values:

$$Z_{load} = \frac{50}{1 + j2\pi (1.5\times 10^9)(50)(3\times 10^{-12})}$$

$$Z_{load} = \frac{50}{1 + j1.4137}$$

$$Z_{load} = \frac{50\,(1 - j1.4137)}{1 + 1.4137^2} = \frac{50\,(1 - j1.4137)}{2.9986}$$

$$Z_{load} = 16.673 - j23.573\ \Omega$$

**Step 6 — Recompute the reflection coefficient:**

$$\Gamma_{AC} = \frac{(16.673 - j23.573) - 50}{(16.673 - j23.573) + 50} = \frac{-33.327 - j23.573}{66.673 - j23.573}$$

**Magnitudes:**

$$\vert -33.327 - j23.573 \vert = \sqrt{1110.7 + 555.7} = \sqrt{1666.4} = 40.82$$

$$\vert 66.673 - j23.573 \vert = \sqrt{4445.2 + 555.7} = \sqrt{5000.9} = 70.72$$

$$\vert \Gamma_{AC} \vert = \frac{40.82}{70.72} = 0.5772$$

**Step 7 — Voltage at the receiver in dB:**

$$S_{11} = 20\log_{10}(0.5772) = -4.77\text{ dB}$$

**Conclusion:** Without termination, $\vert \Gamma \vert = 1$ (total reflection, full ringing). With AC termination, $\vert \Gamma \vert \approx 0.58$ — a $4.77\text{ dB}$ reduction in reflection. Adding a T-coil can push this below $-15\text{ dB}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Stub Length for a Given Clock Frequency

**Given:** $f_{max} = 1\text{ GHz}$, FR-4 substrate ($\varepsilon_r = 4.3$).

**Step 1 — Effective permittivity of microstrip (empirical):**

$$\varepsilon_{r,eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12h/w}}$$

For $w/h = 1$ (typical):

$$\varepsilon_{r,eff} = \frac{4.3+1}{2} + \frac{4.3-1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12}} = 2.65 + 1.65 \cdot 0.2774 = 2.65 + 0.458 = 3.108$$

**Step 2 — Signal velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{3.108}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.7629} = 1.701 \times 10^8\text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum stub length (electrical $\lambda/10$ rule):**

$$l_{stub,max} = \frac{v_p}{10\,f_{max}} = \frac{1.701 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.01701\text{ m} = 17.0\text{ mm}$$

**Practical KTU rule:** keep $l_{stub} < 5\text{ mm}$ at $1\text{ GHz}$, $< 15\text{ mm}$ at $300\text{ MHz}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: T-Coil Inductance for Stub Cancellation

**Given:** $C_{in} = 3\text{ pF}$, target $Z_{T} = 50\ \Omega$, target flat-impedance bandwidth = $1\text{ GHz}$ to $5\text{ GHz}$.

**Step 1 — Required inductance:**

$$L = \frac{Z_T^{\,2}\,C_{in}}{4} = \frac{50^2 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-12}}{4} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-9}}{4} = 1.875\text{ nH}$$

**Step 2 — Resonance check:**

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L\,C_{in}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{1.875\text{ nH} \cdot 3\text{ pF}}}$$

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{5.625 \times 10^{-21}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 7.5 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{4.712 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.122\text{ GHz}$$

This is comfortably above the fundamental $1\text{ GHz}$ — the pole-zero pair of the T-coil cancels the pole created by $C_{in}$ across the operating band.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Decoupling Method

| Topology | Impedance Match | DC Power | Bias | Best Use Case | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Series $R_S$** | Source-end only | Low | Self | Point-to-point, daisy-chain | Half-swing delay, sensitive to $R_S$ tolerance |
| **Parallel $R_T$** | Full match | **High** | DC path | Low-frequency clocks, end-of-bus | Power waste, level shift |
| **Thevenin** | Full match | Medium | $V_{TT}$ provided | GTL/HSTL/SSTL clocks | Requires $V_{TT}$ rail |
| **AC ($R$–$C$)** | Full at $f_{clk}$ | **Zero** | Self | CMOS clock trees, low-power | Not DC-biasable |
| **T-coil** | Broadband | Low | Self | GHz ASICs, SerDes | Discrete inductor design complexity |
| **Differential $100\ \Omega$** | Differential mode | Low | Self | LVDS/CML/PECL | Common-mode reflection |
| **On-Die (ODT)** | Programmable | Configurable | Self | DDR3/4/5, FPGA inputs | Limited drive strength |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `clkSrc`, `rcvBlock1`). All node labels contain raw uppercase text only — no markdown bold, no italics, no HTML inside double-quoted labels.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Clock Distribution with Decoupling Networks

```mermaid
graph LR
    clkSrc["CLOCK SOURCE PLL OR OSCILLATOR"] -->|Z0 trace| splitPoint["CLOCK BUSBranch Junction"]
    splitPoint -->|series Rs path A| pathA["MICROSTRIP TRACE TO RX1"]
    splitPoint -->|series Rs path B| pathB["MICROSTRIP TRACE TO RX2"]
    splitPoint -->|series Rs path C| pathC["MICROSTRIP TRACE TO RX3"]
    pathA --> decoupA["DECOUPLING NETWORKA"]
    decoupA --> rxA["CLOCK RECEIVER1 CMOS FF OR PLL"]
    pathB --> decoupB["DECOUPLING NETWORKB"]
    decoupB --> rxB["CLOCK RECEIVER2 ASIC LOGIC"]
    pathC --> decoupC["DECOUPLING NETWORKC"]
    decoupC --> rxC["CLOCK RECEIVER3 MEMORY CONTROLLER"]
    decoupA -. examples .-> typesA["AC TERMINATION OR SERIES R OR TCOIL"]
    decoupB -. examples .-> typesB["THEVENIN OR PARALLEL R"]
    decoupC -. examples .-> typesC["DIFFERENTIAL 100 OHM OR ODT"]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Decoupling Network Sub-Block Schematics

```mermaid
graph TD
    busIn["CLOCK BUS INPUT Z0 50 OHM"] --> netA["AC TERMINATION R1 50 OHM IN PARALLEL WITH C1 100NF"]
    busIn --> netB["SERIES R 33 OHM AT SOURCE END"]
    busIn --> netC["THEVENIN R1 100 OHM TO VDD AND R2 100 OHM TO GND"]
    busIn --> netD["DIFFERENTIAL PAIR WITH RT 100 OHM BETWEEN LINES"]
    busIn --> netE["TCOIL L1 AND L2 MUTUALLY COUPLED WITH K 0.7"]
    netA --> outA["RECEIVER INPUT CIN 3 PF"]
    netB --> outB["RECEIVER INPUT CIN 3 PF"]
    netC --> outC["RECEIVER INPUT CIN 3 PF"]
    netD --> outD["DIFFERENTIAL RECEIVER INPUT"]
    netE --> outE["RECEIVER INPUT CIN 3 PF"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Decoupling Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["START CLOCK RECEIVER DECOUPLING DECISION"] --> q1{"IS CLOCK DIFFERENTIAL"}
    q1 -->|YES| diffPath["USE 100 OHM DIFFERENTIAL TERMINATION AT RX"]
    q1 -->|NO| q2{"IS RECEIVER ON DIE OR EXTERNAL"}
    q2 -->|ON DIE| odtPath["USE ODT PROGRAMMABLE TERMINATION DDR STYLE"]
    q2 -->|EXTERNAL| q3{"IS FREQUENCY GREATER THAN 1 GHZ"}
    q3 -->|YES| tcoilPath["USE TCOIL WITH L MATCHED TO CIN"]
    q3 -->|NO| q4{"IS POWER BUDGET TIGHT"}
    q4 -->|YES| acPath["USE AC RC TERMINATION"]
    q4 -->|NO| q5{"IS BIASING NEEDED"}
    q5 -->|YES| thevPath["USE THEVENIN TERMINATION WITH VTT"]
    q5 -->|NO| seriesPath["USE SERIES RS AT SOURCE END"]
```

## 4.4 Physical Topology Variants

```mermaid
graph LR
    a["DAISY CHAIN TOPOLOGY"] --> aDesc["RECEIVERS PLACED INLINE ON SINGLE TRACE GOOD FOR LOW FREQUENCY"]
    b["STAR TOPOLOGY"] --> bDesc["EQUAL LENGTH TRACES TO ALL RECEIVERS GOOD FOR SKEW CONTROL"]
    c["FLY BY TOPOLOGY"] --> cDesc["RECEIVERS TAPPED ALONG A SINGLE CONTROLLED TRACE BEST FOR DDR4 5"]
    d["HYBRID TOPOLOGY"] --> dDesc["COMBINATION OF STAR AND DAISY USED IN HIGH END ASIC CLOCK TREES"]
```

## 4.5 Reflection Behaviour — With and Without Decoupling (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    noTerm["NO TERMINATION"] --> r1["OPEN END RX REFLECTION COEFFICIENT PLUS 1"]
    noTerm --> r2["DAMPED SINUSOIDAL RINGING ON EVERY EDGE"]
    noTerm --> r3["POSSIBLE DOUBLE CLOCKING OR METASTABILITY"]
    withTerm["AC OR PARALLEL TERMINATION"] --> r4["MATCHED LOAD ABSORBS INCIDENT WAVE"]
    withTerm --> r5["RINGING SUPPRESSED TO LESS THAN 5 PERCENT OF SWING"]
    withTerm --> r6["CLEAN MONOTONIC EDGE AT RX INPUT"]
    withTcoil["TCOIL TERMINATION"] --> r7["BROADBAND MATCH FROM 1 GHZ TO 5 GHZ"]
    withTcoil --> r8["POLE ZERO CANCELLATION OF CIN POLE"]
    withTcoil --> r9["S11 LESS THAN MINUS 15 DB ACROSS BAND"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Each question targets Bloom's Level 2 (Understand) / Level 3 (Apply) — these are the most common KTU 3-mark questions.**

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define clock-receiver decoupling. Why is it essential in high-speed digital systems operating above 100 MHz?**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**
Clock-receiver decoupling is the technique of inserting controlled-impedance networks (resistors, capacitors, T-coils) between a clock distribution bus and the input of a clock-receiving device to suppress reflections caused by the receiver's input capacitance $C_{in}$. At clock frequencies above 100 MHz, the wavelength of the clock edge becomes comparable to the PCB trace length, making the bus behave as a transmission line. Without decoupling, $C_{in}$ creates an impedance discontinuity that reflects the incident wave ($\vert \Gamma \vert \to 1$), producing ringing, overshoot, and ground bounce. These corrupt setup/hold margins, cause double-clocking, and may forward-bias ESD diodes. Decoupling ensures $\vert \Gamma \vert \to 0$, yielding a clean monotonic edge and reliable synchronous operation.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Compare series termination, parallel termination, and AC termination for clock buses. State one merit and one demerit of each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 130 words):**

| Type | Merit | Demerit |
|---|---|---|
| **Series $R_S$** | No DC power dissipation; absorbs reflection at source | Half-swing delay, requires accurate $R_S = Z_0 - R_{out}$ |
| **Parallel $R_T$** | Perfect impedance match at load; clean edge | Continuous DC current through $R_T$ → wasted power |
| **AC ($R$–$C$)** | Zero DC power; preserves CMOS levels | No DC bias path; $C_T$ must be sized for lowest harmonic |

**Exam Tip:** Memorise the one-line merit/demerit for each — KTU examiners award the third mark only for crisp comparative language.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Each Part B question is a 14-mark, two-sub-part (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks problem aligned with KTU 2024 Bloom's Levels 3–5 (Apply / Analyse / Evaluate).**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Set 2]**

**A (a)** With the help of a transmission-line model, explain why a clock receiver's input capacitance $C_{in}$ acts as a discontinuity on a high-speed clock bus. Derive an expression for the input reflection coefficient at the receiver, and compute $\vert \Gamma \vert$ for a $3\text{ pF}$ receiver on a $50\ \Omega$ bus at $f = 1.5\text{ GHz}$. (7 marks)

**A (b)** Design a complete AC-termination network for the above receiver that reduces the reflection magnitude to less than $0.3$. Show all steps and verify the result with the reflection-coefficient formula. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**A (a) — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Physical model (2 marks):** Treat the clock bus as a lossless transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$. The receiver's input is dominated at high frequency by $C_{in}$ (CMOS gate capacitance), since the input resistance $R_{in} > 10^9\ \Omega$. The receiver appears as a shunt capacitor to AC ground.

2. **Impedance of the receiver (1 mark):**

$$Z_{RX} = \frac{1}{j 2\pi f C_{in}}$$

3. **Reflection-coefficient derivation (2 marks):** The voltage reflection coefficient at a load is defined as the ratio of reflected to incident voltage. For a load $Z_L$ terminating a line of impedance $Z_0$:

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_{RX} - Z_0}{Z_{RX} + Z_0}$$

Substituting $Z_{RX}$:

$$\Gamma = \frac{\dfrac{1}{j2\pi f C_{in}} - Z_0}{\dfrac{1}{j2\pi f C_{in}} + Z_0} = \frac{1 - j2\pi f C_{in} Z_0}{1 + j2\pi f C_{in} Z_0}$$

4. **Numerical evaluation (2 marks):** At $f = 1.5\text{ GHz}$, $C_{in} = 3\text{ pF}$:

$$2\pi f C_{in} Z_0 = 2\pi(1.5\times 10^9)(3\times 10^{-12})(50) = 1.4137$$

$$\Gamma = \frac{1 - j1.4137}{1 + j1.4137} = \frac{(1 - j1.4137)^2}{1 + 1.4137^2} = \frac{1 - 2.8274j - 2.0}{2.9986} = \frac{-1.0 - 2.8274j}{2.9986}$$

$$\Gamma = -0.3335 - j0.9429$$

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \sqrt{0.3335^2 + 0.9429^2} = \sqrt{0.1112 + 0.8891} = \sqrt{1.0003} \approx 1.0$$

**Conclusion:** The receiver reflects virtually all incident energy — a textbook symptom of an un-terminated high-speed clock input.

**A (b) — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Goal:** Reduce $\vert \Gamma \vert < 0.3$ by adding an AC-termination network $R_T \parallel C_T$ in parallel with $C_{in}$.

2. **Choose $R_T = Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ (2 marks).** Choose $C_T$ such that $X_{C_T} \ll 50\ \Omega$ at $f = 1.5\text{ GHz}$:

$$C_T \gg \frac{1}{2\pi f Z_0} = \frac{1}{2\pi(1.5\times 10^9)(50)} = 2.122\text{ pF}$$

   Choose $C_T = 100\text{ pF}$ (3 decades margin) → $X_{C_T} \approx 1.06\ \Omega$ at 1.5 GHz (negligible).

3. **Combined load impedance (2 marks):**

$$Z_{load} = \frac{R_T}{1 + j2\pi f R_T (C_{in} + C_T)}$$

$$2\pi f R_T (C_{in} + C_T) = 2\pi(1.5\times 10^9)(50)(3 + 100)\times 10^{-12} = 0.04854$$

$$Z_{load} = \frac{50}{1 + j0.04854} \approx \frac{50}{1.00118} \approx 49.94\ \Omega \angle -2.78°$$

4. **Recompute $\Gamma$ (1 mark):**

$$\Gamma_{AC} = \frac{49.94\angle -2.78° - 50}{49.94\angle -2.78° + 50}$$

Since the load is nearly real and approximately equal to $Z_0$:

$$\vert \Gamma_{AC} \vert \approx \frac{\sqrt{0.06^2 + 2.42^2}}{\sqrt{99.94^2 + 2.42^2}} \approx \frac{2.42}{99.97} \approx 0.0242$$

$$\vert \Gamma_{AC} \vert \approx 0.024 \ll 0.3 \quad \checkmark$$

5. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** With $R_T = 50\ \Omega$ and $C_T = 100\text{ pF}$, the reflection drops from $1.0$ to $\approx 0.024$ — a **$32\text{ dB}$ improvement**, easily meeting the $< 0.3$ design goal.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set 1] — Alternative Choice**

**B (a)** Explain the working principle of a T-coil based stub-cancellation network. Derive the condition for broadband impedance matching and compute the required inductance for a $3\text{ pF}$ receiver on a $50\ \Omega$ bus to be matched from $1\text{ GHz}$ to $5\text{ GHz}$. (7 marks)

**B (b)** A $200\text{ MHz}$ clock is distributed across an FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.3$) PCB over a microstrip trace of total length $20\text{ cm}$. The board has a receiver stub of length $8\text{ mm}$ at the far end. Determine (i) the signal velocity, (ii) the propagation delay, (iii) whether the stub is electrically long, and (iv) recommend a suitable termination strategy. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**B (a) — Step-by-Step:**

1. **T-coil concept (2 marks):** A T-coil is a pair of coupled inductors wound on a common core or implemented as planar spirals, with mutual coupling $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ and $k \approx 0.7$. When placed between the bus ($Z_0$) and the capacitive load ($C_{in}$), it generates a pole-zero pair that cancels the pole created by $C_{in}$ at high frequency, giving a *broadband constant input impedance*.

2. **Matching condition (2 marks):** The T-coil presents an effective characteristic impedance:

$$Z_T = 2\sqrt{\frac{L}{C_{in}}}$$

   For broadband match to the bus:

$$Z_T = Z_0 \;\Rightarrow\; L = \frac{Z_0^2\,C_{in}}{4} = \frac{(50)^2 \cdot 3\times 10^{-12}}{4} = 1.875\text{ nH}$$

3. **Resonance check (2 marks):** The pole-zero cancellation works over a band centred on:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_{in}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{1.875\text{ nH} \cdot 3\text{ pF}}} = 2.12\text{ GHz}$$

   This is the *geometric mean* of the $1\text{ GHz}$–$5\text{ GHz}$ band — ideal placement.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Two coupled planar spiral inductors of $L_1 = L_2 = 1.875\text{ nH}$ with $k = 0.7$ provide a flat input impedance of $50\ \Omega \pm 5\%$ from $1\text{ GHz}$ to $5\text{ GHz}$.

**B (b) — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Effective permittivity (1 mark):** For an FR-4 microstrip with $w/h \approx 1$:

$$\varepsilon_{r,eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2\sqrt{1 + 12h/w}} = 2.65 + 0.458 = 3.108$$

2. **Signal velocity (1.5 marks):**

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}} = \frac{3\times 10^8}{\sqrt{3.108}} = 1.701 \times 10^8\text{ m/s}$$

3. **Propagation delay for 20 cm trace (1.5 marks):**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{l}{v_p} = \frac{0.20}{1.701\times 10^8} = 1.176\text{ ns}$$

4. **Stub electrical length (2 marks):**

$$t_{pd,stub} = \frac{0.008}{1.701\times 10^8} = 47.0\text{ ps}$$

   At $200\text{ MHz}$, the edge rate is approximately $t_r \approx 0.5 / f = 2.5\text{ ns}$ (assuming $10\text{–}90\%$ rise time of 0.35 of period for clean sine approximations). The condition for transmission-line behaviour is $t_r > 2 t_{pd,stub}$:

$$2 t_{pd,stub} = 94\text{ ps} \ll t_r = 2.5\text{ ns}$$

   **Conclusion:** The stub is *electrically short* at $200\text{ MHz}$ and behaves as a lumped capacitor — **no transmission-line termination is strictly required at the fundamental**.

5. **Recommended strategy (1 mark):** For $200\text{ MHz}$ clock, use a **simple series source termination $R_S = Z_0 - R_{out}$** at the driver, plus a small AC-decoupling capacitor ($10\text{ nF}$–$100\text{ nF}$) close to each $V_{DD}$ pin of the receiver to suppress power-rail noise. Full AC/parallel termination is unnecessary at this frequency and would waste power.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pm$ sign / phase** of $\Gamma$ — examiners want both magnitude **and** phase, not just $\vert \Gamma \vert$.
> 2. **Mixing $Z_0$ (single-ended) with $Z_{diff}$ (differential)** — for LVDS, use $Z_{diff} = 100\ \Omega$ between the lines, not to ground.
> 3. **Skipping the propagation delay calculation** before declaring "transmission line behaviour" — KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if you apply transmission-line theory without verifying $t_r > 2 t_{pd}$.
> 4. **Forgetting the unit conversion** when $C_{in}$ is given in pF — write $3\text{ pF} = 3\times 10^{-12}\text{ F}$ in the formula.
> 5. **AC termination capacitor too small** — using $C_T = 1\text{ pF}$ at $1\text{ GHz}$ gives $X_C = 159\ \Omega$, completely defeating the purpose. Always use $C_T > 5/(2\pi f Z_0)$.
> 6. **Neglecting DC bias in AC termination** — KTU 2024 paper specifically asks for "the biasing implication" in Q.1(b) of the Dec 2024 paper.
> 7. **Confusing T-coil with common-mode choke** — they are fundamentally different devices.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Use this as your last-minute KTU 2024 revision checklist (5 minutes before the exam).**

- **Clock-receiver decoupling** = controlled-impedance network that isolates a capacitive CMOS clock input from the bus to prevent reflections.
- **Master formula:** $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$. Memorise its derivation path.
- **Three primary topologies:** Series (source-end), Parallel (load-end), AC ($R$–$C$ at load). Know merits/demerits in a single table row each.
- **T-coil** = coupled-inductor stub-canceller for $>\!1\text{ GHz}$ applications; choose $L = Z_0^2 C_{in}/4$.
- **Differential termination:** $100\ \Omega$ between the two lines (not to ground) for LVDS/CML/PECL.
- **Stub rule:** keep $l_{stub} < v_p / (10\,f_{max})$. At $1\text{ GHz}$ on FR-4, this is $\approx 17\text{ mm}$.
- **Condition for transmission-line behaviour:** rise time $t_r > 2\,t_{pd}$. Always verify this before applying transmission-line theory.
- **FR-4 propagation delay:** $\approx 6.6\text{ ps/mm}$ (single-ended microstrip).
- **Receiver input model:** $C_{in} = 2\text{–}5\text{ pF}$, $R_{in} > 1\text{ G}\Omega$ — essentially a pure capacitor at clock frequencies.
- **Thevenin termination** requires a $V_{TT}$ rail; **AC termination** does not — choose based on whether $V_{TT}$ is already generated.
- **ODT (On-Die Termination)** is programmable in modern DDR and FPGA clock inputs — design with it enabled by default.
- **Ground bounce** from simultaneous switching of many clock receivers is mitigated by **multiple short return paths** (via stitching vias), not just by termination.
- **Exam phrasing keywords:** "controlled-impedance bus", "stub cancellation", "pole-zero pair", "broadband match", "monotonic edge", "$\vert S_{11} \vert < -10\text{ dB}$" — use these exact phrases in your answers.
- **KVL/KCL sanity check:** at DC, no current flows through the AC-termination capacitor; the bus is biased only by the driver.
- **Power dissipation in $R_T$:** $P = V_{DD}^2 / (2 R_T)$ for Thevenin — for $V_{DD} = 3.3\text{ V}$ and $R_T = 50\ \Omega$, $P \approx 109\text{ mW}$ per terminated line (often the deal-breaker for AC termination choice).
- **Frequency-domain metric:** aim for $\vert S_{11} \vert < -10\text{ dB}$ ($\vert \Gamma \vert < 0.316$) across the entire clock fundamental and its 3rd harmonic — this is the *KTU/Knack* target for all high-speed clock design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clock Oscillator (KTU 2024 Definition):** A clock oscillator is an **electronic circuit that generates a stable, periodic, and precise timing signal** (typically a square wave or sine wave) used as the master timing reference for synchronous digital systems. It converts **DC power into a precisely periodic AC waveform** whose frequency is governed by a high-Q resonant element (quartz crystal, RC/LC tank, or ring of inverters) and whose spectral purity determines system-level **timing jitter, phase noise, and bit-error-rate (BER)** performance.

In High Speed Digital Design (PEECT526, Module 4), the clock oscillator is treated as the **single most critical analogue sub-block inside an otherwise digital system**, because every flip-flop, latch, and synchronizer in a multi-GHz SoC inherits its noise directly.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Metronome of the Digital Orchestra**
> Imagine a 100-piece orchestra (your digital chip). Every musician (flip-flop, register, state machine) must start their note at *exactly the same instant* — the downbeat of the conductor's baton. The **clock oscillator is that conductor's metronome**. If the metronome ticks are uneven (jitter), the music sounds chaotic (metastability, setup/hold violations). If its tempo drifts with temperature (frequency stability), the entire performance falls apart. The **quartz crystal** is the precisely machined pendulum inside the metronome that keeps tempo locked even when the room gets hot or cold.

### Real-World Mapping
| Digital Domain | Orchestral Analogy |
|---|---|
| Clock edge (rising/falling) | Conductor's downbeat |
| Jitter (σj) | Random variation in beat timing |
| Frequency drift (Δf/f) | Musicians getting faster/slower over time |
| Crystal oscillator | Mechanical pendulum (high-Q resonator) |
| VCO | Metronome whose tempo can be electrically adjusted |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Metrics (Board-Exam Favourites):**
> - **Q (Quality Factor)** of a quartz crystal: typically **10,000 to 100,000**
> - **Stability**: ±10 ppm to ±100 ppm (parts-per-million)
> - **Standard logic levels (CMOS)**: **V_OH ≈ V_DD**, **V_OL ≈ 0 V**, threshold ≈ **V_DD/2**
> - **Start-up (Barkhausen) gain loop requirement**: **|Loop Gain| ≥ 1** with **phase shift = 0° (or 360°)**
> - **Phase noise**: measured in **dBc/Hz** at a given offset Δf from carrier f₀
> - **Jitter**: measured in **ps RMS** or **UI (Unit Interval)**

---

## 1.4 Why Clock Oscillators Belong in High-Speed Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEECT526, Module 4):** Modern serial links (PCIe Gen5/6, USB4, 100G Ethernet, DDR5) operate at multi-GHz symbol rates where **even 1 ps of timing uncertainty** can close the eye diagram. Clock oscillator phase noise and jitter directly determine the **horizontal eye opening**, making oscillator design a *digital-engineer's* problem, not just an analogue one.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Clock Oscillators

Clock oscillators are broadly classified by their **frequency-setting element** and **active gain device**:

### A. By Resonator Type
1. **Crystal Oscillator (XO)** — quartz crystal, ultra-stable, 1 kHz – 250 MHz fundamental.
2. **MEMS Oscillator** — silicon micromechanical resonator, CMOS-compatible.
3. **Ceramic Resonator Oscillator** — lower Q, lower cost, 1–60 MHz.
4. **LC / RC Oscillator** — on-chip, fully integrable, low-to-medium accuracy.
5. **Ring Oscillator** — odd-numbered inverter chain, VCO-friendly.

### B. By Output Waveform
- **Sine-wave** (low-jitter RF clocks for SerDes)
- **Square-wave / CMOS** (logic clocks for digital cores)

---

## 2.2 The Pierce Crystal Oscillator (KTU Favourite)

The **Pierce oscillator** is the most widely used crystal oscillator topology in digital systems.

### Circuit Topology
- Inverting amplifier (CMOS inverter or BJT common-emitter)
- Crystal **X₁** in the **feedback path** (between output and input of the inverter)
- Two external capacitors **C₁, C₂** to ground at each terminal of the crystal (load capacitance)
- Bias resistor **R_B** across the inverter (sets DC bias)

### Operating Principle
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crystal is operated at its **series-resonance frequency f_s** where its impedance is purely resistive (R_s, the small motional resistance). The combination of **X₁ (inductor-like near f_s)**, **C₁**, and **C₂** forms a **π-network** that introduces the 180° phase inversion needed to close the loop (along with the inverting amplifier providing another 180°, totalling 360° = 0° loop phase).

---

## 2.3 Crystal Equivalent Model (KTU High-Yield)

A quartz crystal is modelled as the **Butterworth-Van Dyke** equivalent circuit:

| Component | Physical Meaning | Typical Value (32.768 kHz watch crystal) |
|---|---|---|
| **R_s** | Motional resistance (losses) | 10 – 50 kΩ |
| **L_m** | Motional inductance (mass analogy) | ~10 H (kHz), ~few mH (MHz) |
| **C_m** | Motional capacitance (stiffness) | ~few fF |
| **C_0** | Shunt (package) capacitance | 1 – 7 pF |

### Two Resonant Frequencies
> [!NOTE]
> **Series resonance:** f_s = 1 / (2π √(L_m C_m))
> **Parallel (anti-resonance):** f_a = (1 / 2π) √((C_m + C_0) / (L_m C_m C_0))
> **Pulling:** f_a − f_s ≈ f_s × (C_m / 2 C_0) — extremely small, hence high Q.

The **Quality Factor**:
$$
Q = \frac{1}{R_s} \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{C_m}}
$$

---

## 2.4 Barkhausen Criterion (Loop-Gain Condition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (Sustained Oscillation Theorem):**
> For a linear feedback oscillator to produce **sustained steady-state oscillations**, the **loop gain** must satisfy **both** conditions at the oscillation frequency f₀:
> 1. **Magnitude condition:** $\vert H(j\omega_0) \cdot A(j\omega_0) \vert = 1$
> 2. **Phase condition:** $\angle H(j\omega_0) + \angle A(j\omega_0) = 0^\circ \pm 360^\circ n$

Where **A** is the forward amplifier gain and **H** is the feedback network transfer function.

For **start-up** (cold start), the loop gain magnitude must **exceed unity**:
$$
\vert H(j\omega_0) \cdot A(j\omega_0) \vert > 1
$$

Nonlinearity of the active device (gain compression) then automatically stabilises the loop to exactly unity at steady state.

---

## 2.5 Start-Up Condition (KTU-Marked Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Crystal Oscillator Start-Up Inequality (Pierce):**
> $$R_s < \frac{1}{\omega_0^2 \, C_1 \, C_2 \, R_L \, (R_s + R_L)}$$
> Simplified rule-of-thumb: **R_s must be less than the negative resistance magnitude $-\Re\{Z_{in}\}$** presented by the active + π-network at the crystal terminal.

The **negative resistance** $-R_N$ must exceed **R_s** by a safety factor of **3× to 5×** to guarantee reliable start across temperature, supply, and process corners.

$$
-R_N > 3 \, R_s \quad \text{(start-up margin)}
$$

---

## 2.6 Ring Oscillator (VCO Building Block)

A **ring oscillator** consists of an **odd number N** of inverting gain stages in a closed loop. Each stage contributes a propagation delay **t_d**, giving an oscillation period:

$$
T = 2 \cdot N \cdot t_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{osc} = \frac{1}{2 N t_d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage-Controlled Ring Oscillator (VCO):** If each stage is a current-starved inverter where the bias current **I_bias** controls t_d, then:
> $$f_{osc}(V_{ctrl}) = \frac{I_{bias}(V_{ctrl})}{2 N C_L V_{sw}}$$
> where **V_sw** is the logic swing and **C_L** is the load capacitance.

The **tuning sensitivity (gain) K_VCO** is:
$$
K_{VCO} = \frac{\partial f_{osc}}{\partial V_{ctrl}} \quad \text{[Hz / V]}
$$

---

## 2.7 Phase Noise and Jitter (Modern High-Speed Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Noise L(f)** is the single-sideband noise power in a 1-Hz bandwidth at offset Δf from the carrier f₀, normalised to the carrier power — units **dBc/Hz**.

### Leeson's Equation (Empirical)
$$
\mathcal{L}(\Delta f) = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{f_0}{2 Q \Delta f} \right)^2 \left( \frac{F k T}{P_s} + 1 \right) \right]
$$
where:
- **F** = noise factor of the amplifier
- **kT** = thermal noise power (−174 dBm/Hz at 300 K)
- **P_s** = carrier power
- **Q** = loaded quality factor

### Jitter from Phase Noise
$$
\sigma_{j,rms} = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_0} \sqrt{2 \int_{f_1}^{f_2} 10^{\mathcal{L}(\Delta f)/10} \, d(\Delta f)}
$$

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value to preserve markdown tables:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Series resonance | $f_s = 1 / (2\pi\sqrt{L_m C_m})$ | Hz |
| 2 | Parallel resonance | $f_a = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{(C_m+C_0)/(L_m C_m C_0)}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Quality factor | $Q = (1/R_s)\sqrt{L_m/C_m}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Barkhausen (steady) | $\vert A \cdot H \vert = 1$, $\angle(AH) = 0^\circ$ | linear feedback |
| 5 | Barkhausen (start-up) | $\vert A \cdot H \vert > 1$ | cold-start |
| 6 | Ring osc. frequency | $f_{osc} = 1/(2 N t_d)$ | N = odd integer |
| 7 | VCO gain | $K_{VCO} = \partial f_{osc}/\partial V_{ctrl}$ | Hz/V |
| 8 | Start-up margin | $-R_N > 3 R_s$ | negative resistance |
| 9 | Leeson's phase noise | $\mathcal{L}(\Delta f) = 10\log_{10}[(1/2)(f_0/2Q\Delta f)^2(FkT/P_s+1)]$ | dBc/Hz |
| 10 | Jitter–phase-noise | $\sigma_j = (1/2\pi f_0)\sqrt{2\int 10^{\mathcal{L}/10}\,d\Delta f}$ | seconds (RMS) |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in industry?**
> - **DDR5 memory** uses a 4800 MT/s clock where **jitter budgets are < 1 ps RMS**.
> - **PCIe Gen6** at 64 GT/s uses a **reference clock with sub-100 fs RMS phase jitter**.
> - **5G base-station** PLLs require **−120 dBc/Hz @ 100 kHz offset**.
> - **Crystal-less IoT MCUs** use **on-chip MEMS or ring oscillators** with active temperature compensation.
> - **Automotive ADAS** needs **±50 ppm** clock stability across −40 °C to +125 °C (AEC-Q100).
