# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define clock jitter. Differentiate between period jitter and cycle-to-cycle jitter.**

**Model Answer:**

**Clock jitter** is the short-term deviation of a clock edge from its ideal position in time, measured at the 50% V<sub>DD</sub> threshold. It is quantified as the standard deviation of the time error $\Delta t_n$ over many clock cycles and is expressed in picoseconds (ps) or unit intervals (UI).

**Period jitter** $J_{period}(n) = T_n - T_{clk}$ measures how much *one specific period* $T_n$ deviates from the *nominal* ideal period $T_{clk}$. It retains low-frequency drift and long-term frequency error.

**Cycle-to-cycle jitter** $J_{C2C}(n) = T_{n+1} - T_n$ measures the deviation of *one period* from the *immediately preceding* period. It is the first-difference of period jitter and therefore **removes low-frequency drift**, isolating high-frequency phase noise. For white noise, $\sigma_{C2C} = \sqrt{2}\,\sigma_{period}$.

| Aspect | Period Jitter | Cycle-to-Cycle Jitter |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Nominal $T_{clk}$ | Previous period $T_n$ |
| Captures drift? | Yes | No |
| Std. deviation (white noise) | $\sigma$ | $\sqrt{2}\sigma$ |

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the dual-Dirac jitter model? Why is it used in high-speed serial-link compliance testing?**

**Model Answer:**

The **dual-Dirac model** is a statistical model that represents the total jitter PDF as the convolution of two equal Gaussian (Random Jitter) humps centered at $\pm\mu$ with peak-to-peak DJ of $2\mu$. It is mathematically:

$$p_{total}(t) = \frac{1}{2\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(t - \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(t + \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)\right]$$

**Why it is used:**

1. **Separates** the bounded deterministic part (DJ) from the unbounded random part (RJ) using a single histogram fit.
2. **Extrapolates** measured jitter to very low BER values (e.g., $10^{-12}$ or $10^{-15}$) that would otherwise require impractically long measurement times.
3. **Standardized** by IEEE / OIF / INCITS for compliance of standards such as **PCIe, SATA, USB 3.x, and 100G Ethernet**.
4. Allows the Q-factor based $BER = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})$ calculation, which feeds into the **bathtub curve** and **eye mask** tests.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Full 14-Mark Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) (7 Marks) Derive the relationship between phase noise spectral density $S_{\phi}(f)$ and the RMS time-domain jitter $\sigma_j$ for a sinusoidal clock. State clearly all assumptions used.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The oscillator output is $v(t) = V_0\cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi(t))$, where $\phi(t)$ is a zero-mean, small-amplitude random phase noise.
2. $\phi(t)$ is a wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process.
3. The single-sideband phase noise is $\mathcal{L}(f) = S_{\phi}(f)/2$, where $S_{\phi}(f)$ is the two-sided PSD in rad²/Hz.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous time error corresponding to a phase fluctuation $\phi$ is:

$$\Delta t = \frac{\phi}{2\pi f_0}$$

**Step 2:** The PSD of the time error, using the property that multiplication by a constant scales PSD by the constant squared:

$$S_{\Delta t}(f) = \frac{S_{\phi}(f)}{(2\pi f_0)^{2}} = \frac{S_{\phi}(f)}{4\pi^{2}f_0^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** The mean-square time error (variance) is the integral of the PSD over the offset frequency range of interest $[f_1, f_2]$:

$$\sigma_j^{2} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_{\Delta t}(f) \, df = \frac{1}{4\pi^{2}f_0^{2}} \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_{\phi}(f) \, df$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $S_{\phi}(f) = 2\mathcal{L}(f)$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_j = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0}\sqrt{2\int_{f_1}^{f_2} \mathcal{L}(f) \, df}}$$

**Final statement:** RMS jitter is proportional to the square root of integrated phase noise and **inversely proportional to carrier frequency**.

---

**(b) (7 Marks) A 2.5 GHz oscillator has the following phase noise specification: $\mathcal{L}(f) = -80$ dBc/Hz for $f$ from 10 kHz to 1 MHz, and rolls off as $1/f^{2}$ for $f$ from 1 MHz to 10 MHz. Compute the RMS jitter in picoseconds and the corresponding UI value.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert dBc/Hz to linear:**

$$\mathcal{L}(f) = 10^{-80/10} = 10^{-8} \text{ rad}^{2}/\text{Hz} \quad \text{(flat from 10 kHz to 1 MHz)}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate the flat portion ($f_1$ to $f_2$ = 10 kHz to 1 MHz):**

$$I_1 = 10^{-8} \cdot (10^{6} - 10^{4}) = 10^{-8} \cdot 9.9 \times 10^{5} = 9.9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate the $1/f^{2}$ roll-off portion (1 MHz to 10 MHz):**

The phase noise is $\mathcal{L}(f) = 10^{-8} \cdot (10^{6}/f)^{2} = 10^{4}/f^{2}$ for $f$ in Hz.

$$I_2 = \int_{10^{6}}^{10^{7}} \frac{10^{4}}{f^{2}} df = 10^{4} \left[-\frac{1}{f}\right]_{10^{6}}^{10^{7}} = 10^{4}\left(10^{-6} - 10^{-7}\right) = 10^{4} \cdot 9 \times 10^{-7} = 9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Total two-sided integral:**

$$\int S_{\phi}(f) df = 2(I_1 + I_2) = 2(9.9 \times 10^{-3} + 9 \times 10^{-3}) = 2 \cdot 18.9 \times 10^{-3} = 37.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute RMS jitter:**

$$\sigma_j = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{9}} \sqrt{37.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\sigma_j = \frac{1}{1.5708 \times 10^{10}} \cdot 0.19441 = 1.2376 \times 10^{-11} \text{ s} = 12.376 \text{ ps (rms)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute UI:**

$$UI = \frac{1}{f_0} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = 400 \text{ ps}$$

$$J_{UI} = \frac{12.376}{400} = 0.03094 \text{ UI} = 3.094\% \text{ UI}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_j \approx 12.38 \text{ ps (rms)}, \quad J_{UI} \approx 0.0309 \text{ UI}}$$

---

### Question B — Alternative Full 14-Mark Question `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) (7 Marks) Explain the different types of jitter: Period jitter, Cycle-to-cycle jitter, Phase jitter, and TIE. Show their mathematical relationships.**

**Model Solution:**

Let the $n$-th ideal clock edge be at time $t_n^{ideal} = nT$, where $T$ is the nominal period.

**1. Period Jitter (Absolute):**

$$J_{period}(n) = T_n - T = (t_{n+1} - t_n) - T$$

This measures the deviation of the *current* period from the *nominal* period. It includes any slow drift in the oscillator.

**2. Cycle-to-Cycle (C2C) Jitter:**

$$J_{C2C}(n) = T_{n+1} - T_n = (t_{n+2} - t_{n+1}) - (t_{n+1} - t_n)$$

This is the first difference of period jitter. It removes low-frequency drift because subtracting adjacent periods cancels the slowly varying component.

**3. Time Interval Error (TIE) / Phase Jitter:**

$$TIE(n) = t_n - nT = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} J_{period}(k)$$

The TIE is the **cumulative sum** of period jitter (plus an initial offset). It measures how far the $n$-th edge has drifted, in time, from where it *should* be.

**4. Mathematical Relationships (box of three equations):**

$$\boxed{J_{C2C}(n) = J_{period}(n+1) - J_{period}(n) = TIE(n+2) - 2 \cdot TIE(n+1) + TIE(n)}$$

$$\boxed{TIE(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} J_{period}(k)}$$

**Statistical behaviour for white noise with variance $\sigma^2_{period}$:**

$$\sigma^{2}_{C2C} = 2\sigma^{2}_{period}$$

$$\sigma^{2}_{TIE}(N) = N\cdot\sigma^{2}_{period} \quad \text{(random walk)}$$

This means TIE grows as $\sqrt{N}$ for random walk behaviour, which is critical in long-pattern PRBS (Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence) testing for serial links.

---

**(b) (7 Marks) With the dual-Dirac model, derive the total peak-to-peak jitter at a target BER of $10^{-12}$. An oscillator has measured RMS RJ of 2.5 ps and DJ (peak-to-peak) of 8 ps. Determine if the link meets a 50 ps total-jitter budget.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Dual-Dirac Model Recap:**

In the dual-Dirac model, the total jitter PDF is two Gaussians centered at $\pm\mu$ where $\mu = J_{DJ,p-p}/2$. The Q-factor is:

$$Q = \frac{\mu}{\sigma_{RJ}} = \frac{J_{DJ,p-p}}{2\sigma_{RJ}}$$

**Step 2 — BER Formula:**

$$BER = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{J_{DJ,p-p}}{2\sqrt{2}\,\sigma_{RJ}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Total Jitter at Given BER:**

The total peak-to-peak jitter at BER is the sum of DJ and twice the Gaussian tail $\alpha\sigma$:

$$J_{total}(BER) = J_{DJ, p-p} + 2\alpha_{BER}\cdot\sigma_{RJ}$$

For $BER = 10^{-12}$, $\alpha_{BER} \approx 7.03$ (this is the standard table value).

**Step 4 — Substitute the given values:**

Given:
- $J_{DJ, p-p} = 8$ ps
- $\sigma_{RJ} = 2.5$ ps
- $\alpha = 7.03$

$$J_{total}(10^{-12}) = 8 + 2 \cdot 7.03 \cdot 2.5 = 8 + 35.15 = 43.15 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 5 — Compare to Budget:**

Budget = 50 ps. Computed jitter = 43.15 ps.

$$43.15 \text{ ps} < 50 \text{ ps} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{LINK PASSES}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{J_{total}(10^{-12}) = 43.15 \text{ ps} < 50 \text{ ps budget} \quad \Rightarrow \text{LINK COMPLIES}}$$

The link **passes** the jitter compliance test with a margin of $50 - 43.15 = 6.85$ ps.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in front of $\mathcal{L}(f)$ in the phase-noise-to-jitter formula. Single-sideband $\mathcal{L}$ is *half* the two-sided PSD $S_{\phi}$. The correct formula has $2\int\mathcal{L}(f)\,df$, not $\int\mathcal{L}(f)\,df$. (Common loss: **2 marks**)
> 2. **Mixing period, C2C, and TIE** without converting the variances. If the problem gives $\sigma_{period}$ and asks for $\sigma_{C2C}$, you must use $\sigma_{C2C} = \sqrt{2}\,\sigma_{period}$. (Common loss: **1 mark**)
> 3. **Not specifying units** in the final answer. A bare number "43.15" is incomplete; write "43.15 ps".
> 4. **Confusing BER with the probability of edge exceeding bound.** A single DJ peak is a *delta*, so the BER comes from the *Gaussian tail* alone, not the delta directly. The Q-factor derivation must be shown.
> 5. **Using $\alpha = 6$ or 7 indiscriminately** — use 7.03 specifically for $10^{-12}$ BER; do not round to 7.
> 6. **In the dual-Dirac derivation, omitting the $1/2$ factor** in the BER expression. The two delta peaks each contribute half the BER, so the total is $BER = \text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})/2$, **not** $\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Clock jitter** = deviation $\Delta t$ of clock edge from ideal time; measured in ps or UI at the **50% V<sub>DD</sub>** threshold.
- [ ] **Period jitter** = $T_n - T_{clk}$; **C2C** = $T_{n+1} - T_n$; **TIE** = $\sum$ period jitter.
- [ ] **White-noise relation:** $\sigma_{C2C} = \sqrt{2}\,\sigma_{period}$; TIE grows as $\sigma\sqrt{N}$.
- [ ] **Phase-noise-to-jitter** master formula: $\sigma_j = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_0}\sqrt{2\int_{f_1}^{f_2}\mathcal{L}(f)\,df}$.
- [ ] **Dual-Dirac** model: $BER = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})$; $Q = J_{DJ,p-p}/(2\sigma_{RJ})$.
- [ ] **Total jitter** at BER: $J_{total}(BER) = J_{DJ,p-p} + 2\alpha_{BER}\sigma_{RJ}$, with $\alpha = 7.03$ at $10^{-12}$ and $8.26$ at $10^{-16}$.
- [ ] **Random jitter (RJ)** = unbounded, Gaussian, from thermal/shot noise.
- [ ] **Deterministic jitter (DJ)** = bounded, includes DDJ, PJ, DCD, BUJ.
- [ ] **ADC SNR limit** from jitter: $SNR = -20\log_{10}(2\pi f_{in}\sigma_j)$ dB.
- [ ] **Unit Interval** $UI = 1/f_{clk}$ (NRZ) or $1/(2\cdot DR)$ (PAM4/half-rate).
- [ ] **Jitter transfer** of a PLL: high-frequency jitter is attenuated by the loop, low-frequency wander passes through.
- [ ] **Eye diagram** width = $1 - J_{total,p-p} \cdot DR$ (NRZ).
- [ ] **Reduce jitter by:** (i) higher carrier, (ii) lower phase noise (better VCO, narrower loop BW), (iii) better supply decoupling, (iv) controlled-impedance clock traces, (v) jitter cleaners in the clock path.
